item.title: US, others express concern over violations of ceasefires in eastern Congo
item.description: WASHINGTON, March 5 (Reuters) - The United ‚ÄčStates, European ‚ÄĆUnion and others said ‚Äčin ‚Äča joint statement on ‚Ā†Thursday ‚Äčthey were ‚Äčprofoundly concerned about "continued and recent" ‚Äčviolations of ‚Äčceasefires in eastern ‚ÄĆDemocratic ‚Ā†Republic of Congo and called ‚Äčon ‚Äčall ‚Ā†parties to recommit ‚Äčto end ‚Äčfighting ‚Ā†and return to ‚Ā†negotiations.

item.title: Flight to bring French nationals from UAE forced back, minister says
item.description:
PARIS, March 5 (Reuters) - An Air France flight chartered ‚Äčby the French government ‚ÄĆto bring French nationals back from the United ‚ÄčArab Emirates was ‚Äčforced to turn back ‚Ā†on Thursday due to ‚Äčmissile fire in ‚Äčthe area, French Transport Minister Philippe Tabarot said on X.
"This ‚Äčsituation reflects the ‚Äčinstability in the region and ‚ÄĆthe ‚Ā†complexity of repatriation operations," Tabarot added.
France started r epatriation flights from the ‚ÄčMiddle ‚ÄčEast ‚Ā†on Wednesday as governments rush to ‚Äčbring home tens ‚Äčof ‚Ā†thousands of citizens stranded by the intensifying ‚Ā†U.S. ‚Äčand Israeli ‚Äčconflict with Iran.

item.title: Trump ousts Kristi Noem, names Oklahoma senator as homeland security nominee
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 5 (Reuters) - Kristi Noem, who oversaw U.S. President Donald Trump's aggressive immigration crackdown and faced bipartisan criticism in hearings this week, will leave her role as homeland security chief at the end ‚Äčof the month.
Trump will tap Oklahoma Senator Markwayne Mullin to replace her, he said on his Truth Social platform, a move that would require U.S. Senate ‚ÄĆconfirmation.
Noem, a former governor of South Dakota, became one of Trump's most high-profile Cabinet secretaries with social media posts that portrayed immigrants in harsh terms, highlighting cases of alleged criminal offenders and using vitriolic language.
She faced criticism in January when she quickly labeled two U.S. citizens fatally shot by federal immigration agents in Minneapolis as committing "domestic terrorism." Videos that emerged after the deaths undercut the assertion by Noem and other Trump officials that ‚Äčthe two deceased - Renee Good and Alex Pretti - were violent aggressors.
The public backlash for deaths led the Trump administration to move to a more targeted approach to immigration ‚Äčenforcement in Minnesota after months of sweeps through U.S. cities that led to violent clashes with residents opposing the crackdown.
Democrats in the U.S. House ‚Ā†of Representatives moved to impeach Noem and at least two Republicans in Congress called for her to lose her job after the incidents. During congressional hearings in March, Democrats and some Republicans ‚Äčcriticized Noem for her approach to the immigration crackdown and management of DHS, including concern over a $220 million ad campaign that heavily featured Noem.
Trump told Reuters on Thursday that he did not ‚Äčsign off on the ad campaign.
"I never knew anything about it," he said in a phone interview.
The ads prominently featured Noem, including a scene of her on horseback at Mount Rushmore in her home state of South Dakota.
In one of the congressional hearings this week, U.S. Senator John Kennedy, a Republican from Louisiana, asked Noem if Trump had approved the commercials.
"The president approved ahead of time you spending $220 million running TV ‚Äčads across the country in which you are featured prominently?" Kennedy asked Noem.
"Yes, sir. We went through the legal processes, did it correctly," she replied.
The staffing change raises questions about whether the Trump administration could seek to intensify its mass deportation push or retreat to a more targeted approach. Under Noem's leadership, masked immigration agents surged into Los Angeles, ‚ÄĆChicago and Washington, ‚Ā†D.C. , scouring neighborhoods and Home Depot parking lots in search of possible immigration offenders.
Mullin, who spent a decade in the House of Representatives before becoming a senator in 2023, also supports Trump's hardline immigration agenda. To become DHS secretary, Mullin would require Senate confirmation.
The popularity of Trump's immigration approach fell as agents detained U.S. citizens and tear-gassed streets in an attempt to drive up deportations, which last year fell short of the administration's goal of 1 million per year.
While Noem, 54, served as a prominent proponent of Trump's agenda, White House Deputy Chief of Staff Stephen ‚ÄčMiller, a long-time Trump aide, controls Trump's immigration policy.
Noem ‚Äčwas quickly confirmed to lead the 260,000-employee ‚Ā†Department of Homeland Security in January 2025 after Trump took office. On social media, she referred to immigrants convicted of crimes as "scumbags" even as the number of non-criminals arrested by immigration authorities rose under Trump.
She joined immigration enforcement operations on the ground in New York City and visited a ‚Äčmaximum-security prison in El Salvador where Venezuelan immigrants deported by the Trump administration were being held without charges or access to lawyers.
The ‚Äčnumber of migrants caught trying ‚Ā†to illegally cross the U.S.-Mexico border plummeted under Trump's restrictive policies, a steep drop after high levels of illegal immigration under former President Joe Biden, a Democrat.
Noem, reflecting Trump's agenda, also took steps to cut legal immigration programs and increase vetting. She ended several Temporary Protected Status programs that provided work permits to hundreds of thousands of immigrants from Venezuela, Haiti and other nations, drawing ‚Ā†legal challenges.
After an ‚ÄčAfghan immigrant was accused of attacking National Guard members in Washington, D.C., Noem said she recommended that Trump ‚Äčplace "a full travel ban on every damn country that‚Äôs been flooding our nation with killers, leeches, and entitlement junkies."
Critics said Noem demonized immigrants and promoted an immigration enforcement strategy that targeted non-criminal, working immigrants and families.
During Noem's tenure, ‚Äčthe number of deaths in immigration detention rose to a two-decade high while staff in DHS oversight offices were slashed sharply.

item.title: US military says it has sunk over 30 Iranian ships so far
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 5 (Reuters) - The U.S. military said on ‚ÄčThursday it has ‚ÄĆsunk over 30 Iranian ships so far during ‚Äčthe U.S. ‚Äčand Israel's war against Iran, ‚Ā†including an Iranian ‚Äčdrone ship that ‚Äčis on fire.
Ballistic missile attacks by Iran have decreased ‚Äčby 90% since ‚Äčthe first day of the ‚ÄĆwar, ‚Ā†U.S. Admiral Brad Cooper, who leads U.S. forces in the ‚ÄčMiddle ‚ÄčEast ‚Ā†as the head of Central ‚ÄčCommand, told reporters.

item.title: Syria opens Mediterranean-Aleppo air corridor that could help ease regional snarl
item.description:
DAMASCUS, March 5 (Reuters) - Syria has opened a newly reactivated air corridor from the northern city of Aleppo toward the Mediterranean Sea for use by ‚Äčforeign airlines as well as its national carrier, the head of ‚ÄĆthe country‚Äôs Civil Aviation Authority told Reuters on Thursday, as air traffic gradually resumes through Aleppo International Airport.
Civil Aviation Authority director Omar Hosari said the route is intended to provide a safe flight path for ‚Äčaircraft arriving at and departing from Aleppo and will be available to ‚Äčany airline as long as it ‚Äúmeets international safety standards.‚ÄĚ
‚ÄúThe corridors are ‚Ā†not dedicated exclusively to Syrian Air,‚ÄĚ said Hosari. ‚ÄúOther airlines can use them to transit ‚Äčor operate through Syrian airspace according to the usual regulatory procedures.‚ÄĚ
In a Thursday ‚Äčstatement, the authority said it had also reopened air routes in the northern part of Syrian airspace toward Turkey after conducting operational and technical assessments and reviewing regional developments affecting ‚Äčaviation.
The authority said the first Syrian Air flight departed Aleppo for Istanbul ‚Äčon Thursday and arrived safely, marking the gradual resumption of operations at Aleppo International Airport. ‚ÄĆA flight ‚Ā†from Aleppo to the Saudi city of Jeddah is scheduled for Friday, with authorities studying the addition of other destinations including Riyadh.
Hosari said Royal Jordanian is expected to use the Mediterranean corridor on Friday for a flight from ‚ÄčAmman to Aleppo as ‚Äčpart of the ‚Ā†phased return of air traffic.
Commercial air traffic remained largely absent across much of the Middle East, with major Gulf ‚Äčhubs - including Doha and Dubai, the world's busiest airport for ‚Äčinternational passengers - ‚Ā†largely shut for a sixth straight day after the U.S.-Israeli launched air strikes on Iran , which responded with retaliatory missile and drone strikes on the entire region.
A week ‚Ā†into ‚Äčthe regional conflict, Syria is emerging relatively sidelined, ‚Äčwith Iranian presence ceased after the fall of President Bashar al-Assad in late 2024, and U.S. military bases ‚Äčin the country evacuated last February.

item.title: Trump: want to finish Iran then Cuba 'question of time'
item.description:
March 5 (Reuters) - U.S. ‚ÄčPresident Donald Trump said ‚ÄĆon Thursday he wants to finish the war ‚Äčin Iran first, ‚Äčbut then it "will be ‚Ā†just a question ‚Äčof time before you ‚Äčand a lot of unbelievable people are going to ‚Äčbe going back ‚Äčto Cuba."
Trump, speaking at an event ‚ÄĆwith ‚Ā†the Inter Miami soccer team at the White House, said ‚ÄčCuba wants "to ‚Äčmake ‚Ā†a deal so badly."
"We want ‚Äčto finish this ‚Äčone ‚Ā†first," he said, referring to the conflict ‚Ā†in ‚ÄčIran.

item.title: Oilfield in Iraqi Kurdistan halts production after drone strike, sources say
item.description:
March 5 (Reuters) - A drone attack struck an oilfield operated by U.S. firm HKN Energy in Iraq‚Äôs ‚ÄčKurdistan region on Thursday, causing a fire ‚ÄĆand halting production, security sources and an oilfield engineer said.
The Sarsang field produces about 30,000 barrels of oil per ‚Äčday and is operated by HKN Energy, ‚Äčwhich has a 62% stake. HKN is a ‚Ā†privately held U.S. oil and gas company owned ‚Äčby Hillwood Energy, part of the Hillwood group ‚Äčfounded by Ross Perot Jr.
No group claimed responsibility, but Kurdish officials accused Iraqi militias allied with Iran of carrying out ‚Äčthe attack.
If so, the attack would mean Iran‚ÄĎaligned ‚ÄčIraqi militias, who have vowed to retaliate for the killing ‚ÄĆof ‚Ā†Iran‚Äôs supreme leader, have expanded targets from U.S. military bases in Iraqi Kurdistan to U.S. energy interests.
Production at the field was halted as a precaution ‚Äčafter an ‚Äčexplosion at ‚Ā†its power unit, the engineer told Reuters.
Kurdistan regional authorities confirmed the attack and ‚Äčsaid it was carried out by two ‚Äčdrones.
Some ‚Ā†energy companies operating in Iraqi Kurdistan shut oil and gas production at their fields as a precaution ‚Ā†after ‚Äčthe United States and Israel ‚Äčlaunched strikes on neighbouring Iran.

item.title: Zelenskiy says Ukraine will help US to counter Iranian drones
item.description:
KYIV, March 5 (Reuters) - President Volodymyr Zelenskiy said on Thursday that Ukraine would provide assistance to the U.S. in response to its request for help in dealing ‚Äčwith Iranian drones in the Middle East.
Tehran has fired hundreds of drones at ‚ÄĆU.S. targets in neighboring countries after the U.S. and Israel began a massive campaign of air strikes against Iran on Saturday.
During its war with Russia, Ukraine has developed highly ‚Äčeffective means of downing the Iranian-designed Shahed kamikaze drone, which Moscow has ‚Äčbeen using for much of the four-year war.
"We received a ‚Ā†request from the United States for specific support in protection against 'Shaheds' in ‚Äčthe Middle East region," Zelenskiy wrote in English on the X social media ‚Äčplatform.
"I gave instructions to provide the necessary means and ensure the presence of Ukrainian specialists who can guarantee the required security."
The U.S. had requested assistance in protecting its bases and ‚Äčsoldiers in certain countries in the region, according to a source familiar ‚Äčwith the matter, who said Zelenskiy had instructed his military to do so.
Ukrainians would start ‚ÄĆwork "in ‚Ā†the next few days", the source said.
In a telephone interview with Reuters on Thursday, U.S. President Donald Trump said that he would take assistance from any country, when asked about Zelenskiy's offer of support to help defend against Iranian drones.
Zelenskiy ‚Äčexpressed concern earlier ‚Äčthis week that ‚Ā†the war in Iran - if it proves long and intense - could diminish weapons supplies to Ukraine from its Western ‚Äčallies, in particular the air defence systems it needs to ‚Äčprotect against ‚Ā†Russian missiles.
In recent months, Ukraine has already experienced shortages of the U.S.-made Patriot air defence systems - the only effective weapon in its arsenal for downing Russia's ‚Ā†ballistic missiles.
On ‚ÄčTuesday, Zelenskiy said Ukraine would be open to ‚Äčexchanging interceptor drones for air defence missiles from the Middle East allies, which have their own ‚Äčstocks of Patriots.

item.title: Canada, EU sign deal to modernize economic and trade agreement
item.description:
TORONTO, March 5 (Reuters) - Canada and the European Union ‚Äčagreed on Thursday ‚ÄĆto expand trade ties and ease trade ‚Äčrules under their ‚Äčexisting pact as countries ‚Ā†across the world ‚Äčstrive to increase trade ‚Äčand diversify away from the United States.
Canadian Trade ‚ÄčMinister Maninder Sidhu ‚Äčand European Union Commissioner for ‚ÄĆTrade ‚Ā†and Economic Security MaroŇ° Ň†efńćovińć signed an agreement in Toronto ‚Äčon ‚Äčsettling ‚Ā†investment disputes and launched talks on ‚Äča digital ‚Äčtrade ‚Ā†deal that would support data sharing, digital ‚Ā†services, ‚Äčcybersecurity cooperation ‚Äčand emerging technologies.

item.title: Iran calling US about a deal, says Trump
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 5 (Reuters) - President Donald Trump on Thursday said Tehran was reaching out to ‚Äčthe United States about making a deal amid ‚ÄĆU.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran, adding that further action to reduce pressure on oil was imminent.
"They're calling, they're saying 'how do we ‚Äčmake a deal?' I said you're being ‚Äča little bit late," said Trump, speaking at ‚Ā†an event with the Inter Miami soccer team ‚Äčat the White House.
Trump touted the U.S. ‚Äčmilitary actions in Iran, saying they were destroying Tehran's missile and drone capability and that "their navy is gone - 24 ships ‚Äčin three days," as he called on Iranian ‚Äčdiplomats to request asylum and help shape a better country.
"We also ‚ÄĆurge ‚Ā†Iranian diplomats around the world to request asylum and to help us shape a new and better Iran," he said.
Iran's mission to the United Nations ‚Äčin New ‚ÄčYork declined to ‚Ā†comment.
While Trump said oil had "pretty much stabilized," he said further action ‚Äčto reduce pressure on it was imminent.
The U.S. ‚ÄčTreasury ‚Ā†Department is expected to announce measures as soon as Thursday aimed at combating rising energy prices, including potential action involving the ‚Ā†oil ‚Äčfutures market, a senior White ‚ÄčHouse official said earlier on Thursday.

item.title: US not expanding military objectives in Iran, Hegseth says
item.description:
TAMPA, Florida, March 5 (Reuters) - U.S. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said ‚Äčon Thursday the United States was ‚ÄĆnot expanding its military objectives in Iran, after President Donald Trump told Reuters that the United States ‚Äčmust be involved in choosing the ‚Äčnext leader of Iran .
The Pentagon earlier this week ‚Ā†said the military campaign, known as ‚ÄčOperation Epic Fury, is focused on destroying ‚ÄčIran's offensive missiles, missile production and Navy, while not allowing Tehran to have a nuclear weapon.
"There's no ‚Äčexpansion in our objectives. We know exactly ‚Äčwhat we're trying to achieve," Hegseth said.
He added that ‚ÄĆTrump ‚Ā†was "having a heck of a say in who runs Iran given the ongoing operation."
In a telephone interview with Reuters on Thursday, ‚ÄčTrump said ‚Äčthe United ‚Ā†States would have to help pick the next person to ‚Äčlead the country.
The United States has ‚Äčhit ‚Ā†more than 2,000 targets in Iran over the past six days, including Iranian warships.

item.title: Ukraine says Ukrainian bank employees detained with cash shipment in Hungary
item.description:
March 6 (Reuters) - Ukrainian Foreign Minister Andrii Sybiha accused Hungary early on ‚ÄčFriday of detaining seven employees ‚ÄĆof Ukraine's state savings bank while they were transporting cash from Austria ‚Äčback to Ukraine.
Andrii Sybiha ‚Äčwas writing on the X social ‚Ā†media platform after Hungarian ‚ÄčPrime Minister Viktor Orban said Budapest ‚Äčwould force Ukraine with "political and financial tools" to reopen the Druzhba pipeline carrying ‚ÄčRussian oil to Hungarian ‚Äčrefineries.
Sybiha said the employees were travelling in ‚ÄĆtwo ‚Ā†cars through Hungary when they were detained. He said their whereabouts were unknown at the moment.
"In ‚Äčfact, we ‚Äčare ‚Ā†talking about Hungary taking hostages and stealing money," ‚ÄčSybiha wrote. "If this is ‚Äčthe 'force' ‚Ā†announced earlier today by Mr Orban, then this is a force ‚Ā†of ‚Äča criminal gang. ‚ÄčThis is state terrorism and racketeering."

item.title: US, Venezuela agree to re-establish diplomatic and consular relations
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 5 (Reuters) - The United States and interim ‚Äčauthorities in Venezuela have agreed ‚ÄĆto re-establish diplomatic and consular relations, the U.S. State Department said in a ‚Äčstatement on Thursday, adding that it ‚Äčwas focused on creating conditions ‚Ā†for a peaceful transition to a ‚Äčdemocratically elected government.
"This step will ‚Äčfacilitate our joint efforts to promote stability, support economic recovery, and advance political reconciliation ‚Äčin Venezuela," the State Department ‚Äčsaid.
"Our engagement is focused on helping the Venezuelan ‚ÄĆpeople ‚Ā†move forward through a phased process that creates the conditions for a peaceful transition to a democratically ‚Äčelected ‚Äčgovernment."
After months ‚Ā†of heightened tensions, the U.S. captured Venezuela's President ‚ÄčNicolas Maduro in January, setting ‚Äčoff ‚Ā†a chain of changes in the country, including the swearing-in of interim ‚Ā†President ‚ÄčDelcy Rodriguez.
The two ‚Äčcountries have since gradually resumed bilateral relations.

item.title: Kosovo's parliament fails to elect president, risking new election
item.description:
PRISTINA, March 5 (Reuters) - Kosovo's parliament ‚Äčmissed the deadline to elect a new president ‚ÄĆon Thursday, plunging the country into a fresh political crisis that could trigger another snap election.
The 120-seat assembly had ‚Äčuntil midnight to vote for a head ‚Äčof state, but Prime Minister Albin Kurti's ‚Ā†ruling party, Vetevendosje, was unable to bring opposition ‚Äčlawmakers on board and vote for its candidate.
The ‚Äčopposition parties have asked for a consensual candidate, but Kurti has nominated his foreign minister, Glauk Konjufca. "There is no quorum ‚Äčto continue this session... we cannot proceed with ‚Äčthe vote," speaker of the parliament Albulena Haxhiu said, noting ‚ÄĆthat ‚Ā†only 66 lawmakers were present, well below the 80 required.
If a snap election is called, it would be the third parliamentary vote in just over ‚Äča year. Kosovo ‚Äčalready held ‚Ā†a snap election on December 28 after failing to form a government following ‚Äčthe February 2025 poll.
The two largest ‚Äčopposition ‚Ā†parties, the Democratic Party of Kosovo and the Democratic League of Kosovo, have urged the president to call ‚Ā†fresh ‚Äčelections.
Outgoing President Vjosa Osmani, who ‚Äčhas another month in office, is expected to outline the way ‚Äčforward on Friday.

item.title: US House rejects war powers resolution, backs Trump on Iran war
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 5 (Reuters) - The U.S. House of Representatives rejected an effort on Thursday to stop President Donald Trump's air war ‚ÄĆon Iran and require that any hostilities against Iran be authorized by Congress, backing the Republican president's military campaign on the sixth day of the expanding conflict .
The vote was 219 to 212, largely along party lines, in the House, where Trump's fellow Republicans control a narrow majority of seats. ‚ÄčTwo Republicans voted in favor of the resolution and four Democrats voted against it.
Opponents accused Democrats of taking the issue ‚Äčto a vote only because they oppose Trump, putting Americans at increased risk.
"We all know that we ‚Ā†wouldn't be here today if the president's name wasn't Donald Trump," Representative Rick Crawford of Arizona, the Republican chairman of ‚Äčthe House Intelligence Committee, said during debate on Wednesday.
Sponsors of the resolution described it as a bid to take back Congress' ‚Äčresponsibility to authorize war, as spelled out in the U.S. Constitution.
The U.S. and Israel launched attacks on Iran on Saturday, a conflict that has killed more than 1,000 people, including at least six U.S. service members, and caused damage and instability throughout the Middle East.
Supporters said the resolution, ‚Äčby requiring Trump to come to Congress for a war authorization, would force him to explain to Americans why the ‚ÄčU.S. is fighting and how it might end.
"This is a war of choice, launched by this administration without authorization, without clearly stated objectives or ‚ÄĆa ‚Ā†defined endgame, and without explaining how they intend to keep Americans safe," said Representative Gregory Meeks of New York, the top Democrat on the Foreign Affairs Committee.
Just before the resolution vote, House members from both parties overwhelmingly passed a measure "Reaffirming Iran remains the largest state sponsor of terrorism."
The vote would not have stopped the conflict even if ‚Äčthe House had voted yes.
To go ‚Äčinto effect, the resolution ‚Ā†would also have had to pass the Senate and garner the two-thirds majorities needed to override Trump's expected veto.
The Senate, also narrowly controlled by Trump's party, backed his military campaign against Iran ‚Äčin a vote on Wednesday, voting to block a bipartisan resolution similar to the measure passed ‚Äčby the House. The ‚Ā†votes this week are not the end of the matter. The War Powers Resolution of 1973, which provides for votes on the resolutions, says a president can only involve the military in an armed conflict when Congress has declared war or provided specific authority ‚Ā†or in ‚Äčresponse to an attack.
Trump and his Republicans have argued that Iran posed an "imminent ‚Äčthreat" so that his actions were legal under that law.
However, the War Powers measure also requires unauthorized military actions to be terminated within 60 days, giving ‚Äčthe Trump administration a deadline at the end of April to seek Congress' approval.

item.title: Exclusive: US investigation points to likely US responsibility in Iran school strike, sources say
item.description:
March 5 (Reuters) - U.S. military investigators believe it is likely that U.S. forces were responsible for an apparent strike on an Iranian girls' school that killed scores of children on Saturday but have not yet reached a final conclusion or completed ‚Äčtheir investigation, two U.S. officials told Reuters.
Reuters was unable to determine more details about the investigation, including what evidence ‚ÄĆcontributed to the tentative assessment, what type of munition was used, who was responsible or why the U.S. might have struck the school.
U.S. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth on Wednesday acknowledged the U.S. military was investigating the incident.
The officials, who spoke on condition of anonymity to discuss sensitive military matters, did not rule out ‚Äčthe possibility that new evidence could emerge that absolves the U.S. of responsibility and points to another responsible party in ‚Äčthe incident.
Reuters could not determine how much longer the investigation would last or what evidence U.S. investigators are ‚Ā†seeking before the assessment can be completed.
The girls' school in Minab, in southern Iran, was hit on Saturday during the first day ‚Äčof U.S. and Israeli attacks on the country. Iran's ambassador to the U.N. in Geneva, Ali Bahreini, said the strike killed 150 students. Reuters ‚Äčcould not independently confirm the death toll.
The Pentagon referred questions from Reuters to the U.S. military‚Äôs Central Command, whose spokesperson, Captain Timothy Hawkins, said: ‚ÄúIt would be inappropriate to comment given the incident is under investigation.‚ÄĚ
The White House did not directly comment on the investigation, but press secretary Karoline Leavitt said in a statement ‚Äčto Reuters, ‚ÄúWhile the Department of War is currently investigating this matter, the Iranian regime targets civilians and children, not the United States ‚Äčof America.‚ÄĚ
Asked about the incident during a news briefing on Wednesday, Hegseth said: ‚ÄúWe‚Äôre investigating that. We, of course, never target civilian targets. But we‚Äôre taking a ‚ÄĆlook and ‚Ā†investigating that.‚ÄĚ
U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio told reporters on Monday that the United States would not deliberately target a school.
"The Department of War would be investigating that if that was our strike, and I would refer your question to them," Rubio said.
Israeli and U.S. forces have until now divided their attacks in Iran both geographically and by target type, a senior Israeli official and a source with direct ‚Äčknowledge of the joint planning said. ‚ÄčWhile Israel was striking missile ‚Ā†launch sites in western Iran, the United States was attacking such targets, as well as naval ones, in the south.
The U.N. human rights office, without saying who it believed was responsible for the strike on ‚Äčthe school, called on Tuesday for an investigation.
"The onus is on the forces that carried out ‚Äčthe attack to investigate ‚Ā†it," U.N. human rights office spokesperson Ravina Shamdasani told a press briefing in Geneva.
Images of the girls' funeral on Tuesday were shown on Iranian state television. Their small coffins were draped with Iranian flags and passed from a truck across a large crowd towards the grave site.
Deliberately attacking ‚Ā†a school ‚Äčor hospital or any other civilian structure would likely be a war crime under ‚Äčinternational humanitarian law.
If a U.S. role were to be confirmed, the strike would rank among the worst cases of civilian casualties in decades of U.S. conflicts in the ‚ÄčMiddle East.

item.title: Hezbollah warns Israeli residents to evacuate towns near border
item.description:
March 6 (Reuters) - Lebanon's Hezbollah warned Israeli residents to evacuate towns within ‚Äč5 km (3.11 miles) of the border between ‚ÄĆthe countries in a message posted on its Telegram channel in Hebrew early on ‚ÄčFriday.
Hezbollah's message came less than a ‚Äčday after Israel warned residents to ‚Ā†leave Beirut's southern suburbs, prompting an ‚Äčexodus from a swathe of the capital ‚Äčknown as Dahiyeh.
Lebanon was pulled into the war in the Middle East on Monday, when ‚ÄčHezbollah opened fire, sparking Israeli airstrikes ‚Äčfocused on Beirut's southern suburbs and on southern and ‚ÄĆeastern ‚Ā†Lebanon.
"Your military's aggression against Lebanese sovereignty and safe citizens, the destruction of civilian infrastructure and the expulsion campaign it ‚Äčis carrying ‚Äčout will ‚Ā†not go unchallenged," Hezbollah said.
Israel has said it will not ‚Äčevacuate its border towns and ‚Äčhas ‚Ā†sent more soldiers into Lebanon, saying this was a defensive measure meant to ‚Ā†protect ‚Äčits citizens who live ‚Äčnearby.

item.title: Partner in Malaysia‚Äôs ruling bloc says it is reviewing ties as rifts deepen over graft scandal
item.description:
KUALA LUMPUR, March 6 (Reuters) - A key partner in Malaysian Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim‚Äôs ruling coalition is reconsidering ‚Äčits role in the pact ahead of the next elections, a senior leader said, as frustration mounts among the premier‚Äôs reformist allies over the government‚Äôs ‚ÄĆresponse to a scandal at the anti-graft agency.
Over the last decade, Malaysia has been working to regain public and investor confidence after a multibillion-dollar fraud at state fund 1MDB left the country with years of debt and saw a former prime minister jailed.
While smaller in scale than 1MDB, allegations of widespread misconduct at the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) this year have renewed scrutiny on Anwar‚Äôs pledge to tackle graft, after the ‚Äčpremier resisted calls for a comprehensive probe.
The allegations, along with existing concerns over the slow progress of governance reforms , have pushed the Democratic Action Party (DAP) ‚Äď the largest party ‚Äčin the ruling bloc and a staunch supporter of Anwar through his more than two decades as a progressive opposition leader ‚Äď to ‚Ā†rethink its ties.
‚ÄúI expect the party to evaluate its position in the relevant coalitions when the time comes for elections, to determine the best strategy going forward,‚ÄĚ the DAP's ‚Äčdisciplinary chief Tony Pua told Reuters.
Pua, however, said the party will not withdraw support for the government this term as it does not want to cause political instability.
Anwar's office did ‚Äčnot respond to a request for comment. Anwar has said his administration is working hard to address governance issues and eliminate graft, while acknowledging the challenge of stamping out systemic corruption.
MACC chief Azam Baki has faced repeated calls to step down , after Bloomberg reported in separate articles last month that he may have breached shareholding rules for public officials, and that the agency has been helping ‚Äča group of businessmen to seize control of companies. Both Azam and the MACC have denied wrongdoing.
The government has formed a committee to probe the allegations, with its findings expected ‚Äčthis week.
But Anwar has so far dismissed calls by DAP for a royal commission of inquiry (RCI) into the broader misconduct claims, prompting warnings from coalition partners and analysts that a weak response risked alienating ‚ÄĆvoters, deepening ‚Ā†internal rifts and threatening the government‚Äôs stability.
"If this issue is not contained or solved quickly, it will be a major issue until the next election," said Adib Zalkapli of political risks consultancy Viewfinder Global Affairs.
Malaysia's national elections are due by early 2028, but two government lawmakers said Anwar could call for snap polls as early as July.
One of them, a member of Anwar's People's Justice Party (PKR), said they and several others were considering contesting the election as independents, having lost confidence in the premier's leadership. The lawmakers declined to be ‚Äčidentified due to the sensitivity of the ‚Äčmatter.
Hassan Karim, another PKR lawmaker, said full-scale ‚Ā†reforms were needed to address systemic corruption.
"The prime minister and his government must terminate the contract of the MACC chief immediately and promptly form the RCI," he said.
Anwar's premiership has been marked by internal disagreements among cabinet members, public discontent over rising living costs , ‚Äčand fears over a rollback in democratic freedoms.
While his administration has implemented some progressive reforms, such as abolishing the mandatory death penalty , ‚Äčothers have faltered.
The parliament ‚Ā†this week narrowly failed to pass a constitutional amendment that would limit the prime minister's term to 10 years, after several government-aligned lawmakers were absent for the vote.
Anwar's coalition was also trounced in a regional election in November, the first in several expected over the coming months that will test support for the premier ahead of national polls.
DAP's Pua described the November loss as a ‚Ā†shock to ‚Äčthe party, spurring a renewed push among its leaders for speedier reforms.
The party, which currently holds 40 seats ‚Äčcompared with PKR's 31, plans to meet in July to decide whether its leaders should resign from all government positions, including the cabinet - a move that Pua said was necessary if promised reforms were not implemented.
"If ‚Äčwe don't withdraw, the party will lose all credibility and can expect a punishing outcome from the elections," Pua said.

item.title: Three Australians were aboard US submarine that sank Iranian ship, PM Albanese says
item.description:
SYDNEY, March 6 (Reuters) - Prime Minister Anthony Albanese said on Friday that three Australian defence personnel ‚Äčwere on board a United States submarine that ‚ÄĆsank an Iranian warship with a torpedo in the Indian Ocean, but stressed they did not take part in the attack.
The ‚Äčstrike, which occured off the southern coast ‚Äčof Sri Lanka this week, marked the first ‚Ā†time since World War Two that the United States ‚Äčhas sunk an enemy vessel with a torpedo. Sri ‚ÄčLankan authorities recovered the bodies of 87 sailors.
(This story has been refiled to fix a typo in the headline)

item.title: Falling Ukrainian drone injures nine in Sevastopol in Russian-held Crimea, governor says
item.description:
March 6 (Reuters) - A downed Ukrainian drone fell next ‚Äčto a five-storey apartment ‚ÄĆbuilding in the port of Sevastopol in Russian-held ‚ÄčCrimea, injuring nine ‚Äčpeople and causing considerable ‚Ā†damage, the Russia-appointed ‚Äčgovernor said early on ‚ÄčFriday.
Mikhail Razvozhayev said the drone was filled with metal ‚Äčpieces and explosives ‚Äčfell next to the building, badly damaging ‚ÄĆit.
Nine ‚Ā†people were injured, including six requiring hospital treatment, three of ‚Äčthem children.
Sevastopol ‚Äčhosts ‚Ā†the Russian Black Sea Fleet ‚Äčin Crimea, annexed ‚Äčby ‚Ā†Russia in 2014, though Western countries do ‚Ā†not ‚Äčrecognise Moscow's control ‚Äčover the peninsula.

item.title: Trump urges Iranian Kurds to attack Iran as war widens
item.description:
DUBAI/WASHINGTON, March 6 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump encouraged Iranian Kurdish forces in Iraq to launch attacks against Iran as the Middle East conflict widened, with Azerbaijan warning it would retaliate for being targeted by Iranian missiles.
Israel on ‚ÄčFriday said it had started a "broad-scale" wave of attacks against infrastructure targets in Tehran, as Gulf cities came under renewed bombardment by Iran.
The seven-day war has now seen Iran ‚ÄĆtarget Israel, the Gulf states, Cyprus, Turkey and Azerbaijan, and spread to the Indian Ocean off Sri Lanka where a U.S. submarine sank an Iranian naval ship.
On the possibility of the Iranian Kurdish forces entering Iran, Trump told Reuters on Thursday: "I think it's wonderful that they want to do that, I'd be all for it."
Two Iranian drone attacks targeted an Iranian opposition camp in Iraqi Kurdistan on Thursday, security sources said.
Iranian Kurdish militias have consulted with the United States in recent days ‚Äčabout whether, and how, to attack Iran's security forces in the western part of the country, according to three sources with knowledge of the matter.
The Iranian Kurdish coalition of groups based ‚Äčon the Iran-Iraq border in the semi-autonomous region of Iraqi Kurdistan has been training to mount such an attack in hopes of weakening the country's military, ‚Ā†as the United States and Israel pound Iranian targets with bombs and missiles.
Trump, speaking with Reuters in a telephone interview, also said the United States must have a role in deciding who will be the next ‚Äčleader of Iran after airstrikes killed Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei last week.
"We're going to have to choose that person along with Iran. We're going to have to choose that person," he said.
U.S. Defense Secretary ‚ÄčPete Hegseth said on Thursday that the U.S. was not expanding its military objectives in Iran, despite what Trump said about choosing the country's next leader.
"There's no expansion in our objectives. We know exactly what we're trying to achieve," he said.
The attack on Iran is a major political gamble for the Republican president, with opinion polls showing little support and Americans concerned about the rise in gasoline prices caused by disruption to energy supplies. Trump dismissed that concern .
Shares on Wall Street fell on ‚ÄčThursday, weighed by surging oil prices, as the economic impact , opens new tab of the campaign intensified, with countries around the world cut off from a fifth of global supplies of oil and liquefied natural gas and air transport ‚Äčstill facing chaos and global logistics increasingly snarled .
Azerbaijan was preparing unspecified retaliatory measures on Thursday after it said four Iranian drones crossed its border and injured four people in the Nakhchivan exclave.
"We will not tolerate this unprovoked ‚ÄĆact of ‚Ā†terror and aggression against Azerbaijan," President Ilham Aliyev told a meeting of his Security Council.
Iran, which has a significant Azeri minority, denied it targeted its neighbor.
Lebanon's Iran-backed Hezbollah militia warned Israeli residents to evacuate towns within 5 km (3 miles) of the border between the countries in a message posted on its Telegram channel in Hebrew early on Friday.
"Your military's aggression against Lebanese sovereignty and safe citizens, the destruction of civilian infrastructure and the expulsion campaign it is carrying out will not go unchallenged," Hezbollah said.
Hegseth and Admiral Brad Cooper, who leads U.S. forces in the Middle East, said during a briefing about operations that the U.S. ‚Äčhas enough munitions to continue its bombardment indefinitely.
"Iran ‚Äčis hoping that we cannot sustain this, which ‚Ā†is a really bad miscalculation," Hegseth told reporters at Central Command headquarters in Florida. "Our munitions are full up and our will is ironclad."
The Pentagon earlier this week said the military campaign, known as Operation Epic Fury, is focused on destroying Iran's offensive missiles, missile production and navy, while not allowing Tehran to have ‚Äča nuclear weapon.
Cooper said the U.S. had now hit at least 30 Iranian ships, including a large drone carrier that he said was the ‚Äčsize of a World War Two ‚Ā†aircraft carrier.
He added that B-2 bombers had in the past few hours dropped dozens of 2,000 penetrator bombs targeting deeply buried ballistic missile launchers, and that bombings were also targeting Iran's missile production facilities.
Iran's ballistic missile attacks had decreased by 90% since the first day of the war, while drone attacks had decreased by 83% in that time frame, he said.
In Iran, at least 1,230 people have been killed, according to the Iranian Red ‚Ā†Crescent Society, including ‚Äč175 schoolgirls and staff killed at a primary school in Minab in the country's south on the first day of the ‚Äčwar. Another 77 have been killed in Lebanon, its Health Ministry says. Thousands fled southern Beirut on Thursday after Israel warned residents to leave.

item.title: Nepal's early vote count puts ex-rapper's party ahead
item.description:
KATHMANDU, March 6 (Reuters) - Rapper-turned-politician Balendra Shah's party gained leads in early counting in Nepal's general election , far outpacing his other rivals, including the country's former ‚Äčprime minister who was forced to resign after a historic ‚ÄĆyouth-led uprising last year.
Shah, 35, former mayor of the capital city of Kathmandu, has dominated the race to become Nepal's PM during the campaign, gaining popularity across the ‚Äčcountry.
Shah's centrist Rastriya Swatantra Party, which was founded three years ‚Äčago, was leading in 37 seats while former Prime Minister ‚Ā†K P Sharma Oli's Communist Party of Nepal (Unified Marxist-Leninist - UML) was ‚Äčleading in three, early counting trends from the election commission showed on ‚ÄčFriday morning.
The centrist Nepali Congress led by 49-year-old Gagan Thapa, the country's oldest party, was leading in five seats.
Results are likely to be clear by Friday evening or ‚ÄčSaturday, officials said.
Out of a total 275 seats in Nepal's lower ‚Äčhouse of parliament, counting was in progress for 165 seats that are elected ‚ÄĆby ‚Ā†direct election, while the remaining 110 will follow a system of proportional representation.
Shah drew large crowds during his election campaign, connecting with legions of young voters clamouring for change on the ground and online, even ‚Äčas he took ‚Äčon Oli, 74, ‚Ā†on his home turf in the Jhapa 5 constituency along the Indian border.
Perched between China and India, ‚Äčthe country of 30 million people has been plagued ‚Äčfor ‚Ā†decades by political instability, rippling a largely agrarian economy and worsening unemployment - structural issues compounded by rampant corruption.
The long-festering malaise erupted into street demonstrations last ‚Ā†September, ‚Äčtriggered by a social media ban, that ‚Äčbrought thousands on the streets, leading to clashes and fatalities that forced the resignation of ‚ÄčOli.

item.title: Hong Kong media tycoon Jimmy Lai will not appeal outcome in landmark security case
item.description:
March 6 (Reuters) - Hong Kong media tycoon Jimmy Lai will not appeal against his conviction and 20-year jail term for collusion with ‚Äčforeign forces and sedition, his lawyer said on Friday, after a landmark case ‚ÄĆthat sparked international criticism.
The founder of the now-shuttered pro-democracy Apple Daily newspaper, Lai , 78, was one of the most outspoken critics of the ruling Chinese Communist Party.
A nearly five-year legal saga ended with his sentencing in ‚ÄčFebruary following a December conviction on two counts of conspiracy to collude with ‚Äčforeign forces and one count of publishing seditious materials.
A member of Lai's ‚Ā†domestic legal team told Reuters his client would not appeal the conviction and sentencing , which ‚Äčdrew criticism from countries such as Britain and the United States.
"We can confirm we have ‚Äčclear and definitive instructions not to lodge an appeal against conviction or sentence," he said, speaking on condition of anonymity, but gave no reason for the decision.
Separately, Fung Wai-kong, the former editor-in-chief of the ‚Äčpaper's English news section, launched an appeal on Monday against his 10-year jail term in ‚Äčthe same national security case.
Lai's son and daughter have warned he might die in prison, as his ‚ÄĆhealth ‚Ā†is deteriorating after spending more than five years in solitary confinement. He has diabetes and health problems such as heart palpitations and high blood pressure.
Rights groups and numerous democratic countries have called for Lai's release.
U.S. President Donald Trump raised the matter with his Chinese counterpart, ‚ÄčXi Jinping, and is ‚Äčexpected to follow ‚Ā†up in a closely anticipated visit to Beijing at the end of this month.
Hong Kong and Beijing, however, say Lai received a fair ‚Äčtrial and all are treated equally under the national security law ‚Äčthat has restored ‚Ā†order to the city since mass pro-democracy protests in 2019.
Last month, in a separate case, Hong Kong's Court of Appeal handed Lai a rare legal victory by overturning his conviction for fraud ‚Ā†and ‚Äčquashing a 69-month sentence.

item.title: Exclusive: Trump on rising gas prices during Iran operation: 'If they rise, they rise'
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 5 (Reuters) - President Donald Trump said on Thursday he was not concerned about rising U.S. gas prices driven by the widening Iran conflict , telling Reuters ‚Äčin an exclusive interview that the U.S. military operation was his priority.
"I don't have any concern about it," he said, when asked about the higher prices at ‚ÄĆthe pump. "They'll drop very rapidly when this is over, and if they rise, they rise, but this is far more important than having gasoline prices go up a little bit."
The comments mark a shift in tone for the president, who touted a drop in gas prices in his State of the Union address last month and at a Texas rally focused on energy that took place just hours before the U.S. launched its air strikes ‚Äčon Saturday.
Political analysts say a persistent rise in gas prices could hurt Republicans in the November midterm elections when control of the U.S. Congress will be at stake. Voters ‚Äčare already unhappy about the high cost of living and Trump's stewardship of the economy.
Despite Trump's public efforts to play down the price rises, White House Chief ‚Ā†of Staff Susie Wiles and Energy Secretary Chris Wright have both engaged with oil CEOs to gauge possible options on combating rising energy prices, White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt said ‚Äčon Thursday.
Another White House official, speaking on condition of anonymity, said there was a scramble across the White House energy and national security teams to develop measures aimed at bringing down gas prices.
The ‚Äčofficial said Wiles had warned in White House meetings that failure to act on price rises would be "catastrophic" for Republicans in the elections.
US weighs oil futures market action to combat price spikes, White House official says
Trump has outlined a four-to-five-week timeline for the military campaign against Iran, but political and military experts have questioned it, noting that the U.S. government has yet to articulate its end goal while the conflict continues to spread to the region and beyond.
In the interview, Trump said he was not looking to tap the Strategic ‚ÄčPetroleum Reserve , the largest emergency crude stockpile in the world, and that he was confident the Strait of Hormuz, the critical channel for oil shipping near Iran, will remain open because Iran's navy ‚Äčis at the "bottom of the sea."
Global oil prices have jumped 16% since the war started on Saturday, as the spreading conflict disrupted Middle East supplies.
The national average cost of gas has risen 27 cents since last week ‚ÄĆto $3.25 per ‚Ā†gallon, according to AAA, a U.S. travel organization that tracks fuel prices. The current national average is 15 cents higher than a year ago.
Trump said the costs "haven't risen very much."
The White House is betting the conflict with Iran - and the resulting pain at the gas pump - will prove short-lived.
White House energy advisers have told Trump aides the initial sticker shock in fuel markets has been less severe than many feared and have urged patience, according to two people who were granted anonymity to describe internal deliberations.
The advisers warned that any intervention by the Trump administration ‚Äčthat fails to quickly bring down prices could ‚Äčrattle markets and prove counterproductive.
Secretary of State ‚Ā†Marco Rubio said early this week that the administration was rolling out a package of measures to combat rising energy prices, but the only plan unveiled so far is U.S.-backed risk insurance for oil tankers and the promise of potential naval escorts through the Strait of Hormuz.
Three energy executives ‚Äčtold Reuters the White House has few great options to push down energy prices.
"When you look across the menu of policy options, domestically ‚Äčor within other countries, they ‚Ā†can be helpful, but they don't move the needle far," one energy executive said, speaking on condition of anonymity so they could speak candidly about the administration's policies. "I think the primary focus is ... to do whatever they can to restore transits through the Strait of Hormuz itself."
Has the Iran war changed the Gulf forever?
Officials are also discussing a wide array of other options, including a federal gasoline tax holiday and loosening environmental regulations around summer gasoline ‚Ā†that will allow ‚Äčhigher blends of ethanol, according to the two sources familiar with the internal deliberations.
Officials were also weighing a potential ‚Äčrelease from the Strategic Petroleum Reserve, the sources said, but the president ruled out that option - at least for now - in his comments to Reuters.
Congressional Republican leaders, like House Speaker Mike Johnson , have also dismissed concerns about rising gas prices, even ‚Äčas the party plans to focus its midterm election strategy on economic successes.

item.title: Gulf carriers resume limited flights, but missile fire fuels uncertainty
item.description:
HONG KONG, March 6 (Reuters) - Emirates and Etihad Airways were resuming limited flight schedules to key global cities from their United Arab Emirates ‚Äčhubs on Friday, though the ongoing threat of missile fire piled pressure on airlines as they scramble to accommodate travellers.
With most airspace in ‚ÄĆthe Middle East still closed over missile and drone concerns since the start of the U.S.-Israel war against Iran , authorities have been arranging charter flights and securing seats on limited commercial services to evacuate tens of thousands of people.
A government-chartered Air France (AIRF.PA) , opens new tab flight to bring French nationals back from the United Arab Emirates was forced to turn back on Thursday due to missile fire in the ‚Äčarea, French Transport Minister Philippe Tabarot said.
"This situation reflects the instability in the region and the complexity of repatriation operations," he said.
Etihad said on Friday ‚Äčit would resume a limited flight schedule through March 19. The flights will operate to and from Abu Dhabi and 25 destinations ‚Ā†including London, Paris, Frankfurt, Delhi, New York and Toronto.
As of Thursday, traffic at Dubai airport, normally the world's busiest, had almost doubled from Wednesday, but remained only about ‚Äč25% of normal levels, flight-tracking website Flightradar24 said.
Dubai-based Emirates, one of the UAE's two flag carriers, said late on Thursday it was operating a reduced flight schedule to ‚Äč82 destinations including London, Sydney, Singapore and New York until further notice, and customers transiting in Dubai would only be accepted if their connecting flight was operating.
The limited operations at Middle Eastern hubs have hit travellers on routes from Europe to the Asia-Pacific region particularly hard.
Combined, Emirates, Qatar Airways and Etihad Airways normally fly about one-third of passengers from Europe to Asia and more ‚Äčthan half of all passengers from Europe to Australia, New Zealand and nearby Pacific Islands, according to Cirium data.
Qatar's Doha hub remains shut, though it has been arranging ‚Äča limited number of relief flights from Oman and Saudi Arabia.
Data from Cirium showed that from February 28 - when the conflict started - to March 5, there were more than 44,000 flights ‚ÄĆscheduled in ‚Ā†and out of the Middle East, with more than 25,000 flights cancelled so far.
Higher oil prices have sent jet fuel costs soaring, with Singapore jet fuel reaching a record high of $225 a barrel this week, which traders attributed to concerns about supply shortages from Middle Eastern refiners.
The price eased slightly on Thursday to about $195 a barrel after some profit-taking but remained nearly double that of last week.
Shares of Qantas Airways (QAN.AX) , opens new tab fell more than 3% on Friday, Air New Zealand (AIR.NZ) , opens new tab was down ‚Äčnearly 7%, Hong Kong's flagship carrier Cathay ‚ÄčPacific (0293.HK) , opens new tab dropped more than 2%, while ‚Ā†Singapore Airlines (SIAL.SI) , opens new tab was down over 1%.
The Hong Kong-listed shares of major Chinese carriers including Air China , China Eastern Airlines , and China Southern Airlines were down between 2% and 4%.
Passengers have been forking out huge sums of ‚Äčmoney to get out of the Middle East, with some who managed to travel back by commercial flight on Thursday ‚Äčfrom Oman saying it ‚Ā†had been "absolute chaos" to find their way back home from Dubai.
"We paid 1,500 pounds ($2,005.05) to get across to Muscat (Oman) to get on the plane," said Ed Short after he arrived at London's Heathrow Airport on a British Airways flight.
"We'd spent about 20,000 pounds booking Emirates flight instead. So we're hoping we get those back."
With the conflict showing little sign of ‚Ā†easing, wider ‚Äčaviation and air-cargo disruption looked set to linger.
Saudi budget carrier flynas will run a limited number of flights ‚Äčbetween Saudi Arabia and Dubai starting on Friday.
($1 = 0.7481 pounds)

item.title: Oil spill disrupts livelihoods in Mexico's Veracruz state
item.description:
JICACAL, Mexico, March 5 (Reuters) - Coastal residents in the Mexican state of Veracruz are concerned about the recent appearance of oil on local beaches, which has ‚Äčdisrupted their livelihoods, fearing it could contaminate a nearby lagoon where ‚ÄĆcommunities farm fish and shrimp.
Residents of Jicacal, on the Gulf of Mexico, have organized themselves to remove as much of the hardened tar as possible from the beaches, while the viscous ‚Äčspill spreads along the coast. Authorities have not told residents what caused ‚Äčthe spill.
Veracruz is heavy on oil activity in Mexico, both on ‚Ā†land and at sea. State oil company Pemex ruled out any leak or ‚Äčspill from its facilities this week.
‚ÄúAs usual, we went out fishing and cast ‚Äčour nets, and we realized that the net was full of oil,‚ÄĚ said Aurora Apolonia Martinez, describing how she noticed the presence of the spilled crude, which rendered the fishing nets unusable.
Pemex ‚Äčdid not immediately respond to a request for comment about whether it had ‚Äčnew information about the spill following the surveillance efforts it said it would carry out ‚ÄĆin the ‚Ā†area.
Cemda, an environmental organization, said this week that since March 1, over a dozen spill sites have been identified along the beaches of Veracruz and neighboring Tabasco along the coast of the Gulf of Mexico.
Pemex has denied responsibility for the ‚Äčspill. But the company ‚Äčhas been involved ‚Ā†in several such incidents in recent years. The most recent was in October in Veracruz, where 2.7 million liters were ‚Äčrecovered from the Pantepec River following a leak in an ‚Äčoil pipeline.
Both ‚Ā†Jicacal and the neighboring town of Las Barrillas, also in Veracruz, are fishing villages and local tourist destinations. The spill has also impacted the local tourism industry, discouraging ‚Ā†visits to ‚Äčthe coast.
‚ÄúWhat we are interested in is cleaning ‚Äčup the entire beach,‚ÄĚ said Nicol√°s Vargas, a tourism service provider in Las Barrillas, who said that ‚Äčotherwise, tourists will not visit the area.

item.title: Taiwan defence minister rejects opposition's cut‚ÄĎprice budget and U.S. arms deadline
item.description:
TAIPEI, March 6 (Reuters) - Taiwan's Defence Minister Wellington Koo on Friday rebuffed the main opposition party, which ‚ÄĆhad proposed a defence budget roughly one-third of the amount sought by the government and set a deadline for U.S. arms purchases that he said was impossible.
Taiwan has faced pressure from the United States to sharply raise defence spending. ‚ÄčTaiwan President Lai Ching-te last year proposed extra defence spending of $40 billion to counter China, which ‚Äčhas ramped up military pressure to force the island to accept its claim ‚Ā†of sovereignty.
But the main opposition Kuomintang, which together with a smaller party holds a majority in ‚Äčparliament, refused to review the proposal and instead this week advanced its own, less expensive proposals, which ‚Äčonly fund around 30% of the spending that Lai wants.
The move came after growing pressure from politicians in the United States, Taiwan's most important international backer and arms supplier, to not hold up spending.
In the KMT's counter-proposal, a copy of ‚Äčwhich was reviewed by Reuters, the party set a cap of T$380 billion ($11.96 billion) on the spending ‚Äčand a deadline for completion by the end of 2028.
Talking to reporters in Taipei, Koo said the government's proposal ‚ÄĆincluded ‚Ā†precision artillery and anti-armour unmanned systems.
"If everything is required to be delivered and fully implemented before that deadline, it would in effect shut down these projects, making their execution impossible," he said.
The KMT also said it backs U.S. arms deals handled between the two governments and opposes deals arranged through commercial ‚Äčchannels, which it views as ‚Äčvulnerable to irregularities ‚Ā†and inadequate scrutiny.
In response, Koo said omitting any channel for acquiring weapons would "create a major gap in our overall defence and operational capabilities and significantly undermine ‚Äčthe improvement of our joint combat capabilities."
He added the government's proposal has ‚Äčthe backing ‚Ā†of the U.S administration and congress.
The KMT has described Lai's plan as "sky‚ÄĎhigh" and vague, citing the need for clearer oversight.
KMT chairperson Cheng Li-wun has said her party has been in communication with the Chinese Communist Party ‚Ā†and ‚Äčthat she hopes to visit China this year for a meeting ‚Äčwith Chinese President Xi Jinping.
China regularly stages military exercises around Taiwan, and refuses to talk to Lai, calling him a "separatist". Lai says ‚Äčonly Taiwan's people can decide their future.
($1 = 31.7700 Taiwan dollars)

item.title: Cuba confirms fifth casualty in speedboat attack
item.description:
HAVANA, March 5 (Reuters) - A fifth casualty from ‚Äčlast week's speedboat ‚Äčattack on Cuba has ‚Ā†been confirmed, the ‚ÄčCuban interior ministry ‚Äčsaid on Thursday night.
Roberto Alvarez Avila died on ‚ÄčMarch 4 in ‚Äčthe hospital, the Cuban authorities ‚ÄĆsaid, ‚Ā†where he was being held after being injured in ‚Äčan ‚Äčincident ‚Ā†in which Cuban forces killed ‚Äčfour Cuban ‚Äčnationals ‚Ā†and wounded six others aboard a speedboat ‚Ā†that ‚Äčentered Cuban ‚Äčwaters.
(This story has been corrected to fix the name to Alvarez Avila, not Avila Alvares, in paragraph 2)

item.title: Trading Day: War fuels stagflation fears
item.description:
ORLANDO, Florida, March 5 (Reuters) - Stocks sank on Thursday and another surge in oil prices sent bond yields shooting higher, as the spiraling conflict across the Middle East raised investor fears over energy supplies , higher inflation and slower growth.
More on that below. In my column today I offer a reminder that, amid the fog of ‚Äčwar, economic fundamentals can't be forgotten completely. Friday's U.S. payrolls data, and any hint of AI's impact on jobs, will avert eyes from the ‚ÄĆMiddle East, at least temporarily.
If you have more time to read, here are a few articles I recommend to help you make sense of what happened in markets today.
Today's Key Market Moves
Today's Talking Points
* Learning the "transitory" lesson
As energy prices soar, markets are betting that central bankers won't repeat the 2021-2022 playbook of looking through supply shocks and a subsequent surge in "transitory" inflation. They've learned that lesson , right?
That appears to be traders' bet - only ‚Äčone Fed rate cut this year is baked in now, and that's not until October; another BoE cut isn't fully priced at all; the ECB is more likely to ‚Äčhike than cut; and the RBA could even hike again this month.
* No room for complacency
There was a glimmer of hope on Wednesday that back-channel U.S.-Iran diplomacy might pave the ‚ÄĆway for ‚Ā†peace to break out in the Middle East. Traders seized upon it, bought back beaten-down stocks, and Europe and Wall Street rallied.
Predictions are dangerous at the best of times, but that looks like a false dawn. The war is spreading, messy, and getting more entrenched. Energy prices and bond yields are spiking, and risk assets are feeling the heat. Yet the Nasdaq is flat on the week. Justified calm, or complacency?
* A job lot
With investors' focus firmly on the market implications of events ‚Äčin the Middle East, economic fundamentals are ‚Äčunderstandably taking a back seat. They ‚Ā†should be a driving force, however, at 8:30 a.m. Eastern Time on Friday, when the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics releases the February jobs data.
A strong report will let Fed officials breathe more easily, while signs of cracks in the labor ‚Äčmarket will be tricky to navigate - yields are spiking on the energy supply shock, yet the curve is the ‚Äčflattest this year. Stagflation ‚Ā†on the horizon?
What could move markets tomorrow?
Want to receive Trading Day in your inbox every weekday morning? Sign up for my newsletter here .
Opinions expressed are those of the author. They do not reflect the views ‚Äčof Reuters News, which, under the Trust Principles , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: Lloyd Blankfein memoir dispels Goldman's mystique
item.description:
LONDON, March 5 (Reuters Breakingviews) - ‚ÄúIts mystery is its life. We must not let in daylight upon magic.‚ÄĚ Walter Bagehot‚Äôs famous prescription for the British monarchy , opens new tab could have been written for Goldman Sachs (GS.N) , opens new tab . For many years the Wall Street investment bank hid its inner workings, its clout in boardrooms and government departments, and its partners‚Äô wealth behind a thick veil of discretion. Under Lloyd Blankfein‚Äôs leadership it reluctantly became more transparent. His new memoir blows ‚Äčaway any remaining mystique.
Blankfein, who was chairman and CEO from 2006 to 2018, admits he is an outlier. A working-class kid from Brooklyn, his initial application to the firm was rejected. The Harvard alumnus eventually arrived at ‚ÄĆGoldman through the back door in 1982 by joining J. Aron, a commodity trading outfit the investment bank had acquired the previous year.
Self-deprecating humour and frequent references to his humble background set Blankfein apart from the intense, highly competitive types who tend to populate Goldman‚Äôs upper ranks. While previous leaders like Robert Rubin and Hank Paulson moved smoothly into the role of Treasury Secretary in the administrations of Presidents Bill Clinton and George W. Bush, respectively, Blankfein has shown little interest in higher office.
This lack of pretension helps make ‚ÄúStreetwise: Getting to and Through Goldman Sachs‚ÄĚ highly readable. Blankfein recounts his upbringing and career with amusing anecdotes ‚Äčas well as honest and at times brutal assessments of his fellow executives. His initial evaluation of Paulson, who preceded him as CEO, is typical: ‚Äúa driven and highly capable but often inarticulate Christian Scientist who drank milk and loved bird-watching.‚ÄĚ He ‚Äčquotes a former colleague describing John Thain and John Thornton, the bank‚Äôs co-chief operating officers and rivals for the top job in the early 2000s, as acting like ‚Äúthe owner‚Äôs sons‚ÄĚ.
Blankfein‚Äôs career spanned Goldman‚Äôs ‚Ā†transformation from a Wall Street private partnership to a publicly traded global investment bank. In 1998 it earned revenue of $8.5 billion. Last year it brought in seven times that sum. Since listing on the stock market in May 1999, Goldman shares have risen more than 1,000%, ‚Äčfar outpacing other big U.S. banks.
That transformation owed much to the expansion of financial activity and the breakneck growth of markets around the world after 1999. It also reflected a deliberate transition as Goldman ‚Äď and its rivals ‚Äď moved from acting as an adviser and broker to ‚Äčalso trading and investing more aggressively on its own account.
Blankfein exemplified the shift to a more aggressive risk-taking mentality. The change is often attributed to the J. Aron acquisition. Yet ‚ÄúStreetwise‚ÄĚ makes clear the commodity broker was a risk-averse firm which only started making big bets after Goldman absorbed it. Notable trades included an audacious scheme conceived by Gary Cohn ‚Äď later President Donald Trump‚Äôs chief economic adviser ‚Äď to snap up cheap aluminium due to a Russian production glut and stockpile it in a Rotterdam warehouse until prices recovered.
These proprietary bets often clashed with the interests of Goldman clients, who expected bankers to provide them with unbiased ‚Äčadvice and traders to secure the best price for securities. Blankfein is unapologetic about those tensions, arguing that wearing multiple hats allowed Goldman to act like economist Adam Smith‚Äôs famous invisible hand. ‚ÄúWe weren‚Äôt just serving our clients better. We were playing a primary role in ‚Äčthe allocation of capital, to the benefit of the economy and society.‚ÄĚ Many clients had a less charitable interpretation.
The defining period of Blankfein‚Äôs tenure was the global financial crisis that kicked off in 2007. His nose for risk and attention to detail served Goldman well as financial markets melted ‚ÄĆdown. He recalls ‚Ā†spotting early signs of liquidity drying up in mid-2007 while reading emails on his BlackBerry in the movie theatre.
Unlike many rivals, Goldman decided to hedge its exposure to U.S. subprime housing debt, in part by buying protection from American International Group (AIG.N) , opens new tab against defaults in mortgage-backed securities. When the U.S. government ‚Äď with Paulson as Treasury Secretary ‚Äď bailed out the insurance giant in September 2008, many on Wall Street suspected the rescue had indirectly saved Goldman. Blankfein insists that the firm, which had also taken the precaution of buying insurance against an AIG default, would have survived its counterparty‚Äôs collapse. Still, whether the banks that sold that protection could have honoured their obligations in such a meltdown remains an open question.
Yet if Blankfein nimbly guided Goldman through the storm, he stumbled in the aftermath. Intense public scrutiny and criticism from politicians came ‚Äčas a shock for a firm unused to being a household ‚Äčname. Blankfein offers a spirited defence against Goldman‚Äôs many critics. ‚Ā†Yet he acknowledges that bailouts helped polarise public opinion, clearing the way for Trump.
A more damaging accusation is that Blankfein was slow to recognise deeper changes in financial markets. Tighter regulation meant fewer bets using the firm‚Äôs own balance sheet, while extra capital requirements made Goldman‚Äôs bond-trading operation less profitable. Arch-rival Morgan Stanley (MS.N) , opens new tab , which barely survived 2008, made an early decision under post-crisis CEO James Gorman to double ‚Äčdown on its wealth management business instead. Its stock has comfortably outperformed Goldman‚Äôs over the past decade.
The final years of Blankfein‚Äôs tenure were marked by some damaging scandals, most notably the bank's ‚Äčwork for 1MDB. The Malaysian sovereign fund ‚Ā†paid Goldman suspiciously high fees for bond issues in 2012 and 2013 and then siphoned off the proceeds in an audacious fraud. The only plausible explanations are that Goldman‚Äôs fabled risk management systems failed, or that it turned a blind eye in return for a big payday. Neither reflects well on the firm. Blankfein offers the tepid excuse that Goldman‚Äôs size made it hard to be on top of everything, and that he expected the committees that vetted the transactions to make sure they were above board. The saga ended up costing Goldman more than $3 billion in regulatory ‚Ā†fines .
Since replacing Blankfein ‚Äčin 2018, current Goldman boss David Solomon has focused on making the bank's earnings less volatile. The swashbuckling pursuit of lucrative trades has given way to more ‚Äčstable, technology-enabled operations like managing cash for corporations. Towards the end of the book, Blankfein observes that Goldman has become ‚Äúa little less special‚ÄĚ. By choosing to shine a bright and uncompromising light on the firm‚Äôs inner workings, the bank‚Äôs former boss has done his bit to dispel the magic.
Follow Peter Thal Larsen on Bluesky , opens new tab and LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: How South Korea's largest and deadliest wildfire spread
item.description:
Spread by strong winds in bone-dry conditions, the wildfires have killed scores of people and charred thousands of hectares in the southeastern part of the country.
While wildfires are common in South Korea at this time of year, the rapid spread in late March was unprecedented.
What started as two fires near Uiseong town over March 22 to 24, soon spread overnight leaving a trail of death and destruction.
On March 25, three bodies were found near a burnt-out car trying to escape the fire in Yeongyang, and a woman in her 60s was found burnt on a road in Cheongsong. Parts of Gounsa temple, a heritage site first built in 681, was also burned to the ground that day.
A day later, a pilot was killed after his firefighting helicopter crashed near a mountain in Uiseong county on March 26.¬†A monk at a Buddhist temple in Yeongyang was also discovered dead on the same day.
In the aftermath, more bodies were found. On March 27, a 90-year-old woman was found dead at her house in Cheongsong.
Days later, the wildfire was contained but the damage was done, with scores dead and thousands of hectares burned.
The wildfires in North Gyeongsang province began in Uiseong county before spreading 70 km (44 miles) east, to devastate an area equivalent to about two-thirds of the island of Singapore and much larger than the Los Angeles fires in January.
‚ÄúStrong, dry winds blew from the west. The wind had the biggest impact,‚ÄĚ said Lim Sang-seop, the minister of the Korea Forest Service, adding that smoke and fog reduced visibility this week, presenting a bigger challenge to helicopters seeking to douse the flames.
Experts have said the spread of the Uiseong fire was extremely unusual in terms of scale and speed, while climate change is expected to make wildfires more frequent and deadly globally.
The wildfires have displaced more than 30,000, many of them elderly, as is typical in rural areas across fast-ageing South Korea. The flames consumed everything in their path, from historic temples¬†to homes , in mountainous North Gyeongsang.
Charred, black ruins at South Korea's Gounsa temple stood on Thursday in stark contrast to the kaleidoscope of colours that are a trademark of the country's historic palaces and Buddhist temples.
A massive, ornate bronze bell sat in the ruins of its tower, cracked from top to bottom. Two out of the three national treasures at the site burned, while a handful of other structures escaped the blaze.
A man takes a photograph of a damaged temple bell lying amidst debris at Gounsa temple. REUTERS/Kim Hong-Ji
A damaged temple bell lies amidst debris at Gounsa temple. REUTERS/Kim Hong-Ji
Firefighters work at the Gounsa temple on March 27, 2025. REUTERS/Kim Hong-Ji
Gounsa was one of at least 18 designated heritage sites or objects, including two national treasures, that had been destroyed or damaged as of Thursday, according to the Korea Heritage Service.
At the picturesque Hahoe Folk Village, a UNESCO World Heritage Site, authorities deployed retardant and other measures in a desperate attempt to save its thatch-roof buildings nestled on a bend in the Nakdong River.
While residents were urged to evacuate, as of Thursday the village had survived.
Lee Byung-doo, a forest disaster expert at the National Institute of Forest Science said on March 25 that the Uiseong fire was exacerbated by gusty winds and showed ‚Äúunimaginable scale and speed‚ÄĚ
Even the Gounsa temple chief Deungwoon told Reuters that ‚Äúthere was a wind stronger than a typhoon, and flames whipped through the air like a tornado, burning the whole area in an instant.‚ÄĚ
Embers
Spotting
Wind can carry burning embers and sparks downwind of fires, increasing the rate at which fires can spread.
An illustration explaining how wind can influence and speed up spread of fire.
Terrain can also play a part in it‚Äôs spread.
Rob Rogers, the New South Wales Rural Service commissioner said ‚Äúwhere there‚Äôs mountains, they‚Äôll obviously accelerate the speed of the fire when it‚Äôs going up the mountains, but also they can change. Mountain ranges can change the direction of the wind‚ÄĚ
Topography
The angle of slope can also affect fire behaviour, especially if coupled with wind.
Wind
Indraft
Convection
Radiation
pre-heats fuels
Downward slope
Upward slope
Usually slower to burn. However, compressed air and down-slope winds may still contribute to wildfire spread.
Fires burn faster uphill than on level ground as energy is easily transferred to un-burnt fuel.
Less energy
transferred to fuels
An illustration explaining the role of topography on fire spread.
The rapid spread of the fire on March 25, 2025 was also caught via satellite imagery. It shows the winds pushing clouds from west to east, and a flash of red lights up that area overnight.
Satellite imagery from March 25 to March 26
The fire burned about 48,000 hectares (119,000 acres), destroyed an estimated 4,000 structures, and forced tens of thousands of people to evacuate.
A forest fire that broke out in Uljin in March 2022 is the most recent major incident to occur in South Korea. It burned over 17,000 hectares of land according to The Korea Times.
The 10-year average from 2015 - 2024 was 4,003 hectares burned annually. 2022 saw the most number of hectares burned at 24,797 while 2024 saw the lowest at 132 hectares.
While wildfires are common across South Korea, the March fires burned more than the combined hectares of land burned between 2015 and 2024.
2025

Gwangju
More detailed surface area data is available from NASA Fire Information for Resource Managment System (FIRMS) showing how this compares to the past 10 years.

Small multiple maps of South Korea showing the burned areas as determined by satellite image analysis from NASA.
Note: The map for March 2025 shows active fire hotspots only. The previous yearly maps show burned areas.
Source: Korea Forest Service, NASA
Authorities believe the possible cause for the March wildfire is a man, in his 50s, who began the fire in southeastern Uiseong County when he performed an ancestral rite by a family grave on March 22, an official from Gyeongbuk Provincial Police said.
He was booked on Sunday, March 30 and evidence was still being processed according to officials. Yonhap news agency said the man had denied the allegations.
Sources
Fire Information for Resource Management System (FIRMS); Shuttle Radar Topography Mission (SRTM); Sentinel-2; Global Land Analysis and Discover team at the University of Maryland (UMD GLAD); OpenStreetMap; Cooperative Institute for Research in the Atmosphere at Colorado State University (CSU/CIRA); Japan Meteorological Agency (JMA); Japan Aerospace Exploration Agency (JAXA)
Additional reporting by
Heejung Jung and Vijdan Mohammad Kawoosa

Rebecca Pazos, Simon Scarr and Raju Gopalakrishnan

item.title: Gaza‚Äôs garbage crisis
item.description:
After 15 months of war, Gaza is suffering from a destroyed waste system. As the cleanup begins, the impacts on its people‚Äôs health and land will be long felt.
More than a year of conflict has crippled the already limited waste collection infrastructure in Gaza. With access to designated landfills blocked and no transport due to a fuel blockade imposed by Israel, hundreds of thousands of tons of solid waste has piled up in the streets.
Even before the war, Gaza faced severe waste management challenges, generating 1,700 tons of waste daily, with only three operational but overcrowded landfills. One of the sites, Johr Edeek, operated beyond capacity, with waste piling up to 35 meters high, according to a report from the United Nations Development Programme (UNDP).
However, despite the strains, a structure was in place, with towns and cities sending waste to the three landfill sites.
Limited resources caused frequent fires and environmental risks. From 2017 to 2022, the number of waste collection vehicles dropped to 73 from 112, with ageing equipment struggling to meet the needs of the growing population. The war has worsened the crisis, destroying waste collection vehicles, facilities, and medical waste treatment centers, leaving municipalities overwhelmed.
The solid waste removal network previously relied on three main landfills, all closed during the latest conflict.
Deir Al Balah
Khan Yunis
Small cities
or towns
Refugee camps
Rafah
Jabalia
Gaza City
Beit Hanoun
Main cities
Beit Lahiya
Three operational
landfills
The distribution of Gaza‚Äôs population has also changed dramatically, with over 60% of buildings likely damaged or destroyed, forcing many to relocate closer to humanitarian zones. The closure of the three main landfills by the Israel Defense Forces has also forced local authorities to find temporary dumpsites.
At least 225 temporary waste dumps were operating across the Gaza strip, as of May 2024.
Humanitarian Zone
Deir Al Balah
Khan Yunis
UN designated
waste dump

waste dump
Rafah
