Beit Lahiya
Closed
landfills
Currently operational
landfill
These waste dumps were identified through an analysis of open-source information including satellite imagery, social media posts and official reports. It is suspected that many more such dumps could exist.
The silent threat of accumulated and improperly handled waste is made worse in a conflict zone that is also plagued by high temperatures, destroyed sanitary infrastructure and a population weakened by food scarcity and lack of medical attention.
The United Nations has repeatedly warned of the public health and environmental crisis arising from widespread pollution caused by solid waste in Gaza.
A key issue is the leachate, a polluted liquid that results when water filters through layers of accumulated waste in improperly managed garbage piles, posing a risk when it infiltrates soil and contaminates groundwater.
Single-use plastics
Glass
Contaminated ground water
The risk of pollution of the
groundwater by the leachate from
landfill sites is considered to be the
most significant risk for the natural
environment and human health.
External water dependence
Due to the contamination of natural
water sources, the population becomes
overly reliant on external water sources.
Contaminated grazing lands
Heavy metals and toxins can harm
animals grazing in and around areas
causing health problems and reduced
fertility in animals.
Disruption of aquatic systems
Leachate can enter in aquatic body
through surface runoff, can be ingested
and accumulated in different tissues of
fish, and lead to perturbations in fish
biological systems.
Reduced crop yields
Heavy metals and acidic compounds
reduce crop yields and the viability of
agriculture land.
Contamination of food crops
Consuming contaminated crops can
cause chronic health issues, such as
neurological and developmental
damage in children, reproductive
issues and cancer.
Types of waste
Biodegradable waste such as paper and textiles can attract pests, emit methane, and cause respiratory illnesses if burned.
Non-biodegradable waste like single-use plastics can break down into microplastics and be inhaled or ingested.
Hazardous and toxic waste like batteries, metals and chemicals can leach toxins, bioaccumulate and increase cancer risk.
Plastic bottles and containers
Paper and cardboard
Attracting pests
Houseflies, mosquitoes, rats, cockroaches, and fleas spread diseases like malaria, typhoid, and cholera. They contaminate food, water, and air, trigger allergies, and thrive in waste, posing serious health risks.
Houseflies
Scrap metal
Textiles
Rats
Medical waste
Contaminated surface water
Leachate seepage
Leachate seeps from landfill, or trash piles, and is made up of various chemical substances.
Batteries and household chemical waste
Organic waste
Industrial plastic
Ground water level
Ground water level
Root system damage
Roots absorb water mixed with
toxins and heavy metals which can
accumulate in plant tissue.
Bioaccumulation of pollutants
Pollutants can bioaccumulate in the
meat and milk of animals, which adds
to the human risk while consuming.
Soil degradation
Leachate contamination depletes
soil fertility and disrupt nutrient
availability.
While cleanup efforts have been attempted at various points throughout the war, recent UNDP research warned that most of these efforts are localised in Gaza‚Äôs south, with only 600 to 700 tons of waste collected daily. This barely covers the estimated 2,000 tons of waste generated every day.
Most of Gaza‚Äôs displaced people ‚ÄĒ communities sheltering in refugee camps or makeshift areas ‚ÄĒ have been forced to live near the piles of rotting garbage. No early relief for their plight is in sight, despite a ceasefire signed by Israel and Hamas in January.
‚ÄúWe can‚Äôt sleep, we can‚Äôt eat, we can‚Äôt drink. The smell is killing us,‚ÄĚ said Ahmed Shaloula, originally from Gaza City but now one of many displaced Palestinians living in Khan Younis.
Gaza‚Äôs oldest educational institution, Al-Aqsa University was first established in 1955, with its first campus in Gaza City. Its second campus, located in Khan Younis, was built in the past decade. It‚Äôs this campus that has now become the home of many displaced people, with the campus‚Äô surroundings converted into a makeshift waste field.
The waste dump stretches over 300 metres and is estimated to cover nearly 10,000 square metres.
Before - October 20, 2023
After - October 10, 2024
Waste
dump

space

or new structures

buildings

Satellite images: Planet Labs
A waste dump near the Nuseirat refugee camp in central Gaza can also be seen in satellite images.
Before - May 12, 2023
After - October 24, 2024
New structures
or tents
Waste
dump
Open
space
Satellite images: Planet Labs
A landfill in a public area in Central Gaza, shown in the video below, has become part of the grim reality for many Palestinians residing nearby.
Abou Mohammed, a Palestinian, who lives about 100-150 meters away from the landfill, says he is worried about the health of his children. ‚ÄúThis waste here has brought diseases. Our children are suffering from various ailments that we have never seen before,‚ÄĚ he said.
The video shows a landfill in Central Gaza in May 2024.
The video shows a landfill in Central Gaza in May 2024. The two photos are taken at other dump sites in Gaza. REUTERS
Youssef Hammad, a municipality worker, also explained that the area was not originally designated for waste disposal. However, the conflict has given them little choice but to dump the waste there.
‚ÄúThe municipality was compelled to urgently dispose of waste in this location due to the inability of municipal crews to access the official dumping grounds ... With the onset of the war, we were unable to reach these locations due to the lack of mechanisms of movement (for access) caused by road blockages,‚ÄĚ said Hammad.
Hammad also warned of the environmental hazard associated with such landfills. ‚ÄúThese include stray dogs, the proliferation of insects, as well as environmental pollution and the degradation of the surrounding environment in this area,‚ÄĚ he said.
Abu Mazen Khatab, a displaced man living near the waste dump with his family, including six-month-old daughter, says he worries sewage water will soon seep into his tent. ‚ÄúIf we don't die from the missiles; we will die from the sewage and the bad smells here and the waste.‚ÄĚ
Sources
United Nations, Planet Labs
Edited by
Simon Scarr, Rebecca Pazos and William Maclean
Reuters, the news and media division of Thomson Reuters, is the world‚Äôs largest multimedia news provider, reaching billions of people worldwide every day. item.title: LA's fast fires show a larger trend in fire speed
item.description:
Fires are growing faster in the western United States. The fastest of them ‚Äď termed ‚Äúfast fires‚ÄĚ ‚Äď often erupt near towns and account for most structural wildfire damage according to researchers who analyzed over 60,000 fires from 2001 to 2020.
Fast fires grow about 4,000 acres or more on their fastest day, at least two football fields a minute. That single-day growth threshold for a ‚Äúfast fire‚ÄĚ is even higher in California at almost four football fields a minute.
Humans and our infrastructure start nearly all of the fires that threaten our homes. Exactly why fires become fast is a developing area of research, but weather and fuel conditions can feed the speed.
Of the 20 fastest fires between 2001 and 2020, 16 destroyed structures and all but one fire came within 2.5 miles of a property.
The fastest LA fires and overall top 20 fastest-growing fires across the continental US from 2001 to 2020. A structure is considered exposed when it is within 2.5 miles of the fire perimeter even if it was not damaged.

Small multiple maps of the 20 fastest-growing fires in the continental US, the Palisades fire and Eaton fire. Each map shows the fire perimeter, the year the fire took place, the state the fire burned in, and the settlement area around the fire.
Although many fast fires start near populated areas, the location alone doesn't determine whether a fire will be fast.
‚ÄúProbably hundreds, maybe thousands of ignitions happen around the Hollywood Hills every year. Most of those fires are able to be put out,‚ÄĚ said Maxwell Cook, a research assistant with the Cooperative Institute for Research in Environmental Sciences and geography PhD candidate at the University of Colorado Boulder.
A wildfire not only needs the initial spark but also the optimal fuel and weather. When an ignition overlaps with poor conditions like heat, drought and wind, fires can spread faster than firefighters can contain them.
In the case of the fires in Los Angeles, a three-month drought parched the vegetation and buildings, making for piles of kindling. The seasonal high-speed winds blew embers to nearby fuel and made the pace of fire growth impossible to match.
Remnants of a house structure burning from the Eaton Fire in Pasadena, California January 7, 2025. REUTERS
‚ÄúWhen fires are moving that fast, often times there‚Äôs not much to do in terms of stopping the fire. It really becomes a situation of getting people out,‚ÄĚ Cook said.
Researchers have observed an increase in human-started fires and expect that pattern to continue as the population grows and more people move into wild areas. The conditions that lead to faster fires are also expected to worsen as the West becomes hotter and drier in line with climate projections.
Maximum daily fire growth, sized by structures destroyed

destroyed 18,804 structures,

on its fastest day

A scatterplot of square markers representing fast-moving fires in California from 2001 to 2020. The size of each square reflects the number of structures damaged by each fire. The plot shows an increasing trend over time, with more fires causing greater structural damage.
Note: Data shows fast fires (maximum growth rate of at least 7,092 acres in a day) in California between 2001-2020.
The type of vegetation in an area can determine how quickly a wildfire spreads. The majority of fast fires burn through grasslands where the short, dry grass is easily ignitable. Broadleaf forests tend to burn more slowly in part due to higher moisture levels in the vegetation. The fires in LA were different, spreading by structure-to-structure ignition.
‚ÄúWhen a house burns, it produces several million embers and flames up to 5 meters (14 feet) high for an hour, contributing to other houses catching fire,‚ÄĚ said Guillermo Rein, professor of Fire Science at Imperial College London.
Housing density can work for or against fire suppression and potential damage. Heavily populated areas can often act as a fuel break for a wildfire because they tend to have less flammable vegetation. However, they face the risk of one burning house threatening another. More remote areas don‚Äôt have the same house-to-house risk but can be difficult for firefighters to reach.
‚ÄúPretty much any fire that starts in California is near where a lot of people live,‚ÄĚ Cook said. ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs its own beast.‚ÄĚ
Top 20 fastest-growing fires with structure loss in California from 2001 to 2020

Note: Damage inspections are ongoing to determine the exact number of structures destroyed from the LA fires. As of January 22, the damage assessment team has verified 6,662 structures were destroyed in the Palisades fire and 9,418 in the Eaton fire.
The frequency and size of wildfires is well documented, but Cook and his team say measurements of fire speed deserve more attention and are integral to assessing human safety in fire-prone areas.
‚ÄúIncorporating understanding of fire speed into city planning and into fire management, that's gonna give you a lot more nuanced understanding of what risk you‚Äôre actually assuming.‚ÄĚ
Note
Average maximum fire growth rate in the American West is derived from an updated FIRED dataset documenting fires recorded between 2000 and 2024. The Palisades, Eaton and Hughes fires maximum growth come from comparing the fire sizes noted in CalFire status reports.
Sources

item.title: Indonesia says will withdraw from Board of Peace if it does not benefit Palestinians
item.description:
JAKARTA, March 6 (Reuters) - Indonesia's President Prabowo Subianto has reassured local Islamic groups that he will withdraw from U.S. President Donald Trump's 'Board of ‚ÄčPeace' if the platform does not benefit Palestinians, a government ‚ÄĆstatement said on Friday.
The participation of Indonesia, the world's largest Muslim-majority nation, on the board, and its decision to contribute troops for a Gaza stabilisation ‚Äčforce, has drawn criticism from experts and Muslim groups at ‚Äčhome, who say it compromises its long-standing support for the ‚Ā†Palestinian cause.
Prabowo gathered the groups' leaders for a meeting on ‚ÄčThursday evening where he reiterated his reasoning behind joining the board, according ‚Äčto a statement put out by the government communication office.
Prabowo will withdraw from the board if it does not benefit Palestinian and Indonesian interests, the statement quoted ‚ÄčHanif Alatas of the Islamic Brotherhood Front as saying.
"The president ‚Äčsaid that if he sees that there is no longer any benefit for Palestine...and ‚ÄĆthat ‚Ā†it is not in line with Indonesia's national interests, he will withdraw," Hanif said in the statement.
The Indonesian Ulema Council, a leading clerical body, had previously called for Indonesia to exit the board due ‚Äčto the U.S. ‚Äčrole in the ongoing ‚Ā†Iran war .
Nahdlatul Ulama, the country's largest Muslim group, said Indonesia's government could use the board to encourage ‚Äčde-escalation in the Middle East.
"Indonesia could declare that ‚Äčthe (board's) agenda ‚Ā†is on hold until there are talks on de-escalation and peace from the American-Israeli war against Iran," its chief Yahya Cholil Staquf said ‚Ā†in ‚Äča statement issued by Prabowo's office late on Thursday.
All ‚ÄčBoard of Peace discussions have been halted due to the war, Indonesia Foreign Minister Sugiono ‚Äčhas previously said.

item.title: US weighs oil futures market action to combat price spikes, White House official says
item.description:
March 5 (Reuters) - The U.S. Treasury Department is expected to announce measures ‚Äčas soon as Thursday aimed at combating rising energy prices in the wake of the Iran conflict , including potential action involving the ‚ÄĆoil futures market, a senior White House official said.
The potential move would mark an unusual attempt by Washington to influence energy prices through financial markets rather than physical oil supplies, as officials race to blunt the political and economic impact of rising fuel costs.
The details of the plan are unclear and the White House official, speaking on condition ‚Äčof anonymity to discuss internal matters, declined to provide specifics, saying they did not want to get ahead of the Treasury announcement.
U.S. crude futures ‚Äčhave jumped nearly 21% since the war with Iran started on Saturday, as the spreading conflict disrupted Middle East supplies. The ‚Ā†national average cost of gasoline, meanwhile, has risen 27 cents since last week to $3.25 per gallon, according to AAA, a U.S. travel organization that ‚Äčtracks fuel prices.
The idea of U.S. intervention in the futures market reflects the background of Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, a former hedge fund manager and ‚Äčglobal macro investor who spent decades trading currencies, bonds and commodities before joining the administration.
Bessent previously served as chief investment officer at Soros Fund Management and later founded the macro hedge fund Key Square Group.
A Treasury spokesperson could not be immediately reached for comment.
Energy analysts said the effectiveness of such a move would depend heavily on ‚Äčthe specifics.
"The devil is in the details‚Ä¶ we will have to see what the U.S. government‚Äôs plans are," said Ben Hoff, head of commodity ‚Äčquant research at Societe Generale, who called the potential step unprecedented.
He said financial tools can only go so far in influencing energy markets, which are driven primarily by ‚ÄĆphysical supply ‚Ā†and demand.
The U.S Federal Reserve intervened to combat the financial crisis in 2008 by purchasing massive amounts of mortgage-backed securities and Treasury bonds in a policy called Quantitative Easing.
Treasury last October also used its Exchange Stabilization Fund to prop up Argentina's currency by buying pesos in the open market and backing a $20 billion swap line for Latin America's third-largest economy.
That fund, created during the Great Depression, had total assets of $220.85 billion as of January 31.
In recent ‚Äčyears, it has been used to ‚Äčback Federal Reserve lending facilities ‚Ā†during crises such as the 2008-2009 global financial crisis, the COVID-19 pandemic and the 2023 U.S. bank stability crisis.
There have been examples of government energy market interventions outside of the United States.
Mexico, for example, has for years executed ‚Äča hedging program called the "Hacienda hedge" - once the world's largest financial oil deal - to protect the country's oil ‚Äčrevenues from price ‚Ā†crashes on the world market.
However, the Latin American country is hedging physical oil inventory rather than using purely financial instruments.
President Donald Trump said on Thursday he was not concerned about rising U.S. gas prices driven by the widening Iran conflict , telling Reuters in an exclusive interview that the U.S. military operation was his priority.
"I don't have ‚Ā†any concern ‚Äčabout it," he said when asked about the higher prices at the pump. "They'll drop ‚Äčvery rapidly when this is over, and if they rise, they rise, but this is far more important than having gasoline prices go up a little bit."

item.title: Asia struggles to find fuel oil as Middle East exports plummet, sources say
item.description:
SINGAPORE, March 6 (Reuters) - Fuel oil traders in Asia are struggling to secure alternative ‚Äčsupply as the Iran war curtails shipments from key Middle Eastern suppliers through the Strait of Hormuz , prompting them to look to ‚ÄĆthe West for replacement cargoes.
The shortage of Middle Eastern fuel oil volumes is set to dent the supply of bunker fuel for powering ships, with prices at key bunker ports such as Singapore set for further hikes in coming weeks, raising refuelling costs for vessel owners. Those higher costs will translate into increased prices for ‚Äčcompanies transporting goods.
Expectations for a growing shortage sparked a sharp rally in fuel oil markets this week, especially for high-sulphur fuel oil ‚Äčwhich typically comes from the Middle East.
Volumes of fuel oil exports transiting the Strait of Hormuz and bound ‚Ā†for Asia typically average 1.2 million metric tons per month, or about 246,000 barrels per day, Kpler data showed, with about 70% ending ‚Äčup in Southeast Asia.
Overall fuel oil exports via the Strait of Hormuz usually total about 3.7 million tons per month, the data showed.
Tanker transits ‚Äčare now about 90% lower than last week, Kpler's analysis of vessel activity showed.
"When such a large share of the global high-sulphur complex depends on a single chokepoint, even partial transit disruption can tighten balances quickly and amplify bunker volatility," said Sumit Ritolia, lead analyst for refining and supply modelling at Kpler.
Prices for ‚Äčhigh-sulphur bunker fuel delivered in Singapore, the world's largest ship refuelling hub, have risen more than 40% since the start of the war, ‚Äčwhile prices for delivered low-sulphur fuel oil have climbed more than 30%.
Some high-sulphur supply could come from Western refineries, though sky-high tanker rates make trading economics extremely ‚ÄĆchallenging, ‚Ā†fuel oil traders said.
"Everyone is struggling to find oil for the second half of March. Tankers are too expensive and arbitrage to Singapore is closed," a trader based in Singapore said.
Possible supply sources include the United States and Mexico, traders said, but volumes are insufficient. Another possible source is Venezuela, but these cargoes have remained in the West so far this year.
"Obviously there is also Russia but these barrels remain sensitive for some ‚Äčbuyers," another trader said. Russian fuel ‚Äčremains under sanction because of the ‚Ā†war in Ukraine.
Iranian fuel oil is also under long-standing sanctions but China continues to purchase it. However, those shipments have also halted because of the conflict.
Any pullback in Iranian HSFO supply would prompt China's independent ‚Äčasphalt producers to pull more straight-run fuel oil from Russia, limiting availability in the Singapore Strait, said ‚Äčconsultancy FGE NexantECA.
Others are ‚Ā†looking towards regional Asian refiners but volumes are set to decline as they cut production amid a crude oil shortage caused by the Middle East war.
In the low-sulphur market, price hikes were less steep as some supply still comes from Brazil and Nigeria, though cargoes from Kuwait's al-Zour refinery are shut ‚Ā†in the ‚ÄčGulf.
Costs of future replenishment are expected to jump due to wider tightness in the ‚Äčmarket, said traders.
While the market is coping with an existing large build in onshore inventories in Singapore as well as volumes stored on ships, stockpiles are set to draw sharply in ‚Äčthe coming weeks, traders said.

item.title: Wealthy Asians look to move Dubai assets closer to home on Iran war fears
item.description:
SINGAPORE/DUBAI, March 6 (Reuters) - Soon after the first Iranian missile and drone attacks on Dubai last week, two Indian entrepreneurs based there tried to move more than $100,000 each from their ‚Äčlocal bank accounts to Singapore to hedge risk.
Technological glitches in the aftermath of the Iranian attacks initially scuppered those plans, the entrepreneurs, who did not ‚ÄĆwish to be identified due to the sensitivity of the matter, told Reuters.
One of them said he managed to subsequently transfer the sum to his Singapore bank account via another Emirates-based bank.
Scores of other wealthy Asians are making enquiries or taking similar steps to move their Dubai-parked assets to the regional financial hubs of Singapore and Hong Kong, industry advisers and lawyers said, as the U.S.-Israel war on Iran clouds ‚Äčthe Gulf's safe-haven aura and rattles investors.
While the rich typically diversify their investments across regions and asset classes, they choose where to be based depending on tax, regulatory, ‚Äčprivacy and operational considerations.
Towards that end, Dubai has emerged in recent years as a preferred wealth hub for entrepreneurs and rich families in ‚Ā†Asia, mainly from China, as they look to take advantage of its favourable policies. Moreover, with a property and infrastructure boom, the Gulf region has also become an investment destination.
The ‚Äčtrend is now under sharp scrutiny, as the attacks on Dubai and Abu Dhabi have thrown into doubt the United Arab Emirates' (UAE) reputation for stability .
Singapore-based private wealth lawyer Ryan Lin said ‚Äčsix or seven of his 20 Dubai-based clients, each holding an average of $50 million in assets, contacted him this week, with three planning immediate asset transfers to the city-state.
One client is "checking how quickly they can transfer everything to Singapore", Lin said.
Iris Xu, principal at global corporate and fund services provider Anderson Global, said 10 to 20 family offices have enquired this week with her firm about moving assets back ‚Äčto Singapore from the Middle East on worries the conflict might drag on.
Family offices are one-stop firms that manage the portfolios of the wealthy.
"Dubai was always about tax benefits but ‚Äčnow I think the tax benefits may not be the top priority for them," she said.
A wealth management adviser in Singapore, who did not wish to be identified because they were not authorised ‚ÄĆto talk ‚Ā†to the media, said they had spoken to 13 UAE-based clients so far with more than half serious about moving assets to Singapore.
"Flying back and forth will be a challenge even if the conflict ends tomorrow. It is a confidence thing," said the adviser.
Grace Tang, CEO of Phillip Private Equity, said her predominantly Asian clients are skittish, with 10 to 20 asking about moving their wealth to Singapore and looking to preserve their capital.
Not all wealth managers, however, view the ongoing Middle East conflict as prompting immediate capital flight.
Dhruba Jyoti ‚ÄčSengupta, CEO of Dubai-based WRISE Private Middle ‚ÄčEast, a wealth management group, said the ‚Ā†firm has not seen "serious capital flight discussions", as clients were confident about the UAE's long-term resilience.
"They are sophisticated global investors, already diversified internationally, but deeply invested ... in the UAE's growth story," he said. "Despite the broader geopolitical turmoil in the region, clients are feeling safe and ‚Äčsecure."
The UAE's banking and financial sector was resilient , strong, stable, and well-positioned to navigate regional developments, its central bank governor Khaled Mohamed Balama ‚Äčsaid on Thursday, adding ‚Ā†that banks, financial firms, and insurers were operating normally and without disruption.
Leading Singapore-based wealth managers, Bank of Singapore and DBS Group (DBSM.SI) , opens new tab said their clients were closely observing the developments in the region and were taking a wait-and-watch approach, for now.
As the UAE scrambled to maintain its safe-haven status, some were going ahead with their expansion plans in the Emirates.
Jeremy Lim, co-founder of GrandWay Family Office, ‚Ā†is in ‚Äčthe process of opening a family office in Abu Dhabi and said his plans have not changed - as ‚Äčlong as the UAE does not become directly involved in the conflict and barring any further escalation from Iran.
"The real deal-breaker for businesses would be if the UAE were to...become directly involved alongside one side in a ‚Äčconflict," said Lim.

item.title: Justice Department releases missing FBI interviews in Epstein files with woman who made claims against Trump
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 5 (Reuters) - The U.S. Justice Department released FBI records on Thursday that summarize interviews of an unidentified woman in ‚ÄĆwhich she made accusations against President Donald Trump related to an alleged sexual encounter.
FBI agents interviewed the woman four times in 2019 as part of their investigation into accused sex trafficker Jeffrey Epstein. The Justice Department had previously released a log confirming that the interviews took ‚Äčplace but released a summary of only one of those four meetings, in which she accused Epstein of ‚Äčmolesting her when she was a teenager.
The newly disclosed records , opens new tab , which were posted on the department's website on ‚Ā†Thursday, show she also claimed Trump attempted to force her to perform oral sex after Epstein introduced her to ‚Äčthe future president in New York or New Jersey in the 1980s when she was between 13 and 15 years old.
The ‚ÄčWhite House did not immediately respond to questions about the disclosures. Politico, which first reported the disclosures, said White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt called the woman's claims "completely baseless accusations, backed by zero credible evidence."
The Justice Department has cautioned that some of the documents include "untrue and ‚Äčsensationalist claims made against President Trump." Reuters could not independently confirm the accuracy of the woman's allegations, and FBI records ‚Äčsuggest agents stopped speaking with her in 2019.
The Justice Department said in a post on the social media platform X that the records ‚ÄĆit ‚Ā†released Thursday were among 15 documents that it had ‚Äúincorrectly coded as duplicative‚ÄĚ and not published as a result.
The disclosure comes as the Justice Department faces scrutiny in Congress over its handling of documents from the Epstein investigation, which it is required to make public. Democrats have accused Trump's administration of concealing records related to Trump, and a committee in the House of Representatives ‚Äčvoted to subpoena Attorney General ‚ÄčPam Bondi so lawmakers ‚Ā†can question her about how the government is handling the disclosures.
Trump has said his association with Epstein ended in the mid-2000s and that he was never aware of the financier's sexual ‚Äčabuse. Records previously released by the department show Trump flew several times on Epstein's plane in ‚Äčthe 1990s, ‚Ā†which Trump has denied. After the financier was first accused of sexual misconduct, Trump called the police chief in Palm Beach to say that "everyone has known he's been doing this," according to an FBI interview record.
In the report of the woman's final , opens new tab interview, conducted in October 2019, ‚Ā†during Trump's ‚Äčfirst presidency, agents asked whether she would be willing to provide more ‚Äčinformation about Trump. In response, the agent wrote, she "asked what the point would be of providing the information at this point in her life when ‚Äčthere was a strong possibility nothing could be done about it."

item.title: India's tech state Karnataka bans social media for children under 16
item.description:
NEW DELHI, March 6 (Reuters) - The southern Indian state ‚Äčof Karnataka, home to ‚ÄĆthe tech hub of Bengaluru, has banned ‚Äčthe use of ‚Äčsocial media by those under ‚Ā†the age of ‚Äč16, state Chief Minister ‚ÄčSiddaramaiah said on Friday, becoming the first in India ‚Äčto do so.
"With ‚Äčthe objective of preventing adverse ‚ÄĆeffects ‚Ā†of increasing mobile usage on children, usage of social media ‚Äčwill be ‚Äčbanned ‚Ā†for children under the age ‚Äčof 16," Siddaramaiah, ‚Äčwho ‚Ā†uses only one name, said in his ‚Ā†budget ‚Äčspeech.

item.title: Oil falls but set for steepest weekly gain since 2022
item.description:
SINGAPORE, March 6 (Reuters) - Oil prices fell ‚Äčon Friday for the first time in six days as the U.S. government is weighing potentially ‚ÄĆintervening in the futures market to blunt rising prices and issued waivers for Russian oil purchases to ease supply constraints from the Middle East war.
Brent crude futures were down 95 cents, or 1.1%, to $84.46 per barrel and West Texas Intermediate was down $1.08, or 1.3%, ‚Äčto $79.93 as of 0440 GMT.
Still, Brent has surged 16.4% this week while the WTI jumped 19.2% on ‚Äčtrack for the steepest weekly gain since Russia launched its full-scale invasion of Ukraine in ‚Ā†February 2022.
The gains followed the start on February 28 of the war between the U.S. and Israel, on one side, and Iran that has halted tankers ‚Äčmoving through the Strait of Hormuz , which typically carries roughly one-fifth of the world's daily oil supply.
The conflict has since spread ‚Äčacross the key Middle East energy-producing region, causing disruptions to oil output and the shutdowns of refineries and liquefied natural gas plants.
"With every passing day, halted activities in Hormuz will have two major impacts on oil: the inability to store 20 million barrels per day and the ‚Äčlack of flow to the world, which could drive global energy prices higher," said Priyanka Sachdeva, senior market analyst ‚Äčat Phillip Nova.
In the face of the potentially higher gains, the U.S. Treasury Department is expected to announce measures to combat rising energy ‚ÄĆprices from ‚Ā†the Iran conflict, including potential action involving the oil futures market, a senior White House official said on Thursday, without providing any details.
Such a move would mark an unusual attempt by Washington to influence energy prices through financial markets rather than physical oil supplies.
The U.S. Treasury also on Thursday granted waivers for companies to start buying sanctioned Russian oil ‚Äčstored on tankers to ease ‚Äčsupply constraints which have ‚Ā†pushed refineries in Asia to reduce their fuel processing.
The first waivers were given to Indian refiners , who have responded by buying millions of barrels of Russian crude oil cargoes, reversing months of ‚Äčpressure on them to halt the purchases.
Data from ship-tracking firm Kpler shows about 30 ‚Äčmillion barrels of ‚Ā†Russian oil are available and loaded on vessels in the Indian Ocean, Arabian Sea region and Singapore Strait, including volumes in floating storage.
Still, the recent gain in prices is relatively subdued compared to other price shocks such as in 2022 when ‚Ā†Russia attacked ‚ÄčUkraine and prices rose above $100 a barrel.
"It's important to put this ‚Äčmove into perspective: despite crude's almost 20% surge this month, the price is currently just $3.40 above its average over the last four years,‚ÄĚ IG ‚Äčanalyst Tony Sycomore said on Friday.

item.title: Exclusive: Indian refiners buying prompt Russian oil as Iran war hits supplies
item.description:
NEW DELHI/WASHINGTON, March 5 (Reuters) - Indian refiners are buying millions of barrels ‚ÄĆof prompt Russian crude oil cargoes as the South Asian nation seeks to navigate an oil supply crunch triggered by the Middle East conflict, six sources familiar with the matter said.
After months of Washington pressuring New Delhi to avoid buying Russian barrels in an effort to reduce money flowing to Moscow's war effort in Ukraine, the U.S. Treasury Department issued a 30-day ‚Äčwaiver on Thursday allowing India to buy Russian oil currently stuck at sea.
"To enable oil to keep flowing into the global market, the ‚ÄčTreasury Department is issuing a temporary 30-day waiver to allow Indian refiners to purchase Russian oil," Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent ‚Ā†said.
"This deliberately short-term measure will not provide significant financial benefit to the Russian government as it only authorizes transactions involving oil already stranded at sea," ‚Äčhe said in a statement.
He called it a stopgap measure, as Washington expects India to eventually buy more U.S. oil.
India is vulnerable to energy supply shocks , with crude ‚Äčstocks covering only about 25 days of demand. India gets about 40% of its oil imports from the Middle East through the Strait of Hormuz .
India was the top buyer of Russian seaborne crude after Moscow's 2022 Ukraine invasion, but in January, its refiners started to reduce purchases under pressure from Washington.
Cutting Russian oil purchases helped New Delhi avoid 25% tariffs and clinch ‚Äčan interim trade deal with the U.S.
It is unclear whether the United States has allowed India to increase Russian purchases to offset potential Middle Eastern supply ‚Äčlosses.
A source directly involved with the matter said India had approached U.S. President Donald Trump's administration seeking approval to buy Russian crude imports due to the Iran conflict.
India's oil ‚ÄĆand foreign ‚Ā†ministries did not respond to Reuters emails seeking comments. The White House and the U.S. Treasury Department did not immediately respond to requests for comment.
State refiners Indian Oil Corp (IOC.NS) , opens new tab , Bharat Petroleum Corp (BPCL.NS) , opens new tab , Hindustan Petroleum Corp (HPCL.NS) , opens new tab and Mangalore Refinery and Petrochemicals Ltd (MRPL.NS) , opens new tab are talking to traders for prompt delivery of Russian cargoes, according to the Reuters sources.
One of the sources said Indian state refiners have bought about 20 million barrels of Russian oil from traders so far.
HPCL and MRPL last received ‚ÄčRussian oil in November, according to ‚Äčdata obtained from industry sources.
The traders ‚Ā†are selling Russian Urals to India at a premium of $4-$5 per barrel to Brent on a delivered basis for arrival at Indian ports in March and early April, three of the sources said.
This is in contrast to a discount of ‚Äčabout $13 per barrel for cargoes traded in February, traders said.
HPCL had bought two cargoes of Russian oil at a $13 ‚Äčdiscount before the war ‚Ā†started on February 28.
"India refiners are back in the market ... nowadays more than prices, availability of molecules is the issue," said one of the traders involved in Russian oil sales to India.
This source said Reliance Industries (RELI.NS) , opens new tab also approached his company for the purchase of prompt Russian oil cargoes.
Refiners in India had already started tapping Russian oil aboard ‚Ā†vessels floating ‚Äčoff the country's coast to make up for the loss of Middle Eastern crude, two sources ‚Äčwith direct knowledge of the matter said earlier in the day.
Indian refiners did not immediately respond to Reuters emails sent out after business hours.

item.title: China in talks with Iran to allow safe oil and gas passage through Hormuz, sources say
item.description:
LONDON, March 5 (Reuters) - China is in talks with Iran to allow crude oil and Qatari liquefied natural gas vessels safe passage through the Strait of Hormuz ‚Äčas the U.S.-Israeli war on Tehran intensifies, three diplomatic sources told Reuters.
The war, ‚ÄĆwhich entered its sixth day on Thursday, has left the critical shipping passageway all-but shut, with countries around the world cut off from a fifth of global oil and liquefied natural gas supplies.
China, which has friendly ‚Äčrelations with Iran and relies heavily on Middle Eastern supplies, is unhappy about the ‚ÄčIslamic Republic's move to paralyse shipping through the Strait and is pressing Tehran ‚Ā†to allow safe passage for the vessels, according to the sources.
The world's second-largest economy gets ‚Äčabout 45% of its oil from the Strait.
Ship tracking data showed a vessel called the Iron ‚ÄčMaiden passed through the Strait overnight after changing its signalling to 'China-owner,' but far more sailings will be needed to calm global markets.
Crude oil prices are up more than 15% since the conflict began amid production stoppages ‚Äčas Tehran attacks energy facilities in the Gulf as well as ships crossing the Strait.
Its ‚Äčmissiles have also reached as far afield as Cyprus, Azerbaijan and Turkey, destabilising global markets and prompting major ‚ÄĆeconomies to ‚Ā†warn about inflation risks.
Crude tanker transits through the strait fell to four vessels on March 1, the day after hostilities broke out, versus an average of 24 a day since January, Vortexa vessel-tracking data showed.
Around 300 oil tankers remain inside the Strait, according to Vortexa and ship tracker ‚ÄčKpler.
Sugar industry veteran Mike ‚ÄčMcDougall told Reuters that ‚Ā†Middle East sugar executives say there are some ships transiting the Strait at the moment, all of which are either Chinese or Iranian-owned.
Jamal ‚ÄčAl-Ghurair, the managing director of Dubai-based Al Khaleej Sugar, told Reuters some ‚Äčships carrying ‚Ā†sugar are currently allowed to pass through the Strait while others are not, without giving further details.
Iran's government said earlier in the week that no vessels belonging to the United States, Israel, European ‚Ā†countries or ‚Äčtheir allies would be allowed to pass through the ‚ÄčStrait of Hormuz, but the statement made no mention of China.

item.title: Seven countries warn EU not to upend energy market design
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 5 (Reuters) - Governments of seven EU countries, including the Netherlands ‚Äčand Sweden, warned the bloc's executive on Thursday against interfering with the system that ‚ÄĆsets Europe's energy prices, as officials in Brussels scramble to find ways to bring down bills.
Energy prices have lurched onto Europe's political agenda this year, as industries warn they cannot compete with companies in the United States and China, where bills ‚Äčare lower. Soaring global oil and gas prices due to the Iran conflict have increased pressure on the ‚ÄčEuropean Commission to step in.
President Ursula von der Leyen has pledged to assess whether ‚Ā†the EU's current price-setting system should be revised, and the Commission is drawing up options to attempt ‚Äčto curb energy prices. But analysts and officials say there is no quick fix.
In a letter to Energy ‚ÄčCommissioner Dan Jorgensen, seen by Reuters, seven EU governments warned that changing the system which underpins Europe's energy market would lead to a less efficient mechanism and ultimately increase bills.
"Intervening in the electricity market design and changing price formation mechanisms ‚Äčwould also increase market and regulatory uncertainty, which is harmful for investments and European competitiveness," the letter ‚Äčsaid.
Dated March 5, the letter was also signed by the energy ministers of Denmark, Finland, Latvia, Luxembourg and Portugal.
The EU's electricity system is designed so that the last power plant needed to meet total demand sets the power price. Often, that is a gas plant - so gas price spikes can send electricity prices soaring, even when much of the power is being produced from cheaper renewable sources.
The seven countries said "the EU ‚Äčdependency on expensive, imported ‚Äčgas" is the root ‚Ā†cause of high prices, and not the power market design.
The signatories urged Brussels to instead expand cheaper renewable energy sources faster to limit the price-setting role of ‚Äčgas in the power mix, and increase measures that encourage consumers to ‚Äčuse energy when ‚Ā†prices are low.
The letter sets up a clash with governments including Italy, which has announced national plans to remove carbon costs from gas power plants' bills - an intervention that would upend the price-setting system.
The signatory countries are ‚Ā†largely ‚Äčwealthier EU members, and include Sweden and Finland, who have the ‚Äčlowest power prices in the EU. Both produce more than 90% of their electricity from low-carbon sources - a stark difference from others ‚Äčincluding Poland, which gets most of its electricity from CO2-emitting coal.

item.title: Lloyd‚Äôs market engaging with US government over Gulf maritime plan, officials say
item.description:
LONDON, March 5 (Reuters) - The Lloyd‚Äôs of London market is engaging with the U.S. government‚Äôs International Development ‚ÄčFinance Corporation over a plan to provide political risk insurance ‚ÄĆand guarantees for maritime trade in the Gulf , Lloyd‚Äôs market officials said on Thursday.
"Lloyd‚Äôs is engaging constructively with the U.S. Development Finance Corporation and relevant stakeholders, with ‚Äča clear focus on ensuring that the Lloyd‚Äôs market ‚Äčcontinues to lead as the global centre of excellence for ‚Ā†war risk insurance," a Lloyd‚Äôs spokesperson said.
The Lloyd‚Äôs Market Association (LMA), ‚Äčwhich represents the interests of all underwriting businesses in the Lloyd‚Äôs market, ‚Äčwelcomed the engagement of U.S. President Donald Trump, its CEO Sheila Cameron said separately in a statement on Thursday.
"Since Sunday 1 March, there have been at ‚Äčleast 40 transits of vessels through the Strait of Hormuz.
"There remain ‚Äčapproximately 1,000 vessels, approximately half of which are oil and gas tankers, with ‚ÄĆan ‚Ā†aggregate hull value exceeding $25 billion in the Persian/Arabian Gulf and surrounding waters,‚ÄĚ Cameron said, citing data.
Cameron added that the vast majority of these vessels were insured in the London market and insurance "currently remains in ‚Äčplace".
Insurance broker Marsh ‚Äčsaid on Wednesday it ‚Ā†had met with U.S. officials to explore solutions for restoring maritime trade.
The U.S. Navy could begin ‚Äčescorting oil tankers through the Strait of Hormuz if ‚Äčnecessary, Trump said ‚Ā†on Tuesday, adding he had ordered the International Development Finance Corporation to provide political risk insurance guarantees for maritime trade in the Gulf.
Earlier ‚Ā†this ‚Äčweek, London's marine insurance market widened the area ‚Äčin the Gulf it deems as high risk as the conflict in the Middle ‚ÄčEast escalates.

item.title: EU countries give final approval to 2040 climate target for 90% emissions cut
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 5 (Reuters) - European ‚ÄĆUnion countries gave the final approval on Thursday to a new climate target to slash greenhouse gas emissions 90% by 2040, pressing ahead with the bloc's ambitious climate agenda despite political resistance.
The new climate target ‚Äčis a hard-fought political compromise, struck by governments and EU lawmakers last year. ‚ÄčIt is more ambitious than most major economies' emissions-cutting commitments, including China's.
In ‚Ā†practice, the target will require an 85% emissions reduction from European industries against 1990 ‚Äčlevels. The EU will pay developing countries via carbon credits, so they cut emissions on ‚ÄčEurope's behalf to make up the rest, to reach 90%.
The EU agreed the target last year following months of wrangling between countries, such as Spain, which say worsening droughts and wildfires justified more ambitious ‚Äčgoals, and those like Poland and Italy, which sought to soften the emissions cuts, ‚Äčarguing that struggling industries cannot afford the upfront investments.
A reinforced majority of EU countries' ministers gave the ‚ÄĆfinal ‚Ā†formal sign-off to the legally binding goal at a meeting in Brussels. The Czech Republic, Slovakia, Poland and Hungary opposed it. The climate target will now pass into EU law.
As part of the agreement, the EU will also consider the option in future to ‚Äčuse international carbon credits ‚Äčto meet a ‚Ā†further 5% of its 2040 emissions reductions - potentially further softening the domestic efforts required.
The deal also delays the launch of a politically ‚Äčsensitive new EU carbon market by one year, to 2028 - a ‚Äčmove designed ‚Ā†to win over countries sceptical of the climate goal.
The target, which is designed to keep Europe on track to meet its pledge to reach net-zero emissions by 2050, falls short of the ‚Ā†90% domestic ‚Äčemissions cut recommended by the EU's climate science ‚Äčadvisers.
It is also weaker than Brussels' original plan for the goal, reflecting disagreement between EU governments over the speed and ‚Äčcost of their green agenda.

item.title: Israeli airstrikes pound Beirut suburb, Hezbollah warns Israelis
item.description:
BEIRUT/JERUSALEM, March 6 (Reuters) - Israel carried out heavy airstrikes on the Hezbollah-controlled southern suburbs of Beirut overnight after ordering its residents to leave, while the Iran-backed group warned Israelis to ‚Äčleave towns and villages at the frontier.
Explosions and flashes lit up the ‚ÄĆnight sky over the Beirut southern suburbs, Reuters footage showed. The Israeli military said it had carried out 26 waves of strikes overnight in the southern suburbs, saying targets included Hezbollah's command ‚Äčcentres and weapons storage facilities.
On Thursday, an Israeli military spokesperson told residents ‚Äčof the southern suburbs to move east and north, posting a ‚Ā†map showing four large districts of the capital he said they must leave, including ‚Äčareas adjacent to Beirut airport.
Hezbollah, in a message published in Hebrew on its Telegram ‚Äčchannel early on Friday, warned Israelis to leave towns within 5 km (3 miles) of the border.
"Your military's aggression against Lebanese sovereignty and safe citizens, the destruction of civilian infrastructure and the expulsion campaign ‚Äčit is carrying out will not go unchallenged," Hezbollah said.
During fighting between Hezbollah ‚Äčand Israel in 2024, tens of thousands of Israelis were evacuated from towns in the border area ‚ÄĆbut ‚Ā†many have since returned. Israeli officials have previously said there are no plans to remove them for now.
Lebanon was pulled into the war in the Middle East on Monday, when Hezbollah opened fire, igniting a new Israeli offensive, with airstrikes focused on ‚ÄčBeirut's southern suburbs and ‚Äčon southern and ‚Ā†eastern Lebanon.
Israel has also ordered Lebanese to leave large areas of southern and eastern Lebanon.
The Lebanese health ministry has reported 123 ‚Äčpeople have been killed and another 683 wounded as a result ‚Äčof Israeli ‚Ā†attacks this week. Its figures do not distinguish between civilians and combatants.
There have been no reported fatalities in Israel as a result of Hezbollah attacks.
Hezbollah, a Shi'ite Muslim group ‚Ā†established ‚Äčby Iran's Revolutionary Guards in 1982, was badly weakened ‚Äčby Israel during the 2024 war.

item.title: New Zealand‚Äôs ruling party plunges in polls, PM Luxon rules out resigning
item.description:
March 6 (Reuters) - Support for New Zealand‚Äôs ruling National party has fallen to its lowest level in more than four years and Prime Minister Christopher Luxon is no ‚Äčlonger voters‚Äô preferred leader, polling showed on Friday.
The monthly Taxpayers‚Äô Union-Curia ‚ÄĆpoll showed support for the conservative Nationals dropped 2.9 points to 28.4%, the worst showing for the party since late 2021.
Luxon, 55, and his coalition government, which has been in ‚Äčpower since the 2023 election, have lost popularity over the past year ‚Äčdue to a weakening economy and higher unemployment. The decline exposes ‚Ā†the centre-right bloc to strong competition from the Labour party which has seen ‚Äčits polling numbers improve in recent months.
While the poll results fuelled speculation of ‚ÄčLuxon‚Äôs resignation, he told local media he would "absolutely not" stand aside ahead of a general election on November 7.
"We‚Äôve got a lot of work to do and I‚Äôve got skills that are ‚Äčactually very useful at this time when we‚Äôre trying to navigate some challenging ‚Äčglobal environments," he told Newstalk ZB.
"Our biggest challenge in this country is our economy, and I ‚ÄĆunderstand ‚Ā†our economy well."
The Nationals' coalition partners New Zealand First and ACT polled at 9.7% and 7.5% respectively.
Support for Labour rose 0.3 points to 34.4%, while the Greens recorded 10.5% and Te Pati Maori at 3.2%.
That would translate to ‚Äč61 seats at the ‚Äčelection, enough for ‚Ā†the three parties to form government as a centre-left bloc, the poll said.
New Zealand has used a mixed-member proportional electoral ‚Äčsystem since 1996, making coalitions the norm.
The poll, which surveyed ‚Äč1,000 people, ‚Ā†also found approval for Luxon fell 1 point to 21%.
Labour leader Chris Hipkins gained 4.7 points to 22.7%, overtaking Luxon as preferred prime minister.
Luxon, the former CEO of ‚Ā†Air ‚ÄčNew Zealand, has faced criticism for some of ‚Äčhis policies, including the closure of a separate health authority for indigenous people and a lack of ‚Äčfunding increases for frontline employees.

item.title: More LNG tankers divert as Asian buyers seek cargoes
item.description:
SINGAPORE, March 6 (Reuters) - More tankers carrying liquefied natural gas are diverting towards Asia as buyers ‚Äčscramble for replacement cargoes after the Middle East war halted tanker traffic through the Strait of Hormuz and ‚ÄĆdisrupted supplies from Qatar, the world's second-largest seller of the fuel.
Shiptracking data by analytics firms Kpler and LSEG show three LNG tankers diverting towards Asia so far.
Carrying U.S. cargoes from the Plaquemines and Corpus Christi LNG terminals, respectively, the Simsimah and Clean Mistral tankers pivoted toward the South Atlantic ‚Äčon March 4 after heading northeast towards Europe.
Earlier this week, the BW Brussels, carrying a Nigerian cargo from Bonny ‚ÄčLNG, pivoted away from its initial Atlantic-bound course on March 3, and is now Asia-bound via the ‚Ā†Cape of Good Hope.
The price of the U.S. Henry Hub gas benchmark was $2.97 per million British thermal units on Thursday versus $17.01 ‚Äčper mmBtu for the European Title Transfer Facility (TTF) benchmark and $15.495 per mmBtu for the Japan-Korea Marker, the Asian benchmark.
The higher European and ‚ÄčAsian prices are more than enough to offset the cost of longer distances to move the cargoes to Asia.
"Cargoes have started to be diverted to Asia, away from Europe in recent days, and the prevailing JKM-TTF spreads, averaging much above the U.S. shipping differentials, indicate flexible U.S. cargoes will ‚Äčlikely start to come to Asia on stronger netbacks," said Energy Aspects analyst Kesher Sumeet.
While some Asian buyers are delaying spot ‚Äčpurchases or not awarding tenders due to elevated prices, others are paying up to secure cargoes.
"Buyers from South Korea, India, Taiwan, Bangladesh, and Thailand ‚ÄĆhave ‚Ā†been seeking replacement spot cargoes, though many remain hesitant to award tenders due to high prices or a lack of offers," said Sumeet.
"India and Bangladesh are reportedly securing spot cargoes above $20 per mmBtu, but the volumes awarded remain well below the levels impacted due to Qatari disruptions," he said, adding that Asian buyers will unlikely be able to replace all the lost Qatari cargoes for ‚ÄčMarch and April.
Bangladesh has secured two ‚Äčspot cargoes from Gunvor and ‚Ā†Vitol at $28.28 per mmBtu and $23.08 per mmBtu, respectively.
Additionally, a power utility in western Japan is seeking replacement cargoes after previously expecting deliveries from Qatar beginning in June 2026, while another major Japanese ‚Äčutility has been seeking prompt cargoes amid growing concerns that supply availability for late March and ‚Äčearly April delivery ‚Ā†may tighten, said Rystad Energy analyst Masanori Odaka.
Cargo diversions could intensify competition between the Atlantic and Pacific basins. Asia takes more than 80% of Qatar's LNG exports, and Europe is increasingly relying on LNG to fill gas storage since the region halted most Russian pipeline ‚Ā†gas imports ‚Äčafter Moscow's full-scale invasion of Ukraine.
While global front-month arbitrage was open to Asia ‚Äčearlier this week, the U.S. front-month arbitrage firmly pointed to Europe on Thursday, as high freight prices and a falling JKM-TTF spread make Europe more competitive, ‚Äčsaid Spark Commodities analyst Qasim Afghan.

item.title: India invokes emergency powers, orders refiners to boost LPG output
item.description:
NEW DELHI, March 6 (Reuters) - India has invoked emergency powers and directed refiners to maximise production of ‚Äčliquefied petroleum gas (LPG) to prevent a shortage of the ‚ÄĆcooking fuel after supply disruptions caused by the Middle East crisis, a government order showed.
The world's second-biggest importer of LPG last year ‚Äčconsumed 33.15 million metric tons of cooking gas, which ‚Äčis a mixture of propane and butane.
Imports account for ‚Ā†about two-thirds of LPG consumption, with the Middle East ‚Äčmaking up about 85‚Äď90% of that supply.
All oil refiners are ‚Äčasked to "maximise and ensure that propane and butane available with them are utilized for production of LPG", the order issued late on Thursday ‚Äčshowed.
The government has asked producers to make LPG, propane and ‚Äčbutane available to state refiners - Indian Oil Corp (IOC.NS) , opens new tab , Hindustan Petroleum Corp (HPCL.NS) , opens new tab and ‚ÄĆBharat ‚Ā†Petroleum Corp (BPCL.NS) , opens new tab for distribution to households.
The country has about 332 million active LPG consumers, government data showed.
A mandatory diversion of propane and butane for LPG production would curtail the output ‚Äčof alkylates, ‚Äča gasoline blending ‚Ā†component, by Reliance Industries Ltd (RELI.NS) , opens new tab .
Reliance exported about four cargoes per month of alkylates last year, ‚Äčaccording to LSEG data.
The government also ordered ‚Äčrefiners not ‚Ā†to divert propane and butane for petrochemical production.
Diverting propane and butane for LPG production will hurt margins for petrochemical companies ‚Ā†that make ‚Äčproducts such as polpropyline and alkylates, ‚Äčas they fetch better prices than LPG, a trade source said.

item.title: Pakistani, Afghan border forces clash as UN says war displaces 100,000
item.description:
KABUL, March 6 (Reuters) - Pakistani and Afghan troops exchanged fire at dozens of points along their border on Friday as ‚Äčthe U.N. said their week-old conflict has forced the displacement of more than 100,000 people.
The South Asian nations show ‚ÄĆno signs of rapprochement in their worst fighting in years, adding to the volatility in a region also contending with U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran - a nation that borders both Afghanistan and Pakistan.
Combat has included Pakistani air strikes on Taliban government installations, such as the Bagram air base north of the Afghan ‚Äčcapital Kabul.
Afghanistan's Ministry of Defense said Taliban forces struck Pakistani military installations along the 2,600-km (1,600-mile) border, ‚Äčdestroying numerous posts and shooting down a drone.
Pakistani security sources said they carried out ground and air operations ‚Ā†against military targets including Kandahar, the heartland of the Taliban and where its core leadership resides, and destroyed several Afghan border posts.
Dozens ‚Äčgathered in Kabul on Friday to protest Pakistan's attacks on Afghan territory, chanting anti-Pakistan slogans, a witness said, while the Bakhter news ‚Äčagency said a large gathering in Laghman Province demonstrated against Pakistan's recent attacks.
People living in border towns have told Reuters that troops begin exchanging heavy shelling after sunset, placing homes in the firing line just as families sit down to break their fast in the holy month of Ramadan.
Several said relatives and neighbours ‚Äčhave fled.
"The situation in Afghanistan and Pakistan remains tense amid active conflict along the border," the United Nations refugee agency said, adding that ‚Äčsome 115,000 people in Afghanistan and 3,000 in Pakistan were thought to have fled their homes.
Several countries have offered to negotiate a truce, most recently ‚ÄĆTurkey, although ‚Ā†the Iran war has diverted the attention of most Gulf states that had stepped forward.
Pakistani government spokesperson Mosharraf Zaidi said no negotiations were taking place to end the conflict.
"There is nothing to talk about. There will be no dialogue and no negotiations," he told state-owned Pakistan TV. "Terrorism from Afghanistan has to end - that is Afghanistan‚Äôs problem. Pakistan's responsibility is to protect its citizens."
The conflict began ‚Äčlast week with Pakistani air strikes ‚Äčinside Afghanistan that Islamabad said ‚Ā†targeted militant strongholds. Afghanistan called the strikes a violation of sovereignty and announced retaliatory operations.
Islamabad has said Kabul provides safe haven to militants executing attacks on Pakistan from its soil. The Taliban has denied ‚Äčaiding such groups and said militancy in Pakistan is an internal problem.
On Friday, the Taliban's defence ‚Äčministry said it had ‚Ā†also struck a military base in Pakistan's southwestern Balochistan province. Reuters could not verify the strike, and Pakistan's military has not reported any damage in the region.
Both sides have regularly said they inflicted heavy damage on the other and killed hundreds of opposition troops, without providing evidence. ‚Ā†Reuters has ‚Äčnot been able to verify the reports.
The U.N. mission in Afghanistan has said 56 ‚Äčcivilians have been killed in the country and 128 wounded since fighting began. The Taliban government has said 110 civilians have been killed.
Pakistan has rejected both sets of ‚Äčfigures, saying it targets only militants and support infrastructure.

item.title: CK Hutchison steps up legal push as Panama moves to seize ports
item.description:
March 6 (Reuters) - Hong Kong conglomerate CK Hutchison (0001.HK) , opens new tab said on Friday it has escalated its legal fight in Panama after ‚Äčthe government seized its port operations, petitioning for a review ‚ÄĆof the decree behind the takeover.
The company said Panama unlawfully occupied facilities, seized property and ignored consultations, prompting further national and international ‚Äčlegal action.
CK Hutchison's Panama Ports Company (PPC), which manages ‚Äčtwo terminals near the globally strategic Panama Canal, filed ‚Ā†an administrative petition seeking reconsideration of Panama's executive action ‚Äčthat led to the occupation of its facilities and ‚Äčconfiscation of its property, CK Hutchison said.
Panamanian authorities searched the local ports unit of CK Hutchison a week ago, esclating the rift over ‚Äčthe control of the two ports.
Panama's government in January annulled ‚Äčthe deals that gave the company control of the two canal ‚ÄĆports ‚Ā†following a court ruling deeming the contracts unconstitutional.
The government said last week The Balboa and Cristobal ports at the centre of the dispute would be operated temporarily by Maersk (MAERSKb.CO) , opens new tab ‚Äčand MSC.
CK Hutchison ‚Äčhad agreed last ‚Ā†year to a $23 billion sale of dozens of ports worldwide, including the Panamanian terminals, to ‚Äča consortium led by BlackRock (BLK.N) , opens new tab and Mediterranean Shipping ‚ÄčCompany.
The ‚Ā†deal has been criticised by Beijing but welcomed by U.S. President Donald Trump, who had declared he wanted to "reclaim" the ‚Ā†Panama ‚ÄčCanal to reduce Chinese influence over ‚Äčthe canal's key assets.

item.title: Iran war chokes aid corridors, obstructing global relief efforts
item.description:
GENEVA, March 6 (Reuters) - Key humanitarian air, sea and land ‚Äčroutes are being constricted by disruption from the war in the Middle East, delaying life-saving shipments to ‚ÄĆsome of the world's worst crises, 10 aid officials have told Reuters.
The U.S.‚ÄďIsraeli war on Iran entered its seventh day on Friday, convulsing global markets and disrupting supply chains with airspace closures and the halt of shipping through the critical Strait of Hormuz .
Aid to Gaza and Sudan is grinding to ‚Äča halt and costs are soaring for help to the hundreds of millions suffering hunger crises around the ‚Äčworld.
"People in dire need of assistance will have to wait longer for food," said Jean-Martin Bauer, ‚Ā†Director of Food Security at the World Food Programme.
Already, tents, tarpaulins and lamps destined for the Israeli-occupied Palestinian territories ‚Äčof Gaza and the West Bank have become stuck in the supply chain, the International Organization for Migration said.
Aid groups say higher operational costs are straining budgets already facing massive donor cuts . The IOM said shipping firms were demanding emergency surcharges of approximately $3,000 per container.
Humanitarian groups stocking goods for rapid regional deployment at warehouses in Dubai's Humanitarian Hub face challenges moving ‚Äčsupplies onto transit routes.
The International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies cannot move trauma kits to help the ‚ÄčIranian Red Crescent with search and rescue from its Dubai hub, where they sit in a 10 million Swiss franc ($13 million) emergency stockpile, ‚ÄĆsaid ‚Ā†Cecile Terraz, a director at the IFRC.
The group cannot move stock through Jebel Ali port - the region's largest container terminal, which was set on fire by the debris of an intercepted missile - from where cargo normally moves onto planes or into the Strait of Hormuz.
The World Health Organization's Dubai hub operations are also frozen, regional director Hanan Balkhy said, obstructing 50 emergency ‚Äčrequests from 25 countries and ‚Äčhampering operations such as ‚Ā†polio vaccination.
Ripple effects farther afield are also likely.
Famine-struck Sudan is particularly exposed due to additional restrictions since February 28 on the Suez Canal and the Bab el-Mandeb Strait at the southern entrance ‚Äčto the Red Sea, the UNHCR said.
"We are particularly concerned about Africa," said a spokeswoman, ‚Äčadding that ‚Ā†some cargoes were being sent around the Cape of Good Hope. The route takes up to three weeks longer.
Costs for fuel, transportation and insurance are also rising, and Terraz said the IFRC may have to cut deliveries to the Iranian Red Crescent.
Emma Maspero, ‚Ā†senior manager ‚Äčin Copenhagen of the supply division of the U.N. children's body UNICEF, ‚Äčsaid she hoped flights carrying perishable humanitarian goods such as vaccines could be prioritised amid the airspace restrictions. ($1 = 0.7799 Swiss francs)

item.title: Kosovo president dissolves parliament, calls snap election after failed presidential vote
item.description:
March 6 (Reuters) - Kosovo's President Vjosa Osmani on Friday dissolved parliament and called snap elections after lawmakers failed to elect ‚Äča new head of state within a constitutional deadline.
The parliament ‚ÄĆhad until midnight on Thursday to choose a president before Osmani's term ends in April, but opposition parties refused to participate in the vote. Under the Balkan country's ‚Äčlaw, failure to elect a new president triggers snap parliamentary ‚Äčelections.
The move extends a political deadlock in Kosovo, which is ‚Ā†Europe's youngest nation and has aspirations to join the European Union. It ‚Äčhad no functioning government for most of last year as the fractured ‚Äčparliament failed to elect a speaker for months.
The next elections will be the third in just over a year. Kosovo already held a snap election on December ‚Äč28 after failing to form a government following the February 2025 poll.
"A ‚Äčparliament that cannot elect a president cannot continue indefinitely to drag out the process ‚ÄĆas ‚Ā†is being attempted," Osmani said in a statement. ‚ÄúNo one should wish for another political cycle, especially at this moment when the country needs stability."
Osmani is due to meet political parties on Friday before setting an election ‚Äčdate.
A resounding election ‚Äčwin by Prime ‚Ā†Minister Albin Kurti's Vetevendosje party in December looked likely to end the stalemate. At the time, many political analysts ‚Äčbelieved that the opposition would participate in votes to ‚Äčelect ‚Ā†the president.
However, although Vetevendosje won enough seats in the parliament to form the government it failed to secure the opposition's participation required to elect ‚Ā†the ‚Äčpresident.
The opposition parties have asked for a ‚Äčconsensual candidate, but Kurti has nominated his foreign minister, Glauk Konjufca.

item.title: Indonesia to restrict social media access for children under 16, minister says
item.description:
JAKARTA, March 6 (Reuters) - Indonesia will restrict access to social media platforms for children under 16, its communications and digital ministry said on Friday, making it the latest country to install online guardrails ‚Äčto reduce the risks of addiction and cyberbullying.
A number of governments have imposed curbs on ‚ÄĆsocial media for children amid mounting concerns over the impact that social media is having on the safety and mental health of minors.
Australia introduced a ban on social media for under-16s in December , and Spain also said last month ‚Äčthat it would ban access to social media for minors under 16.
Indonesia's neighbour Malaysia announced in ‚ÄčNovember that it would also ban social media for users under ‚Ā†the age of 16 starting from 2026.
Meutya Hafid, Indonesia's Communications and Digital Minister, ‚Äčsaid in a video statement that the government will "delay access" to social media accounts for children ‚Äčunder 16 through a ministerial regulation issued on Friday.
Starting March 28, accounts owned by children under 16 on "high risk platforms" will be gradually deactivated, Meutya said, adding the platforms include TikTok, Facebook, Instagram, ‚Äčand Roblox.
"The process will be done gradually until all platforms perform their obligations," ‚Äčshe said, without elaborating on what they need to do to meet the new requirements.
"We realise this ‚ÄĆmay ‚Ā†cause discomfort in the beginning. Children may complain and parents may be confused dealing with their complaints."
"Our children are facing risks, from porn, cyberbullying, online fraud to most importantly, addiction," she said, adding Indonesia will be the first non-Western country to impose ‚Äčsuch restrictions.
TikTok, Meta, which ‚Äčowns Facebook and ‚Ā†Instagram, and Roblox did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
The details of the new regulation have yet to be revealed. Ministry officials did ‚Äčnot immediately respond to a request for comment.
Internet penetration in ‚ÄčIndonesia, a ‚Ā†country of about 280 million people, reached 79.5% in 2024, according to a survey of 8,700 people by the Indonesia internet service providers' association.
The survey showed 48% of children ‚Ā†under ‚Äč12 had access to the internet, with some ‚Äčrespondents of that age group using Facebook, Instagram, and TikTok. The survey showed internet penetration stood at 87% ‚Äčamong "Gen Z" users aged 12 to 27.

item.title: Romania will not host nuclear components on its territory for deterrence, says president
item.description:
BUCHAREST, March 6 (Reuters) - Romania will not host nuclear components on its territory in the medium term, centrist President Nicusor ‚ÄčDan said late on Thursday, in response to ‚ÄĆFrench plans to expand nuclear deterrence and collaborate with European partners.
Earlier this week, President Emmanuel Macron said France would expand its nuclear arsenal ‚Äčand potentially allow European partners to host French ‚Äčwarplanes on nuclear deterrence missions.
France and Germany have ‚Ā†established a nuclear steering group to discuss deterrence issues and ‚Äčwill start concrete cooperation this year. Greece, Poland, the ‚ÄčNetherlands, Belgium, Denmark and Sweden could take part in French nuclear wargames, Macron said, while on Tuesday Romania said it too had ‚Äčbeen invited to talks.
"As a NATO state, Romania is ‚Äčunder the NATO nuclear umbrella provided by the United States," Dan ‚ÄĆtold ‚Ā†reporters after an official visit to Warsaw.
"Being protected by the NATO umbrella does not imply the presence of nuclear elements on Romanian territory, and in the medium term, ‚Äčhosting nuclear ‚Äčcomponents is out ‚Ā†of the question."
Though France and Britain are both nuclear powers, most European countries ‚Äčhave relied primarily on the United States for ‚Äčdeterring ‚Ā†any potential adversaries ‚ÄĒ a decades-old pillar of transatlantic security.
Macron's initiative comes amid criticism from the Trump administration of Europe's ‚Ā†willingness ‚Äčand ability to defend itself ‚Äčand also amid concerns about growing global geopolitical instability as the United ‚ÄčStates and Israel strike Iran .

item.title: Australia complains to China after encounter between military helicopters
item.description:
SYDNEY, March 6 (Reuters) - Australia has raised concerns with China following an "unsafe and unprofessional" encounter between two military ‚Äčhelicopters, the defence department said on Friday.
An ‚ÄĆAustralian military helicopter was flying over international waters in the Yellow Sea when it was intercepted by a Chinese helicopter ‚Äčon Wednesday, a statement said.
The Chinese helicopter ‚Äčmatched the Australian aircraft's altitude before "closing in to ‚Ā†an unsafe distance", increasing speed and then ‚Äčrolling towards it, requiring the Australian crew to take "evasive ‚Äčaction".
"This was an unsafe and unprofessional manoeuvre that posed a risk to our aircraft and its personnel," the statement said.
Australia ‚Äčwas undertaking a routine patrol in the ‚ÄčYellow Sea as part of the international effort to enforce ‚ÄĆUnited ‚Ā†Nations Security Council sanctions against North Korea, it said.
No injuries were reported in the encounter between the People's Liberation Army-Navy helicopter and the aircraft of ‚Äčthe Australian ‚ÄčDefence Force.
The ‚Ā†incident is the latest in a series of military encounters involving China that Australia ‚Äčhas called out publicly in similar terms.
In October , ‚Äčit ‚Ā†also criticised as "unsafe and unprofessional" the actions of a Chinese fighter jet that dropped flares near one ‚Ā†of ‚Äčits maritime patrol planes.
The Chinese ‚Äčembassy did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

item.title: Maersk suspends two shipping services due to Middle East crisis
item.description:
COPENHAGEN, March 6 (Reuters) - Maersk (MAERSKb.CO) , opens new tab , one of the world's biggest container shipping groups, said on Friday it ‚Äčhas temporarily suspended two services linking ‚ÄĆthe Middle East to Asia and Europe as the Iran conflict continues to disrupt global supply ‚Äčchains.
The Danish group said in a ‚Äčstatement it would halt its FM1 service ‚Ā†connecting the Far East to the ‚ÄčMiddle East and its ME11 service linking the ‚ÄčMiddle East to Europe
"This decision has been taken as a precautionary measure to ensure the safety of ‚Äčour personnel and vessels while minimizing ‚Äčoperational disruption across our wider network," Maersk said in ‚ÄĆan ‚Ā†advisory to customers.
Tensions in the Middle East have escalated sharply after the United States and Israel launched their most ambitious attacks on ‚ÄčIran in decades ‚Äčon ‚Ā†Saturday in an operation that killed Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei.
The ‚Äčcrisis has left 147 container ships ‚Äčsheltering ‚Ā†in the Gulf, triggering port congestion and freight rate increases that are rippling across ‚Ā†global supply ‚Äčchains from Asia to ‚ÄčEurope, according to freight analytics firm Xeneta.

item.title: South Korea, US militaries discuss moving Patriot missiles to Iran war, Seoul says
item.description:
SEOUL, March 6 (Reuters) - South Korean Foreign Minister Cho Hyun said on Friday the ‚ÄčU.S. and South Korean militaries are discussing the possible redeployment of some ‚ÄĆU.S. Patriot missile defence systems based in South Korea to be used in the war against Iran .
Cho was responding to questions at a parliamentary hearing following ‚Äčmedia reports on Friday that units of the U.S. ‚Äčmobile missile interceptor system had been moved to the ‚Ā†Osan Air Base in South Korea from other locations in ‚Äčthe country.

item.title: 52 French ships stuck in the Gulf amid Iran conflict, minister says
item.description:
PARIS, March 6 (Reuters) - Around 50 French ships are ‚Äčcurrently blocked in the ‚ÄĆPersian Gulf and another eight in the Red ‚ÄčSea, France's Transport Minister ‚ÄčPhilippe Tabarot said on ‚Ā†Friday, as Paris ‚Äčseeks support to build a ‚Äčcoalition of countries to secure shipping traffic in the ‚Äčregion.
"There are around ‚Äčfifty ships - 52 to be precise - ‚ÄĆin ‚Ā†the Persian Gulf and eight in the Red Sea, and we ‚Äčare ‚Äčalso ‚Ā†in permanent contact with the crews, ‚Äčas there are ‚ÄčFrench ‚Ā†sailors aboard a number of these vessels," Tabarot ‚Ā†told ‚ÄčFrench broadcaster ‚ÄčCNews/Europe 1.

item.title: China's decarbonisation plan takes cautious steps as world backtracks on climate
item.description:
BEIJING, March 5 (Reuters) - China on Thursday released a new five-year plan to reduce the carbon emissions of its economy, mainly by relying on its booming renewable sector to limit coal use and greenhouse gases, but some analysts viewed it as underwhelming.
Under the plan, China aims to reduce its carbon intensity, or carbon emissions per unit of gross domestic product, by 17% ‚Äčfrom 2026 to 2030, not enough to meet its pledge under the Paris climate agreement of a 65% cut from 2005 to 2030, analysts said.
The new plan did not, as international ‚Äčobservers hoped it would, set a goal for total emissions to head lower before 2030.
China's carbon intensity goal is "alarmingly lax," said Lauri Myllyvirta, co-founder ‚Ā†of the Helsinki-based Centre for Research on Energy and Clean Air (CREA), who said it would allow emissions to increase by 3% to 6% over the next five years given China's economic growth ‚Äčtarget .
CREA research previously found that China would need a 23% cut over the next five years to meet its Paris commitment.
Since Washington exited the Paris agreement and retreated from its climate policies last ‚Äčyear, China has looked to fill a void in global leadership on climate and clean energy left by the world's biggest historic emitter.
During the five-year plan that ended last year, China reduced its carbon intensity by just 12%, missing its previous target of 18%. That failure and its modest new decarbonisation goal are unlikely to inspire other countries to set their own ambitious emissions-reductions targets, analysts said.
"China is quickly becoming the largest emitter historically speaking, ‚Äčand so the legitimacy to claim that developed economies have to do a much larger share is kind of running out for China, so they know that they have to do ‚Äčmore," said Nis Grunberg, lead analyst for the Mercator Institute for China Studies in Germany.
Under its new program, Beijing outlined annual targets for reaching 30 million metric tons of coal replaced by renewable energy in ‚ÄĆfive years but ‚Ā†did not impose limits on coal consumption. The country is rapidly expanding its wind and solar power capacity.
With the new plan, Beijing has shifted focus from controlling the energy intensity of its economy to carbon intensity.
The so-called "dual control" system will introduce industry, company and project-level carbon emissions controls, according to the five-year plan.
In 2026, China plans to cut its carbon intensity by around 3.8%, according to a report from China's top state planner, the National Development and Reform Commission (NDRC). China has said it expects that its carbon emissions will peak before 2030.
Still, the new carbon intensity ‚Äčtarget will not be easy to achieve, said ‚ÄčYao Zhe, a Beijing-based policy adviser for ‚Ā†Greenpeace East Asia, pointing to continued energy demand growth from Chinese manufacturing .
"The focus increasingly shifts to more challenging areas - decarbonising hard-to-abate sectors, integrating a much larger share of renewables into the power system and building a more flexible and resilient electricity system," said Muyi Yang, a senior energy analyst ‚Äčfor Ember, an energy think tank.
China's carbon emissions fell 0.3% last year thanks to reductions in the transport, power, cement and metals sectors, but ‚Äčit is not yet clear ‚Ā†whether emissions will go up again before peaking.
While China has fallen short of some short-term climate targets, Li Shuo, director of the China Climate Hub at the Asia Society Policy Institute in the U.S., said renewable energy technology will help China bend its emissions curve downward.
President Xi Jinping told the United Nations in September that his country would expand wind and solar power capacity, ‚Ā†already the ‚Äčworld leader, six fold from 2020 levels by 2035. Based on current trends, China is on pace to exceed ‚Äčthat target.
In the next five years, China will introduce minimum quotas for renewable energy consumption, according to the NDRC, as well as measures to phase out outdated coal-fired equipment and facilities and reach a peak in coal consumption. Previously, ‚ÄčChina had set out to phase down its coal use.
(This story has been corrected to fix the base year for China's Paris agreement carbon intensity target to 2005, not 2026, in paragraph 2)

item.title: Trump administration considering looser protections for North Atlantic right whale
item.description:
March 3 (Reuters) - The Trump administration on Tuesday took the first ‚Äčstep toward loosening regulations ‚ÄĆthat protect the endangered North Atlantic right whale from ‚Äčvessel strikes.
Here are some ‚Äčdetails:

item.title: Coastal El Nino threatens Peru's booming agro-exports with heavy rains
item.description:
LIMA, March 3 (Reuters) - Peru's booming agricultural export sector could suffer this year from heavy rains and floods caused by the coastal El Nino weather phenomenon, business leaders and climate agencies warn, ‚Äčas effects begin to be felt on local fruit plantations.
The coastal El Nino is ‚ÄĆa periodic climate event tied to several months of abnormally warm waters primarily off the northern Pacific coast of Peru and Ecuador, generating heavy rainfall.
Gabriel Amaro, leader of the agricultural producers' association, said that although the phenomenon ‚Äčis currently weak, it has destroyed some 6,000 hectares (14,826 acres) of northern Peruvian fruit ‚Äčcrops and could become moderate before July.
