In recent decades, there have been some liberal shifts within part of the Church. GAFCON was set up in 2008, drawing ‚Äčon the resistance to these changes, especially in Africa and Asia, where the ‚Äčchurch is expanding ‚Ā†fastest.
On Thursday, a spokesperson for the Anglican Communion Office in London said GAFCON was ignoring years of dialogue aimed at reforming the church.
On whether the church had now split, Diarmaid MacCulloch, emeritus professor of church history at the ‚Ā†University of ‚ÄčOxford, told Reuters: "Of course it's a schism."
But MacCulloch said the ‚Äčrupture need not necessarily be permanent.
"Schisms do eventually get healed, when both sides see that the issues that caused the schism don't seem ‚Äčthat important any longer," he said.

item.title: Germany's Merz warns against spread of war in Middle East
item.description:
MUNICH, March 6 (Reuters) - German Chancellor Friedrich Merz said on Friday Berlin was working with ‚ÄĆpartners to find an approach to end the fighting with Iran as he warned against allowing the country to fall into chaos as the war widens across the Middle East.
Merz said Germany shared the aims of the United States and ‚ÄčIsrael to stop Tehran's nuclear and missile programme , its threats to Israel and its support for ‚Äčproxy groups and he said the Iranian people had the right to decide ‚Ā†their own future.
"However, as the fighting continues and spreads, we also see growing risks," he said in ‚Äča statement. "An endless war is not in our interest."
He said Germany was working with partners to formulate ‚Äča common perspective on ending the conflict. "These considerations are ongoing," he said, without giving details.
Merz's comments reflect the unease felt in Europe at the war unleashed by the U.S. and Israeli operation that killed Iran's Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei a ‚Äčweek ago and which has since spread into Lebanon with no clear sign of an end in ‚Äčsight.
As American and Israeli jets have continued to pound targets across Iran and Iranian missiles and drones have hit ‚ÄĆcities ‚Ā†in the Gulf and Israel, the economic risks have risen with global air transport in chaos and the Strait of Hormuz, one of the world's key oil shipping lanes, shut down.
Iran has not yet named a successor to Khamenei, who held power for 37 years, and Merz said the collapse of Iranian statehood ‚Äčor proxy conflicts fought ‚Äčon Iranian soil would ‚Ā†not be in anyone's interest.
"Such scenarios could have far-reaching consequences for Europe," he said, including uncontrolled migration by Iranians fleeing across the border if the country ‚Äčfell into chaos.
"The Iranian state must remain functional, and public order and ‚Äčbasic services must ‚Ā†be maintained. The Iranian economy must not collapse," Merz said.
Merz said a political process of dialogue and trust building in the region would have to begin once fighting ends. Iran's nuclear and ballistic missile programmes ‚Ā†would have ‚Äčto end and be subject to verification. Iran would have ‚Äčto stop supporting proxy groups and all nuclear material would have to be secured.
If that happened sanctions might be lifted and aid ‚Äčcould be provided.

item.title: White House says US well on way toward controlling Iran airspace
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 6 (Reuters) - The United States is well on its way toward controlling Iranian airspace, ‚ÄčWhite House press secretary Karoline Leavitt said on Friday, adding that ‚ÄĆWashington expects the achievable U.S. objectives to be completed in four to six weeks.
Speaking to reporters at the White House, Leavitt also said ‚ÄčWashington was looking at potential candidates to lead Iran, ‚Äča day after President Donald Trump told Reuters in ‚Ā†an interview that the United States must be involved ‚Äčin choosing the next leader of Iran.
"I know there's a number ‚Äčof people that our intelligence agencies and the United States government are looking at, but I won't get any further on that," Leavitt said.
‚ÄčIn the interview on Thursday, Trump said that he thinks ‚Äčthe next leader of Iran is unlikely to be the late Supreme ‚ÄĆLeader ‚Ā†Ayatollah Ali Khamenei's son, who has emerged as a frontrunner to succeed his father, who was killed in a military strike at the start of the war.
Earlier on Friday, Trump ‚Äčsaid there would ‚Äčbe no ‚Ā†deal struck with Iran except "unconditional surrender."
"What the President means is that when he, as Commander in Chief ‚Äčof the US Armed Forces, determines that Iran ‚Äčno ‚Ā†longer poses a threat to the United States of America, and the goals of Operation Epic Fury has been fully realized, ‚Ā†then Iran ‚Äčwill essentially be in a place ‚Äčof unconditional surrender, whether they say it themselves or not," Leavitt said.

item.title: Pentagon taps former DOGE official to lead its AI efforts
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 6 (Reuters) - The Pentagon on Friday named as Chief Data Officer ‚Äča computer scientist who aided billionaire Elon Musk's efforts to ‚ÄĆoverhaul the government last year and who has boosted white supremacists and misogynists online.
In a social media post, the Pentagon said Gavin Kliger's new role "places him at the center of the Department‚Äôs most ‚Äčambitious AI efforts," focusing on "day-to-day alignment and execution of the Department‚Äôs ‚ÄčAI projects, working directly with America's frontier AI labs to ‚Ā†support the warfighter."
The Pentagon did not immediately respond to a Reuters request for ‚Äčmore comment. Kliger did not immediately return a message.
Kliger, in social media posts between ‚ÄčOctober 2024 and January 2025, has voiced controversial views and reposted content from white supremacist Nick Fuentes and self-described misogynist Andrew Tate.
The Pentagon's use of AI has taken center ‚Äčstage after a heated weeks-long dispute with Anthropic over guardrails on how the ‚Äčmilitary can use its AI tools led to last week's decision by the Trump administration to ‚ÄĆshun ‚Ā†the company and replace it with OpenAI.
On Thursday, the Pentagon slapped a formal supply-chain risk designation on Anthropic - an extraordinary rebuke by the United States against an American tech company that was earlier than its rivals to work ‚Äčwith the Pentagon.
Anthropic was ‚Äčthe most aggressive ‚Ā†of its rivals in courting U.S. national-security officials. But the company and the Pentagon have been at odds for ‚Äčmonths over how the military can use its technology on ‚Äčthe battlefield. ‚Ā†This conflict erupted into public view earlier this year.
Anthropic has refused to back down on bans for its Claude AI to power autonomous weapons and mass ‚Ā†U.S. surveillance. ‚ÄčThe Pentagon has pushed back, saying it ‚Äčshould be able to use this technology as needed, so long as it complies with U.S. law.

item.title: Iranian attacks on civilians won't go unanswered, CENTCOM commander says
item.description:
March 6 (Reuters) - The United States ‚ÄĆwill respond to Iranian attacks on civilians ‚Äčthroughout the Middle ‚ÄčEast, the leader of the ‚Ā†U.S. Central Command, ‚ÄčBrad Cooper, said on ‚ÄčFriday.
Cooper said that Iran has attacked 12 different countries ‚Äčsince the U.S. ‚Äčand Israel launched strikes on ‚ÄĆthe ‚Ā†Islamic Republic last week, including firing seven attack drones at ‚Äčcivilian, residential ‚Äčneighborhoods ‚Ā†in Bahrain on Thursday night.
"This ‚Äčis unacceptable and ‚Äčwill ‚Ā†not go unanswered," Cooper said in a ‚Ā†statement.

item.title: Oil derivatives signal traders see Middle East shock as short-lived
item.description:
March 6 (Reuters) - Oil options and futures are signalling that the latest Middle East conflict may be short‚ÄĎlived, as traders pile into structures that profit from a retreat in prices after the initial spike.
Options and futures markets often provide the earliest signal of whether traders see ‚Äča supply shock as fleeting or structural, creating opportunities to profit from sharp swings in prices.
The Israel‚ÄĎU.S. attack on Iran has ‚ÄĆsent shockwaves through energy markets as war‚ÄĎrisk insurance costs surge, freight rates hit record levels and disruptions at the Strait of Hormuz snarl oil flows and strand hundreds of vessels. Oil prices , on Friday were at multi-year highs.
In a sign traders see the price shock as temporary, 30-day at-the-money Brent implied volatility jumped 17.5 points to 68% over the past ‚Äčweek through Tuesday, while 60- and 90-day tenors rose only 5.9 and 2.8 percentage points, LSEG data shows.
"What we're watching in real time is ‚Äčthe difference between a logistics crisis and a structural one," Brian E. Kinsella, former Goldman Sachs energy specialist, told ‚Ā†the Reuters Global Markets Forum. , opens new tab
"The market is betting it's logistical and I think that is the right read," he added.
The Brent futures curve is sending a ‚Äčsimilar signal.
The spread between the front-month Brent contract and the six-month contract widened to about $10, the steepest backwardation since the Russia-Ukraine war in 2022, pointing to ‚Äčtight near-term supply while suggesting short-term disruption.
Meanwhile, the put-to-call ratio on West Texas Intermediate options roughly halved to 0.35 on Monday from Friday's close, CME data showed, pointing to heavy bullish call buying before rebounding to 0.56 on Tuesday as demand returned for downside protection.
A call option gives the holder the right, but not the obligation, to buy a ‚Äčcrude futures contract at a set price, while a put option gives the right to sell one.
The ratio, meanwhile, compares bearish put options, which profit ‚Äčfrom declines, with bullish call options that benefit from rising prices to gauge market sentiment.
"Dealers are already short a meaningful amount of these deep out-of-the-money calls, creating a more ‚ÄĆnegative gamma ‚Ā†profile in crude," said Rebecca Babin, senior energy trader at CIBC Private Wealth US. That contrasts with the more typical environment where dealers are long gamma and sell into rallies.
Gamma shows how much an option's sensitivity to the futures price (delta) will change if the market moves.
Much of the 2027 Brent strip still trades below $70 a barrel, a sign markets are not yet pricing a structural shift in long-term supply, Babin said.
Producers have also used the rally to hedge ‚Äčforward output, creating natural selling pressure on ‚Äčlonger-dated volatility, she added.
Risk premiums ‚Ā†remain concentrated at the front of the futures curve, reinforcing the view that traders still see the disruption as temporary, said Darrell E. Fletcher, managing director of commodities at trading firm Bannockburn Capital Markets.
Brent options open interest plunged in late ‚ÄčFebruary before rebounding in early March, suggesting traders unwound positions before rebuilding hedges.
Front-month open interest fell from around ‚Äč388,000 contracts on Feb. ‚Ā†18 to roughly 73,000 by Feb. 27, before surging to more than 700,000 contracts on March 2 as fresh positions were established.
"The open interest data seems like a pretty clear tell that it was a sharp unwind in positioning and not a structural repricing. The front end is clearly closing a trade while the ‚Ā†back end is ‚Äčwhat to pay attention to,‚ÄĚ Kinsella said.
Futures positioning shows a similar pattern, with more than ‚Äč40% of open interest concentrated in April through July expiries and thinner positioning further out the curve, according to CME data.
(Join GMF, a chat room hosted on LSEG Messenger for live interviews: https://lseg.group/3KFHrhe , opens new tab )

item.title: US drillers add oil, gas rigs for first time in four weeks, Baker Hughes says
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 6 (Reuters) - U.S. energy firms ‚ÄĆthis week added oil and natural gas rigs for the first time in four weeks, energy services firm Baker Hughes (BKR.O) , opens new tab said in its closely followed report on Friday.
The oil and gas rig count, an early indicator of future output, rose by one ‚Äčto 551 in the week to March 6. , ,
Despite this week's rig increase, Baker Hughes said the ‚Äčtotal count was still down 41 rigs, or 7% below this time last year.
Baker ‚Ā†Hughes said oil rigs rose by four to 411 this week, their highest since early February, while gas ‚Äčrigs fell by two to 132, their lowest since early February.
In the Haynesville Shale in Louisiana and Texas, ‚Äčthe rig count rose by one this week to 53, the highest since May 2023.
In the Marcellus Shale in Pennsylvania, Ohio and West Virginia, the rig count rose by one to 27, the highest since May 2024.
In the Williston Shale in North ‚ÄčDakota and Montana, the rig count fell by one to 27, the lowest since January 2022.
In North Dakota, ‚Äčthe rig count fell by one to 25, the lowest since November 2021.
The oil and gas rig count declined by about ‚ÄĆ7% ‚Ā†in 2025, 5% in 2024, and 20% in 2023 as lower U.S. oil prices prompted energy firms to focus more on boosting shareholder returns and paying down debt rather than increasing output.
Financial services firm TD Cowen said 18 of the 21 exploration and production (E&P) companies it tracks planned to spend about 1% less in capital ‚Äčexpenditures in 2026 than in ‚Äč2025.
That compares with a ‚Ā†decline of around 4% in 2025, roughly flat year-on-year spending in 2024, and increases of 27% in 2023, 40% in 2022, and 4% in 2021.
With U.S. spot crude ‚Äčprices expected to fall for the fourth year in a row in 2026, ‚Äčthe U.S. Energy ‚Ā†Information Administration (EIA) projected crude output would hold at 13.6 million barrels per day (bpd) in 2026, matching 2025's record high.
The EIA, however, made that oil price projection before the U.S.-Iran war. Since then, oil prices have spiked to their highest ‚Ā†since 2023.
While ‚Äčon the gas side, EIA projected output would rise from a ‚Äčrecord 107.6 billion cubic feet per day (bcfd) in 2025 to 110.0 bcfd in 2026 with spot prices at the Henry Hub benchmark in ‚ÄčLouisiana forecast to jump by about 22% in 2026.

item.title: South Bow says US, Canada policy shift boosts prospects for Keystone XL revival
item.description:
CALGARY, March 6 (Reuters) - The company ‚Äčbehind a plan to revive the Keystone XL pipeline system to increase Canadian oil exports to ‚ÄĆthe United States is hopeful a North American policy shift in favour of energy development will shelter its project from the political and regulatory hurdles that led to its cancellation in 2021.
CEO Bevin Wirzba of Canadian pipeline operator South Bow said Friday the world ‚Äčhas changed since U.S. President Joe Biden canceled the permit for Keystone XL after years of Indigenous ‚Äčand environmental opposition.
"The policy environment in North America has been far more constructive," Wirzba said. ‚Ā†He said the current war in Iran as well as the ongoing conflict in Ukraine emphasize the need ‚Äčfor energy security.
"Those realities are a great backdrop for us to provide maybe a solution," Wirzba said on a ‚Äčconference call.
South Bow, which was set up by former Keystone XL proponent TC Energy in 2024 to take over its oil pipeline business, is considering reviving some of the line that was already built in Alberta and already has all necessary Canadian permits.
The proposal is ‚Äčnot identical to Keystone XL, as the U.S. segment of the pipeline would be built not by South Bow, ‚Äčbut by U.S. partners. The full route and details of how the various segments would come together remain unknown, but the ‚ÄĆgoal is ‚Ā†to ultimately transport up to 55,000 bpd of Canadian oil to the U.S. Gulf Coast.
The company's plan comes as U.S. President Donald Trump has sought to speed energy project permitting times and boost domestic oil and gas production. A presidential permit would be required for the proposed new pipeline to ‚Äčcross the Canada-U.S. border.
In Canada, ‚ÄčPrime Minister Mark Carney ‚Ā†is depending on the energy sector to help boost the country's economy, and has pledged to speed permitting times in addition to dropping some climate legislation that the oil industry said ‚Äčwas impeding its growth.
Carney's moves thus far have materially moved the needle in favour ‚Äčof considering a ‚Ā†pipeline development, Wirzba said. He said South Bow has launched a formal open season process aimed at gauging commercial interest in new pipeline space, and more broadly, gauging the Canadian oil industry's confidence in its ability to grow production in the ‚Ā†coming ‚Äčyears.
Wirzba declined to say what its proposed project ‚ÄĒ which South Bow is ‚Äčcalling the "Prairie Connector" pipeline ‚ÄĒ would cost or when construction might start.
But he said many of Canada's oil sands companies have indicated they plan ‚Äčto increase output materially over the next three to five years.

item.title: Brazil's Petrobras raises possibility of extra dividends as oil prices surge
item.description:
RIO DE JANEIRO, March 6 (Reuters) - Brazil's state-run oil company Petrobras (PETR3.SA) , opens new tab could distribute extra dividends this year if ‚Äčcash flow is high, as oil prices surge while its operations ‚ÄĆhave so far been unaffected by the Middle-East U.S.-Iran conflict, executives told analysts on Friday.
Petrobras will need to be ready for variations in oil prices, CEO Magda Chambriard said, adding ‚Äčthat the company will not pass the sudden swings onto Brazilian consumers after attacks ‚Äčby the U.S. and Israel in Iran disrupted global energy flows earlier this week.
"We're living in ‚Ā†a time of high geopolitical instability, where our concern is to leave the ‚Äčcompany prepared for any scenario that may occur regarding the price of oil," ‚ÄčChambriard said.
In 2025, Petrobras did not distribute extraordinary dividends, as lower Brent oil prices made it difficult for the firm to generate surplus cash flow. But Petrobras could do so this ‚Äčyear, if the sharp upturn in prices remains, said CFO Fernando Melgarejo.
"I would ‚Äčlove to submit an extraordinary dividend offering, provided we are certain that it will not impact ‚ÄĆthe ‚Ā†financing of our projects," said Melgarejo, adding that Petrobras will remain cautious in its decision-making due to the unstable scenario.
In the short-term Petrobras has benefited from the conflict, said Petrobras' head of logistics, commercialization and markets, Claudio Schlosser, as it ‚Äčships mainly to China, ‚ÄčIndia and Europe, ‚Ā†regions far from the conflict zone.
"We've seen better margins," he said. "In a way, there is an increase in value and ‚Äčan interesting positioning for the company."
Oil prices have hit highs ‚Äčin the ‚Ā†wake of the conflict, with Brent hitting $90 per barrel for the first time since April 2024 and on track for its strongest weekly gain since the volatility seen ‚Ā†during ‚Äčthe COVID-19 pandemic in the spring of 2020.
On Thursday ‚ÄčPetrobras posted results for 2025 with record exports offsetting a drop in Brent oil prices for the year.

item.title: Hezbollah saw new war with Israel as inevitable and rearmed for months, sources say
item.description:
BEIRUT, March 5 (Reuters) - Lebanese armed group Hezbollah spent months restocking its arsenal of rockets and drones, using support from Iran and its own weapons factories to prepare for a new war with Israel, six sources familiar with the group's preparations said.
Down but not ‚Äčout after its devastating 2024 conflict with Israel, Hezbollah had concluded that another round of fighting was inevitable - and that this time, it could face an existential threat, according to the sources.
Reuters spoke ‚ÄĆto three Lebanese sources briefed on Hezbollah's activities, two foreign officials in Lebanon and an Israeli military official, who all spoke on condition of anonymity as they were not authorised to speak to the press.
The details of Hezbollah's recent efforts to rearm have not been previously reported.
The head of Hezbollah's media office, Youssef al-Zein, told Reuters that Hezbollah would not comment on its military operations, though he said the group had decided to "fight to the last breath."
Founded by Iran's Revolutionary Guards in 1982, Hezbollah launched rockets and drones at Israel on Monday to ‚Äčavenge the death of Iran's Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei, pulling Lebanon into the war raging across the Middle East.
Although the decision caught some of its own officials off guard , Hezbollah had been readying its military stockpiles and its command-and-control ‚Äčstructure for an eventual rematch with Israel, the six sources said.
To do so, it had drawn on a monthly budget of $50 million, most of it from Iran and earmarked ‚Ā†for fighters' salaries, according to one of the Lebanese sources, who has been briefed on the group's finances and military activities. One of the foreign officials confirmed the $50 million budget.
It was not immediately clear how long the group had been ‚Äčrelying on that monthly budget and how it compared to its previous financial resources.
The group has said funds from Iran helped finance rents for people displaced by the 2024 war. Around 60,000 Lebanese, most of them from the Shi'ite Muslim community from which ‚ÄčHezbollah draws its popular support, remained displaced over the last year, with their homes still in ruins.
Hezbollah had also worked to replenish its drone and rocket stashes through local manufacturing, the first Lebanese source, the foreign officials and the Israeli military official said. The Israeli military official said Hezbollah had used Iranian funding both to smuggle arms and make its own weapons, but added that its manufacturing capability had been diminished.
The second foreign official said the group had stationed new rockets and Iranian-made logistical materials in southern Lebanon before the latest war began.
Hezbollah's ‚Äčmedia office did not immediately respond to questions on its rearmament and Iranian support for it.
Israeli military spokesperson Lieutenant Colonel Nadav Shoshani told Reuters that Hezbollah "had a lot of arms left" and was also seeking to rearm. "They were trying to ‚Äčsmuggle and we were preventing that," Shoshani said.
In 2024, a punishing two-month war with Israel ended with a ceasefire brokered by the United States. Hezbollah halted its attacks on Israel, which continued strikes on what it said were Hezbollah's efforts ‚ÄĆto rebuild military ‚Ā†capabilities.
Israel also kept troops in five hilltop positions in southern Lebanon.
Last year, Lebanon also began confiscating Hezbollah weapons in the country's south - but Israel said the group was rearming faster than it was being disarmed.
Speaking to Reuters weeks before Hezbollah entered the regional war, the first Lebanese source confirmed that the group had been rebuilding its military capabilities "in parallel" with Israel's campaign to destroy them.
The pace of Hezbollah's attacks this week provides clues about its weapons stocks.
The group launched 60 drones and rockets on March 2, the first day it attacked Israel, and a similar number the following day, said the second foreign official, who tracks Hezbollah's activities closely.
But on March 4, Hezbollah launched more than double that number of projectiles, a sign it had been ‚Äčable to draw from its larger caches, the official said.
ALMA, ‚Äčan Israeli think tank that monitors security on Israel's ‚Ā†northern border, said it assessed that Hezbollah's arsenal on the eve of its attack included approximately 25,000 rockets and missiles, most of them short- and medium-range.
A video published by Hezbollah on March 4 showed a fighter setting up a drone in a wooded area. Riad Kahwaji, a Dubai-based defence analyst and founder of the Institute for Near East and Gulf Military ‚ÄčAnalysis, identified the drone in the video as a Shahed-101, which he told Reuters could be produced locally.
Hezbollah has also dispatched fighters from its ‚Äčelite Radwan force back to ‚Ā†southern Lebanon, Reuters reported this week . They had been withdrawn from the area after the 2024 conflict.
Israeli strikes after the 2024 ceasefire included the targeting of what Israel said were Hezbollah training camps. In late February, the Israeli military said it struck eight military compounds used by the Radwan force to store weapons and prepare for a confrontation.
The Israeli official and the first foreign official said Hezbollah had been struggling to recruit new operatives as a result.
The group lost 5,000 fighters in the 2024 war, an unprecedented blow ‚Ā†to its fighting ‚Äčforce, though the second Lebanese source said it still had some 95,000 fighters left.
In the lead-up to its entry into the current regional war, ‚ÄčHezbollah had become convinced Israel would carry out a major strike on the group that would seek to "disable its ability to retaliate," the first Lebanese source said.
A third foreign official familiar with Hezbollah's thinking said that assessment had driven the group's decision to launch the first salvo, fearing Israel would eventually ‚Äčturn its attention from Iran to Hezbollah.
"They knew they were next on the list," the official said.

item.title: US files complaints for $15.3 million in proceeds allegedly linked to Iranian oil network
item.description:
March 6 (Reuters) - The United States on Friday filed two civil ‚Äčforfeiture complaints for more ‚ÄĆthan $15.3 million used to allegedly fund an illicit Iranian oil ‚Äčdistribution network, the Justice ‚ÄčDepartment said in a statement.
The ‚Ā†complaints by the Justice ‚ÄčDepartment allege that the funds ‚Äčwere being used by Mohammad Hossein Shamkhani to "operate multiple distribution companies" ‚Äčengaged in selling and ‚Äčshipping Iranian oil and other commodities in ‚ÄĆviolation ‚Ā†of U.S. sanctions.
Hossein Shamkhani is the son of Ali Shamkhani, it said, a ‚Äčtop adviser ‚Äčto ‚Ā†the former Supreme Leader of Iran Ayatollah ‚ÄčAli Khamenei. Both Ali ‚ÄčShamkhani ‚Ā†and Ali Khamenei were killed in the U.S. and ‚Ā†Israeli strikes ‚Äčagainst Iran ‚Äčlast week.

item.title: Swedish coast guard boards suspected stateless ship in the Baltic Sea
item.description:
March 6 (Reuters) - The Swedish coast guard said it boarded and took control of a suspected stateless vessel in Swedish ‚Äčwaters of the Baltic Sea on Friday, and it ‚ÄĆwas carrying out investigations on the ship.
"At 3:50 p.m. today, the Coast Guard took control of a vessel with unclear flag ‚Äčstatus and thus suspected of being a stateless vessel," ‚Äčit said in a statement, adding that the ‚Ā†boarding had taken place off the coast of the ‚Äčsouthern Swedish town Trelleborg.
The ship Caffa was sailing under a ‚ÄčGuinean flag, but the coast guard said it regarded the vessel to be stateless "based on national and international legislation". Swedish police said the ‚Äčship was suspected of sailing under a false flag.
According ‚Äčto ship-tracking service Marine Traffic, Caffa is a 96-meter general cargo ‚ÄĆship.
Sweden's ‚Ā†minister for civil defence, Carl-Oskar Bohlin, said in a post on X that the ship was a freighter on Ukraine's sanctions list.
"The ownership structure is unclear and there are ‚Äčsuspicions that the ‚Äčvessel is ‚Ā†not insured. As recently as this summer, the ship is said to have changed from ‚Äča Russian to a Guinean flag," Bohlin ‚Äčwrote.
The coast ‚Ā†guard said it had launched a preliminary investigation into suspected violation of maritime law regarding lack of seaworthiness.
"The Coast Guard ‚Ā†has personnel ‚Äčon board to gather information ‚Äčabout the condition of the vessel and crew, conduct searches, and conduct interviews," ‚Äčit added.

item.title: Ghana peacekeeping battalion hit by missile attacks in Lebanon, Ghana army says
item.description:
ACCRA, March 6 (Reuters) - The headquarters of Ghana's United ‚ÄčNations peacekeeping battalion in Lebanon ‚ÄĆwas hit by missile attacks on Friday, leaving two soldiers critically ‚Äčinjured, Ghana's armed forces ‚Äčsaid in a statement.
Lebanon was pulled ‚Ā†into the war in the ‚ÄčMiddle East on Monday, when Hezbollah ‚Äčfired rockets at Israel that ignited a new Israeli offensive against the group.
Ghanaian ‚Äčsoldiers are deployed as part ‚Äčof the UNIFIL mission in Lebanon.
The statement ‚ÄĆfrom ‚Ā†Ghana's armed forces did not say who fired the missiles, but it said Ghana's foreign ‚Äčministry ‚Äčhad protested ‚Ā†the incident at U.N. headquarters in New York.
In ‚Äčaddition to the two ‚Äčcritically ‚Ā†injured soldiers, the statement said that another soldier "has been traumatised" ‚Ā†while ‚Äčthe officers' mess ‚Äčfacility was hit and burned down.

item.title: Germany pulls further troops out of Middle East
item.description:
BERLIN, March 6 (Reuters) - Germany has pulled additional Bundeswehr troops out of the Middle East, ‚Äča military spokesman told Reuters on Friday, ‚ÄĆthe seventh day of a U.S.‚ÄďIsraeli war on Iran that has convulsed the region.
Soldiers deployed ‚Äčwith the UNIFIL mission in Lebanon ‚Äčare being withdrawn due to the security ‚Ā†situation, the spokesperson said after the ‚ÄčBundeswehr already sharply reduced its presence in Erbil ‚Äčin northern Iraq.
The RND newspaper network, which first reported the news, said that German troops stationed ‚Äčin Bahrain had already returned home, and ‚Äčpreparations for withdrawals from Kuwait were underway. The spokesperson ‚ÄĆdeclined ‚Ā†to comment on this.
The RND report also said that soldiers and staff from the German embassy in Baghdad were being ‚Äčrelocated to Jordan. ‚ÄčThe ‚Ā†foreign ministry did not respond to a request for comment from ‚ÄčReuters on the move.
Roughly 500 German ‚Äčsoldiers ‚Ā†are deployed across the region, mainly in Iraq and Jordan. Officials have recently moved ‚Ā†personnel ‚Äčout of camps and ‚Äčreduced some contingents amid heightened security risks.

item.title: Barclays says Brent could test $120/bbl if Middle East tensions persist
item.description:
March 6 (Reuters) - Barclays said on Friday that Brent crude could potentially test $120 a barrel if the Middle ‚ÄčEast conflict persists for another couple of weeks.
"These numbers ‚ÄĆmight seem too high, especially given widespread pessimism about the oil market outlook heading into this year, but we reiterate that fundamentals are stronger ‚Äčand risks are bigger than the Russia-Ukraine conflict , when we ‚Äčsaw these levels materialize," Barclays added.
Oil prices have jumped ‚Ā†sharply as the widening U.S.-Israeli conflict with Iran has effectively closed ‚Äčthe Strait of Hormuz, constraining Middle East supplies.
Shipping through the Strait ‚Äčof Hormuz, which carries about a fifth of global oil and oil and liquefied natural gas, has been disrupted after Iran threatened to fire on ‚Äčpassing vessels.
Brent crude futures were trading around $93.60 per barrel and ‚ÄčWest Texas Intermediate were at $91.62 as of 1857 GMT.
Barclays said oil volumes stranded ‚ÄĆon ‚Ā†tankers in the Middle East Gulf have risen by about 85 million barrels since the conflict began, adding that risks to oil prices remain skewed to the upside.
U.S. President Donald Trump demanded ‚ÄčIran's "unconditional surrender" on ‚ÄčFriday, a dramatic ‚Ā†escalation of his demands a week into the war he launched alongside Israel, which could make ‚Äčit more difficult to negotiate a swift end ‚Äčto hostilities.
"Production shut-ins ‚Ā†in Iraq and Kuwait are already happening and might spread to UAE and even Saudi Arabia over time," Barclays said.
Barclays said ‚Ā†the ‚Äčfar‚ÄĎend 10% scenario now implies Brent could ‚Äčhit $150 a barrel before the end of the month.

item.title: Trump to forge ahead with immigration crackdown driven by top aide Stephen Miller
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 6 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump is expected to forge ahead with his aggressive immigration crackdown driven by top aide Stephen Miller and a new homeland secretary nominee who shares Trump's ‚Äčhardline view, current and former U.S. officials and lawmakers said.
The Republican president fired Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem on Thursday after months of controversy over heavy-handed immigration enforcement tactics ‚ÄĆand lawmaker questions about government contracts and turmoil within her department.
But Miller - the White House deputy chief of staff and the architect of Trump‚Äôs immigration agenda - retains control of the issue, three U.S. officials said. And Trump‚Äôs pick to become homeland secretary, U.S. Senator Markwayne Mullin of Oklahoma, is viewed as closely aligned with Trump‚Äôs approach.
‚ÄúStephen is a survivor,‚ÄĚ one of the officials said, requesting anonymity to discuss internal dynamics. The official said Noem‚Äôs firing was not a referendum on Trump's restrictive ‚Äčagenda, but rather on the execution of it.
In response to a request for comment, a White House official said Miller helps coordinate a wide range of issues - from immigration to counter-cartel ‚Äčoperations - and is "working to ensure the President's policy agenda is implemented."
The Department of Homeland Security and Mullin's office did not respond to requests for comment.
Trump won ‚Ā†back the White House in 2024 with a campaign that heavily focused on stopping illegal immigration and ramping up deportations. Immigration was one of Trump's best-polling issues after returning to office - and a key ‚Äčfocus for the Republican Party broadly. But amid backlash over his aggressive enforcement in U.S. cities, support for his immigration approach declined in recent months , Reuters/Ipsos polls show.
Republicans currently hold narrow majorities in both chambers of ‚Äčthe U.S. Congress but are heading into midterm elections in November that threaten their control on Washington.
White House spokesperson Karoline Leavitt said on Thursday in an X post related to Noem‚Äôs dismissal that Trump‚Äôs immigration goals remained unchanged.
‚ÄúPresident Trump‚Äôs immigration agenda is keeping our border secure and deporting illegal alien criminals from our country, and it will continue without interruption,‚ÄĚ she said.
Under Noem‚Äôs leadership, DHS sent thousands of federal immigration agents to Los Angeles, Chicago, Minneapolis and other Democratic-led cities to seek ‚Äčout immigration offenders, sweeping through residential neighborhoods and chasing day laborers in Home Depot parking lots.
But after federal agents shot and killed two U.S. citizens in Minneapolis - Renee Good and Alex Pretti - the administration said it ‚Äčwould move to a less public-facing ‚Äútargeted‚ÄĚ approach to immigration arrests.
Current and former federal immigration officials said there had been no immediate policy shift since Noem‚Äôs departure was announced, but several said they expected the administration to continue to avoid ‚ÄĆhigh-profile surges into ‚Ā†U.S. cities following the fallout in Minneapolis.
Mullin, 48, became a senator in 2023 after a decade in the U.S. House of Representatives. Like Noem, he is a rancher and owns a cattle ranch in Oklahoma that also serves as a wedding venue, among other businesses.
Even as a freshman, Mullin has stood out from the crowd of 100 senators, at times presiding over the Senate wearing a large, light-colored cowboy hat.
He had a short-lived career as a mixed martial arts fighter and garnered widespread media attention in 2023 when he rose from his chair during a Senate hearing to challenge Teamsters President ‚ÄčSean O'Brien to "stand your butt up" and fight.
Mullin voted ‚Äčfor a Republican-backed funding package last year ‚Ā†that devoted a historic $170 billion to immigration enforcement through September 2029 and backs core elements of Trump‚Äôs immigration platform.
Republican lawmakers praised Mullin after the news that Trump would nominate him for DHS secretary, a position that requires majority confirmation in the Senate.
‚ÄúHe's strong on the border and that's what we need,‚ÄĚ Republican Senator Eric ‚ÄčSchmitt of Missouri told reporters on Thursday. ‚ÄúThe deportations will continue."
Noem was sharply criticized by both Democrats and Republicans for swiftly saying Good and Pretti were ‚Äčengaged in ‚Äúdomestic terrorism‚ÄĚ after they ‚Ā†were killed and before a full investigation.
Mullin similarly portrayed Pretti as a threat despite video evidence that undercut that claim. He said Pretti was "a deranged individual" who had a loaded pistol and intended "to cause max damage," during an interview with Fox News on January 24, the day Pretti was killed.
Democrats on Capitol Hill welcomed Noem‚Äôs removal but said the Trump administration‚Äôs approach to immigration enforcement still needed to change. Democrats have blocked funding for DHS ‚Ā†since mid-February in ‚Äča push to force the White House to moderate its tactics.
Senator Chris Murphy, a Democrat from Connecticut, said funding talks are ‚Äčbeing run out of the White House and that he does not expect Noem‚Äôs firing to break the stalemate.
‚ÄúI think we‚Äôre better off without her, but she wasn‚Äôt running the department,‚ÄĚ Murphy said on Thursday. ‚ÄúStephen Miller runs that department and will continue to ‚Äčrun the department so I don‚Äôt really think much will change.‚ÄĚ

item.title: Israeli military says unintentionally struck UN agency truck in Gaza
item.description:
TEL AVIV, March 6 (Reuters) - Israel's military said on Friday that a "firing component" launched by its navy unintentionally struck a fuel ‚Äčtruck belonging to a United Nations agency in Gaza ‚ÄĆthe previous day, an incident that prompted the agency to publicly call for a full investigation.
The United Nations Office for Project Services, ‚Äčwhich oversees fuel distribution in Gaza, said that the ‚Äčempty fuel truck was struck on Thursday around ‚Ā†5 a.m. from the direction of the sea, causing damage ‚Äčto the vehicle. There were no injuries.
"Our teams are taking ‚Äčextraordinary risks every day to keep humanitarian operations and life-sustaining services running," UNOPS Executive Director Jorge Moreira da Silva said in a statement, ‚Äčcalling for an investigation into the incident.
"They should not ‚Äčhave to do that under fire," he said.
In response to Reuters questions, ‚ÄĆthe ‚Ā†Israeli military said that the incident occurred during defensive naval activity, and that a firing component deviated from its intended trajectory.
The fuel truck sustained "minor damage", the military said in a ‚Äčstatement. The military ‚Äčdid not ‚Ā†say what type of munitions had been fired, or what had been the navy's intended ‚Äčtarget.
"The incident was reviewed, and lessons were learned ‚Äčaccordingly," ‚Ā†it said, without providing further details.
The fuel truck had been on its way to the Kerem Shalom crossing when it was ‚Ā†struck, ‚Äčand the truck's movements had been ‚Äčcoordinated with Israeli authorities in advance, UNOPS said.

item.title: Private credit woes come for Larry Fink‚Äôs legacy
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 6 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Rising disquiet in private credit is uncomfortable for a lot of Wall Street titans, but especially for Larry Fink. The BlackRock (BLK.N) , opens new tab boss moved aggressively to transform his firm, the kingpin of public-markets investing for ‚Äčthe masses, into a private-markets rival to the likes of Blackstone (BX.N) , opens new tab or Apollo Global Management (APO.N) , opens new tab . The timing now looks awkward, as worries ‚ÄĆabout overheated lending mount and a key retail fund caps investor withdrawals. Fink‚Äôs legacy project , and his company‚Äôs strengths, will be tested.
As part of a $28 billion string of deals to move into private markets, BlackRock agreed to acquire non-bank lender HPS Investment Partners in 2024. Included in that $12 billion acquisition was HPS Corporate Lending Partners, or HLEND, a nontraded business development ‚Äčcompany (BDC). Such funds target affluent individual investors, allowing them to withdraw their cash up to a set cap, typically of 5% of shares per ‚Äčquarter. According to a shareholder letter , opens new tab , HLEND received requests totaling 9.3%, but will only offer redemptions up to its limit, ‚Ā†as it was designed to do.
This is far from the first hiccup in private credit. A Blue Owl (OWL.N) , opens new tab fund barred regular withdrawals, instead selling assets to raise ‚Äčcash and return a huge chunk at once. To satisfy elevated redemptions at BCRED, the largest nontraded BDC around, Blackstone employees and the firm itself put in ‚Äča combined $400 million to ensure all requests were covered.
HLEND, though, is the first BDC of its kind to actually prorate redemptions, though others are expected to end up following suit, according to Kevin Gannon of investment bank RA Stanger. Turning theoretical fine-print restrictions into reality may well annoy, and possibly frighten, customers.
When BlackRock bought HPS, private credit was running red-hot. ‚ÄčAssets under management in direct lending funds alone had climbed to $889 billion, according to Preqin data. Apollo and its ilk saw an addressable market in ‚Äčthe tens of trillions of dollars by expanding into IOUs secured by specific assets. These strategies are increasingly pitched as a fit for the kind of mom-and-pop savers that made ‚ÄĆBlackRock a ‚Ā†colossus, with $14 trillion of assets under management.
The company got there in part by inking perhaps the most successful deal in modern asset management history, snapping up Barclays‚Äô BGI in 2009 for $13.5 billion and riding then-explosive growth in exchange traded funds. If anyone has a track record of cracking new markets and reaching the retail masses through M&A, it‚Äôs Larry Fink.
His public-markets business is still immensely successful, and HLEND is only one small piece of a larger plan. Still, rivals ‚Äčlike Blackstone are increasingly trying to eat ‚ÄčBlackRock's lunch by selling retail products. ‚Ā†Meanwhile, from Apollo boss Marc Rowan to JPMorgan CEO Jamie Dimon, warnings of a coming private credit , opens new tab shakeout are rising. As HLEND‚Äôs investor letter put it, disruptive periods breed new opportunities. BlackRock‚Äôs challenge is to prove it can capitalize on ‚Äčthem.
Follow Jonathan Guilford on X , opens new tab and LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: Iran's UN envoy says 1,332 Iranian civilians killed in war
item.description:
March 6 (Reuters) - Iran's U.N. ambassador ‚ÄčAmir Saeid Iravani ‚ÄĆon Friday said at ‚Äčleast 1,332 ‚ÄčIranian civilians had ‚Ā†lost their ‚Äčlives so ‚Äčfar in the conflict with Israel ‚Äčand the ‚ÄčUnited States and ‚ÄĆthousands ‚Ā†more had been injured.
He made his comments ‚Äčwhile ‚Äčspeaking ‚Ā†to reporters at ‚Äčthe United ‚ÄčNations ‚Ā†in New York.

item.title: EU countries joining US, Israel attacks would become Iran's "legitimate target", Deputy Minister says
item.description:
PARIS, March 6 (Reuters) - European Union member states that join the U.S. and ‚ÄčIsrael attacks on Iran would ‚ÄĆbecome "legitimate targets" for Iran, Deputy Foreign Minister Majid Takht-Ravanchi said in an interview ‚Äčwith TV station France 24.
"Any ‚Äčcountry that joins in the ‚Ā†aggression against Iran, joins America and ‚ÄčIsrael in the aggression against ‚ÄčIran, definitely, they will be also legitimate targets for Iran retaliation," Takht-Ravanchi said.

item.title: UK's Starmer discusses military, intelligence support with Saudi crown prince
item.description:
March 6 (Reuters) - British Prime Minister Keir Starmer spoke ‚Äčto Saudi Arabia Crown ‚ÄĆPrince Mohammed bin Salman after Iran's strikes ‚Äčon the kingdom ‚Äčamid a sprawling conflict in the ‚Ā†region, Downing Street ‚Äčsaid in a statement , opens new tab on ‚ÄčFriday.
"Additional British fighter jets, helicopters and a destroyer were ‚Äčbeing sent to ‚Äčthe region, and the UK ‚ÄĆstood ‚Ā†ready to support the defence of the Kingdom of Saudi ‚ÄčArabia should ‚Äčit ‚Ā†be needed," Starmer told bin ‚ÄčSalman.
The two leaders ‚Äčalso ‚Ā†discussed greater intelligence cooperation in support of ‚Ā†defensive ‚Äčoperations and civilians.

item.title: US, Ecuador bomb drug trafficker camp near Colombia border, militaries say
item.description:
WASHINGTON/QUITO, March 6 (Reuters) - The U.S. and Ecuador carried out a ‚Äčjoint operation targeting drug trafficking operations in the South American ‚ÄĆcountry, authorities in both countries said on Friday, with the U.S. calling the move "lethal kinetic operations."
Neither the U.S. Southern Command, a branch of its military that oversees forces ‚Äčin Latin America, nor Ecuador's defense ministry, said if anyone ‚Äčwas killed or captured in the strike, which Ecuador dubbed ‚Ā†operation "Total Extermination."
The operations used helicopters, aircraft, river boats and drones to ‚Äčlocate and bomb a drug traffickers' training camp in north-east Ecuador near ‚Äčthe Colombian border, Ecuador's defense ministry said in a statement.
The camp belonged to the Comandos de la Frontera (CDF), a Colombian crime group made up of FARC dissidents, and ‚Äčhad a capacity for 50 people, it added.
Ecuadorean President Daniel Noboa ‚Äčhas made a military crackdown on organised crime a cornerstone of his administration, and ‚ÄĆhis ‚Ā†government imposed tariffs on its larger neighbor Colombia, accusing it of not doing enough to fight drug trafficking.
He is set to travel to Miami this weekend to take part in the Trump administration's "Shield of the Americas" ‚Äčsummit, which brings ‚Äčtogether many right-wing ‚Ā†leaders across the region with a focus on regional security and organized crime .
"The United States is a key ally ‚Äčin this fight," the defense ministry said.
"At the ‚Äčrequest of ‚Ā†Ecuador, the Department of War executed targeted action to advance our shared objective of dismantling narco-terrorist networks," Pentagon spokesman Sean Parnell wrote on X.
The ‚Ā†operation followed ‚Äča similar U.S-Ecuadorean operation announced by the ‚ÄčU.S. Southern Command earlier this week .

item.title: US skips congressional review to approve munitions sale to Israel
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 6 (Reuters) - The U.S. State Department ‚Äčsaid on Friday ‚ÄĆit has made a determination approving a ‚Äčpossible foreign military ‚Äčsale to Israel of ‚Ā†munitions and munitions ‚Äčsupport for $151.8 million, without congressional ‚Äčreview.
Israel has requested to buy 12,000 BLU-110A/B general ‚Äčpurpose, 1,000-pound bomb ‚Äčbodies, the State Department said ‚ÄĆin ‚Ā†a statement.
Secretary of State Marco Rubio determined that an ‚Äčemergency existed ‚Äčthat ‚Ā†required the immediate sale to ‚ÄčIsrael. The announcement ‚Äčcomes ‚Ā†during the U.S. and Israel's war ‚Ā†against ‚ÄčIran.

item.title: US judge dismisses case by deported student who declined flight back
item.description:
BOSTON, March 6 (Reuters) - A U.S. judge who had ordered President Donald Trump 's administration ‚Äčto facilitate the return of a college student it deported to Honduras said on Friday the "sad truth" is ‚ÄĆthat her decision not to board a plane it arranged back to the United States meant her case must be dismissed.
Any Lucia Lopez Belloza, a 20-year-old freshman at Babson College in Massachusetts, declined on February 27 to board a flight arranged by U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement to bring her back ‚Äčto the United States after the administration said it would try to deport her again if she returned.
Lopez Belloza, ‚Äčwho came to the United States from Honduras when she was 8, has said she was unaware ‚Ā†she was subject to a final order of removal that was entered when she was 11.
Her lawyers had urged Boston-based U.S. ‚ÄčDistrict Judge Richard Stearns to let Lopez Belloza continue the lawsuit she filed after being detained by immigration authorities at Boston's ‚ÄčLogan International Airport in November while traveling to spend the Thanksgiving holiday with her family in Texas.
But Stearns reaffirmed his earlier conclusion that he did not have jurisdiction to hear the case concerning her detention because by the time it was filed on November 21, she had been flown by ‚Äčimmigration authorities to Texas.
His sole remaining basis for jurisdiction would have been to enforce an order issued by another judge just ‚Äčminutes after Lopez Belloza's case was filed barring her from being deported or transferred out of Massachusetts for 72 hours. Lopez Belloza was flown ‚ÄĆfrom Texas ‚Ā†to Honduras the next day despite that judicial order.
A U.S. government lawyer later apologized to Stearns for a "mistake" made by a ICE officer who failed to properly alert others in the agency about the existence of the judicial order.
Stearns on February 13 ordered the administration to rectify the error by facilitating her return to the United States. The administration last week said it would do so ‚Äčby having Lopez Belloza board the ‚ÄčICE flight from Honduras to ‚Ā†Texas.
But the administration also said ICE planned , opens new tab to move to deport her again upon arrival and had the authority to detain her. Lopez Belloza said the "nightmare" situation caused her to decline to board ‚Äčthe flight and remain in Honduras.
"The sad truth is that when Any declined the flight she ‚Äčalso waived this ‚Ā†court's only remaining basis for jurisdiction," Stearns wrote.
Stearns said had she boarded the plane, the judicial order barring her swift deportation would have remained in effect, giving her "ample opportunity" to file a new case in Texas to challenge her detention.
Todd Pomerleau, Lopez Belloza's lawyer, said ‚Ā†he will ‚Äčappeal.

item.title: Iran war enters second week as Trump demands 'unconditional surrender'
item.description:
BEIRUT/WASHINGTON/JERUSALEM, March 7 (Reuters) - The expanding war in Iran entered its second week on Saturday amid renewed uncertainty about how or when hostilities will end, as U.S. President Donald Trump declared he would only accept Tehran's "unconditional surrender" and Israel traded fresh attacks with Iran and Lebanon.
Trump's comments ‚Äčon social media on Friday came hours after Iran's president announced that unnamed countries had begun mediation efforts, briefly raising the possibility, however faint, of a diplomatic resolution a week after ‚ÄĆthe U.S. and Israel launched strikes on Iran.
"There will be no deal with Iran except UNCONDITIONAL SURRENDER!" Trump wrote. "After that, and the selection of a GREAT & ACCEPTABLE Leader(s), we, and many of our wonderful and very brave allies and partners, will work tirelessly to bring Iran back from the brink of destruction, making it economically bigger, better, and stronger than ever before."
Trump has offered shifting explanations of his war aims, raising the possibility of an extended regional conflict that has already spilled well beyond Iran's borders, shaken global financial markets ‚Äčand sent oil prices soaring.
In response to the attack, Iran has targeted Israel as well as multiple Gulf states that host U.S. military installations.
Inside Israel on Friday, explosions could be heard as Israeli defenses ‚Äčactivated to shoot down incoming Iranian fire. The UAE, Kuwait, Qatar, Bahrain and Saudi Arabia all reported fresh drone and missile attacks.
Meanwhile, Israel pursued a major expansion of the ‚Ā†war in Lebanon, pounding the capital Beirut on Friday after ordering an unprecedented evacuation of the city's entire southern suburbs.
Israel also launched a new wave of attacks on Iran, saying 50 of its warplanes had hit a bunker ‚Äčstill being used by Iran's leadership beneath slain Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei's destroyed Tehran compound.
Early on Saturday, Iran's semi-official Tasnim news agency reported that Mehrabad Airport in Tehran had been struck.
There was no immediate comment from Iran's Revolutionary ‚ÄčGuard or Hezbollah.
Israel has extended its bombing to Lebanon to root out Hezbollah , the Shi'ite militia allied to Iran that has been a dominant faction in Lebanese politics since the 1980s. Hezbollah fired on Israel this week to avenge the death of Khamenei.
"We're sleeping here in the streets - some in cars, some on the street, some on the beach," said Jamal Seifeddin, 43, who fled Beirut's southern suburbs and spent the night on the streets in the downtown district.
About 300,000 people have been displaced in Lebanon in the past four days, ‚Äčaccording to the Norwegian Refugee Council.
The Israeli military says it has destroyed 80% of Iran's air-defense systems in the first week of the campaign and disabled more than 60% of its missile launchers.
Trump's demand for Iran's surrender, ‚Äčand the likelihood that it would complicate any quick path to ending the conflict, sent European and U.S. stock indexes tumbling on Friday. Oil prices hit their highest prices in years with the critical shipping lane of the Strait of Hormuz effectively ‚ÄĆclosed down.
Trump ‚Ā†told Reuters in a telephone interview on Thursday that he must have a say in selecting Iran's new supreme leader to replace Khamenei, killed on the war's first day , a demand he repeated on Friday in a remarkable assertion of power over the country of more than 90 million people.
Iran's U.N. ambassador, Amir Saeid Iravani, told reporters that new leadership would be chosen "in accordance with our constitutional procedures and solely by the will of the Iranian people - without any foreign interference."
Israel has said openly that it aims to overthrow Iran's ruling system. It has been bombing parts of western Iran to support Iranian Kurdish militias who hope to exploit the war to seize towns near the frontier, according to three sources ‚Äčfamiliar with Israel's talks with the factions.
Iran has cast the ‚Äčwar as an unprovoked attack and describes the ‚Ā†killing of Khamenei as an assassination.
Earlier on Friday, Iran President Masoud Pezeshkian posted on social media: "Some countries have begun mediation efforts." He did not identify the countries or provide further details.
Russia is providing Iran with locations of U.S. warships and aircraft in the Middle East after Iran's ability to locate U.S. forces was degraded, the Washington ‚ÄčPost reported, citing three officials familiar with the intelligence.
Russian missions in the U.S. did not immediately respond to requests for comment on the report.
Trump met with executives from ‚Äčseven defense contractors on Friday, who ‚Ā†he later said had agreed to accelerate weapons production. The administration has been pressuring contractors as Iran and other recent operations have drawn down supplies.
Karoline Leavitt, a White House spokeswoman, said the U.S. has enough weapons stockpiles to meet the needs of its Iran operations, which she said would take about four to six weeks to complete.
At least 1,332 people have been killed in Iran since the U.S. and Israel launched strikes on February 28, Iravani said, citing the Iranian Red Crescent ‚Ā†Society.
The Lebanese health ‚Äčministry has reported 123 people killed and 683 wounded as a result of Israeli attacks. Iranian attacks have killed 11 people in ‚ÄčIsrael since the war started, and at least six U.S. service members have been killed.
Two U.S. officials told Reuters that military investigators believed it was likely that U.S. forces were responsible for an apparent strike on an Iranian girls' school that killed scores of children on the first day ‚Äčof the war. The investigators have not yet reached a final conclusion.

item.title: Pakistani convicted of plotting to kill Trump over death of Iran commander
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 6 (Reuters) - A Pakistani man was convicted on Friday of planning to kill President Donald Trump and other prominent U.S. politicians two years ago at the behest ‚Äčof Iran , the Department of Justice said.
Asif Merchant was accused of trying to recruit ‚ÄĆpeople in the U.S. in a plan targeting Trump and others in retaliation for Washington's killing of Iranian military commander Qassem Soleimani in 2020, when Trump was in his first term.
Targets in the ‚Äč2024 plot also included then-President Joe Biden and Nikki Haley, who ran ‚Äčagainst Trump that year for the Republican presidential nomination, federal prosecutors said.
Merchant ‚Ā†was convicted of "murder for hire and attempting to commit an act of terrorism transcending ‚Äčnational boundaries," directed by the Iranian authorities, the DOJ said , opens new tab in a statement.
The trial in ‚Äčthe New York City borough of Brooklyn started last week, days before Trump ordered an assault on Iran, carried out with Israel, that has expanded into the region's biggest war in years.
Merchant admitted to ‚Äčjoining the plot with Iran's elite Islamic Revolutionary Guards Corps but testified he did ‚Äčso unwillingly, to protect his family in Tehran.
Merchant said he was never ordered to kill a ‚ÄĆspecific person ‚Ā†but that his Iranian handler named three people in the course of conversations in the Iranian capital.
Law enforcement thwarted the plan before any attack occurred. A person Merchant contacted in April 2024 to help with the plot reported his activities and became a ‚Äčconfidential informant, the DOJ ‚Äčsaid. Merchant was arrested ‚Ā†and pleaded not guilty that year.
The Revolutionary Guards have a central role in Iran, with a combination of military and economic power and ‚Äčan intelligence network. Tehran has denied accusations that it targeted Trump ‚Äčor other ‚Ā†U.S. officials.
U.S. and Israeli attacks since Saturday have killed at least 1,332 Iranian civilians and wounded thousands, according to Iran's U.N. ambassador. Many top Iranian leaders, including Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali ‚Ā†Khamenei, ‚Äčhave been killed.
The U.S. military has said six of its service ‚Äčmembers were killed in a strike on a facility in Kuwait, while Israeli tallies show at least 10 ‚Äčcivilians have been killed across Israel.

item.title: Tornadoes kill four, injure more than a dozen in rural¬†Michigan
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 6 (Reuters) - Tornadoes and strong storms killed at least four people and injured more than a dozen in southern Michigan on ‚ÄčFriday, destroying multiple homes and leaving hundreds of residents without power, ‚ÄĆlocal authorities said.
Soon after the National Weather Service issued tornado warnings for the area on Friday, videos on social media claiming to be from the towns of Three ‚ÄčRivers and Union City showed massive whirlwinds tearing roofs off buildings ‚Äčand lifting debris into the air.
Local media footage showed smashed buildings ‚Ā†and cars, and poles, trees and road signs that had been blown ‚Äčover.
Three fatalities and a dozen injuries were reported in Branch County, where Union ‚ÄčCity is located, the county's sheriff's office said.
One person was killed and several injured in Cass County, according to local authorities there.
"Multiple large structures - including homes and pole barns - ‚Äčsustained damage ranging from major structural impacts to complete destruction," according to ‚Äča statement on a website for Cass County.
Power outages had affected hundreds of people, local ‚ÄĆofficials ‚Ā†said.
"We have multiple utility poles down, transformers damaged, and long stretches of power lines on the ground. The north side of Union Lake sustained some of the most severe damage, with roughly two miles of line brought ‚Äčdown in that area ‚Äčalone," according to ‚Ā†a posting on Union City's official Facebook page that said restoration may take several days.
Michigan Governor Gretchen Whitmer said ‚Äčshe activated a State Emergency Operations Center for monitoring ‚Äčthe situation.
"The ‚Ā†state is ready to fulfill any resource requests that may arise. We've yet to receive any just yet. It's sounding like the local response is and ‚Ā†has ‚Äčbeen able to address what's been a very ‚Äčdevastating afternoon and evening in southwest Michigan," Clayton Cummins, a spokesperson for Michigan emergency services and ‚Äčpolice, told Reuters.

item.title: Iran conflict boosts U.S. Gulf oil prices to highest since 2020
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 6 (Reuters) - U.S. Gulf Coast heavy grades continued to surge on Friday as the Iran conflict spurred several Middle Eastern producers of heavy crude to curtail ‚Äčproduction and drove buyers to scoop up U.S. barrels.
Prices of Mars ‚ÄĆsour crude, the flagship crude produced in the U.S. Gulf of Mexico and favored by refiners globally, traded at a $11 premium to U.S. benchmark West Texas Intermediate (WTI) crude on Friday, ‚Äčbrokers said. That was the highest since April 2020, and up $4 from ‚ÄčThursday.
Just a week ago it traded at a premium of $1.50.
Other heavy grades ‚Ā†such as the Heavy Louisiana Sweet and the West Texas Sour also ‚Äčrose.
Benchmark crude oil prices have surged since the initial attacks last week, with Brent ‚Äčcrude settling at $92.69 a barrel, its highest level since October 2023 on Friday.
The effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz has forced several countries, including Iraq, to curb output. The strait is a ‚Äčkey route for medium and heavy sour crude from the Persian Gulf, and ‚Äčthose flows are now largely cut off. Additional production cuts announced in Kuwait on Friday ‚ÄĆalso ‚Ā†helped lift Mars prices, a trader said.
‚ÄúRefiners that rely on these grades will need to find similar, or roughly similar, alternatives to replace the lost barrels, so Mars and other U.S. Gulf sour heavies and mediums are natural substitutes and are ‚Äčgetting bid up ‚Äčaggressively,‚ÄĚ Kpler lead ‚Ā†Americas oil analyst Matt Smith said, adding that buyers, especially in Asia, are scrambling for more of these medium and ‚Äčheavy crude barrels.
"This time of year also marks the shift ‚Äčfrom winter ‚Ā†into driving season, when demand typically rises across all crude grades," said Tim Snyder, chief economist at Matador Economics, adding that ultimately the supply disruption caused by war ‚Ā†was ‚Äčdriving prices.
"In the short term we will continue ‚Äčto see these grades rise until we see the Strait of Hormuz open up," Snyder said.

item.title: US pump prices surge as Iran war upends global energy supply
item.description:
MARIETTA/NEW YORK, March 6 (Reuters) - U.S. retail gasoline and diesel prices are soaring as the U.S.-Israel war with Iran constrains oil and fuel exports, which could be a political test for President Donald Trump's Republican Party ahead ‚Äčof midterm elections in November.
Fuel prices jumped more than 10% this week as oil rose above $90 a barrel, its highest in years, adding pain at the ‚ÄĆpump for consumers already strained by inflation. Trump on Thursday shrugged off higher gasoline prices in an interview with Reuters , opens new tab , saying "if they rise, they rise."
The president had vowed to lower energy prices and unleash U.S. oil and gas drilling during his second term, but much of his tenure has been marked by volatility and uncertainty amid shifts in policies like tariffs and geopolitical turmoil. The U.S. is the world's largest oil producer. It is a major exporter ‚Äčbut also imports millions of barrels a day since it is the world's largest oil consumer.
As of Friday, the national average prices for regular gasoline stood at $3.32 a ‚Äčgallon, up 11% from a week ago and the highest since September 2024, according to data from the motorists association AAA. Diesel was at $4.33, ‚Ā†up 15% from a week ago, surging to the highest since November 2023.
U.S. motorists in parts of the Midwest and the South, including states that supported Trump, have ‚Äčseen some of the steepest increases in fuel costs since the conflict in Iran started.
In Georgia, a swing state, average retail gasoline prices rose 40.1 cents a gallon over the past week, ‚Äčaccording to fuel tracking site GasBuddy.
Andrenna McDaniel, a healthcare insurance worker in South Fulton, Georgia, said she was surprised to see prices skyrocket overnight.
"They jumped up so quickly," she said on Friday, adding that she does not agree with the war at all.
McDaniel, a Democrat, said that for now she is only driving for the most important things, and feels lucky that she works from home so she does not have to drive as ‚Äčmuch as other people do.
Georgia voted for Donald Trump in the 2024 election.
Trump voter Richard Soule, 69, a U.S. Air Force veteran and a retired firefighter, said a little pain at the pump ‚Äčis worth Trump's efforts to protect America.
"When President Trump went in there and bombed out their nuclear, and they just thumbed their nose at it, I believe he did the right thing at the right ‚ÄĆtime," Soule said ‚Ā†on Friday as he filled up his Ford F-150 truck in Marietta, Georgia.
Other states, including Indiana and West Virginia have seen prices rise by 44.3 cents and 43.9 cents, respectively.
More pain may be on the way, analysts said, as oil prices continue to trend upward. On Friday, U.S. oil futures settled at $90.90 a barrel, up nearly $10 and the biggest single-day rise since April 2020.
"Given current market conditions, the national average price of gasoline could climb toward $3.50 to $3.70 per gallon in the coming days if oil continues rising and supply disruptions persist," GasBuddy analyst Patrick De ‚ÄčHaan said.
The disruptions in the Middle East and ‚Äčthe Strait of Hormuz, a key trade ‚Ā†conduit, have boosted demand for U.S. oil abroad, which in turn has driven up prices for domestic refiners too.
"The U.S. has weaned itself off of its dependence on Middle Eastern crude, but obviously Asian refineries, and to a lesser extent, European refineries have not," Denton Cinquegrana, chief oil ‚Äčanalyst with OPIS. "That's what you're seeing happen in the spot market, because the demand for U.S. exports rise, and so the price rise."
Seasonal ‚Äčfactors could add further ‚Ā†pressure. Gasoline prices typically go up in the spring and peak in the summer due to higher gasoline demand and production of summer-blend gasoline, which is more costly to produce.
Diesel fuel saw an even more aggressive jump since Iran began retaliating against U.S. and Israeli strikes, significantly disrupting shipping in the Strait of Hormuz.
Global diesel inventories have remained in tight supply due to heavy demand for heating and power generation ‚Ā†during a ‚Äčprolonged winter in the U.S. and other parts of the world and a structural tightness of refining capacity.
Sticker prices ‚Äčof everything from food to furniture go up when the cost of diesel goes up, as the fuel is mainly used in freight transportation, manufacturing, agriculture, and global shipping, analysts said.
"In a world where buzzword seems to be 'affordability', that is ‚Äčcertainly not going to help," Cinquegrana said.

item.title: World's largest urban coffee plantation welcomes new trees in Brazil
item.description:
SAO PAULO, March 6 (Reuters) - The world's largest urban coffee ‚Äčplantation, located in Brazil's sprawling city of Sao Paulo, this week welcomed some ‚ÄĆ1,500 new coffee plants as researchers prepare to study their capacity to resist climate change and pests.
Sao Paulo's Biological Institute was established in 1927 with the mission of tackling a crisis caused by pests ‚Äčlike the coffee berry-borer beetle which devour the beans hidden inside coffee cherries.
Brazil ‚Äčis the world's top producer of arabica coffee, as well as the ‚Ā†second-biggest producer of canephora coffees, which include varieties like robusta and conilon.
The plantation in ‚ÄčSao Paulo's Vila Mariana neighborhood, which already boasted more than 2,000 coffee plants, welcomed arabica ‚Äčvarieties this week billed as resistant to pests and coffee rust, a type of fungus, as well as other plants that are more tolerant of drought-like conditions.
"The Biological Institute was created to control the ‚Äčcoffee berry-borer (which) was controlled using parasitoids, a biological control method," institute researcher and agricultural engineer ‚ÄčHarumi Hojo told Reuters on Thursday.
In her hand, Hojo held two coffee cherries, showing the difference between ‚ÄĆa ‚Ā†healthy fruit with smooth white coffee beans and another rotten on the inside after being devoured by a beetle.
Over the years, the institute began to investigate other factors affecting coffee plants, like soil and climate, and now its range of varieties grown side by side ‚Äčunder the same conditions ‚Äčshows how different ‚Ā†plants handle pests, disease and climate pressures, Hojo said.
Arabica coffee plants are also sensitive to hotter and drier weather caused by climate ‚Äčchange, and 300 of the new plants at the institute's plantation ‚Äčare tolerant to ‚Ā†water deficits.
Now, research has produced coffee varieties that are resistant to droughts, Hojo said, adding that it would be valuable in the future to have coffee plants that can hold out ‚Ā†for ‚Äčirrigation with captured rain, rather than groundwater sources that ‚Äčmay be scarce.
"We know that climate change and water availability are going to be problems for our future," Hojo ‚Äčsaid.

item.title: One week into Iran war, the dangers for the US and Trump multiply
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 7 (Reuters) - One week into the U.S.-Israeli war against Iran that has plunged the Middle East into turmoil, President Donald Trump faces a growing list of risks and challenges that raise questions about whether he will be able to translate military successes into a clear geopolitical win.
Even after the killing of Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei and devastating blows against Iranian forces on land, at sea and in the air, the crisis has ‚Äčquickly widened into a regional conflict that threatens a more prolonged U.S. military engagement with fallout beyond Trump‚Äôs control.
That is a scenario that Trump had avoided in his two terms in the White House, preferring swift, limited operations like the January 3 lightning raid in Venezuela ‚ÄĆand June‚Äôs one-off strike on Iran‚Äôs nuclear sites.
‚ÄúIran is a messy and potentially protracted military campaign,‚ÄĚ said Laura Blumenfeld of the Johns Hopkins School for Advanced International Studies in Washington. ‚ÄúTrump is risking the global economy, regional stability and his own Republican Party's performance in the U.S. midterm elections.‚ÄĚ
Trump, who came to office promising to keep the U.S. out of "stupid‚ÄĚ military interventions, is now pursuing what many experts see as an open-ended war of choice unprompted by any imminent threat to the U.S. from Iran, despite claims to the contrary by the president and his aides.
In doing so, analysts say he has struggled to articulate a detailed set of objectives or a clear endgame for Operation Epic Fury, the biggest U.S. military operation since the 2003 Iraq invasion, offering shifting rationales ‚Äčfor the war and definitions of what would constitute victory.
White House spokesperson Anna Kelly rejected that assessment, saying Trump has clearly outlined his goals to "destroy Iran‚Äôs ballistic missiles and production capacity, demolish their navy, end their ability to arm proxies, and prevent them from ever obtaining a nuclear weapon.‚ÄĚ
However, if the ‚Äčwar drags on, American casualties mount and the economic costs of interrupted Gulf oil flows multiply, Trump‚Äôs biggest foreign policy gamble could also hurt his Republican Party politically.
Despite criticism from some Trump supporters opposed to military ‚Ā†interventions, members of his Make America Great Again movement have largely backed him on Iran so far.
But any softening of their support could imperil Republicans‚Äô control of Congress in the November midterm elections, given opinion polls showing opposition to the war among the broader electorate, including a crucial bloc of independent voters.
‚ÄúThe American people are ‚Äčnot interested in repeating the mistakes of Iraq and Afghanistan,‚ÄĚ said Brian Darling, a Republican strategist. ‚ÄúThe MAGA base is split between those who relied on no-new-war promises and ones who are loyal to Trump‚Äôs judgment.‚ÄĚ
High on the list of analysts‚Äô concerns is the mixed messaging from Trump and his aides on whether he is seeking ‚Äúregime change‚ÄĚ in ‚ÄčTehran.
At the outset of the conflict, he suggested that overthrowing Iran‚Äôs rulers was a goal, at least by fomenting internal rebellion. Two days later, he stopped short of mentioning that as a priority.
But then on Thursday, Trump told Reuters he would play a role in picking Iran‚Äôs next leader and encouraged Iranian Kurdish rebels to launch attacks. That was followed by his demand in a social media post on Friday for Iran‚Äôs "unconditional surrender."
Across the region, the dangers have escalated with Iran's retaliatory strikes on Israel and other neighbors as it seeks to sow chaos and raise the costs for Israel, the U.S. and its allies.
Showing that Iran may still be able to activate proxy groups, Lebanon‚Äôs Hezbollah militia has renewed hostilities with Israel, expanding the war to another ‚Äčcountry.
American casualties have been low so far, with six service members killed, and Trump has largely shrugged off the prospects for more to come while declining to completely rule out deployment of U.S. ground troops.
Asked whether Americans should worry about Iran-inspired attacks at home, Trump said in a Time magazine interview published on Friday: ‚ÄúI guess ‚Ä¶ ‚ÄčLike I said, some people will die.‚ÄĚ
But Jonathan Panikoff, a former deputy U.S. national intelligence officer for the Middle East, said: ‚ÄúNothing is likely to hasten an early end to the war more than American casualties ‚Ä¶ That‚Äôs what Iran is counting on.‚ÄĚ
VENEZUELA MISCALCULATION?
Many analysts believe Trump, who has shown an increasing appetite for military action in his second term, miscalculated that the ‚ÄĆIran campaign would unfold ‚Ā†like the Venezuela operation earlier this year.
U.S. special forces captured Venezuelan President Nicolas Maduro, opening the way for Trump to coerce more compliant former loyalists into giving him considerable sway over the country‚Äôs vast oil reserves ‚Äď without any extended U.S. military action needed.
By contrast, Iran has proved a much tougher, better-armed foe with an entrenched clerical and security establishment.
Even the joint U.S.-Israeli "decapitation" strike that killed Khamenei and some other senior leaders has failed so far to prevent Iran from mounting a military response and has raised questions whether they could be replaced by even more hardline figures.
Looming over the conflict, however, is whether Iran could slide into chaos and break apart if its current rulers fall, further destabilizing the Middle East.
Mark Dubowitz, CEO of the Foundation for the Defense of Democracies, a nonprofit research institute considered hawkish on Iran, praised Trump‚Äôs overall war strategy but said the president needs to make clear publicly that he does not want to see the country disintegrate.
For now, however, one of the most pressing ‚Äčconcerns is Iran‚Äôs threat to the Strait of Hormuz, the narrow chokepoint through ‚Äčwhich a fifth of the world's oil passes. Tanker traffic has halted, ‚Ā†which could have grave economic consequences if it lasts.
Though Trump has publicly dismissed any concern about already-rising U.S. gas prices, he and his aides have scrambled for ways to mitigate the war‚Äôs impact on energy supplies as voters tell pollsters that the cost of living is their top concern.
‚ÄúIt's an economic pain point on the U.S. economy that it seems was not fully anticipated," said Josh Lipsky at the Atlantic Council think tank in Washington.
One former U.S. military official close to the U.S. administration said ‚Äčthe widening of the war's economic impact had caught Trump‚Äôs team by surprise in part because those with knowledge of oil markets were not consulted ahead of the attack on Iran.
The White House's Kelly said, ‚ÄúThe Iranian regime is being ‚Äčabsolutely crushed" but did not specifically address concerns ‚Ā†about preparations for a war.
Trump made his decision to press ahead with the strikes
despite warnings from some senior aides that the escalation could be difficult to contain, according to two White House officials and a Republican close to the administration.
Some traditional U.S. allies were caught off guard. "It's a decision-making circle of one," said one Western diplomat.
The war‚Äôs duration is a major unknown likely to determine the extent of its repercussions. With the price tag of the Iran campaign mounting by the day, Trump has said that the operation could last four or five weeks or ‚Äúwhatever it takes‚ÄĚ but has offered little explanation of what he envisions will follow.
Retired U.S. Army Lieutenant General Ben Hodges, who served in Iraq and Afghanistan ‚Ā†and formerly commanded the ‚ÄčU.S. Army in Europe, commended the U.S. military for its tactics in Iran. But he told Reuters: "From a political, strategic and diplomatic standpoint, it seems not to have been thought all the way ‚Äčthrough.‚ÄĚ
Trump also has a lot riding on helping oil-producing Gulf Arab states weather the Iran crisis given they have long hosted U.S. bases and have made pledges of massive new U.S. investments to him.
While Gulf allies appear to have fallen in line to support the campaign, especially after Tehran targeted them with missile and drone strikes, not everyone in the region is onboard with Trump‚Äôs war.
In an open ‚Äčletter to Trump published on Thursday, UAE billionaire Khalaf Al Habtor, a frequent visitor to Trump's Mar-a-Lago resort in Florida, asked: "Who gave you the right to turn our region into a battlefield?"

item.title: As war spreads, airline pilots contend with drones, missiles - and stress
item.description:
LONDON/COPENHAGEN/BEIRUT, March 7 (Reuters) - Airline pilots have faced escalating risks in recent years, from drone incursions to flight paths squeezed by conflict. Now U.S.-Israeli attacks on Iran are making the skies even more perilous and ratcheting up the pressure on those flying through them.
The outbreak of war in the Middle East has put hundreds of ballistic ‚Äčmissiles and attack drones into the skies above some of the world's busiest airports. Tehran's retaliation against the U.S. and its allies has included hitting airports, grounding scores of flights from Dubai to Abu Dhabi. A trickle ‚ÄĆof rescue flights has made it through for thousands of stranded passengers.
Reuters spoke to eight pilots and more than a half-dozen aviation and security insiders who said the accumulation of conflicts - from Ukraine to Afghanistan and Israel - has increased the burden on pilots, forcing them to manage shrinking airspace and the wider use of military drones far from active war zones. That's increased the strain on the mental health of pilots desperate to keep themselves and their passengers safe.
"We are not military pilots. We are not trained to deal with these kinds of threats in the air," Tanja Harter, a pilot with Middle ‚ÄčEast experience and president of the European Cockpit Association, told Reuters.
The current crisis is the latest in a string of security threats the sector has faced over the years, she said, which could cause "fear and anxiety" for pilots. Airlines ‚Äčnow often have peer programs to help, she said, adding as a pilot she would not want to "share airspace with missiles."
Airspace safety has worsened in the last two-and-a-half years as conflicts have grown, ‚Ā†industry experts said, through a combination of GPS spoofing - maliciously tricking planes about their position - and increased numbers of missiles and drones .
An Air France (AIRF.PA) , opens new tab flight to bring stranded French nationals home from the United Arab Emirates turned back due to missile fire on Thursday. A ‚ÄčLufthansa (LHAG.DE) , opens new tab pilot on Friday diverted from Riyadh to Cairo over regional security fears.
Middle East-trained pilots have become wearily used to emergencies, the head of Lebanon's civil aviation body said. The escalating conflict put those skills to the test quickly. Video footage ‚Äčon March 5 showed planes taking off from Beirut airport as smoke billowed over buildings in the Lebanese capital.
