"Middle East pilots have always faced crises, so from the start we trained how to deal with contingencies, emergencies and everything else," said Captain Mohammed Aziz, director general of Lebanon's civil aviation authority.
"No one can give you a guarantee that they won't bomb the airport or will bomb the airport."
One Middle East Airlines' pilot with a decade of experience said routes to Beirut have become more complex. In the past, shoulder-fired anti-aircraft missiles in Lebanon usually had a range of 15,000 feet, so ‚Äčpilots would increase altitude to stay out of range, he said, while planes often carried extra fuel in case they were forced to divert.
Still, most missile strikes are far enough away not to be a risk and pilots are often too busy to ‚Äčworry about them.
"You're actually busy enough on the plane trying to make sure you have clearance to land, that everything is in order, so you don't have time to process your emotions over what's happening outside the plane," he said.
The risks are not ‚ÄĆconfined to the ‚Ā†Middle East. Since Russia's 2022 invasion of Ukraine, drones have become a key weapon on both sides. Airports in European cities from Stockholm to Munich have faced drone disruptions - suspected though not confirmed to be linked to the conflict.
Airline Captain Christian von D'Ahe, a commercial aircraft pilot for 15 years and head of the Danish Air Line Pilots Association, is alarmed by the emerging threat.
"Drones are not easily detected," Von D'Ahe said. "We can see them in the air, and they're very small. So sooner or later, something will happen."
Drones striking an aircraft's engines could cause total power loss, while damage to wings could compromise a jet's ability to manoeuvre.
Most registered planes emit a signal via a transponder, a device that identifies aircraft to radar, but drones do not, leaving pilots in the dark. Regular radars used ‚Äčby airports struggle to catch drones. Specialised radar exists, but is ‚Äčtypically operated by law enforcement or the military.
Dedrone, a ‚Ā†company that produces counterdrone technology, said there were over 1.2 million drone violations in the U.S. in 2025, with more expected in coming years.
Airports can use radar, frequency sensors and jamming tools to counter drones, while some systems can "spoof" them off course. But safety concerns mean airports cannot shoot drones down.
Tim Friebe, an air traffic controller in Germany and a ‚Äčvice president at the Air Traffic Controllers European Unions Coordination (ATCEUC), said drones were a "threat that is growing," while airports often had limited options.
"For now we have reports, pilot reports, or ‚Äčsometimes controllers spot drones. The problem ‚Ā†is there's not much you can do except shutting down the airport," he said.
Drones shut down , opens new tab some of the world's biggest airports from Munich to London's Gatwick last year, driving operators to beef up their foreign object and drone detection systems, according to half a dozen industry officials.
Moritz Burger, a commercial pilot based in Germany, recalled spotting an object that looked like a balloon with a structure underneath as he was about to land at a European airport.
"I was looking out of the window and suddenly there appeared an object that passed by just ‚Ā†below our aircraft. ‚ÄčWe could see it for maybe one, maximum two seconds," he said, adding it startled him and left him no time to take any evasive ‚Äčaction.
"When you encounter such a near-miss or some passing object, there is not enough time to react. So it is unrealistic to expect that pilots could fly around such an object. There's pretty much nothing we can do."

item.title: Russia hits Ukraine with drones, missiles, kills at least seven in Kharkiv
item.description:
KYIV, March 7 (Reuters) - Russia launched a barrage of drones and missiles ‚Äčat Ukraine overnight on Saturday, killing at least ‚ÄĆseven people, including two children, in the northeast city of Kharkiv, Ukrainian officials said.
Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskiy said that ‚ÄčRussia launched 480 drones and 29 missiles ‚Äčtargeting the energy sector and railway infrastructure across ‚Ā†the country.
"There should be a response from partners ‚Äčto these savage strikes against life," Zelenskiy said on ‚Äčthe Telegram app.
"Russia has not abandoned its attempts to destroy Ukraine's residential and critical infrastructure, and therefore support should continue," ‚ÄčZelenskiy said, urging partners to continue air defence ‚Äčand weapons supplies.
Oleh Syniehubov, Kharkiv regional governor, said that seven ‚ÄĆpeople, ‚Ā†including two children, were killed after a Russian ballistic missile slammed into a five-storey residential building.
Rescue workers continue to work on the site, clearing the rubble, ‚Äčhe said.
In ‚ÄčKharkiv, seven ‚Ā†residential apartment houses, commercial and administrative buildings, electricity distribution lines, and cars were ‚Äčdamaged by the Russian attacks, Syniehubov said.
Ukrainian ‚Äčofficials ‚Ā†said that Russia attacked four railway stations and other railway infrastructure in central Ukraine and port infrastructure in ‚Ā†the ‚Äčsouthern Odesa region, setting on ‚Äčfire containers with vegetable oil and damaging a grain warehouse.

item.title: Refugees, migrants in Lebanon find rare sanctuary from Israeli strikes in Beirut church
item.description:
BEIRUT, March 7 (Reuters) - When Israeli strikes began pummelling Beirut‚Äôs southern suburbs early on Monday, Sudanese refugee ‚ÄčRidina Muhammad and her family had no choice but to flee home on foot, eventually reaching the only shelter ‚ÄĆthat would accept them: a church.
Eight months pregnant, Muhammad, 32, walked with her husband and three children for hours in the dark streets until they found a car to take them to the St. Joseph Tabaris Parish, which has opened its doors to refugees and migrants.
They are among 300,000 people displaced across Lebanon this week by heavy ‚ÄčIsraeli strikes, launched in response to a rocket and drone attack into Israel by the Lebanese armed group Hezbollah.
Just 100,000 of the ‚Äčdisplaced are in government shelters. Others are staying with relatives or sleeping in the streets. But migrants and ‚Ā†refugees say government shelters were never an option for them, saying they were turned away during the last war between Hezbollah and Israel.
Muhammad‚Äôs oldest ‚Äčdaughter, now seven, stopped speaking after the 2024 war.
This time, they are even more vulnerable: their home was destroyed in this week‚Äôs strikes and ‚ÄčMuhammad is due to give birth at the end of the month.
‚ÄúI don't know if there's a doctor or not, but I'm really scared about it because I haven't prepared any clothes for the baby, nor arranged a hospital, and I don't know where to go,‚ÄĚ she told Reuters as her younger daughter leaned ‚Äčagainst her pregnant belly.
Muhammad said she was registered with the United Nations‚Äô refugee agency (UNHCR) but had not received support.
‚ÄúUs, as refugees, ‚Äčwhy did we register with the U.N., if they are not helping us in the most difficult times?‚ÄĚ she said.
Dalal Harb, a spokesperson for UNHCR Lebanon, ‚ÄĆsaid the ‚Ā†agency had mobilized but reaching everyone immediately was extremely challenging given the scale and speed of displacement. The UNHCR operation in Lebanon is currently only around 14% funded, she said.
The Jesuit Refugee Service (JRS), which helped the church host displaced in 2024, is doing so again.
Michael Petro, JRS‚Äô Emergency Shelter Director, said the church was full within the first day of strikes, with 140 people from South Sudan, Ethiopia, Bangladesh, and other countries sheltering ‚Äčthere.
‚ÄúThere are many, many more people ‚Äčcoming than there were in 2024, ‚Ā†and we have fewer and fewer places to put them,‚ÄĚ he said.
Petro said he was told weeks ago that government shelters would be open to migrants if war erupted.
But when the strikes began and even ‚ÄčLebanese struggled to find shelter, the policy seemed to change, he said.
‚ÄúWe're hearing from hotlines up to ‚Äčgovernment officials and ‚Ā†ministries that migrants are not welcome,‚ÄĚ Petro said.
Lebanon‚Äôs Minister for Social Affairs Haneen Sayyed did not respond to a request for comment. On Thursday, Sayyed said Beirut shelters were full.
When Israeli strikes began, Othman Yahyeh Dawood, a 41-year-old Sudanese man, put his two young sons on his motorcycle.
They drove 75 ‚Ā†kilometres (46 miles) ‚Äčfrom the southern Lebanese town of Nabatieh to St. Joseph‚Äôs, where they had sheltered ‚Äčin 2024.
‚ÄúI know the area is safe and there are people who will welcome us,‚ÄĚ he said.
‚ÄúWe don't know where to go; there's war there (in the south), war here (in ‚ÄčBeirut), war in Sudan, and nowhere else to go,‚ÄĚ he said.

item.title: In Perpignan, French far right tests tough-on-crime blueprint
item.description:
PERPIGNAN, France, March 7 (Reuters) - In the streets of Perpignan, a faded Mediterranean city near France's border with Spain, the incumbent far-right mayor Louis Aliot is pitching a simple message as he asks voters for another term: more police, more cameras and more order.
The National Rally (RN) mayor ‚Äčhas made security the centrepiece of his administration, and his party is holding up this city of 122,000, the biggest it controls, as a blueprint for governance that it hopes to ‚ÄĆreplicate elsewhere ahead of nationwide municipal elections this month.
Aliot, who leads in polls despite an embezzlement conviction that could bar him from office if his appeal fails, said Perpignan has been a laboratory for RN governance since he won city hall in 2020 and a showcase for its national ambitions when the French vote for a new president in 2027.
"When the National Rally is in office, well, we govern, we run cities and we run them well," he said in an interview.
Wins in other cities would give Marine ‚ÄčLe Pen's party a springboard going into next year's presidential election. Polls show the RN performing well in Toulon, Nice and Marseille, although a two-round system makes final outcomes hard to forecast.
The ‚ÄčRN's law-and-order message resonates with many voters in Perpignan, where just streets from the Catalan-influenced, historic centre lie run-down neighbourhoods that are among France's poorest.
Two weeks before the ‚Ā†March 15 and 23 elections, RN leader Jordan Bardella and hard-left rival Jean-Luc M√©lenchon - both presidential hopefuls - held duelling weekend rallies, turning the city into a symbolic battleground.
"Faced with the violence engulfing our country year after year, we ‚Äčintend to make public order an absolute priority," Bardella told supporters.
Aliot promised 50 more police officers and 200 surveillance cameras, particularly in outer suburbs to which the middle class has fled from poverty-stricken inner neighbourhoods that are home ‚Äčto the city's sizeable North African and Roma populations.
The city has already expanded its municipal force to 199 officers from 161 in 2020, and now deploys 1.6 municipal police per 1,000 residents - proportionally the highest among French cities of more than 100,000 and nearly three times Paris's 0.6, according to Reuters' analysis of Interior Ministry data.
More aggressive policing has seen drug-trafficking cases climb sharply, with Perpignan now reporting the seventh most among over 50 large French cities, up from 18th in 2020. Overall trafficking cases ‚Äčopened by police have more than doubled, while fixed-penalty fines for minor possession have quadrupled.
The security focus comes at a cost. Municipal debt stands at 1,600 euros per inhabitant, well above the 1,200 average in similar-sized ‚Äčcities, according to Finance Ministry data.
Property tax rates are higher than in 71% of comparable cities, and 98% of peers have lower local business taxes.
Even so, business weekly Challenges ranked Perpignan the third best-run city in France last week, a ‚ÄĆtalking point for ‚Ā†Aliot's allies as they try to convert security-minded voters beyond the RN's base.
Aliot leads with 44% in the first round according to an early December Ifop poll, benefiting from a fractured opposition split among five rivals.
A far-right political veteran, Aliot has been central to Le Pen's campaign to soften the image of the anti-immigration party and make it more palatable to the mainstream voter. Opinion polls project either Le Pen or Bardella, her prot√©g√©, winning the first round of next year's presidential vote and facing a tight run-off contest.
On the ground, reactions were mixed. At a city-centre market, retiree Marie Nivet said she welcomed cleaner streets and visible policing, though she wanted more action on ‚Äčdrugs.
She was undecided on whether to support Aliot because ‚Äčof the looming appeal court verdict over misuse ‚Ā†of EU funds that also threatens Le Pen's political survival .
Aliot said he would appeal any unfavourable outcome to France's highest court, but that others in his team could take his place if needed.
The legal uncertainty didn't bother Laure Gu√©rin, a retired private-school administrator, who said she would back Aliot because of the RN's law-and-order stance.
Others were more ‚Äčwary. Christian Pyguillem, a retired property manager, credited Aliot with cleaner streets and better security, but said entrenched poverty in some neighbourhoods requires deeper urban renewal ‚Äčthan any mayor has attempted.
"We'll ‚Ā†give him another six years, but it's a vote motivated by the local situation, I don't agree with their other ideas," he said.
Rivals said the security push has failed to deliver and that city finances have deteriorated. Centre-right challenger Bruno Nougayr√®de accused Aliot of neglecting local duties for national politics and putting publicity before results.
"These five years have been marked by complete inaction for the people of Perpignan," Nougayr√®de told Reuters. "A lot of resources went into PR, but the ‚Ā†city itself ‚Äčhas not changed."
Hard-left candidate Micka√ęl Idrac called Aliot's record "catastrophic," blaming "frenzied" investment in arming police and surveillance cameras. He also criticised what he ‚Äčdescribed as divisive rhetoric.
Aliot countered that voters would judge by how they feel in their streets. He casts the RN as the party willing to enforce order after decades of drift by rivals on left and right.
"If the left had defended the poor, we wouldn't be ‚Äčhere today. If the right had defended order, we wouldn't be here today," Aliot said.

item.title: India let Iran warship dock the day US sank another off Sri Lanka, officials say
item.description:
NEW DELHI, March 6 (Reuters) - India has allowed an Iranian warship to dock as a humanitarian gesture, Foreign Minister Subrahmanyam Jaishankar said on Saturday, after the U.S. ‚Äčsank another Iranian navy vessel off neighbouring Sri Lanka.
The Lavan docked ‚ÄĆat India's southern port of Kochi on Wednesday, the same day the U.S. submarine struck Iranian navy frigate Dena, after an urgent request from Tehran, an Indian government source told ‚ÄčReuters.
U.S. President Donald Trump has said destroying the Iranian navy is one ‚Äčaim of the war he and Israel launched against the Islamic ‚Ā†Republic a week ago.
The Lavan - an amphibious landing vessel, according to the U.S. ‚ÄčNaval Institute's online news site - and two other ships "were coming in for a ‚Äčfleet review and then they got, in a way, caught on the wrong side of the events," Jaishankar told the annual Raisina Dialogue event.
"I think we really approached it from ‚Äčthe point of view of humanity, of other than whatever the legal ‚Äčissues were," he said. "I think we did the right thing."
At least 87 people were killed in ‚ÄĆthe U.S. ‚Ā†attack on the Dena in Sri Lanka's exclusive economic zone 19 nautical miles off the coast, outside its maritime boundaries.
India received the docking request for the Lavan on February 28, the day the Iran war started, the source ‚Äčsaid late on Friday, ‚Äčadding that the ‚Ā†request "was urgent as the vessel had developed technical issues".
Its 183 crew members have been accommodated at naval facilities in ‚ÄčKochi, said the source, who asked not to be identified ‚Äčciting confidentiality.
The ‚Ā†Dena was on its way back from a naval exercise organised by India, according to the drill's website and Sri Lankan officials.
Sri Lankan authorities said on Friday ‚Ā†that ‚Äčthey were escorting the Iranian naval ship Booshehr ‚Äčto a harbour on the eastern coast and moving most of its crew to a navy camp ‚Äčnear Colombo.

item.title: Iran to suspend strikes on neighbours unless attacks come from them
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - Iran's President Masoud Pezeshkian ‚Äčsaid on ‚ÄĆSaturday that its temporary leadership ‚Äčcouncil had ‚Äčapproved the suspension ‚Ā†of attacks ‚Äčagainst neighboring countries ‚Äčunless an attack on Iran came ‚Äčfrom those ‚Äčcountries.
The announcement came as ‚ÄĆIran ‚Ā†continued to launch attacks in the ‚Äčregion ‚Äčin ‚Ā†response to U.S.-Israeli strikes ‚Äčon the ‚ÄčIslamic ‚Ā†Republic.

item.title: Azerbaijan says it foils Iranian plots including plan to attack major pipeline
item.description:
BAKU, March 7 (Reuters) - Azerbaijan said it had prevented several acts of "terrorist" sabotage planned by Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC), including ‚Äča plot to attack a major oil pipeline running ‚ÄĆthrough the South Caucasus to Turkey.
The targets included the Baku-Tbilisi-Ceyhan (BTC) oil pipeline, the Israeli embassy in Azerbaijan, an Ashkenazi synagogue, and a leader ‚Äčan ancient Jewish community in Azerbaijan called the Mountain ‚ÄčJews, according to a State Security Service statement cited ‚Ā†by the Azertag state news agency late on Friday.
Iran ‚Äčhas not commented on the statement.
The BTC pipeline travels via Georgia ‚Äčand Turkey and sends oil to Europe, and also accounts for roughly a third of Israeli oil imports. Any damage to its infrastructure ‚Äčcould drive global energy prices even higher as the war in ‚Äčthe Middle East enters its second week.
The Azerbaijani statement came just a ‚ÄĆday ‚Ā†after Baku vowed to retaliate for what it said was an incursion of four Iranian drones into its Nakhchivan exclave, which injured four people and damaged airport infrastructure. Iran flatly denied it ‚Äčsent the drones ‚Äčinto Azerbaijan.
Baku said ‚Ā†an investigation found two Iranian citizens and an Azerbaijani national had colluded to bring over ‚Äčseven kilograms of the C-4 explosive into Azerbaijan ‚Äčon ‚Ā†the instructions of the IRGC. Authorities have issued international arrest warrants for four people.
On Friday Azerbaijan ordered the evacuation of its ‚Ā†diplomats ‚Äčfrom Iran, citing safety concerns, amid ‚Äčalready tense relations between the two countries over Baku's ties to Israel and ‚ÄčTurkey.

item.title: Putin calls for immediate halt to Iran conflict
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 7 (Reuters) - Russian President Vladimir Putin expressed his condolences to Iran's president over the ‚Äčnumerous civilian casualties resulting from "the armed Israeli-American ‚ÄĆaggression against Iran" and called for an immediate halt to hostilities, the Kremlin said.
In a phone ‚Äčcall late on Friday with Iranian President ‚ÄčMasoud Pezeshkian, Putin expressed his deep condolences ‚Ā†over the killing of Iranian Supreme ‚ÄčLeader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, members of Khamenei's family, ‚ÄčIranian political and military leaders and "numerous civilians".
"Vladimir Putin reaffirmed Russia's principled stance in favour of an immediate ‚Äčcessation of hostilities, the rejection of force ‚Äčas a method to solve any issues surrounding Iran or ‚ÄĆarising ‚Ā†in the Middle East, and a swift return to the path of diplomatic resolution," the Kremlin said.
Putin said he was in ‚Äčconstant contact with ‚Äčthe leaders ‚Ā†of Gulf Cooperation Council member states.
"Masoud Pezeshkian expressed gratitude for Russia's ‚Äčsolidarity with the Iranian people as ‚Äčthey ‚Ā†defend their sovereignty and the independence of their country. He also provided a detailed update ‚Ā†on ‚Äčthe developments during the ‚Äčlatest active phase of the conflict," the Kremlin said.

item.title: Inside the Nepal campaign that could vault Balendra Shah to power
item.description:
KATHMANDU, March 7 (Reuters) - The campaign that swept aside Nepal's established political heavyweights and brought 35-year-old rapper-turned-politician Balendra Shah to the cusp of power emerged out of the top floors of a six-story building in west Kathmandu.
Shah is the candidate for prime minister from the ‚ÄčRastriya Swatantra Party, which is leading the vote count two days after Nepal's first election since youth-led protests in September forced the previous elected government out of power.
The final results ‚ÄĆfor 165 seats in direct, first-past-the-post tallies and 110 seats through proportional representation are expected by next week, according to the election commission.
And if current trends hold, Shah - or Balen, as he is better known - will have successfully harnessed a wave of support unleashed by the September uprising with one of the most sophisticated campaigns ever seen in the small Himalayan nation.
Reuters interviewed six RSP officials to gain insight into Shah's campaign, much of it coordinated out of the ‚Äčtop three floors of the party's headquarters in the capital city's Balaju neighbourhood and heavily bankrolled by the Nepali diaspora.
"We are overwhelmed by the support and love we received from ‚Äčpeople on the ground," said Bigyan Gautam, a member of RSP's national campaign team.
Shah's stunning performance stands in contrast with Bangladesh's election last month, where ‚Ā†a youth-driven party fizzled at the polls after emerging out of the "Gen-Z" protests that unseated the country's long-serving premier in 2024.
The heart of RSP's political machine is the Research, Strategy and ‚ÄčDocumentation Department, run by an 11-member board overseeing 300 party workers divided into three national groups that supplement smaller teams led by individual candidates.
These national-level teams plan election tactics, organise rallies, create and manage online ‚Äčcontent, and track ground-level campaigning and feedback from across Nepal, three officials said.
In the run-up to Thursday's vote, the party also followed a carefully calibrated media strategy, with Shah delivering a major speech every eight days, allowing enough time for each rally to be amplified by a 660-strong social media team.
The RSP also held road shows in five to seven districts each day, backed by daily brief appearances in one of Nepal's seven provinces where Shah would ‚Äčmeet and connect with the voters.
"If you keep giving speeches, people get confused," a party official said, asking not to be named. "We let opposition parties raise some issues, and then respond once. This way, ‚Äčour message stays clear."
The centralised campaign system and large events were funded directly by the party, which has received large donations from Nepalis living abroad, particularly from those in the United States, according to two officials.
But individual ‚ÄĆcandidates were ‚Ā†responsible for organising and financing their own campaign events, said RSP's Treasurer Lima Adhikari.
Before he joined RSP in December ahead of the polls, Shah served as the mayor of Kathmandu, a position he had won in 2022 by leveraging his popularity as one of Nepal's biggest rap stars, with millions of followers on social media.
On January 19, Shah stood with RSP founder and TV host-turned-politician Rabi Lamichhane at a rally in the capital city of Nepal's Madhesh province and told the thousands gathered: "A Madhesi boy is going to become prime minister."
Although Madhesh and the surrounding Terai plains are the most populous ‚Äčregions of Nepal, the country has long been ‚Äčdominated by political elites from Kathmandu and its ‚Ā†mountainous belt.
Shah's January speech went viral on social media, underlining the early success of the RSP's bet that he could become the first leader from the plains to secure the premiership, three party officials said.
"It was very clear to us that the nation was fed up with the old corrupt leaders ‚Äčand they were seeing hope in young leaders like Balen Shah and Rabi Lamichhane," said Birendra Kumar Mehta, a member of RSP's central committee.
"The ‚Äčparty saw this as an ‚Ā†opportunity."
Shah himself is contesting the election from Jhapa-5, a constituency in the plains that has long been part of the home turf of K.P. Sharma Oli, who quit as prime minister in the wake of the September protests.
In this rural pocket of Nepal, Shah has stuck with his unorthodox campaign playbook, shunning sit-down media interviews, making impromptu stops to talk with voters, and forming a group of youth volunteers across the constituency.
Part of ‚Ā†his team, reinforced ‚Äčby the RSP campaign in Kathmandu, has also been collecting voter feedback and complaints, including on development projects and general ‚Äčgovernance.
"People from every part of Nepal will feel that the government is theirs and most importantly, that it is there for them," said D.P. Aryal, RSP's vice chairperson, adding that Shah's administration will look to bring in outside experts to bolster ‚Äčits capacity.
"Youth engagement and the maximum integration of technology will also be among our top priorities."

item.title: Israel carries out rare airborne raid in Lebanon; 16 killed in strikes
item.description:
BEIRUT, March 7 (Reuters) - Israel's military carried out an airborne operation that ‚Äčdropped troops into a town in eastern Lebanon overnight, residents and Lebanese state media said on ‚ÄĆSaturday, as heavy Israeli strikes on the area left more than a dozen people dead.
Lebanon was plunged deeper into the conflict engulfing the Middle East on Monday when Iran-backed Hezbollah launched rockets and drones into Israel, which has responded with heavy bombardment across Lebanon's south, east and ‚Äčnear the capital Beirut.
Overnight, Israeli helicopters dropped troops near the town of Nabi Chit in Lebanon's eastern ‚ÄčBekaa Valley, Lebanese state media and residents said. Residents and Hezbollah fighters fired on the ‚Ā†troops as they moved on foot, the reports said.
The Israeli troops withdrew and left by helicopter as Israeli strikes ‚Äčpounded Nabi Chit and nearby towns, state media and residents said. Israel's military did not immediately respond to Reuters questions ‚Äčon the incident.
Lebanon's health ministry said 16 people had been killed in Israeli attacks in the last 24 hours in Nabi Chit. A spokesperson told Reuters the toll included people killed in strikes throughout Friday as well as during intense overnight bombardment, and that it was ‚Äčexpected to rise further.
Two security sources told Reuters that three army troops were among the dead.
Israeli strikes have killed ‚Äčmore than 200 people across Lebanon, and orders to evacuate have displaced around 300,000 people, only a third of whom are now ‚ÄĆliving in ‚Ā†government shelters.
Shawki al-Masri, who lives in a town adjacent to Nabi Chit, described the overnight bombing as "a night of hell".
"We heard the helicopters over our house all night - they were so low we thought they would land on us," he told Reuters.
"People in the town woke up and started shooting at them, then the warplanes started bombing. ‚ÄčIt was a very violent ‚Äčnight and only calmed ‚Ā†down when the sun came up," he said.
In a statement overnight, Hezbollah said it had fired on Israeli troops who had been dropped near Nabi Chit by four Israeli helicopters. ‚ÄčThe group said the Israeli troops withdrew.
While Israeli forces are carrying out ground operations ‚Äčin southern Lebanon, ‚Ā†pushing deeper into a strip of border villages, airborne operations are rare. In a 2024 war between Israel and Hezbollah, Israeli naval forces kidnapped a man from a Lebanese coastal town.
The United Nations on Saturday warned that the conflict was set to get "even ‚Ā†worse," ‚Äčand that talks between Israel and Lebanon "must be pursued with urgency" to ‚Äčend hostilities.
Its Special Coordinator for Lebanon, Jeanine Hennis-Plasschaert, said in a statement that it was "clear that ongoing military actions will not deliver a lasting win ‚Äčto anyone".
"Rather, they will only deepen instability and inflict further suffering," she said.

item.title: India raises cooking gas prices as Iran war hits supply
item.description:
NEW DELHI, March 7 (Reuters) - Indian companies have raised the prices of liquefied petroleum ‚Äčgas, mostly used as a cooking ‚ÄĆfuel, for the first time in about a year, as global prices surge with the U.S.-Israel war ‚Äčon Iran disrupting supplies from the ‚ÄčMiddle East.
Indian Oil Corp, the country's top ‚Ā†refiner and LPG seller, has increased ‚Äčthe prices of a 14.2-kg LPG cylinder in ‚ÄčDelhi by 7% to 913 rupees ($9.93), according to its website.
State refiners IOC, Bharat Petroleum Corp (BPCL.NS) , opens new tab and Hindustan ‚ÄčPetroleum Corp (HPCL.NS) , opens new tab raised prices in tandem.
India, the ‚Äčworld's second-biggest importer of LPG, last year consumed 33.15 ‚ÄĆmillion ‚Ā†metric tons of cooking gas, a mixture of propane and butane, with imports accounting for about two-thirds of LPG consumption. Middle ‚ÄčEastern LPG accounts ‚Äčfor ‚Ā†85% to 90% of those imports.
India on Friday asked refiners to boost ‚ÄčLPG production to avoid any shortage ‚Äčof ‚Ā†cooking gas in the country.
Indian companies have also raised the prices of 19-kg commercial LPG ‚Ā†cylinders, ‚Äčmainly used by hotels and ‚Äčrestaurants, to 1,883 rupees from 1,768.50 rupees.
($1 = 91.9280 Indian ‚Äčrupees)

item.title: White House seeks bolder action on energy prices amid Iran conflict
item.description:
March 6 (Reuters) - The White House is asking federal agencies to step up efforts ‚Äčto address soaring energy prices from the Iran conflict , signaling a concern that steps taken so far may not be enough, ‚ÄĆaccording to two people familiar with the matter.
Senior officials have asked the departments of Energy, Transportation and Treasury and the Environmental Protection Agency to provide more policy options, with an emphasis on measures that President Donald Trump could implement without congressional approval, one of the sources told Reuters.
The requests indicate that the White ‚ÄčHouse is preparing for the possibility that bolder action may be needed if oil and gas prices continue to climb. ‚ÄčPolitical analysts say higher gasoline prices could hurt Trump and his Republican Party in November's midterm elections, ‚Ā†when control of Congress is at stake.
‚ÄúObviously the White House is coordinating with the interagency on this important issue, if we were ‚Äčnot, it would be a problem. President Trump and his entire energy team have had a strong game plan to keep oil prices ‚Äčstable well before Operation Epic Fury began, and they will continue to review all credible options and execute on them when appropriate," White House spokeswoman Taylor Rogers said in a statement.
U.S. and global crude oil futures climbed above $90 a barrel on Friday, with U.S. prices rising more than 12% as Middle Eastern ‚Äčsupply remained constrained by the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz amid the expanding U.S.-Israeli war against Iran.
Gasoline prices in the U.S. have ‚Äčsurged in recent weeks, reaching levels not seen since late 2024. The national average for regular unleaded has climbed above $3.30 per gallon, while diesel has ‚ÄĆrisen to $4.26 ‚Ā†per gallon.
The White House has taken a cautious approach to intervening in energy markets, wary that an overly aggressive strategy could backfire. Officials say any broad measures must be carefully calibrated, noting that heavy-handed steps that fail to lower gasoline or crude prices could unsettle markets, erode confidence and trigger political backlash. Analysts, meanwhile, have expressed skepticism about how much the White House can do to rein in prices.
Officials ‚Äčhave discussed a wide array ‚Äčof options, including a federal ‚Ā†gasoline tax holiday and looser environmental regulations around summer gasoline that will allow higher blends of ethanol, Reuters previously reported .
The Treasury Department is weighing a plan that involves using the oil futures market, Reuters reported , but ‚Äčthere is no immediate plan to announce the move.
Trump ordered on Tuesday that the U.S. International ‚ÄčDevelopment Finance Corporation provide ‚Ā†insurance against losses stemming from political instability or conflict for maritime trade in the Gulf. The move came after oil and liquefied natural gas tanker transit ground to a halt in the Strait of Hormuz , a chokepoint for about 20% of the world's daily oil supply.
Markets met the ‚Ā†move with ‚Äčsome skepticism. Analysts question whether financial guarantees alone can offset the operational and security ‚Äčrisks posed by the growing tensions in the region.
On Friday, the administration announced it would provide reinsurance for losses up to $20 billion in the Gulf region to bolster confidence ‚Äčfor oil and gas shippers during the war with Iran.

item.title: Shipper MSC to introduce emergency fuel surcharge
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - Shipping company MSC said on Saturday it would implement an ‚Äčemergency fuel surcharge to all ‚ÄĆcargo from the Mediterranean (including West Mediterranean, Adriatic, East Mediterranean, Greece and Turkey) ‚Äčand Black Sea to the ‚ÄčIndian sub-continent, Red Sea and East ‚Ā†Africa, effective March 16.
It said ‚Äčthe surcharge would be $30 per ‚Äčtwenty-foot equivalent unit (TEU) from the Mediterranean and Black Sea to the Red Sea for ‚Äčdry containers, and $50 per TEU ‚Äčfor refrigerated containers.
Dry containers from the Mediterranean ‚ÄĆand ‚Ā†Black Sea to East Africa will be charged $60 per TEU, while refrigerated containers will be charged $90 ‚Äčper TEU, ‚Äčthe world's ‚Ā†largest carrier of ocean container cargo said.
MSC will ‚Äčalso impose a surcharge of $40 ‚Äčper ‚Ā†TEU from the Mediterranean and Black Sea to the Indian sub-continent ‚Ā†for ‚Äčdry containers, and $60 ‚Äčper TEU for refrigerated containers.

item.title: South Africa central bank to redraft risk scenarios as Iran war boosts oil price
item.description:
LONDON, March 6 (Reuters) - South Africa's central bank will redraft its risk scenarios for its next rate-setting ‚ÄĆpolicy meeting as the widening Middle East conflict pushes up oil prices, the country's central bank governor told Reuters.
The central bank is scheduled to decide on March 26 on interest rates after keeping its main lending rate unchanged at 6.75% in a split decision in late January, citing at ‚Äčthe time that policy makers wanted to see inflation expectations fall further.
"We had our baseline (in the January meeting) and ‚Äčwe had an optimistic scenario and an adverse scenario," said South African Reserve Bank Governor Lesetja ‚Ā†Kganyago. He said the adverse scenario had assumed the average oil price for the year would reach $75 per barrel and ‚Äčthe rand would weaken to 18.50 to the dollar.
Now the previous adverse scenario, he said, "is gone - it was in the past ... ‚Äčwe will come up with a completely new one."
The Middle East crisis set off by Israel and Washington's bombing of Iran lifted Brent crude futures to more than $94 per barrel this week, while the rand weakened to 16.82 to the dollar.
Kganyago said a 10% move in the exchange ‚Äčrate would have a much stronger impact on inflation in South Africa than a similar jump in oil prices.
"Yes, it's the ‚Äčadverse scenario, but it is not playing out as we had feared," Kganyago said, adding that policy makers would only get concerned once ‚ÄĆthey saw ‚Ā†the impact of the exchange rate filter through to prices.
"The call that as a policy maker you must make is - is this transitory, or is it persistent? And you only respond to the persistent, not to the transitory - and that is not an easy call to make."
The knock-on effects from global tensions that have kept markets on edge in recent months - and the ‚Äčmost recent crisis - would be ‚Äčdominating discussions at the next ‚Ā†meeting, Kganyago added, speaking on the sidelines of an investor conference in London.
"We will discuss what is the impact of this geopolitics on the markets, on oil, emerging markets, and on ‚Äčexchange rates."
Asked whether South Africa would continue to buy dollars - as it has since the ‚Äčstart of the ‚Ā†year - after the rand weakened nearly 4% this week against a strong greenback, the governor said the policy had not changed.
"If we think that there are cheap dollars available in the market, we will pick them up," he said, adding that overall reserve levels ‚Ā†had been ‚Äčbolstered not only by dollar purchases, but by the rising value of gold in ‚Äčthe central bank's war chest and the proceeds of borrowing by the Treasury.
Central bank data released earlier on Friday showed that South Africa's net foreign reserves rose ‚Äčto $75.84 billion at the end of February from $74.88 billion in January.

item.title: Flash floods in Nairobi kill 10, disrupt flights at major airport
item.description:
NAIROBI, March 7 (Reuters) - Aid workers ‚ÄĆpulled bodies from floodwaters across Nairobi on Saturday after overnight flash floods killed at least 10 people, swept away dozens of cars and ‚Äčdisrupted flights at East Africa's biggest airport, authorities said.
In ‚Äčthe industrial neighbourhood of Grogan, security guard John Lomayan, ‚Ā†34, looked at the body of an elderly man ‚Äčhe recognised - a roadside egg seller - trapped beneath a car that ‚Äčhad been washed away when the Nairobi River burst its banks.
"I saw him being carried by the water from up there," he said, gesturing ‚Äčup the road. "We didn't know where he had gone. ‚ÄčIt is only now that we see him under the car".
A Reuters reporter ‚ÄĆsaw ‚Ā†three bodies pulled from underneath cars. Police said 10 people had been confirmed dead so far.
Scientists say global warming is worsening floods and droughts across East Africa by concentrating rainfall ‚Äčinto shorter, more ‚Äčintense bursts. ‚Ā†A 2024 World Weather Attribution study found climate change had made devastating rains in the region ‚Äčtwice as likely as before.
Kenya Airways said ‚Äčthe rains ‚Ā†had disrupted flights to Nairobi and forced some to divert to the coastal city of Mombasa.
"So many cars, so much stuff, ‚Ā†I ‚Äčdon't know. Everything was just (washed away). ‚ÄčAll of the water (came) ... from that river," shocked resident Cedric Mwanza said, referring ‚Äčto the Nairobi River.

item.title: Days after Iran strikes, Trump hosts Latin American leaders with China in focus
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - President Donald Trump will host Latin American leaders in Florida on Saturday as his administration seeks to counter China's ‚Äčgrowing influence in the region, a diplomatic show just days after U.S. strikes on Iran opened a new front in the Middle ‚ÄĆEast.
The summit comes as Trump prepares for talks with Chinese President Xi Jinping in Beijing at the end of March. The Trump administration hopes to pull Latin America closer to Washington after years of growing Chinese trade, lending and infrastructure investment in the region.
Saturday's gathering, dubbed the "Shield of the Americas," also gives Trump a chance to project strength closer to home even ‚Äčas the conflict in the Middle East leads to consequences he may not fully control, such as rising prices for oil and gas.
Kristi Noem ‚Äčwill be special envoy for the "Shield of the Americas", Trump posted Thursday. Noem was Homeland Security secretary until Trump removed her ‚Ā†from that post this week after mounting criticism of her from Congress.
The summit brings together conservative leaders aligned with Trump on security, migration and economics, reflecting a ‚Äčbroader rightward shift in parts of Latin America.
Among those expected to attend are Argentine President Javier Milei, Chile's president-elect Jose Antonio Kast and Salvadoran President Nayib Bukele, ‚Äčwhose gang crackdown, criticized by human rights groups, has become a model for parts of Latin America's right.
Politicians from across the region have toured Bukele's sprawling "mega-prison," where the United States last year deported more than 200 Venezuelans without trial.
Also expected are Honduran President Nasry Asfura, who narrowly won a disputed election with Trump's backing, and Ecuador's President Daniel Noboa, who has ‚Äčechoed parts of Trump's economic agenda and recently announced joint operations with the U.S. in a military crackdown on drug trafficking.
Many of the leaders share Trump's ‚Äčhardline view of crime and migration, favoring crackdowns over deeper social fixes and private business over the state. Their rise reflects a broader rightward turn in parts of Latin America ‚ÄĆat a ‚Ā†time when the region is being pulled between Washington and Beijing.
Ryan Berg, who heads the Americas Program at the Center for Strategic and International Studies, wrote this week that the summit is the first time in Trump's second term that Washington has brought together Latin American leaders in this way.
He said the talks are likely to focus on security, drug trafficking, money laundering and China's role in ports, telecommunications and other infrastructure.
Berg said China's trade with the region hit ‚Äča record $518 billion in 2024, with ‚ÄčBeijing loaning more than $120 billion to ‚Ā†governments across the Western Hemisphere.
China's increased involvement in Latin America - from satellite tracking stations in Argentina and a port in Peru to economic support for Venezuela - has been an irritant for successive U.S. administrations.
China has expanded its reach through trade, loans ‚Äčand infrastructure, while the Trump administration has pushed governments in the region to curb Beijing's role in ports, energy ‚Äčprojects and other strategic ‚Ā†assets.
That pressure was on display recently when Panamanian authorities moved against a Hong Kong-based firm tied to operations in the Panama Canal, a key global freight channel.
Washington has also taken more direct steps in the region.
The United States captured Venezuelan President Nicolas Maduro on January 3 and moved to seize control of the country's oil exports, and ‚Ā†has tightened enforcement ‚Äčof the decades-long U.S. embargo on Cuba.
Several Trump administration officials have told Reuters that Trump's ‚Äčmove against Maduro was intended in part to counter China's ambitions, and that Beijing's days of leveraging debt to get cheap oil from Venezuela were "over."

item.title: Nigerian army kills 45 in Katsina clash
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - At least 45 "bandits" were killed in a clash ‚Äčwith Nigerian troops in ‚ÄĆthe Danmusa area of Katsina state in northern Nigeria, according ‚Äčto a statement from ‚Äčthe Katsina State Government on ‚Ā†Saturday.
The violence followed an attack ‚Äčby armed gangs of ‚Äčmen from neighboring Zamfara state who rode into Alhazawa village in Musawa ‚Äčon March 5 and ‚Äčattempted to steal cattle, the statement ‚ÄĆsaid.
The ‚Ā†attackers returned the following day and encountered troops stationed at the army's Forward Operating ‚ÄčBase ‚Äčin ‚Ā†Dan Ali near Maidabino ward, leading to a ‚Äčclash in which ‚Äčthe ‚Ā†army killed 45 of the attackers, the statement said.
A captain ‚Ā†and ‚Äčtwo soldiers ‚Äčdied in the encounter, the statement added.

item.title: Iranian hardline clerics seek swift naming of new supreme leader
item.description:
DUBAI, March 7 (Reuters) - Two influential and hardline Iranian clerics have called for the swift selection of a new supreme leader to help guide ‚Äčthe nation amid a new wave of U.S. and Israeli ‚ÄĆstrikes, Iranian media reported on Saturday.
The calls by the clerics suggest that at least some in the clerical establishment are uncomfortable with leaving a three-man council in ‚Äčcharge, even temporarily under constitutional rules, after the killing of ‚ÄčSupreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei.
U.S. President Donald Trump has said ‚Ā†the U.S. should have a role in choosing the new leader, ‚Äča demand Iran has rejected.
Naser Makarem Shirazi, a grand ayatollah, which means ‚Äčhe commands a broad following for his religious rulings, said an appointment was needed swiftly to "help better organise the country‚Äôs affairs", state media reported.
Last week, two senior Shi‚Äôite ‚Äčreligious authorities also issued fatwas, or religious decrees, calling on Muslims ‚Äčaround the world to avenge the killing of Khamenei. Makarem Shirazi said it was ‚ÄĆa ‚Ā†religious duty for Muslims "until the evil of these criminals is eradicated from the world."
Grand Ayatollah Hossein Nouri Hamedani also urged members of the Assembly of Experts, a clerical body charged with choosing the new leader, ‚Äčto accelerate the ‚Äčprocess of picking ‚Ā†Khamenei's successor, state media reported.
Following rules laid out in Iran's constitution, a three-man council comprising the president, a ‚Äčsenior cleric and the head of the judiciary, ‚Äčhas taken ‚Ā†on the supreme leader's role until the Assembly of Experts decides.
The constitution states a supreme leader should be chosen within three months, although with ‚Ā†war raging, ‚Äčit is not immediately clear how quickly ‚Äčthe 88-member Assembly of Experts can convene. Sources have said some clerics have held some ‚Äčconsultations online.

item.title: Ukraine's interceptor drone makers look at exports to the Gulf as Iran war flares
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - Ukrainian manufacturers of cheap interceptor drones designed to knock out enemy unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) say they have the capacity to export in large volumes, amid enquiries from the United ‚ÄčStates and Middle East prompted by the Iran war .
Hundreds of drones based on Iran's Shahed model and now made in Russia fill Ukraine's skies during frequent attacks, ‚ÄĆand many are downed by air defences including Western missiles, fighter jets, truck-mounted guns and interceptor drones.
Now, as Iran launches drones at U.S. allies across the Gulf and beyond in response to intense U.S. and Israeli strikes, Ukraine is hoping the Middle East crisis will increase its leverage with allies by showing them the expertise it has built during four years of Russia's full-scale invasion could be invaluable to partners' own long-term security.
With missiles sometimes costing millions of dollars each, ‚Äčand in short supply as Western armies scramble to bolster their own defences, interceptors are seen as an efficient and cost-effective way to counter attacks by relatively cheap ‚Äčdrones.
The United States and its allies in the Middle East have swiftly sought Ukraine's help with supplying such interceptors.
President Volodymyr Zelenskiy said on Thursday ‚Ā†Ukraine would provide assistance in response to a U.S. request for help and said earlier that Middle Eastern nations had approached Kyiv.
Zelenskiy did not specify what this would entail, but a source familiar with ‚Äčthe matter told Reuters that the United States and Qatar were in talks to purchase Ukrainian interceptor drones.
SkyFall, a major manufacturer of drones including interceptors, said its manufacturing capacity ‚Äčhad outgrown Ukraine's ability to purchase its systems and the company was ready to export.
"We have had interest and inquiries from our (allies) and countries in the Middle East," said Ares, a representative of SkyFall who wore a balaclava as he spoke to hide his identity.
Ares spoke on condition that only his call-sign be used.
SkyFall says its P1-SUN interceptor has downed more than 1,500 Shaheds and 1,000 other drones since it entered operations four months ago.
"The ‚Äčcompany is ready to provide any necessary assistance if we get the green light from our government," he said, adding that it would only do so if it did not affect Ukraine's ‚Äčability to defend itself.
Ihor Fedirko, CEO of UCDI, Ukraine's state-backed arms manufacturers' association, estimated that manufacturers of interceptor UAVs and other counter-drone systems were making about twice as many as the Ukrainian military needed to ‚ÄĆdeal with Russia's frequent ‚Ā†barrages .
SkyFall asked that the location of its factory not be disclosed. During a recent visit, Reuters reporters saw long rows of 3D printers churning out plastic drone components and halls of workers soldering and assembling drones.
The company estimated it could manufacture up to 50,000 interceptor drones a month and export 5,000 to 10,000 without impinging on Ukraine's needs.
Although Ukraine does not shoot down every drone fired by Russia, this is not always down to munition availability and can instead be a question of co-ordination.
Kyiv is striving to better layer its air defences in order to allow fewer targets ‚Äčthrough, and sees boosting interceptor drone use as ‚Äča key piece of the puzzle.
In February, ‚Ā†they were responsible for 70% of the drones downed in and around Kyiv, according to Ukraine's military.
Most Ukrainian interceptor drones cost a few thousand dollars or less. In comparison, PAC-3 missiles used in the coveted U.S. Patriot air defence system, can cost $4 million each and ‚Äčtend to be used to bring down enemy missiles.
Shahed-136 drones, designed by Iran and now mass-manufactured by Russia, are estimated to cost between $50,000 ‚Äčand $100,000 each.
SkyFall markets the ‚Ā†P1-SUN as a highly cost-effective option. Depending on the model's specifications, it charges Ukraine's military about $1,000 per drone.
"If we are talking about export and ... assistance to partners, then the price will most likely be higher," Ares said, although he believed it would still represent the cheapest option.
Industry representatives said one of the biggest obstacles to using Ukrainian interceptor drones in the Gulf was pilots rather than drones. Ukraine is ‚Ā†the only ‚Äčcountry with drone crews who know how to use these systems in combat.
SkyFall has its own academy that provides ‚Äča three-week course to new pilots, and the company said it would be ready to send instructors abroad in the event that Ukraine's government allowed it to sell drones to other countries.
It also said it has developed capabilities to pilot ‚Äčits drones remotely, meaning drones could potentially be flown in the Gulf from a screen in Ukraine.

item.title: China's Xi says the military must be politically loyal, root out corruption
item.description:
SHANGHAI, March 7 (Reuters) - Chinese President Xi Jinping said on ‚ÄčSaturday the military must ‚ÄĆbe loyal to the Communist Party and root out ‚Äčcorruption, according to a ‚Äčreport by the official Xinhua ‚Ā†news agency.
"There is ‚Äčno place for corruption to ‚Äčhide," said Xi.
"The fight against corruption must move forward."
The remarks ‚Äčcome amid the annual ‚Äčmeetings of the nation's top political ‚ÄĆbodies, ‚Ā†where officials unveiled this year's economic targets.
Two senior Chinese officials were absent ‚Äčon ‚ÄčWednesday from ‚Ā†the ranks of China's highest tier ‚Äčof power at ‚Äčthe ‚Ā†opening of the annual meetings, with top Communist Party ‚Ā†cadres ‚Äčensnared in ‚Äča wave of purges .

item.title: Lebanon's Hezbollah warns residents in Israel's north to evacuate
item.description:
TEL AVIV, March 7 (Reuters) - Lebanese Shi'ite militant group Hezbollah on Saturday warned ‚Äčresidents of a northern Israeli city near ‚ÄĆthe border with Lebanon to evacuate and head south.
Hezbollah did not specify what action, if any, it ‚Äčplanned to take against the city, ‚Äčwhich lies only a few kilometres from ‚Ā†the border.
"Warning. All residents of Kiryat Shmona ‚Äčare asked to evacuate immediately. Head south," it ‚Äčsaid in a statement.
Lebanon was pulled into the widening U.S.-Israel war with Iran on Monday after Hezbollah, an ‚ÄčIranian-aligned group, fired rockets and drones into ‚ÄčIsrael. Israel responded with heavy strikes across Lebanon's south, ‚ÄĆeast ‚Ā†and near Beirut.
Tens of thousands of Israelis living in the north evacuated their homes after war broke out in Gaza between Israel and ‚ÄčPalestinian group ‚ÄčHamas in ‚Ā†2023, as Hezbollah began firing rockets and other projectiles at northern ‚Äčcommunities.
Residents returned after a November 2024 ‚Äčceasefire ‚Ā†between Israel and Hezbollah. Israeli Defence Minister Israel Katz has this week repeatedly urged Israelis ‚Ā†in ‚Äčthe north to remain in ‚Äčtheir homes, saying the military will protect them.

item.title: Trump says Iran will be 'hit very hard' on Saturday
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - U.S. President ‚ÄčDonald Trump said Iran ‚ÄĆwould be "hit very hard" on Saturday and that he ‚Äčwas considering widening the ‚Äčareas and groups of people ‚Ā†being, targeted, without providing ‚Äčdetails.
"Today Iran will be ‚Äčhit very hard! Under serious consideration for complete destruction and certain ‚Äčdeath, because of Iran's ‚Äčbad behavior, are areas and groups ‚ÄĆof ‚Ā†people that were not considered for targeting up until this moment in time" ‚ÄčTrump ‚Äčsaid ‚Ā†in a post on Truth Social.
He also ‚Äčnoted that Iran had ‚Äčapologised ‚Ā†to its neighbours for its strikes against them, ‚Ā†which ‚Äčhe cast as ‚Äča surrender.

item.title: Pope Leo names former envoy to Duterte's Philippines as ambassador to US
item.description:
VATICAN CITY, March 7 (Reuters) - Pope Leo on Saturday named a ‚Äčsenior Church diplomat who represented the Vatican in the Philippines during former President Rodrigo ‚ÄĆDuterte 's war on drugs as the Vatican's U.S. ambassador and envoy to the Trump administration.
Italian Archbishop Gabriele Caccia, 68, served for two years in Manila before becoming the Vatican's U.N. ambassador in 2020.
His appointment follows Vatican ‚Äčcriticism of U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran , with the Vatican's top diplomat, Cardinal Pietro Parolin, saying ‚Äčon Wednesday that the military campaign had undermined international law .
Leo, the first U.S. ‚Ā†pope, has also openly criticised some of Trump's right-wing policies. He has described Trump's immigration crackdown, ‚Äčwhich has convulsed cities across the United States, as " inhuman ."
Caccia, widely seen as one of the more ‚Äčexperienced diplomats in the Vatican's foreign service, will arrive in Washington as some U.S. bishops have ramped up criticism of Trump.
He will replace Cardinal Christophe Pierre, 80, who is retiring and had been the Vatican's U.S. ‚Äčambassador since 2016.
Three top U.S. cardinals in January decried the direction of American foreign policy, saying ‚Äčthe country's "moral role in confronting evil around the world" was in question.
In ‚ÄĆManila, ‚Ā†Caccia was known for tamping down tensions between local Catholic bishops and Duterte, who frequently attacked Church officials in speeches.
Duterte, who left office in 2022, is being held in The Hague over murders committed during his war on drugs .
The Vatican, which maintains about 110 embassies worldwide, considers Washington one ‚Äčof its most important ‚Äčdiplomatic postings.
The ambassador ‚Ā†must represent the Church's priorities at the White House, and envoys over the decades have contested policies of various U.S. administrations.
The Vatican's U.S. ambassador ‚Äčalso plays a sweeping role inside the national Church. He is ‚Äčresponsible for recommending ‚Ā†to the pope U.S. priests who could serve as bishops, shaping the lives of the country's 72 million Catholics.
Caccia, originally from Milan, has previously served as the Vatican's ambassador to Lebanon and ‚Ā†spent seven ‚Äčyears as the number-three official in the Vatican's powerful ‚ÄčSecretariat of State.
The Vatican and the U.S. formally established diplomatic relations in 1984 under President Ronald Reagan. None of the ‚Äčprevious six Vatican ambassadors in Washington had served first at the United Nations.

item.title: Trump cannot end protections for 350,000 Haitians, US appeals court rules
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - A divided U.S. appeals court has refused to let the Trump administration revoke legal protections that allow more than 350,000 Haitians to live and ‚Äčwork in the U.S. and avoid being returned to their gang-violence -stricken country.
A 2-1 ‚ÄĆpanel of the U.S. Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit late on Friday rejected , opens new tab the administration's bid to pause a February 2 ruling that blocked the U.S. Department of Homeland ‚ÄčSecurity from ending Haiti's Temporary Protected Status.
TPS is a humanitarian program that shields ‚Äčeligible migrants from deportation and allows them to work.
Under outgoing Homeland ‚Ā†Security Secretary Kristi Noem, the department has moved to end TPS for a dozen ‚Äčcountries as part of President Donald Trump's immigration crackdown, arguing the program was never ‚Äčintended to serve as a "de facto amnesty."
The administration had asked the D.C. Circuit to stay U.S. District Judge Ana Reyes' February order while it appeals. Her decision came in a class-action lawsuit brought by ‚ÄčHaitians seeking to prevent DHS from exposing them to deportation.
Reyes found that Noem's November ‚Äčmove to end the Haitians' legal protections likely violated TPS termination procedures and the U.S. Constitution's ‚ÄĆFifth ‚Ā†Amendment guarantee of equal protection under the law.
The administration on appeal noted that the U.S. Supreme Court had twice allowed it to end TPS for Venezuelans.
But U.S. Circuit Judges Florence Pan and Brad Garcia, both appointed by Democratic President Joe Biden, distinguished the cases ‚Äčand said Haitians sent ‚Äčhome would "be vulnerable ‚Ā†to violence amid a 'collapsing rule of law' and lack access to life-sustaining medical care."
U.S. Circuit Judge Justin Walker, a Trump appointee, ‚Äčdissented, saying the case and the earlier Supreme Court litigation involving ‚ÄčVenezuelans were "the ‚Ā†legal equivalent of fraternal, if not identical, twins."
DHS did not respond to a request for comment.
Haitians were first granted TPS in 2010 after a devastating earthquake. The U.S. has ‚Ā†repeatedly renewed ‚Äčthe designation, most recently under the Biden administration ‚Äčin July 2024.
At that time, DHS cited Haiti's "simultaneous economic, security, political, and health crises", driven by gangs and ‚Äčthe absence of a functioning government.

item.title: Turkey considering deployment of F-16s to Cyprus, ministry source says
item.description:
ANKARA, March 7 (Reuters) - Turkey is considering ‚Äčthe deployment of ‚ÄĆF-16 aircraft to Cyprus, a Turkish ‚Äčdefence ministry ‚Äčsource said on ‚Ā†Saturday.
The person said ‚Äčsuch a ‚Äčmove was one of the steps being ‚Äčconsidered amid ‚Äčthe phased planning underway ‚ÄĆto ‚Ā†ensure the security of the breakaway Turkish Cypriot ‚Äčstate ‚Äčin ‚Ā†the north of the ‚Äčisland, as ‚Äčconflict ‚Ā†spreads in the region.

item.title: EU looks to soften energy bill pressures for industry, document shows
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 7 (Reuters) - The European Union is examining energy ‚ÄĆtaxes, network charges and carbon costs as possible areas for short-term measures to ease pressure on industries hit by high energy prices, a document seen by Reuters showed.
Brussels is looking ‚Äčfor quick fixes after companies warned they cannot compete with rivals in ‚ÄčChina and the U.S. - even before this week's surge in oil ‚Ā†and gas prices sparked by the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran.
European Commission President ‚ÄčUrsula von der Leyen has pledged to present options for EU leaders to consider at ‚Äča summit on 19 March.
A Commission paper prepared for a meeting of EU Commissioners on Friday showed the bloc is exploring short-term measures to help the hardest-hit regions and sectors, without ‚Äčundermining longer-term climate laws meant to shift Europe to a cheaper, low-carbon energy ‚Äčsystem.
"Any proposal for legislative change will not deliver immediately and a bridge solution may be ‚ÄĆneeded ‚Ā†to reduce energy prices in the next 2-5 years until the clean transition eases pressure on power prices as already seen in some regions," said the document, seen by Reuters.
The paper said the Commission would look at network charges - which make ‚Äčup about 18% ‚Äčof industrial power ‚Ā†bills - and national taxes and levies, as well as carbon costs, which account for around 11% of bills.
It noted that ‚Äčgovernments are underusing existing tools to cut companies' energy bills, ‚Äčincluding state ‚Ā†aid to offset carbon costs and contracts for difference that guarantee industrial consumers a stable power price.
The document said that if energy supplies are disrupted further, Brussels must ‚Ā†be ‚Äčready to introduce measures to encourage consumers to ‚Äčuse less energy, as it did in 2022 when Russia slashed gas deliveries.
A Commission spokesperson did not ‚Äčimmediately respond to a request for comment.

item.title: Kuwait cuts oil production as precaution amid Iran tensions, KPC says
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - Kuwait said it had implemented a ‚Äčprecautionary reduction in crude ‚ÄĆoil production and refining throughput following the ‚Äčongoing attacks by ‚ÄčIran against Kuwait and "Iranian threats ‚Ā†to safe passage ‚Äčof ships through the ‚ÄčStrait of Hormuz," Kuwait Petroleum Corporation (KPC) said in a ‚Äčstatement on Saturday.
The ‚Äčstate oil company said the ‚ÄĆmove ‚Ā†was part of its "risk management and business continuity strategy."
It said ‚Äčthe adjustment ‚Äčwas ‚Ā†strictly precautionary and would be ‚Äčreviewed as the ‚Äčsituation ‚Ā†develops, and it remained ready to restore ‚Ā†production ‚Äčlevels once conditions ‚Äčallow.

item.title: Trump announces new military coalition to 'eradicate cartels' in Western Hemisphere
item.description: March 7 (Reuters) - U.S. President ‚ÄčDonald ‚ÄĆTrump on Saturday ‚Äčannounced ‚Äča new ‚Ā†military ‚Äčcoalition ‚Äčto "eradicate cartels" in the ‚ÄčWestern ‚ÄčHemisphere and ‚ÄĆsaid ‚Ā†17 nations had formally ‚Äčentered ‚Äčthe ‚Ā†alliance.

item.title: Trump says Cuba negotiating deal with him and Rubio
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - U.S. ‚ÄčPresident Donald Trump said ‚ÄĆon Saturday that Cuba wants to make a ‚Äčdeal and is ‚Äčnegotiating with him and ‚Ā†Secretary of State Marco ‚ÄčRubio.
Speaking at the "Shield ‚Äčof the Americas" gathering of Latin American leaders in Miami, ‚ÄčFlorida, Trump said ‚ÄčCuba was "at the end of ‚ÄĆthe ‚Ā†line."
"They want to negotiate, and they are negotiating with Marco (Rubio) and ‚Äčmyself ‚Äčand ‚Ā†some others, and I would think ‚Äča deal would ‚Äčbe ‚Ā†made very easily with Cuba," he said.

item.title: Sweden says ship¬†seized in Baltic Sea had false flag, one crew member notified of crime
item.description:
STOCKHOLM, March 7 (Reuters) - A ship seized by Sweden in its Baltic Sea waters sailed ‚Äčunder a false flag and was suspected ‚ÄĆof violating maritime law and the national ship safety act because of its lack of ‚Äčseaworthiness, the Swedish police said on ‚ÄčSaturday.
One crew member was notified about the ‚Ā†suspected breach of the national and ‚Äčinternational laws, Johan Andersson, deputy head of police's ‚ÄčNational Operations Department, told a press conference.
Police and coast guard on Friday took control of Guinea-flagged Caffa ‚Äčoff southern Sweden, saying it had unclear ‚Äčflag status and was thus suspected of being stateless, ‚ÄĆand ‚Ā†that they had launched a probe into suspected violation of maritime law.
"Our investigation so far strengthens our suspicions and our ‚Äčopinion that this ‚Äčship ‚Ā†has extensive sea safety deficiencies," Andersson said on Saturday.
"We also have ‚Äčinformation that the vessel would be ‚Äčon ‚Ā†the Ukraine sanctions list," he said.
Most of the 11 crew members on Caffa, which ‚Ā†according ‚Äčto ship-tracking service MarineTraffic ‚Äčis a 96-meter general cargo ship, were Russian, Andersson said.

item.title: Flavio Bolsonaro draws even with Lula in Brazil election matchup, Datafolha shows
item.description:
SAO PAULO, March 7 (Reuters) - A second-round runoff between Brazilian President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva and Senator Flavio Bolsonaro would ‚Äčresult in a technical tie, according to a Datafolha ‚ÄĆpoll released on Saturday.
The poll shows that if the runoff were held today, Lula would receive 46% of the vote, compared with ‚Äč43% for Flavio, the eldest son of former President ‚ÄčJair Bolsonaro.
If Lula were to face off against Tarcisio ‚Ā†de Freitas, governor of the state of Sao Paulo, ‚Äč45% of respondents said they would vote for Lula in ‚Äčthe second round, while 42% would vote for de Freitas, also a technical tie.
Brazil's general election is scheduled for October 4. If no presidential ‚Äčcandidate receives a majority of the vote, the two ‚Äčcandidates with the most votes will face off in a runoff on ‚ÄĆOctober ‚Ā†25.
According to the survey, Lula, 80, who will be vying for his fourth presidential term, leads in all first‚ÄĎround scenarios. However, Flavio‚Äôs candidacy has gained strength since his name was put ‚Äčforward in December ‚Äčby Jair ‚Ā†Bolsonaro, who is in jail.
Lula and Flavio, 44, also have the highest rejection rates in the ‚Äčpoll. Some 46% of respondents said they ‚Äčwould not ‚Ā†vote for Lula under any circumstances, while 45% said the same about Flavio - another technical tie.
Datafolha interviewed 2,004 voters from ‚Ā†Tuesday to ‚ÄčThursday in 137 municipalities. The survey ‚Äčhas a margin of error of two percentage points in either direction.

item.title: UAE president says country is well and is no easy prey in first public comments since Iran strikes
item.description:
DUBAI, March 7 (Reuters) - The president of the United Arab Emirates said ‚Äčhis nation was in a ‚ÄĆtime of war but was well and told his enemies it was ‚Äčno easy prey, in his ‚Äčfirst public comments since Iran ‚Ā†launched missiles at its Gulf ‚Äčneighbour amid U.S.-Israeli strikes.
"The UAE has ‚Äčthick skin and bitter flesh - we are no easy prey," Mohammed bin ‚ÄčZayed Al Nahyan, who is ‚Äčalso ruler of Abu Dhabi, said in ‚ÄĆcomments, ‚Ā†made on Friday when visiting those injured in strikes, aired on Abu Dhabi TV on Saturday.
"We ‚Äčwill carry ‚Äčout ‚Ā†our duty towards our country, our people, and ‚Äčour residents who are also ‚Äčpart ‚Ā†of our family," he said.
The UAE, which consists of seven ‚Ā†emirates ‚Äčincluding Dubai, would protect ‚Äčeveryone in the country, he said.

item.title: Associate of liquidated Brazilian lender Banco Master's owner dies, lawyers say
item.description:
SAO PAULO, March 7 (Reuters) - Luiz Phillipi Mourao, an associate of lender Banco Master's owner Daniel Vorcaro, died ‚Äčon Friday night, according to a statement ‚ÄĆreleased by his attorneys on Saturday.
Mourao was declared brain dead after a suicide attempt, police said on Wednesday, when he ‚Äčwas among those arrested in the latest ‚Äčphase of the investigation involving the liquidated ‚Ā†Master.
Master, which held under 1% of Brazil's banking ‚Äčassets, was liquidated in November due to what the ‚Äčcentral bank called a severe liquidity crisis, sharp financial deterioration and serious rule violations.
The investigation into the lender's unraveling ‚Äčhas exposed a network of influence and conflicts ‚Äčof interest shaking trust in some of Brazil's most powerful ‚ÄĆinstitutions.
Vorcaro ‚Ā†was arrested on Wednesday, accused of bribing two central bank officials and also plotting to attack and intimidate people he perceived as acting against his interests ‚Äčwith Mourao, ‚Äčwhom he ‚Ā†called "Sicario" after the hit men employed by Mexican cartels.
Vorcaro's lawyers said they denied ‚Äčthe allegations, and they expected to ‚Äčdemonstrate "the ‚Ā†regularity of his conduct."
Minas Gerais state civil police said on Saturday that Mourao‚Äôs body was sent to ‚Ā†forensic ‚Äčscientists for examination and would ‚Äčbe released to his family after tests are completed.

item.title: Israeli settler fatally shoots Palestinian man in West Bank, health ministry says
item.description:
RAMALLAH, West Bank March 7 (Reuters) - An Israeli settler shot dead a 27-year-old ‚ÄčPalestinian man in the occupied ‚ÄĆWest Bank on Saturday, the Palestinian Health Ministry said.
The man was killed in ‚Äčthe village area of Masafer ‚ÄčYatta, near the Palestinian city of ‚Ā†Hebron, according to the Health ‚ÄčMinistry and the official Palestinian news ‚Äčagency WAFA.
The Israeli military did not immediately respond to a request for comment on ‚Äčthe incident.
Masafer Yatta is among ‚Äčthe sites where Palestinians have been increasingly displaced ‚ÄĆby ‚Ā†settler violence that has risen sharply over the past two years, according to the United Nations.
Two Palestinian brothers ‚Äčwere shot ‚Äčdead ‚Ā†by an Israeli on Monday near the city of ‚ÄčNablus in the northern West ‚ÄčBank, ‚Ā†the Palestinian Health Ministry said.
Israeli newspaper Haaretz said the military police ‚Ā†were ‚Äčinvestigating a reserve soldier ‚Äčfor Monday's shooting.

item.title: Indian Oil Corp books some oil cargoes from Red Sea, source says
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - Top Indian refiner, Indian Oil Corp (IOC.NS) , opens new tab , has booked some oil cargoes for loading ‚Äčfrom the Red Sea port of Yanbu, ‚ÄĆa company source said on Saturday.
The U.S.-Israel war with Iran has disrupted oil and natural gas ‚Äčexports from the Middle East, while ‚Äčtop oil exporter Saudi Arabia is increasing shipments ‚Ā†from the Red Sea as an alternative.

item.title: Earthquake of magnitude 5.5 strikes Indonesia's southern Sumatra region, GFZ says
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - An earthquake ‚Äčof magnitude ‚ÄĆ5.5 struck ‚ÄčIndonesia's ‚Äčsouthern Sumatra region ‚Ā†on ‚ÄčSunday, the ‚ÄčGerman Research Center ‚Äčfor ‚ÄčGeosciences (GFZ) said.
The quake ‚ÄĆwas ‚Ā†at a depth of ‚Äč101 ‚Äčkm (62.76 ‚Ā†miles), ‚ÄčGFZ said.

item.title: Over 150 Iranian nationals leave Lebanon including diplomats, Lebanese security source says
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - More than 150 Iranian nationals, including ‚Äčdiplomats and their families, ‚ÄĆleft Lebanon on Saturday, a senior Lebanese security ‚Äčsource told Reuters, ‚Äčafter an Israeli military ‚Ā†spokesperson threatened "representatives" of Iran in ‚ÄčLebanon.
The security source ‚Äčsaid they were being flown to Russia on a Russian ‚Äčplane, and that ‚Äčanother 20 Iranians had left ‚ÄĆon ‚Ā†Friday following the start of a new war between Lebanese armed ‚Äčgroup ‚ÄčHezbollah ‚Ā†and Israel. An Israeli military spokesperson ‚Äčon Tuesday told ‚Äčrepresentatives ‚Ā†of the Iranian government "still in Lebanon to ‚Ā†leave ‚Äčimmediately before ‚Äčthey are targeted."

item.title: Venezuela's Rodriguez commits to dialogue in message to Trump
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - Venezuela's acting president, Delcy Rodriguez, said on Saturday that "diplomatic dialogue" ‚Äčwith the U.S. was the way ‚ÄĆto resolve the two countries' differences, after both governments agreed on diplomatic and consular relations.
"We reiterate our willingness ‚Äčto build long-term relations based on ‚Äčmutual respect, equality, and international law, with ‚Ā†a view to promoting a work agenda ‚Äčthat strengthens cooperation for the benefit of both ‚Äčcountries," Rodriguez said in a post on X addressed to U.S. President Donald Trump.
The U.S. State Department announced ‚Äčon Thursday that the two countries would formally ‚Äčre-establish diplomatic ties.
Caracas severed diplomatic relations with Washington in 2019 ‚ÄĆafter ‚Ā†the first Trump administration refused to recognize Venezuela's then-President Nicolas Maduro as the country's legitimate leader, following a disputed election, and instead recognized ‚Äčan opposition ‚Äčlawmaker as ‚Ā†the country's president.
U.S. forces captured Maduro in January after months of ‚Äčheightened tensions between the two countries, ‚Äčsetting ‚Ā†off a chain of changes in Venezuela, including the swearing-in of interim President Delcy Rodriguez.
The two ‚Ā†have ‚Äčsince gradually resumed bilateral relations, ‚Äčafter Rodriguez's interim government expressed interest in rebuilding ties with ‚ÄčWashington.

item.title: ADNOC says it is managing offshore output, onshore operations continue
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - Abu Dhabi National Oil Company said on Saturday it is actively managing offshore output levels to ‚Äčaddress storage requirements amid the U.S.-Isaeli war on Iran, ‚ÄĆwhile its onshore operations are continuing.
"This approach preserves operational flexibility and will enable the company to resume normal operations without prolonged delay," ADNOC said in ‚Äča statement.
The war, now in its eighth day, has ‚Äčblocked shipments through the Strait of Hormuz, the crucial waterway ‚Ā†responsible for roughly 20% of global oil and LNG supply.
Analysts ‚Äčhave predicted the UAE and Saudi Arabia would soon have to cut ‚Äčoutput as their oil storage fills up.
ADNOC said its operations are continuing, and that it is using export capacity that bypasses the strait as well ‚Äčas international storage facilities to ensure supply continuity to global markets.
The ‚ÄčUAE can bypass the strait using the Abu Dhabi Crude Oil Pipeline, also ‚ÄĆknown ‚Ā†as the Habshan-Fujairah Pipeline.
The pipeline, which has a capacity of 1.5 million to 1.8 million barrels per day, transports oil from Abu Dhabi's fields directly to the port of Fujairah on the ‚ÄčGulf of Oman, ‚Äčfeeding its storage ‚Ā†terminals and refining facilities.
"Business units are assessing the situation on a product-by-product and transaction-by-transaction basis, considering ‚Äčthe ongoing disruption that is affecting shipping through the ‚ÄčStrait ‚Ā†of Hormuz," it said.
ADNOC has activated well-established protocols and is working closely with authorities to protect its people, assets and operations, it added.
Kuwait ‚Ā†Petroleum ‚ÄčCorporation began cutting oil output on Saturday ‚Äčand declared force majeure, adding to earlier oil and gas reductions from Iraq and ‚ÄčQatar.

item.title: Earthquake of magnitude 5.2 strikes Afghanistan's Hindu Kush region, GFZ says
item.description: March 7 (Reuters) - An earthquake ‚Äčof magnitude ‚ÄĆ5.2 struck ‚ÄčAfghanistan's ‚ÄčHindu Kush region ‚Ā†on ‚ÄčSaturday, the ‚ÄčGerman Research Center for ‚ÄčGeosciences (GFZ) ‚Äčsaid. The quake ‚ÄĆwas ‚Ā†at a depth ‚Äčof ‚Äč206 ‚Ā†km (128 miles), ‚ÄčGFZ ‚Äčsaid.

item.title: Cracks emerge in Iran's leadership
item.description:
DUBAI, March 7 (Reuters) - Iran's hierarchy is showing signs of fracturing over a war its leaders see as existential, with angry divisions between hardliners and more pragmatic factions laid bare by a row over President Masoud Pezeshkian's promise not to strike Gulf states.
Fissures within Iran's ruling elite were ‚Äčlong suppressed under the iron rule of Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, but his killing a week ago has allowed them to spill out into the open as ‚ÄĆU.S. and Israeli strikes pile pressure on Tehran.
The unrelenting bombardment mortally imperils the Islamic Republic and has prompted its fiercest acolytes, the Revolutionary Guards , to seize a bigger role in strategy despite a decapitation campaign that has killed many top commanders.
Sources close to Iran's leadership, speaking from inside the country, told Reuters the strains were starting to show among leading figures still alive after a series of killings in the U.S.-Israeli strikes. They spoke anonymously due to the ‚Äčsensitivity of the matter.
In a sign of the growing stresses to the system, clerics are accelerating the appointment of a new supreme leader with a decision possible on Sunday - though ‚Äčit is far from clear if Khamenei's successor will wield enough authority to stamp out factional disputes.
While his son, Mojtaba Khamenei , is seen as a frontrunner ‚Ā†backed by the Guards and his father's powerful office, he is untested, junior to most of Iran's senior ayatollahs, and has alienated moderates within the system.
Other potential candidates could struggle to uphold ‚Äčthe unquestioning obedience of the Guards required to maintain discipline within the system.
"Wartime tends to clarify power structures, and in this case the decisive voice is not that of the civilian leadership but of ‚Äčthe IRGC," said Alex Vatanka, a senior fellow at the Middle East Institute, using an abbreviation of the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps.
Pezeshkian's apology to Gulf states for a week-long blitz of their territory - and his pledge to rein in such attacks - quickly prompted pushback from hardliners in the Revolutionary Guards and clerical elite, forcing him into a partial climbdown.
In one of the most open criticisms of Pezeshkian - and a sign of ‚Äčinternal division, hardline cleric and lawmaker Hamid Rasai addressed the president on social media, saying: "your stance was unprofessional, weak and unacceptable."
When the president later repeated his earlier statement on social media, he ‚Äčleft out the apology that had so angered the Guards and other hardliners - an embarrassing retreat.
To be sure, all senior figures within the hierarchy are steadfast in their commitment to defending the Islamic Republic and its revolutionary ‚ÄĆtheocracy from ‚Ā†U.S. and Israeli attacks, but there are clear splits over their strategic approach.
Iran's leadership has sometimes played up differences between hardliners and moderates as a tactic in negotiations with the West, but the dispute over Pezeshkian's statement on Saturday revealed genuine divisions, two senior sources said.
A hardliner close to Khamenei's office, which remains a central node in the hierarchy, told Reuters that Pezeshkian's comments had angered many senior commanders in the Guards.
Another senior Iranian source, a moderate former official, said nobody would be able to fill Khamenei's shoes, describing the late leader as a formidable strategist who had led Iran through ‚Äčmany difficult periods.
With anxiety increasing in Iran's top ‚Äčranks, senior ayatollahs began to publicly urge that ‚Ā†the clerical body responsible for appointing a supreme leader accelerate its work.
"It should expedite the process so that it leads to the disappointment of the enemy and the preservation of the unity and solidarity of the nation," Ayatollah Nouri Hamedani said in a statement carried by the semi-official Fars ‚ÄčNews Agency.
In Iran's unusual system, an elected president, government and parliament are subservient to a clerically appointed ayatollah ‚Äčwho wields ultimate authority as ‚Ā†supreme leader and personally oversees the Revolutionary Guards and other powerful bodies of state.
As leader for 36 years, Khamenei often played hardline and moderate factions within the ruling system against each other while retaining the ultimate say, allowing them to voice disagreements so long as they bowed to his writ.
When he died, leadership formally passed to a constitutionally mandated interim council that included Pezeshkian, the clerical head of the judiciary ‚Ā†and another cleric ‚Äčfrom a hardline body called the Guardian Council.
In Khamenei's absence, strains are showing even inside that tight body, with ‚Äčthe judiciary chief, noted hardliner Ayatollah Gholamhossein Mohseni-Ejei, saying some regional states had allowed their territory to be used for attacks.
"Heavy strikes on those targets will continue," he said, contradicting Pezeshkian's more conciliatory statement.
Still, even though Khamenei did sometimes allow moderate ‚Äčor reformist voices to carry the day in disputes with hardliners, they were usually overruled when the system seemed to come under threat.

item.title: As Iran's retaliation began, US officials scrambled to arrange evacuations
item.description:
