He said ‚Äčthat Ukraine is ready ‚Äčto sell ‚Ā†the interceptor drones that are not used by the Ukrainian military, and the first group of Ukrainian ‚Äčmilitary instructors will head to the Middle East ‚Äčon Monday.
"They ‚Ā†are coming with the means to help," Zelenskiy noted, adding that Ukraine would provide the expertise and experience in defending against "Shahed" model drones ‚Ā†and cruise ‚Äčmissiles.
Ukrainian manufacturers of cheap interceptor drones ‚Äčdesigned to knock out enemy unmanned aerial vehicles have said they have the capacity to export ‚Äčin large volumes.

item.title: Two killed, 12 injured after projectile fell on residential location in Saudi Arabia
item.description:
CAIRO, March 8 (Reuters) - Two people were ‚Äčkilled and 12 ‚ÄĆinjured after a projectile fell on ‚Äča residential ‚Äčlocation in Saudi Arabia's ‚Ā†Al-Kharj city ‚Äčon Sunday, the ‚ÄčSaudi Civil Defense said.
The two people killed ‚Äčwere of ‚ÄčIndian and Bangladeshi nationalities, ‚ÄĆit ‚Ā†added in a post on X.
Iran's Revolutionary Guards ‚Äčsaid ‚Äčearlier ‚Ā†on Sunday that they ‚Äčtargeted radar ‚Äčsystems ‚Ā†in locations including Saudi's Al-Kharj.

item.title: Israeli strike kills two Palestinians in a car in Gaza, medics say
item.description:
CAIRO, March 8 (Reuters) - An Israeli air strike killed at least two Palestinians in Gaza City on Sunday, local health officials ‚Äčsaid, the deadliest incident in Gaza since Israel and ‚ÄĆthe United States launched their war against Iran a week ago.
Medics said the two people were travelling in a car near Al-Azhar University ‚Äčin western Gaza City. Their identities were not immediately ‚Äčclear. Several other people in the area were ‚Ā†wounded in the strike, the health officials added.
Such attacks have ‚Äčdeclined since the start of the U.S.-Israeli campaign against Iran, ‚Äčalthough Israeli forces have killed several Palestinians over the past week.
The Israeli military did not immediately respond to a request for comment about ‚ÄčSunday's strike.
Israel and Hamas agreed to a U.S.-brokered Gaza ‚Äčceasefire deal that kicked off last October, but violence has continued on ‚ÄĆa ‚Ā†near-daily basis. Both sides have blamed the other for the violation of the truce agreement.
The Gaza health ministry said at least 640 Palestinians have been killed by Israeli fire ‚Äčsince October. Israel ‚Äčsays four ‚Ā†soldiers have been killed by militants in Gaza over the same period.
Gaza has been devastated ‚Äčby more than two years of an ‚ÄčIsraeli onslaught ‚Ā†that killed over 72,000 Palestinians, according to local health authorities, and left much of the enclave in ruins.
The war was ‚Ā†sparked ‚Äčby Hamas' October 7, 2023 attack ‚Äčon Israel, where the militants killed 1,200 people and took over 250 hostages, ‚Äčaccording to Israeli tallies.

item.title: Iran's hardline succession challenged by shrinking loyalist base
item.description:
DUBAI, March 8 (Reuters) - Iran's next leader faces a massive external assault and growing internal anger, and while previous leaders relied on a hardcore of diehard ideologues in the population, it is far from clear how far his ‚Äčsuccessor can do the same.
Ayatollah Ali Khamenei was killed by U.S. and Israeli strikes a week ago and his hardline son Mojtaba Khamenei is seen as a frontrunner ‚ÄĆto succeed him.
Yet whether loyalists remain as numerous or as committed as in earlier decades - and whether they would rally behind a figure like Mojtaba - is increasingly uncertain.
Reuters interviews with three Basij members, as well as ordinary Iranians, officials, insiders and political analysts, point to a much narrower support base than the Islamic Republic once enjoyed.
"The strategy in choosing a hardliner as the new leader would be to consolidate the base, but they're ending up with an ‚Äčincreasingly small circle of supporters," said Ali Ansari, a modern history professor at the University of St Andrews in the UK.
"And the longer this goes on, the more it will ‚Äčall fray at the edges," he said.
The Islamic Republic emerged from a 1979 revolution backed by millions of Iranians. But decades of rule marked by corruption, ‚Ā†repression and mismanagement have thinned that support, alienating many ordinary people.
Still, a core of loyalists remains ‚ÄĒ people who repeatedly show up at the ballot box to back the Islamic system and who ‚Äčturn out on the streets to crush opposition protests.
Highly organised and able to mobilise quickly, they still pose a major obstacle to any U.S. or Israeli hopes of effecting regime change.
"We have given many ‚Äčmartyrs. They have sacrificed themselves for our leader. Now we must show that the path of the leader Khamenei continues. We will solve any problems and support whoever is chosen as leader. We will even give our lives for him," said Mahdi Rastegari, 32, a religion teacher and member of the Basij, an official volunteer militia.
In the last presidential election, the most hardline candidate, Saeed Jalili, won around 9 million votes in the first round ‚Äčand 13 million in the second, according to official results. Over 61 million of Iran's more than 85 million people were eligible to vote that year.
However, the hardliners' apparent status as a small ‚Äčminority in Iran does not give much hope to all those who hope for change, and the continued bombardment has given rise to fears of chaos.
"The Guards and the system are still powerful. They have tens ‚ÄĆof thousands of ‚Ā†forces ready to fight to keep this regime in place. We, the people, have nothing," said Babak, 34, a businessman in Arak who asked to keep his family name secret.
With their leader killed on the first day of the war and cracks emerging within the country's hierarchy, the extent of continued hardline support for the Islamic Republic will now be tested like never before.
Men like the Basij member Rastegari represent a network of power extending from the supreme leader's now bombed-out office in central Tehran to every village and city neighbourhood that stands in the way of any internal ‚Äčopposition.
Every night since Khamenei's death, hardliners have held ‚Äčstate-backed mourning ceremonies for him despite the bombs ‚Ā†raining down across the country.
Among their number are true believers ready to die as martyrs for their fervent belief in rule by a divinely guided cleric, and those with more mercenary motives who have benefited from their status as public supporters of the system.
Another Basij member, Ali Mohammad Hosseini, goes ‚Äčfrom work at his father's grocery shop in the Shi'ite Muslim seminary city of Qom to spend his evenings manning checkpoints to deter ‚Äčany flashes of public dissent.
"The ‚Ā†most important issue is preserving the regime, which is what the Americans are targeting," the 29-year-old said, saying he would support whichever cleric replaced Khamenei as a "religious duty" that he was prepared to die for.
That degree of commitment is not universal, however. Another Basij member, who asked to give only his first name Hassan, and his location in the Shi'ite shrine city of Mashhad, said he had doubts the Islamic Republic ‚Ā†would survive.
"We ‚Äčneed to be realistic," he said, pointing to continued U.S. pressure and the ruinous aftermath of pulverising airstrikes if a ‚Äčhardliner like Mojtaba Khamenei is named as the new leader.
Members of the Basij and others who demonstrate loyalty to the system have for decades enjoyed privileges including preferential places at university, job offers and subsidised bank loans - but a collapsing economy ‚Äčcould end such perks.
"We do not even have airports any more. No ports. How are they going to rebuild this economy?" Hassan, 29, said.

item.title: Syria's Kurds caution Iran's Kurds against aligning with US against Tehran
item.description:
QAMISHLI, Syria March 8 (Reuters) - Kurdish residents of northeast ‚ÄčSyria warned Iran's Kurds against aligning with the U.S. to fight the Iranian government, citing their own experience in Syria in recent ‚ÄĆmonths as evidence their Iranian counterparts would be "abandoned."
Iranian Kurdish militias based in northern Iraq have consulted with the United States in recent days about whether and how to attack security forces in western Iran, as the United States and Israel pound Iran with air strikes , Reuters has reported .
But Syria's Kurds warned their Iranian counterparts against partnering with Washington.
"I hope that the Kurds of ‚ÄčIran will not ally themselves with America, because they will abandon them," said Saad Ali, a 45-year-old resident of the northeastern Syrian Kurdish ‚Äčtown of Qamishli.
"Tomorrow, if an agreement is made between them (the U.S.) and the Iranians, they will eliminate you. Do not ‚Ā†make our mistakes," he told Reuters.
Syrian Kurdish fighters aligned with the U.S. more than a decade ago to fight the Islamic State group, setting up ‚Äčtheir own semi-autonomous zone in the territory they had seized from the ultraconservative Islamist fighters.
But in January, Syria's new army under President Ahmed al-Sharaa captured most of ‚Äčthe Kurdish-held areas in a sweeping offensive. Syria's Kurds called on the U.S. to intervene on their behalf, and felt betrayed when Washington instead urged them to merge with Sharaa's forces.
'A NEGATIVE EXPERIENCE' WITH THE U.S.
It remains a bitter experience for Syria's Kurds and a lesson they say should be heeded by Iranian Kurds.
"In my opinion, the Kurds in Iran should ‚Äčmaintain a firm stance: they will not engage in any wars within Iranian territory without firm, signed guarantees from the United States regarding the future of ‚Äčthese Kurdish regions in Iran," said Amjad Kardo, a 26-year-old Syrian Kurd in Qamishli.
"We Kurds here in Syria, in particular, have had a negative experience with the Americans in ‚ÄĆSyria, and ‚Ā†their abandonment of Kurdish resistance movements."
An Iranian Kurdish source said Kurdish leaders did have concerns about being "betrayed" like the Kurdish groups in northern Syria.
The source said Iranian Kurdish leaders had requested guarantees from the U.S., without saying what they were.
U.S. President Donald Trump told Reuters on Thursday it would be "wonderful" if Kurdish forces crossed the border from northern Iraq into Iran, but declined to answer a question on whether the U.S. would offer them air support if they did so.
On ‚ÄčSaturday, he appeared to switch positions, ‚Äčtelling reporters he doesn't want Kurdish ‚Ā†fighters going into Iran.
Ahmed Barakat, head of the Kurdish Progressive Democratic Party in Syria, told Reuters that Iranian Kurdish forces should exercise "extreme caution".
Barakat said the decision was ultimately up to them, but he believed that "accepting the ‚Äčinvitation of the United States and being considered the spearhead in confronting or weakening the Iranian regime is ‚Äčnot, at present, in ‚Ā†the best interest of the Kurds of Iran."
Israel has been holding its own talks with Iranian Kurdish insurgent groups based in the semi-autonomous region of Iraqi Kurdistan for around a year, Reuters reported last week .
The Kurds are an ethnic group which was left stateless a century ago when the borders of the modern Middle East emerged from the ‚Ā†collapsing Ottoman ‚ÄčEmpire.
Mostly Sunni Muslims, they speak a language related to Farsi and are concentrated in a mountainous ‚Äčregion straddling the borders of Armenia, Iraq, Iran, Syria and Turkey.
In Iraq, they inhabit three northern provinces led by their own regional government. But in other countries - Iran, Turkey and now Syria - ‚Äčtheir dreams of an autonomous region or state have remained out of reach.

item.title: Brazil President Lula's annual medical checkup test results are normal
item.description:
SAO PAULO, March 8 (Reuters) - Brazilian President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva, ‚Äčknown as Lula, underwent ‚ÄĆhis annual medical checkup on Saturday morning at Sao Paulo‚Äôs Sirio-Libanes ‚ÄčHospital and all test ‚Äčresults were normal, the hospital ‚Ā†said on Sunday.
Lula will receive ‚Äčroutine followup care with his ‚Äčmedical team, led by cardiologist Roberto Kalil Filho and physician Ana Helena ‚ÄčGermoglio, the hospital said ‚Äčin a statement, adding there are currently ‚ÄĆno ‚Ā†plans for additional exams.
Lula, 80, said in 2025 that he plans to seek reelection later ‚Äčthis year. ‚ÄčIn ‚Ā†2024, he underwent two operations to relieve and ‚Äčprevent bleeding in his ‚Äčskull. ‚Ā†He has been Brazil's president two times - from 2003 to ‚Ā†2010, ‚Äčand again since ‚ÄčJanuary 2023.

item.title: Russia says units intercept 170 Ukrainian drones over four hours
item.description:
March 8 (Reuters) - Russia's Defence Ministry said on Sunday ‚Äčits air defence units ‚ÄĆhad intercepted 170 drones over various parts ‚Äčof central and ‚Äčsouthern Russia over a ‚Ā†four-hour period, including two ‚Äčdrones headed for ‚ÄčMoscow.
No damage or casualties were reported.
The ministry figures, higher ‚Äčthan average, covered ‚Äčthe period of 2 p.m. ‚ÄĆto ‚Ā†6 p.m. (1100-1500 GMT). The highest number - 73 - was recorded in Bryansk ‚Äčregion ‚Äčbordering ‚Ā†Ukraine.
Moscow Mayor Sergei Sobyanin, writing on ‚ÄčTelegram, also reported ‚Äčtwo ‚Ā†drones downed while heading for the capital, ‚Ā†with ‚Äčemergency crews ‚Äčexamining fragments at the point ‚Äčof impact.

item.title: US considers idea of special operation to seize Iran‚Äôs uranium, Bloomberg News reports
item.description:
March 8 (Reuters) - U.S. President ‚ÄčDonald Trump ‚ÄĆis weighing the ‚Äčoption of ‚Äčdeploying special forces ‚Ā†on ‚Äčthe ground ‚Äčto seize Iran‚Äôs near-bomb-grade ‚Äčuranium, ‚ÄčBloomberg News reported ‚ÄĆon ‚Ā†Sunday, citing three diplomatic officials ‚Äčbriefed ‚Äčon ‚Ā†the matter.
Reuters ‚Äčcould ‚Äčnot ‚Ā†immediately verify the ‚Ā†report.

item.title: German Greens lead Chancellor Merz's conservatives in state election
item.description:
BERLIN, March 8 (Reuters) - Germany's environmental Greens are set to win a state election in the southern region of Baden-Wuerttemberg, leaving them poised to continue a ‚Äčcoalition with Chancellor Friedrich Merz's conservatives, initial forecasts showed after polls ‚ÄĆclosed on Sunday.
The far-right Alternative for Germany (AfD) party, which has made strong gains in recent years, came third, confirming its position as Germany's main opposition party, even outside its heartland in ‚Äčthe former communist eastern states.
The first forecasts on public broadcaster ARD showed ‚Äčthe Greens in first place with 32% of the vote, followed ‚Ā†by Merz's Christian Democrats (CDU) on 29% and the AfD on 17.5%.
Merz's coalition partners ‚Äčin Berlin, the centre-left Social Democrats were on course for just 5.5% of ‚Äčthe vote, confirming the precipitous drop in support seen in recent years but just scraping past the minimum threshold to enter state parliament.
Baden-Wuerttemberg, home to Mercedes-Benz (MBGn.DE) , opens new tab , Porsche (P911_p.DE) , opens new tab , Bosch (ROBG.UL) and one of the ‚Äčtraditional strongholds of the German car industry, is one of Germany's most prosperous ‚Äčregions.
But its auto sector and wider economy have been hit hard by increasing competition from ‚ÄĆChina's surging ‚Ā†electric vehicles makers.
After a decade of Green-CDU coalition government in Baden-Wurttemberg, the moderate Green candidate for state premier, Cem Ozdemir is unlikely to make life difficult for Merz in the Bundesrat, the upper house of parliament that represents the states ‚Äčin Germany's federal system.
But ‚Äčthe result underlines ‚Ā†Merz's struggles to cut through with voters, as promised reforms have stalled and a sluggish economy comes back slowly from ‚Äčtwo years in recession.
Initially trailing in the race, the Greens ‚Äčovertook the ‚Ā†Christian Democrats as the ballot approached and the result is likely to fuel discontent among conservatives already alarmed at the record low approval levels for Merz's government in ‚Ā†Berlin.
The Baden-Wuerttemberg ‚Äčelection was the first of five state elections ‚Äčthis year, with voters in neighbouring Rhineland-Palatinate due to go to the polls on March 22, followed ‚Äčby Berlin, Saxony-Anhalt and Mecklenberg-Vorpommern in September.

item.title: Iraqi oil production collapses with Strait of Hormuz blocked by conflict, sources say
item.description:
BAGHDAD, March 8 (Reuters) - Iraqi oil ‚ÄĆproduction from its main southern oilfields has fallen by 70% to just 1.3 million barrels per day as the country is unable to export oil via the Strait of Hormuz due to ‚Äčthe Iran war, three industry sources said on Sunday.
Production from the fields ‚Äčstood at around 4.3 million bpd before the war.
"Crude storage has ‚Ā†reached maximum capacity and the remaining output after the major cut will be ‚Äčused to supply the country‚Äôs refineries," said an official with the state‚ÄĎrun Basra Oil ‚ÄčCompany (BOC), which manages production and export operations from the southern fields.
The Strait of Hormuz, a narrow, strategically vital waterway between the Persian Gulf and the Gulf of Oman, is one of the ‚Äčworld's key oil transit chokepoints, carrying roughly a fifth of global oil and ‚Äčliquefied natural gas flows.
The OPEC member's exports also fell sharply to an average of around 800,000 ‚ÄĆbarrels ‚Ā†per day on Sunday, with only two tankers loading because vessels cannot move freely through the strait to Iraq's southern terminals, the source said.
With no new tankers able to reach Iraq‚Äôs southern terminals, exports from there are expected to halt completely ‚Äčat around 8 p.m. ‚Äčlocal time (1700 GMT), ‚Ā†said two oil officials with direct knowledge of terminal operations.
Iraq‚Äôs oil exports from the southern oilfields stood at 3.334 million ‚Äčbarrels per day in February, an oil ministry document showed.
A ‚Äčdrop in ‚Ā†Iraq's oil production and exports is set to strain the country‚Äôs already fragile finances, as the state relies on crude sales for nearly all public spending and more than ‚Ā†90% ‚Äčof its income.
"This is the most serious operational threat ‚ÄčIraq has faced in more than 20 years," a senior Iraqi oil ministry official said.

item.title: Iranian army says at least 104 killed in US attack on Iranian warship last week
item.description:
March 8 (Reuters) - The Iranian army said on ‚ÄčSunday that at ‚ÄĆleast 104 people were killed and 32 ‚Äčwere wounded ‚Äčin an attack by ‚Ā†the U.S. on ‚Äčan Iranian warship ‚Äčoff Sri Lanka's coast last week.
A U.S. submarine sank ‚Äčthe frigate Dena ‚Äčin the Indian Ocean ‚ÄĆabout ‚Ā†19 nautical miles off Sri Lanka's southern port city ‚Äčof Galle ‚Äčon ‚Ā†Wednesday, killing dozens of ‚Äčsailors and dramatically ‚Äčwidening ‚Ā†Washington's pursuit of the Iranian navy.

item.title: New York City police identify device outside Mamdani's home as explosive
item.description:
March 8 (Reuters) - New York City Police said on Sunday a device that was ignited and thrown during protests outside Mayor Zohran Mamdani‚Äôs home over the weekend was an explosive that ‚Äčcould have caused serious injury or death.
The device, a jar filled with nuts, ‚ÄĆbolts and screws and wrapped in black tape with a fuse, was thrown by a counterprotester on Saturday outside Gracie Mansion, but it extinguished itself before any explosion, according to a statement from New York ‚ÄčCity Police Commissioner Jessica Tisch. Two people were in custody, Tisch said.
The device was ‚Äčone of two that were thrown during the protests, which were led ‚Ā†by two opposing groups, according to police. The second device was still being examined, Tisch ‚Äčsaid.
Far-right activist Jake Lang led a protest on Saturday outside Gracie Mansion - where Mamdani lives ‚Äčwith his wife - against a purported Islamic ‚Äútakeover‚ÄĚ of New York City and against public prayer by Muslims. Tisch said at a press conference on Saturday that she did not believe Mamdani and his wife were home ‚Äčat the time.
In a statement on Sunday, Mamdani condemned Lang‚Äôs protest but said the violence ‚Äčthat followed it was more disturbing.
‚ÄúViolence at a protest is never acceptable,‚ÄĚ Mamdani said. ‚ÄúThe attempt to use ‚ÄĆan explosive ‚Ā†device and hurt others is not only criminal, it is reprehensible and the antithesis of who we are.‚ÄĚ
Lang‚Äôs protest, which consisted of about 20 people, was opposed by a far larger counterprotest of 125 people aimed at running ‚ÄúNazis‚ÄĚ out of New York, Tisch said at a ‚Äčpress conference on Saturday. ‚ÄčPolice said Emir ‚Ā†Balat, 18, was among the counterprotesters before he lit and threw the device.
The device rolled near police before it extinguished itself, Tisch said ‚Äčon Saturday. Balat ran after throwing it and eventually lit and dropped ‚Äča second ‚Ā†device in the street, according to Tisch.
Balat and another man, whom police on Sunday identified as Ibrahim Kayumi, were arrested at the scene, Tisch said. New York police are working with the ‚Ā†FBI and ‚Äčthe U.S. Department of Justice on the investigation.
Tisch said ‚Äčon Saturday there was no immediate indication the incident was related to the U.S. attack on Iran , but that authorities ‚Äčwere still investigating.

item.title: Iran names Khamenei's son, Mojtaba, as new supreme leader, state media report
item.description:
DUBAI, March 8 (Reuters) - Iran's Assembly ‚Äčof Experts has named ‚ÄĆMojtaba Khamenei to replace his father, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, as ‚Äčthe country's new ‚Äčsupreme leader, state media reported ‚Ā†on Sunday.
Mojtaba , a mid-ranking ‚Äčcleric with close ties ‚Äčto the powerful Revolutionary Guards, had long been viewed by elements ‚Äčof Iran's ruling ‚Äčestablishment as a potential successor to ‚ÄĆhis ‚Ā†father, who was killed after the United States and Israel launched strikes on ‚ÄčIran.
Although ‚ÄčIran's ‚Ā†ruling ideology frowns on the principle of hereditary ‚Äčsuccession, he has ‚Äča ‚Ā†powerful following within the Guards and his dead father's ‚Ā†still-influential ‚Äčoffice.

item.title: Iran defies Trump, elevates Khamenei's son Mojtaba as successor
item.description:
DUBAI, March 9 (Reuters) - Iran's clerical leadership chose confrontation over compromise in appointing Mojtaba Khamenei to succeed his father, Ali Khamenei, a move regional officials say is a direct rebuke to U.S. President Donald Trump, who had declared the son "unacceptable".
Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei was ‚Äčkilled in a U.S.-Israeli strike at the start of the conflict, now in its second week .
The appointment of Mojtaba as his successor by the Assembly of Experts locks hardliners firmly ‚ÄĆin control in Tehran - a gamble that could reshape Iran's war with the U.S. and Israel and reverberate far beyond the Middle East.
"Having Mojtaba take over is the same playbook," said Alex Vatanka, a senior fellow at the Middle East Institute.
"It's a big humiliation for the United States to carry out an operation of this scale, risk so much, and end up killing an 86-year-old man, only to have him replaced by his hardline son.‚ÄĚ
Under Iran's complex, theocratic system, the supreme ‚Äčleader is the ultimate authority, including over foreign policy and Iran‚Äôs nuclear programme, as well as guiding the elected president and parliament.
Analysts say the choice ‚Äčof Mojtaba, a deeply hardline cleric whose wife, mother and other family members were also killed in U.S.‚ÄďIsraeli strikes - sends an unequivocal message: Iran‚Äôs leadership ‚Ā†has rejected any prospect of compromise to preserve the system and sees no path forward except confrontation, revenge and endurance.
According to insiders, Mojtaba will face immense internal and external strain from a disaffected population and ‚Äčan escalating conflict, but is expected to move swiftly to consolidate power.
That will likely mean expanded authority for the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps, harsher domestic controls and sweeping repression to crush dissent.
"The world will miss ‚Äčthe era of his father," a regional official close to Tehran told Reuters. "Mojtaba will have no choice but to show an iron fist... even if the war ends, there will be severe internal repression."
That stance comes after months of deepening domestic unrest - the bloodiest since the 1979 Islamic Revolution - that had already weakened the Islamic Republic before the war began.
Iran was grappling with a battered economy, soaring inflation, currency collapse and widening poverty, alongside tightening repression that had fuelled public anger and ‚Äčprotests - pressures now likely to intensify under wartime rule.
Difficult days lie ahead under Mojtaba, with far tighter internal controls, intensified pressure at home and an even more aggressive, hostile posture abroad, said ‚Äčanother Iranian insider familiar with the situation on the ground.
Paul Salem, a senior fellow at the Middle East Institute, said Mojtaba was not a figure positioned to strike a deal with the United States or pivot diplomatically.
‚ÄúNobody emerging now ‚ÄĆis going ‚Ā†to be able to compromise,‚ÄĚ Salem said. ‚ÄúThis is a hardline choice, made in a hardline moment.‚ÄĚ
In the eyes of Iran‚Äôs clerics, many of whom famously label America the "Great Satan", the assassination of Khamenei, the Islamic Republic's highest religious authority, has elevated him to "martyrdom".
Clerics have cast the slain leader as a heroic figure, likening him to Imam Hussein - the Shi‚Äôite symbol of sacrifice and resistance against oppression.
"Mojtaba is even worse and more hardline than his father," said Alan Eyre, former U.S. diplomat and Iran specialist, adding that he was the preferred candidate of the Guards. "He's going to have a lot of revenge to exact."
That calculus carries risks. Israel ‚Äčhas warned that any successor to Khamenei would also ‚Äčbe a target, while Trump has said ‚Ā†the war may only end once Iran‚Äôs military leadership and ruling elite are eliminated .
A powerful mid‚ÄĎranking cleric, Mojtaba, 56, has long opposed reformist groups advocating engagement with the West. His close ties to senior clerics and the IRGC - which dominates Iran‚Äôs security forces and its economy - give him leverage ‚Äčacross the state‚Äôs political and coercive security institutions.
He amassed influence under his father as a key figure within the security apparatus and the vast business ‚Äčempire it controls, operating for ‚Ā†years as Ali Khamenei‚Äôs gatekeeper and, in practice, a ‚Äúmini-supreme leader‚ÄĚ, analysts say.
His elevation comes as the U.S.-Israeli campaign against Iran intensifies, with joint strikes hitting fuel depots and other targets inside Iran, while Iranian missiles and drones have struck Gulf states, widening the conflict.
Mojtaba studied under conservative clerics in the seminaries of Qom, the heart of Shi‚Äôite theological learning, and holds the clerical rank of Hojjatoleslam.
The U.S. Treasury sanctioned Mojtaba in 2019, saying he represented the ‚Ā†supreme leader ‚Äčin an official capacity despite never holding elected or formal government office.
A Gulf source familiar with regional government thinking said of ‚ÄčMojtaba‚Äôs appointment: ‚ÄúThis tells Trump and Washington that Iran will not back down, they will fight on until the finish."
Salem, of the Middle East Institute, likened Iran‚Äôs trajectory to Iraq under Saddam Hussein after 1991 or Syria under Bashar al‚ÄĎAssad after 2012 ‚ÄĒ governments that ‚Äčsurvived years of war and isolation but steadily lost control.
‚ÄúThey‚Äôre doubling down on the hard line,‚ÄĚ Salem said. ‚ÄúInternally, it‚Äôs terrible ‚ÄĒ and deeply destabilising.‚ÄĚ

item.title: Jailed Istanbul mayor faces trial amid crackdown on Turkey's opposition
item.description:
ISTANBUL, March 9 (Reuters) - Istanbul's jailed mayor Ekrem Imamoglu goes on ‚ÄĆtrial on Monday in a sweeping corruption case that could extinguish the ambition of Tayyip Erdogan's main rival to become president at Turkey's next election.
Imamoglu, 55, is the chief suspect among more than 400 defendants linked to the Istanbul ‚Äčmunicipality accused of corruption and bribery. He and his main opposition Republican People's Party (CHP) deny ‚Äčthe charges, which carry prison sentences adding up to hundreds of years.
The case ‚Ā†is being held at a courthouse in the Silivri prison complex west of Istanbul, where Imamoglu ‚Äčhas been held for almost a year, and is the centrepiece of a judicial crackdown on the CHP ‚Äčthat is overshadowing politics ahead of elections that are expected to be held next year.
In comments to Reuters , Imamoglu struck a defiant tone, saying Erdogan should call elections right away, but his prospects of challenging the veteran leader look ‚Äčbleak amid a crackdown that rights groups and foreign observers say has undermined the democratic credentials of ‚ÄčNATO member Turkey.
"(I)t's hard to avoid the conclusion that prosecutors are trying to remove Imamoglu from politics and discredit ‚ÄĆhis ‚Ā†party in ways that undermine democracy," said Benjamin Ward, the deputy Europe and Central Asia director at Human Rights Watch, describing the trial as coming after more than a year of "weaponising" the criminal justice system against the CHP.
The government denies exerting influence over the judiciary, which it says is independent.
Newly appointed ‚ÄčJustice Minister Akin Gurlek , who ‚Äčas chief Istanbul ‚Ā†prosecutor led investigations into Imamoglu, said he carried out his duties in the case without bias or consideration for the mayor's position.
"I simply did my duty ‚Äčas a public prosecutor. My conscience is clear," Gurlek told reporters in ‚Äčcomments published ‚Ā†on Friday.
Imamoglu has already suffered a severe blow to his presidential ambitions, with a court in January rejecting his lawsuit challenging the cancellation of his university degree ‚Äď a qualification required of any presidential candidate.
Erdogan, 72, has led ‚Ā†Turkey ‚Äčas prime minister or president since 2003. Presidential and parliamentary elections ‚Äčare not scheduled until 2028 but if he wishes to seek a third term as president he is obliged to hold ‚Äčthe vote early, unless the constitutional term limits are changed.

item.title: US energy chief defends waiver on Russian oil sanctions, blames fear for higher gas prices
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 8 (Reuters) - Trump administration officials on Sunday defended a decision to temporarily lift some sanctions on Russian oil ‚ÄĆand predicted that a sharp increase in gasoline prices resulting from the Iran war would last only weeks.
Appearing on multiple TV talk shows, Energy Secretary Chris Wright and U.S. Ambassador to the United Nations Mike Waltz said a waiver issued last week to allow Indian ‚Äčpurchases of Russian oil would alleviate pressure on the global market.
"It's a 30-day pause to allow, which ‚Äčis just kind of common sense, to allow the millions and millions of barrels of ‚Ā†oil that are sitting out on ships to go to Indian refineries," Waltz said on NBC's "Meet the Press."
Wright ‚Äčtold CNN's "State of the Union" that the waiver can help "tamp this fear of shortage of oil, tamp the price ‚Äčspikes and the concerns we see in the marketplace."
With the war now in its second week and no end in sight, Americans are grappling with higher prices at the pump, a new complicating factor for the U.S. economy, which unexpectedly lost 92,000 jobs in February.
As ‚Äčof Friday, the national average price for regular gasoline stood at $3.32 a gallon, up 11% from the previous ‚Äčweek and the highest since September 2024, according to data from the motorists group AAA. Diesel was at $4.33, up 15% from a ‚ÄĆweek ‚Ā†ago, surging to the highest level since November 2023.
"We believe this is a small price to pay to get to a world where energy prices are returned back to where they were," Wright said on the "Fox News Sunday" program.
There is no shortage of oil or natural gas, said Wright, who asserted that the price increases are based on "fear ‚Äčand perception" that the Iran ‚Äčoperation will be a ‚Ā†drawn-out affair.
"But it won't be," Wright said, echoing President Donald Trump 's prediction that the war will last weeks rather than months.
Trump, in a Reuters interview on Thursday, predicted that gasoline prices ‚Äčwill "drop very rapidly" when the war is over.
Senator John Kennedy, a Louisiana Republican, ‚Äčcriticized energy speculators.
"The ‚Ā†oil prices have gone up because you've got a bunch of oil traders out there in their Gucci loafers, with their caramel Frappuccinos who are bidding up the price," Kennedy said on "Fox News Sunday."
Political analysts say a persistent rise in ‚Ā†gasoline prices ‚Äčcould hurt Republicans in the November midterm elections when control of ‚Äčthe U.S. Congress will be at stake. A Reuters/Ipsos poll last month found that most respondents rejected Trump's characterization of the economy as "booming."

item.title: Europe now world's biggest arms importer, think tank says
item.description:
STOCKHOLM, March 9 (Reuters) - Europe became the world's biggest arms importer over the last five years as governments reacted to Russia's threat and waning confidence in ‚ÄčU.S. security commitments, data from the Stockholm International Peace Research Institute ‚ÄĆshowed on Monday.
European countries more than trebled their arms imports in 2021-25 versus 2016-2020 to supply Ukraine in its defence against Russian invasion and to rebuild their own militaries ‚Äčafter decades of under-investment.
"The sharp increase in arms flows to European ‚Äčstates pushed global arms transfers up almost 10 per cent," Mathew ‚Ā†George, Director of the SIPRI Arms Transfers Programme, said.
Europe accounted for 33% ‚Äčof global arms imports, up from 12% in the previous five-year period, the ‚Äčreport showed.
Despite boosting domestic production, European countries continued to increase purchases of U.S. weapons, especially combat aircraft and long-range air-defence systems.
Arms imports by Middle Eastern states fell 13%, though ‚ÄčSaudi Arabia and Qatar were among the top four individual buyers.
The decline ‚Äčmainly reflected large Saudi orders during the previous period that still needed to be integrated, ‚ÄĆSIPRI ‚Ā†senior researcher Pieter Wezeman said.
Even before the latest U.S. and Israeli attacks on Iran , states in the region had placed significant new orders that have yet to show up in the figures.
The current conflict is likely to lift procurement ‚Äčfurther, particularly of anti-missile ‚Äčand air-defence ‚Ā†systems.
"They will first replace what they have used, but also that they are going to look at buying more equipment ‚Äčto protect themselves even better than they can do ‚Äčnow," Wezeman ‚Ā†said.
In terms of suppliers, the U.S. increased its global share of the arms export market to 42% from 36%, cementing its dominance. France was the second-largest supplier ‚Ā†with ‚Äč9.8% of global exports, while Russia's share dropped ‚Äčto 6.8% from 21% after its 2022 invasion of Ukraine.
Europe's combined export share was 28% - four ‚Äčtimes Russia's and five times China's.

item.title: Oman's Muscat airport limits private jet flights, email says
item.description:
March 8 (Reuters) - Oman's Muscat International Airport has asked private jet operators to avoid using the site for "additional flights," giving priority to government ‚Äčand commercial flights as fresh airspace closures hit the region‚Äôs ‚ÄĆattempts to increase travel, according to an email seen by Reuters.
"Due to the current crisis management measures at Muscat International Airport, flight movements are restricted ‚Äčto approved seasonal scheduled services only," the airport authorities ‚Äčtold charter plane operators on Friday.
The airport said this measure ‚Ā†was necessary to manage congestion and ensure that airport capacity ‚Äčremains within acceptable limits.
The email, first reported by the Financial Times, ‚Äčasked that all airlines and operators cancel any slots falling outside the approved seasonal schedule and to refrain from submitting non-approved flight requests until further notice.
"The ‚Äčonly additional flights that may be considered during this period are ‚Äčembassy-sponsored repatriation flights, subject to prior approval through the applicable diplomatic channels and ‚ÄĆon ‚Ā†the condition that no commercial sale of seats is undertaken in connection with such operations," the email added.
The outbreak of the U.S.-Israel war against Iran has led to flight cancellations across the Middle East , leaving airlines and ‚Äčgovernments scrambling to ‚Äčsupport thousands of ‚Ā†stranded passengers.
With most airspace in the region still closed over missile and drone concerns, some people ‚Äčhave turned to private jets while charter flights and ‚Äčlimited ‚Ā†commercial services struggle to evacuate tens of thousands of travelers.
Travelers have shelled out high prices to get out of the Middle East, rushing ‚Ā†to airports ‚Äčor heading over land to quieter ‚Äčhubs, with fighter jets occasionally escorting passenger jets.

item.title: Australia tells families of diplomats to leave UAE as conflict escalates
item.description:
SYDNEY, March 9 (Reuters) - Australia has asked the family members and dependants of diplomatic officials in the ‚ÄčUnited Arab Emirates to leave the country, ‚ÄĆfollowing an escalation in the conflict in the Middle East as several Gulf cities came under Iranian bombardment.
U.S. ‚ÄčPresident Donald Trump told reporters on Air Force ‚ÄčOne that he was not seeking negotiations ‚Ā†to end the U.S.-Israeli military strikes against Iran, ‚Äčwhich have driven up global energy prices, disrupted ‚Äčbusiness and snarled air travel.

item.title: As Fukushima memories fade, Japan embraces a nuclear-powered future
item.description:
IWAKI, Japan, March 9 (Reuters) - Takuma Hashimoto was three years old when a massive earthquake and tsunami struck on March 11, 2011, triggering nuclear meltdowns at the Fukushima Daiichi plant just an hour's drive from his home.
As the worst nuclear disaster since Chornobyl unfolded, ‚Äčhis terrified family was trapped, unable to flee like their neighbours because they couldn't find gas for their car.
Now 18, Hashimoto wants to become part of Japan's next generation of nuclear talent.
"I don't think nuclear power ‚ÄĆshould be treated as something that's automatically dangerous," said the engineering student at a technical college in Iwaki, where a monitoring station still keeps check on local radiation levels.
Reuters spoke with Hashimoto, as well as a former crisis management official and a nuclear industry veteran, ahead of the 15th anniversary of the disaster. Their stories illustrate how Japan is pivoting back to a power source it had all but shunned.
Resource-poor Japan was once one of the world's biggest proponents of nuclear power, which provided roughly 30% of the country's electricity from 54 reactors.
The Fukushima disaster saw public opinion swing dramatically against nuclear, ‚Äčand all reactors were ordered to be shut down for safety inspections and upgrades. In 2012, the government even decided to phase out nuclear energy. That decision was reversed two years later, but reactor restarts have been slow and ‚Äčmany have been shut permanently.
Now Tokyo's staunchly pro-nuclear leader Sanae Takaichi, buoyed by a thumping election win , is pushing to accelerate restarts and advance new nuclear technologies ‚Ā†to wean the country off costly imported fossil fuels.
The restart of one of the seven reactors at the world's biggest nuclear plant, Kashiwazaki-Kariwa , in January was a milestone. That said, only 15 of 33 reactors in Japan that remain operable are back online.
Middle East ‚Äčchaos unleashed by U.S. President Donald Trump's attack on Iran - a region that accounts for 95% of Japan's oil supplies - and an anticipated surge in energy needs of power-hungry AI data centres promise to bolster a shift in public opinion.
A slim majority of people - 51% - ‚Äčare now in favour of the restarts, an Asahi newspaper survey last month found. That's up from 28% when it began polling on the issue in 2013. The most supportive are young people aged 18 to 29 - at 66%.
Hashimoto's school, which has sent him on visits to nuclear power plants, receives government funding to nurture workers in nuclear power, regulation and decommissioning.
But securing enough talent may be a bottleneck in Japan's nuclear renaissance.
There were just 177 students admitted to nuclear-related courses across Japan in 2024, versus 317 just before the Fukushima disaster and a peak of 673 in the early ‚Äč1990s.
Hashimoto knows not everyone agrees with him. Almost every week, he encounters a vocal band of anti-nuclear protesters at the train station on his commute to school.
But he says: "What matters is using nuclear power properly, having measures in place in case something happens, and ‚Äčdeveloping technology to make sure accidents don't occur."
Seiji Inada, 49, was part of the government's crisis response team in 2011. Tasked with tallying the dead, he hunkered down for days with hundreds of officials in an underground bunker beneath the prime minister's office in ‚ÄĆTokyo.
Inada remembers being ‚Ā†in the crisis room, watching aghast footage of a building that housed a reactor exploding.
Around 150,000 people living around the plant were evacuated, many of whom never returned, while the government assessed the risks that Tokyo, one of the world's biggest cities, might be blanketed in a radioactive plume.
"I remember during my short lunch break, I called my dad and I told him: 'Well, I can't tell you any details, but just prepare for the worst case scenario," recalled Inada, who now works for private consultancy FGS Global.
An inquiry published in 2012 blamed plant operator TEPCO (9501.T) , opens new tab , regulators and the government for failing to develop safety protocols that could have contained the damage, calling it a "man-made" disaster.
The prime minister at the time, Naoto Kan, quit after he was criticised for bungling the response.
"The lesson of 3/11 is ‚Äčhumility: low-probability shocks happen. What matters is governance," said Inada.
Back then, ‚ÄčInada, like most other Japanese, found it hard to ‚Ā†imagine that the country would ever return to nuclear power, but "time heals trauma", he added.
"The shock of 2011 was profound and it shaped attitudes for years. Over time, the debate shifted from emotion to trade-offs."
Nuclear industry veteran Keiji Matsunaga, 59, is putting the lessons learned from Fukushima into practice by developing safer reactors at his company Toshiba.
Joining the firm straight from ‚Äčuniversity in 1991, five years after Chornobyl, he's always been conscious of the stigma attached to the industry.
Fukushima made that worse and some years after the disaster, his teenage daughter ended ‚Äčup in an argument with a ‚Ā†teacher at her school who said nuclear power was dangerous and Japan shouldn't be using it.
But even as Toshiba's nuclear business faltered in the wake of Fukushima and the bankruptcy of its U.S. unit Westinghouse, Matsunaga says he has never wavered from the view that Japan needs nuclear for energy security.
And the gradual increase in restarts is helping business.
Though Toshiba, now a private company, does not release its nuclear power division earnings targets, another big player, Mitsubishi Heavy Industries (7011.T) , opens new tab , told Reuters last week that sales for its nuclear power unit will hit a record 400 ‚Ā†billion yen ($2.5 billion) ‚Äčnext year, a target it didn't expect to reach until 2030.
Any new plants that might be built have been designed to be much safer, says Matsunaga. ‚ÄčFor example, buildings housing reactors would have roofs fortified with steel plate and concrete that can resist the impact of falling aircraft.
The plants would also have more failproof natural circulation systems, in addition to other systems, to keep the reactor cool. That would help avoid a situation like Fukushima when the loss of air-conditioner ‚Äčcooling functionality contributed to the meltdowns.
"By adopting these measures, we believe events like Fukushima can be prevented," he said.

item.title: Trump says ending Iran war will be 'mutual' decision with Netanyahu
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 8 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump ‚Äčsaid on Sunday ‚ÄĆthat when to end the war ‚Äčwith Iran will ‚Äčbe a "mutual" decision made ‚Ā†with Israeli ‚ÄčPrime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu.
Speaking ‚Äčby phone with The Times of Israel, ‚ÄčTrump said Netanyahu ‚Äčwill have input on ‚ÄĆresolving ‚Ā†the conflict.
"I think it‚Äôs mutual ... a little bit. We‚Äôve ‚Äčbeen ‚Äčtalking. ‚Ā†I‚Äôll make a decision at ‚Äčthe right ‚Äčtime, ‚Ā†but everything‚Äôs going to be taken ‚Ā†into ‚Äčaccount," said ‚ÄčTrump.

item.title: South Korea parliament due to finalise draft US investment bill under trade deal
item.description:
SEOUL, March 9 (Reuters) - A South Korean parliamentary committee is expected to finalise the wording of a special bill on Monday, ‚Äčpaving the way for a plenary vote this ‚ÄĆweek to enable $350 billion of U.S. investments under a bilateral trade deal between the countries.
The bill is expected to be put to a ‚Äčfull National Assembly vote on March 12, as ‚Äčthe U.S. ally responds to pressure from Washington over ‚Ā†perceived delays in enacting the deal.
The "Special Act on Investment ‚Äčin the U.S." will set up an investment vehicle, as ‚Äčwell as a risk management committee to implement last year's agreement for South Korea to invest in sectors such as shipbuilding and chips in ‚Äčreturn for lower U.S. tariffs.
U.S. President Donald Trump threatened in January ‚Äčto hike tariffs on imports from South Korea, accusing the country's parliament ‚ÄĆof ‚Ā†delays in enacting the trade deal.
Top South Korean officials have said the U.S.-South Korea trade deal remains valid despite a U.S. Supreme Court decision in February that struck down a large ‚Äčswath of Trump's ‚Äčtariffs.
Officials in ‚Ā†Seoul have, however, voiced concerns about the impact of U.S. investments on an already weak ‚Äčwon currency and said that projects would be ‚Äčbased ‚Ā†on consideration of commercial feasibility and foreign exchange market conditions.
The special committee, which includes lawmakers from the ruling Democratic Party and ‚Ā†the ‚Äčopposition People Power Party, is expected to ‚Äčreview the final bill at a subcommittee on Monday and approve it at a general ‚Äčmeeting in the afternoon.

item.title: Brothers of Epstein accuser Virginia Giuffre visit New Mexico ranch, demand unredacted documents
item.description:
STANLEY, N.M., March 8 (Reuters) - Two brothers of one of Jeffrey Epstein's most prominent accusers visited the sex offender's former New Mexico ranch on Sunday for ‚Äčthe first time to demand the Trump administration release unredacted documents to reveal ‚ÄĆthe identities of men their late sister alleged sexually abused her at the property.
With Epstein's hacienda-style mansion in the background, the brothers of Virginia Giuffre , who took her own life in April, ‚Äčjoined hundreds of protesters at a roadside rally to mark international ‚Äčwomen's day near the gate of the ranch located 30 miles (48 ‚Ā†km) south of state capital Santa Fe.
Giuffre's brother Sky Roberts, 37, called on ‚Äčthe U.S. Department of Justice to release documents showing, among other things, names of ‚Äčvisitors to Epstein's Zorro Ranch where he and his acquaintances are accused of sexually abusing women and girls.
"All those names are in the files and right now the government is covering ‚Äčthose up," said Roberts, flanked by Giuffre's older brother, Daniel Wilson, 47, and ‚Äčtheir families.
The release of millions of records on Epstein has exposed the financier's social connections with ‚ÄĆpoliticians, business ‚Ā†people and scientists who he invited to the ranch.
The files have become a persistent political problem for U.S. President Donald Trump, who was named in FBI records released on Thursday in which an unidentified woman made accusations against him related to an ‚Äčalleged sexual encounter.
New Mexico ‚Äčin February became ‚Ā†the first U.S. state to launch a legislative "truth commission" into how Epstein was able to operate in secrecy at Zorro ‚ÄčRanch for 26 years.
‚ÄúNew Mexico is setting the example and we ‚Äčexpect other ‚Ā†states to follow behind,‚ÄĚ said Amanda Roberts, 37, Sky Roberts' wife, citing New York and Florida where Epstein had residences where similar probes needed to occur.
Americans generally view ‚Ā†the ‚ÄčEpstein case as an example of wealthy and ‚Äčpowerful people rarely being held accountable and believe the U.S. government is still hiding information about Epstein's ‚Äčclients, according to Reuters/Ipsos polling .

item.title: Compounding errors and narrow self-interest threaten global fuel crisis
item.description:
LAUNCESTON, Australia, March 9 (Reuters) - Mounting miscalculations and a retreat to narrow self-interest by major countries, including the United States and China, are threatening to turn the current conflict in Iran into a global crisis for the supply of refined oil products.
Much of the media focus tends to be on the price of crude oil, with benchmark Brent crude futures jumping as much as 20% to $111.04 a barrel, the highest since July 2022, in early Asian trade on Monday.
But while the ‚Äčleap in crude is dramatic, of more concern is the even bigger surge in the cost of refined fuels such as gasoline, diesel and jet fuel, which are the products that consumers actually buy.
The explosive climb in refined ‚ÄĆproduct prices last week was led by jet fuel, with Singapore spot prices hitting a record high of $225.44 a barrel on March 4, before easing to end the week at $155.82.
However, that price is still 66.7% higher than the $93.45 a barrel that prevailed on February 27, the day prior to the United States and Israel launched an aerial campaign against Iran.
Singapore gasoil , the building block for diesel and jet fuel, reached $123.39 a barrel on March 4, the highest since September 2023 and up 33.5% from the close on February 27.
In effect, product markets in Asia are starting to price in a shortage of supply of the key fuels that keep economies running.
The effective ‚Äčclosure of the Strait of Hormuz is cutting some 18 million barrels per day (bpd) of crude and products, roughly split into 14 million bpd of crude and 3 million bpd of products, according to three-month moving average data compiled by commodity analysts Kpler.
While ‚Äčthe administration of U.S. President Donald Trump claims the Strait remains open and has offered to insure vessels transiting the narrow waterway between the gulfs of Persia and Oman, the market has yet to ‚Ā†be convinced that Iran won't attack any ship attempting passage.
It is the closure of the Strait of Hormuz that is the most important strategic error made by the Trump administration and Israel in their attack on Iran.
In common with the majority of analysts and presumably Gulf governments ‚Äčthe assumption was that this conflict would follow the same template as previous flare-ups in the region.
The assumption was that everybody would behave rationally and not target oil production and transportation infrastructure, as it is in nobody's interest to halt the flow of crude and products.
But it turns out ‚Äčif you tell a dictatorial religious government that your aim is regime change, then that government feels little compunction to play by the prior rules.
Iran's decision to target its Gulf neighbours that host U.S. military bases has upended all previous calculations about the conflict.
Gulf countries Saudi Arabia, the United Arab Emirates, Qatar, Kuwait and Iraq are highly dependent on their oil, fuel and liquefied natural gas exports, and right now their revenue is severely curtailed.
Dubai, one of the Emirates, is also increasingly dependent on its status as the region's financial and tourism hub, with both sectors now taking a massive hit from the ongoing conflict.
The compounding errors of ‚Äčthe Trump administration are now becoming more apparent.
It's clear that they did not expect the Strait of Hormuz to be effectively closed, and their stop-gap solution hasn't won market backing.
It's one thing to offer insurance and perhaps naval escorts, it's entirely another to ensure safe passage can happen ‚Äčfor hundreds of vessels each week.
All Iran has to do is hit one fully laden crude tanker with a ballistic missile and all bets will be off, with the situation then likely to be worse than it is currently.
The next U.S. step of offering a waiver to India to resume purchases ‚ÄĆof Russian crude is ‚Ā†a bit like feeding cookies to an elephant, it's nice but largely ineffective.
What is already happening is that Asia's refiners are scrambling to source crude from other suppliers outside the Strait of Hormuz choke point.
What is the likely result of this?
Crude oil prices will rise, and the longer the close of the Strait continues the more short of crude, and thus refined products, the region will become.
The response to this looming shortage is that countries with refining capacity will focus on their domestic needs and cut exports of fuels, thereby exacerbating the shortage of refined products.
This can be seen by reports that China has ordered its major state-owned refiners to stop exports of refined products.
Some Indian refiners are also reported to be ending exports of fuels, while plants in some countries, such as South Korea, are cutting refinery processing.
The obvious question for the authorities in Beijing is ‚Äčhow long before the decision not to supply refined fuels to ‚Äčimporting nations blows back on the Chinese economy.
It is another example ‚Ā†of a miscalculation that will prove more costly than the alternative of actually ramping up fuel exports to help meet demand.
China isn't a major exporter of refined products, with about 600,000 barrels per day.
But it is the only major producer with significant spare capacity and a massive crude oil stockpile, estimated at over 1 billion barrels.
This level of inventories means China could continue to refine oil at its current rate for about ‚Äčthree years, even if its imports dropped to zero.
In effect, China could use its huge stockpile to boost refinery runs, lift exports and ease the looming supply crisis.
This would not only be highly ‚Äčprofitable but would also win favour with ‚Ā†importing nations, who may otherwise run short of fuels.
Australia is Asia's biggest fuel importer, taking about 900,000 bpd, according to Kpler.
Imagine a situation when the country could no longer meet this demand, resulting in shortages.
The government would have to prioritise food production and delivery, and keeping as much of the economy going as possible.
But a brave Australian government could say to China that no more iron ore would be shipped because of diesel shortages unless Beijing supplied refined fuels.
China gets about two-thirds of its iron ore imports from Australia and without these flows its steel industry would be severely curtailed, which in ‚Ā†turn would devastate manufacturing ‚Äčand construction.
Getting to this point would only happen if the Strait of Hormuz remains largely closed and countries follow narrow and short-term self-interest.
The problem is that political leaders ‚Äčso far seem unable to grasp the extent of the stress they are putting on the energy supply system, and their actions suggest they see only their own country's needs without realising that it is a global system that needs global solutions.
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI) , your essential new source for global financial commentary. ROI delivers thought-provoking, ‚Äčdata-driven analysis of everything from swap rates to soybeans. Markets are moving faster than ever. ROI can help you keep up. Follow ROI on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
The views expressed here are those of the author, a columnist for Reuters.

item.title: The Week in Breakingviews: What‚Äôs next for Iran?
item.description:
LONDON, March 8 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Welcome back! A week ago I woke up to find out that the United States and Israel were attacking Iran . It‚Äôs the second time this year that President Donald Trump has targeted another country‚Äôs leader ‚Äď on a Saturday. If this newsletter was forwarded to you, sign up here to get it in your inbox every weekend.
‚ÄúIn Siena‚Äôs brutal Palio horse race, a steed can ‚Äčstill win after losing its rider. Banca Monte dei Paschi di Siena (BMPS.MI) , opens new tab , headquartered in the same picturesque Tuscan town, is applying that tradition to hostile bank M&A.‚ÄĚ
Read more here: Italy‚Äôs MPS adds governance risk to dicey ‚ÄĆM&A .
‚ÄúNo operational plan can extend with any degree of certainty beyond the initial encounter with the main enemy force.‚ÄĚ Field Marshal Helmuth von Moltke‚Äôs advice , opens new tab to military ‚Äčstrategists is also a warning for anyone drawing lasting conclusions from the first days of a war. A week after the United States and Israel attacked Iran and killed its Supreme Leader, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, it would be ‚Äčfoolish to predict the outcome of the conflict, let alone its long-term repercussions. Instead, here are the five most pressing questions:
This key question is the hardest ‚Ā†to answer. Many strategists expect it to be short, in part because the U.S. bombing campaign means Iran will eventually run out of missiles and launching sites it needs to retaliate. With tepid public support at home and the price of petrol rising, ‚ÄčU.S. President Donald Trump may also want to avoid a drawn-out battle. But on Friday he demanded ‚Äú unconditional surrender ‚ÄĚ. Financial markets are beginning to price in a longer conflict: the price of a barrel of oil ended the week above $90. Yet while the spot ‚Äčprice of gas in Europe has jumped, contracts for delivery in a few years have barely budged.
Trump could declare ‚Äúmission accomplished‚ÄĚ and attempt to move on, as his predecessor George W. Bush did six weeks after the invasion of Iraq in 2003. But that historical precedent reminds us that an end to fighting depends on what happens on the ground. As George Hay argues , there are three broad scenarios for Iran: the current government clings on to power under a new leader; the country splits along factional or ethnic lines; or the population rises up ‚Äčand takes power. Only the third scenario is consistent with more enduring calm. Don‚Äôt forget that it‚Äôs less than nine months since Trump claimed to have destroyed Iran‚Äôs nuclear program.
Blocking the Strait of Hormuz was long seen ‚Äčas a move Iran would only make in desperation. About a fifth of the world‚Äôs oil and gas passes through the narrow waterway. Yet the outbreak of hostilities has produced two extraordinary outcomes. First, tanker traffic has stopped largely on the strength of Iranian threats : anxious shipping ‚ÄĆcompanies and jittery ‚Ā†insurers did the rest. Second, the blockage has not yet led to the $200-a-barrel oil price traders once predicted. Markets may expect a swift reopening, but as Yawen Chen points out , this is not entirely in Trump‚Äôs gift.
The United Arab Emirates, Qatar, and their neighbours have presented themselves as hyper-modern and impartial finance and technology hubs. They have attracted banks, tax exiles and tourists, while making splashy investments around the world. Iranian missiles and drones flying over gleaming towers shattered that glitzy image. Even though most of the projectiles have been shot down , George Hay and Afiq Fitri Alias make the case that the blow to the Gulf‚Äôs regional reputation is likely to last ‚Äď and may benefit Saudi Arabia. Meanwhile, extra spending on domestic defence ‚Äčmeans less cash for overseas investments.
The president revels in his unpredictability. After abducting Venezuelan ‚Ā†President Nicolas Maduro and killing Khamenei he may feel emboldened to settle other scores. On Thursday, he hinted at intervention in Cuba as ‚Äújust a question of time‚ÄĚ. Yet overseas military adventures clash with his domestic agenda. Meanwhile, increased military spending, higher interest rates and missing tariff revenue are increasing the risks of a sovereign debt spiral, Gabriel Rubin warns . Success in Iran could backfire ‚Äčat home.
Every financial meltdown brings obscure acronyms to the fore. The Enron scandal taught us about special purpose entities (SPEs). In 2008 collateralised debt obligations (CDOs) were on everybody‚Äôs ‚Äčlips. Today, BDCs are finding a ‚Ā†wider audience. Business development companies are vehicles that allow asset managers to accumulate funds without a set expiration date. Now they‚Äôre under stress. Jonathan Guilford explains what‚Äôs going on .
If you‚Äôre looking for something a bit more optimistic, how about a fresh perspective on Africa? It‚Äôs easy to be gloomy about its prospects. But Joe Studwell, author of ‚ÄúHow Africa Works‚ÄĚ, offers a glass-half-full perspective about the vast continent. He joined me on The Big View , opens new tab to discuss his research and conclusions.
Over on the Viewsroom , opens new tab , George Hay and Yawen Chen joined Aimee Donnellan ‚Ā†and Jonathan Guilford ‚Äčto provide a download on the fast-moving Iran conflict, and the fallout for energy markets.
For years, Goldman Sachs (GS.N) , opens new tab hid its inner workings behind a ‚Äčthick veil of discretion. But the firm has changed, and this week Lloyd Blankfein published ‚ÄúStreetwise: Getting to and Through Goldman Sachs‚ÄĚ, an amusing and at times brutally frank account of his career at the bank he led between 2006 and 2018. It makes for a highly readable book ‚Äď and also reminds us ‚Äčthat Goldman is now a bit less special .
Want to receive The Week in Breakingviews in your inbox every Saturday? Sign up for the newsletter here .
Follow Peter Thal Larsen on Bluesky , opens new tab and LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: Japan should use nuclear plants to offset Iran crisis, opposition party head says
item.description:
TOKYO, March 9 (Reuters) - Japan should operate all of its nuclear power plants available to offset the impact of the ‚ÄčIran war on electricity bills, Yuichiro Tamaki, the ‚ÄĆleader of a Japanese opposition party, said on Monday.
Japan relies on the Middle East for around 95% of its oil supplies ‚Äčand 11% of its liquefied natural gas imports ‚Äčwith around 70% and 6% respectively coming via ‚Ā†the Strait of Hormuz, which is effectively closed due ‚Äčto the war.
"Unless we make full use of nuclear power ‚Äčas a carbon-free power source with less dependence on overseas sources, electricity bills will inevitably rise," Tamaki, who heads the Democratic ‚ÄčParty for the People, said in a statement on ‚ÄčX.
Oil prices surged about 20% on Monday, hitting their highest since ‚ÄĆJuly ‚Ā†2022, as the expanding U.S.-Israeli war with Iran fuelled fears of tighter supply.
Japan shut down all of its 54 nuclear power reactors - which provided roughly 30% of the ‚Äčcountry's electricity - in ‚Äčthe wake ‚Ā†of the 2011 Fukushima Daiichi plant nuclear meltdowns. Thirty-three remain operable but only 15 ‚Äčhave been restarted.
"A policy shift is needed ‚Äčto protect ‚Ā†the lives of the people," said Tamaki, who has consistently supported restarting and expanding nuclear power in Japan.
New Prime ‚Ā†Minister ‚ÄčSanae Takaichi is also staunchly ‚Äčpro-nuclear and Japan's public is also gradually becoming more supportive of nuclear power.

item.title: VIEW Oil surges 20% as Iran war fuels supply fears
item.description:
SINGAPORE, March 9 (Reuters) - Oil prices surged about 20% in early trade on Monday, hitting their highest since July 2022, as the expanding U.S.-Israeli war with Iran led some major Middle East oil producers to cut supplies and on concerns of ‚Äčprolonged disruption to shipping through the Strait of Hormuz.
COMMENTARY:
"I think prices have ‚ÄĆrallied this morning on the reports that Middle East producers are now reducing output due to storage facilities filling up fast."
"I certainly think the spectre now of Middle Eastern producers curtailing output is going to keep those prices elevated. The next flag will be whether it eventually reaches a ‚Äčpoint where they have to start shutting in oil wells, which not only further impacts output but also delays a ‚Äčresponse once the conflict eases. That would potentially sustain those prices for much longer."
"A sudden supply shock reverberates well beyond just who is a net energy exporter and ‚Äčimporter. There are acute supply chain effects beyond how just price eats into margins. Even for a place like Indonesia (where) it is ‚Äčnot unusual to see street protests if pump prices go up."
"Asia takes the brunt of the sharp escalation in oil prices and there are few places to run and hide. The dollar has to be the one outperforming, given Japan and Korea's exposures here and the sharp pain that ‚Äčcan be expected from Brent at $107."
"The market had been complacent about the scope ‚Äčand duration of the war and associated supply disruptions until last Friday.
"Its a case of the oil market who cried wolf. After three years ‚ÄĆof geopolitical ‚Ā†risk premia rising only to fail to translate to supply disruptions, the market became complacent about the current events.
"But this existential Iran war is the energy crisis scenario that has been wargamed for 50 years, finally coming to fore."
"Our baseline scenario is the conflict in Iran will be large but short-lived, though there is a clear risk of a prolonged ‚Äčwar. Among emerging markets, the ‚Äčeconomic impact will be felt ‚Ā†most clearly in the Gulf Cooperation Council, reflecting the shock's adverse impact on trade, logistics, tourism and investment."
"First, Pakistan and India are most vulnerable as they are energy importers with relatively high exposure ‚Äčto the Strait of Hormuz. Second, outside of physical trade disruption, Egypt and Turkiye are ‚Äčmost exposed. This ‚Ā†is due to their high energy import bill, fragile external positions, large energy subsidies and unanchored inflation. Third, commodity producing economies in Sub-Saharan Africa and Latin America are least at risk, including Nigeria, Ghana and Peru."
"The threats to attack refineries ‚Ā†are ‚Äčvery concerning. Because it threatens the worst of all economic worlds. Cutting off ‚Äč15%-20% of the world's oil supply not only slows down every economy globally, but it introduces an inflation impulse. And inflation plus slowing growth is stagflation, and ‚Äčthat's an economic disaster."

item.title: Australians reach for VPNs, find porn sites blocked as online age-restrictions take effect
item.description:
SYDNEY, March 9 (Reuters) - Australians ‚Äčwere downloading virtual private networks (VPNs) in droves, while one of the world's largest pornography distributors said ‚ÄĆit was blocking users as the country rolled out sweeping online age restrictions on Monday.
Last December, Australia became the first country to impose a nationwide ban on teenagers using social media. A separate law now requires AI-powered chatbot services to keep certain content - including ‚Äčpornography, extreme violence and self-harm and eating disorder material - from minors or face fines of up to ‚ÄčA$49.5 million ($34.5 million).
The country also joined Britain, France and dozens of U.S. states requiring ‚Ā†websites which disseminate pornography to verify users are over 18. App stores must also run age checks before ‚Äčallowing downloads of software labelled 18+.
The country's eSafety Commissioner Julie Inman Grant said the measures aimed to afford children ‚Äčthe same protection online as the world expected offline.
"A child today can't walk into a bar and order a drink, they can't stroll into a strip club or browse an adult shop or sit down at a blackjack table in a casino," she told ‚Äčthe Australian Broadcasting Corp.
"This just really brings ... those protections that we put for kids in place to the ‚Äčdigital realm."
Three of the 15 most downloaded free smartphone apps on Monday were VPNs, a chart published by iPhone maker Apple (AAPL.O) , opens new tab ‚ÄĆshowed. The ‚Ā†most downloaded VPN, called VPN - Super Unlimited Proxy, ranked ahead of any social media platform, the chart showed.
VPN - Super Unlimited Proxy did not immediately respond to a Reuters request for comment. All internet-connected devices carry an individual code which discloses their location, and VPNs hide the user's location by assigning a new code to the device.
Canada-based Aylo, ‚Äčowner of a large network ‚Äčof pornography websites, meanwhile ‚Ā†blocked Australians from accessing the platforms RedTube and YouPorn, while presenting a version of Pornhub without explicit content.
All the websites carried a banner saying it was "not currently accepting ‚Äčnew account registrations in your region".
Aylo did not respond to a Reuters request for ‚Äčcomment. The Sydney ‚Ā†Morning Herald quoted an Aylo spokesperson saying the company's Australian users would be "presented with a safe-for-work experience when they view our platforms".
Tom Sulston, deputy chair of advocacy group Digital Rights Watch, said it was unsurprising that people were reaching ‚Ā†for VPNs.
"My ‚Äčhope is that, not only will they discover that this works ‚Äčfor looking at spicier internet sites, but that it's just generally a good idea to use VPNs when you're traversing the internet, because they ‚Äčdo offer you some privacy protections."
($1 = 1.4351 Australian dollars)

item.title: Shots fired at Rihanna's house in Beverly Hills, no injuries reported, media say
item.description:
March 8 (Reuters) - A woman fired numerous shots into the Beverly ‚ÄčHills home of pop music ‚ÄĆstar Rihanna on Sunday, and a round went through a wall ‚Äčof the house, local news ‚Äčoutlets reported Sunday.
The Los Angeles Times ‚Ā†and NBC4, citing a ‚ÄčLos Angeles Police spokesperson, reported ‚Äčthat authorities responded to the shooting at 1:21 p.m. (2021 GMT) Sunday and detained ‚Äča 30-year-old female suspect.
Los ‚ÄčAngeles police and a representative for Rihanna did ‚ÄĆnot ‚Ā†immediately respond to Reuters requests for comment.
Rihanna was at home but no injuries were ‚Äčreported, the ‚ÄčLos ‚Ā†Angeles Times said, citing a source. The ‚Äčnewspaper cited police radio ‚Äčtraffic ‚Ā†that "approximately 10 shots‚ÄĚ had been fired from a vehicle across ‚Ā†the ‚Äčstreet from the ‚Äčproperty‚Äôs gate.

item.title: Bangladesh shuts universities early to save power amid energy crisis
item.description:
DHAKA, March 9 (Reuters) - Bangladesh will close all universities from Monday, bringing forward the Eid al-Fitr holidays as part of emergency measures to conserve ‚Äčelectricity and fuel amid a worsening energy crisis linked to ‚ÄĆthe conflict in the Middle East.
Authorities said the decision applies to all public and private universities across the country, a move that officials said will not only ‚Äčreduce electricity consumption but also ease traffic congestion, which leads ‚Äčto fuel wastage.
Officials said university campuses consume large amounts of ‚Ā†electricity for residential halls, classrooms, laboratories and air conditioning, and the ‚Äčearly closure would help ease pressure on the country‚Äôs strained power ‚Äčsystem.
Government and private schools in Bangladesh are already closed for the Islamic holy month of Ramadan, meaning most educational institutions across the country will now remain shut ‚Äčduring the period.
The move comes as Bangladesh faces mounting uncertainty over ‚Äčfuel and gas supplies following disruptions to global energy markets caused by the ongoing ‚ÄĆconflict ‚Ā†in the Middle East.
The country, which relies on imports for 95% of its energy needs, imposed daily limits on Friday on fuel sales after panic buying and stockpiling.
As part of broader austerity measures, the government has also ‚Äčasked all foreign‚ÄĎcurriculum schools and ‚Äčprivate coaching ‚Ā†centres to suspend operations during this period to limit electricity use.
Severe gas shortages have already forced Bangladesh to halt ‚Äčoperations at four of its five state‚ÄĎrun fertiliser ‚Äčfactories, redirecting ‚Ā†available gas to power plants to avoid widespread outages.
The country has also bought LNG from the spot market at sharply higher prices while seeking additional ‚Ā†cargoes ‚Äčto bridge supply gaps.
‚ÄúWe are doing everything ‚Äčwe can to reduce consumption and ensure stability in power, fuel and import supplies,‚ÄĚ a ‚Äčsenior energy ministry official said.

item.title: Ukraine sent drone experts to protect US bases in Jordan, Zelenskiy tells NYT
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - Ukraine sent interceptor drones and ‚Äča team of ‚ÄĆdrone experts to protect U.S. military ‚Äčbases in ‚ÄčJordan, President Volodymyr Zelenskiy ‚Ā†said in ‚Äčan interview with The ‚ÄčNew York Times published on Monday.
The U.S. ‚Äčmade the ‚Äčrequest for help on Thursday, ‚ÄĆand ‚Ā†the Ukrainian team left the next day, Zelenskiy ‚Äčsaid ‚Äčadding ‚Ā†that it was expected to ‚Äčarrive in ‚Äčthe ‚Ā†Middle East soon, according to ‚Ā†the ‚Äčreport.

item.title: Indonesia searches for missing after landfill collapse kills four
item.description:
JAKARTA, March 9 (Reuters) - Rescuers are still searching for five missing people after a large stack of ‚Äčgarbage collapsed at Indonesia's biggest landfill ‚ÄĆsite over the weekend, killing at least four people, an official said on Monday.
The collapse took place ‚Äčat the Bantargebang landfill on Sunday, located ‚Äčin the region of Bekasi on the ‚Ā†outskirts of the capital Jakarta, said Desiana ‚ÄčKartika Bahari, head of the local rescue agency.
It ‚Äčwas likely triggered by the heavy rain that hit the area from Saturday evening, she told Reuters.
"It was ‚Äčraining all day even from (Saturday) evening and ‚Äčthe mountain of garbage was unstable," Bahari said.
Some trucks were ‚ÄĆunloading ‚Ā†garbage when the collapse took place, and there was also a food stall near the site.
"The missing people are the truck drivers and ‚Äčscavengers," she ‚Äčsaid, adding ‚Ā†that there there could be more people buried in the landfill.
Four ‚Äčpeople survived the accident.
More than 200 rescuers, ‚Äčincluding ‚Ā†police and military personnel and 17 excavators, have been deployed since Monday morning to search ‚Ā†for ‚Äčthe missing.
Bantargebang covers around 110 ‚Äčhectares and receives around 6,500 tons-7,000 tons of garbage per ‚Äčday.

item.title: Lawyers for Bondi Beach shooting suspect seek gag order to protect family
item.description:
SYDNEY, March 9 (Reuters) - Lawyers for a man accused of carrying out a deadly shooting on Sydney's Bondi Beach sought a court order ‚Äčon Monday to stop media naming his family, saying their ‚ÄĆsafety was at risk.
Police allege 24-year-old Naveed Akram opened fire on a Jewish Hanukkah celebration on December 14, killing 15, in Australia's worst mass shooting in decades.
His ‚Äčfather, Sajid Akram, who police also accuse of carrying out ‚Äčthe shooting, was shot dead by police at the scene.
Police ‚Ā†say the men were inspired by the Islamic State militant group ‚Äčto carry out the attack using several high-powered weapons the elder man had legally ‚Äčacquired.
Lawyers for Naveed Akram on Monday requested a court in Sydney prohibit the publication of the names of his mother, brother and sister, as well as their ‚Äčhome address and places of work and schooling. Magistrate Greg Grogan ‚Äčextended an interim suppression order until next month, citing the global media interest in ‚ÄĆthe ‚Ā†case.
Akram faces a total of 59 charges over the attack, including 15 counts of murder, 40 counts of wounding with intent to murder and a terror offence. He is yet to enter a plea.
The case ‚Äčis expected to ‚Äčbriefly return ‚Ā†to court next week, before a longer hearing on April 8, where the prosecution will outline its brief ‚Äčof evidence.
The Bondi attack shocked a country with strict ‚Äčgun laws ‚Ā†and fuelled calls for tougher controls and stronger action against antisemitism.
Australia last month launched a government-backed inquiry into antisemitism and social cohesion in the country ‚Ā†which ‚Äčis expected to report its findings by ‚ÄčDecember this year.
The government has already responded by tightening gun laws and introducing new legislation ‚Äčagainst hate speech.

item.title: Governments' actions in response to oil price surge and the escalating Middle East conflict
item.description:
March 9 - Oil prices have soared while share markets have skidded on fears that the escalating U.S.-Israeli war on ‚ÄčIran will squeeze energy supplies and hamstring industries ‚ÄĆaround the world.
Following are actions that governments are taking or plan to take to reduce the impact of the war on ‚Äčtheir economies.
South Korean President ‚ÄčLee Jae Myung said on Monday that authorities ‚Ā†would cap domestic fuel prices for the first time ‚Äčin nearly 30 years. The country will also look ‚Äčfor sources of energy beyond supplies shipped via the Strait of Hormuz, and a 100 trillion won ($67 billion) market-stabilisation programme should be ‚Äčexpanded if needed, he added.
The Japanese government instructed a national oil ‚ÄĆreserve ‚Ā†storage site to prepare for a possible release of crude, Akira Nagatsuma, a member of the Centrist Reform Alliance opposition party, told Reuters on Sunday.
Details such as ‚Äčthe timing of ‚Äčthe release ‚Ā†remain unclear, Nagatsuma said.
Vietnam is planning to remove import tariffs ‚Äčon fuels to ensure supplies amid disruptions, ‚Äčthe ‚Ā†government said, adding that the measure is expected to last until the end of April.
Bangladesh ‚Ā†will close ‚Äčall universities from Monday, bringing forward ‚Äčthe Eid al-Fitr holidays as part of emergency measures to conserve ‚Äčelectricity and fuel.

item.title: How Europe can play a bad hand over Iran
item.description:
LONDON, March 9 (Reuters Breakingviews) - How many wake-up calls does Europe need? Donald Trump‚Äôs first administration did not do the trick, despite the U.S. president‚Äôs anti-European rhetoric. Nor did Russia‚Äôs full-scale invasion of Ukraine four years ago. Nor did Trump‚Äôs re-election in 2024. Now in quick succession this year, Washington has pummelled Venezuela, threatened Greenland and attacked Iran.
It is hard to see anything good for Europe from the ‚ÄčUnited States-Israeli attack on the Islamic Republic. It strengthens Russia and weakens Ukraine, threatening Europe‚Äôs own security. It undermines the global rule of law, which is valuable for the region given that it is ‚ÄĆnot a geopolitical power.
The latest Middle Eastern conflict is also threatening Europe‚Äôs economy. Much, of course, will depend on how long the conflict goes on ‚ÄĒ in particular whether Iran can keep up a blockade of oil and gas through the Strait of Hormuz. As a hydrocarbon importer, Europe is vulnerable to high energy prices. Its mostly highly indebted governments are not well placed to cushion a blow to either businesses or consumers. Meanwhile, a descent into civil war in Iran, or a further crackdown on internal dissent, could unleash a wave of immigration, with some of its 93 million ‚Äčcitizens heading towards Europe.
In the short run, there is little the region can do but suffer and squirm. A principled response would have been to condemn Trump‚Äôs attack as contrary to international law while at the same time ‚Äčdenouncing Iran‚Äôs nuclear weapons programme. But Europe is too dependent on U.S. military support to do that, especially given the Russian threat. It is also increasingly dependent on American energy ‚Ā†since its gas purchases from Moscow are down dramatically and now Qatar is offline .
Spain has criticised the attack on Iran.
