Iran has not informed the ‚ÄčIAEA of the ‚Ā†status or whereabouts of its highly enriched uranium since the June attacks, nor has it let IAEA inspectors return to its bombed facilities.
Iran's nuclear programme is ‚Äčone reason Israel and the U.S. have given for their current attacks on ‚ÄčIran , arguing ‚Ā†that it was getting too close to being able to produce a bomb, despite Trump saying in June that U.S. strikes had obliterated the programme. The IAEA has said it has no credible indication of a coordinated nuclear ‚Ā†weapons ‚Äčprogramme.
All three Iranian uranium-enrichment plants known to have been operating - two at ‚ÄčNatanz and one at Fordow - were destroyed or badly damaged in June.
"There is an amount (of 60% uranium) in Natanz also, which ‚Äčwe believe is still there," Grossi said.

item.title: Airline shares battered, airfares surge as Iran war pushes oil above $100
item.description:
HONG KONG/FRANKFURT, March 9 (Reuters) - Airline stocks were hammered on ‚ÄčMonday, while airfares soared as the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran sent oil prices surging, sparking fears of a deep travel slump and the potential for the widespread grounding ‚ÄĆof planes.
Oil prices jumped 15% to above $105 a barrel, hitting levels not seen since 2022 as some major producers cut supplies and fears of prolonged shipping disruptions gripped the market. At one point, Brent crude futures jumped as much as 29%.
Some jet fuel prices have doubled since the start of the conflict, piling pressure on carriers already navigating tight airspace as pilots reroute to avoid the Middle East conflict and thousands of stranded passengers try to leave the ‚Äčregion.
"Absent near-term relief, airlines around the world could be forced to ground thousands of aircraft while some of the industry's financially weakest carriers could halt operations," Deutsche analysts said ‚Äčin a note to clients.
In Asia, airline shares tumbled, with the worst-hit, including Korean Air Lines (003490.KS) , opens new tab , which slid 8.6%, Air ‚Ā†New Zealand (AIR.NZ) , opens new tab down 7.8% and Hong Kong's Cathay Pacific (0293.HK) , opens new tab which dropped 5%.
In Europe, Air France KLM (AIRF.PA) , opens new tab , British Airways-owner IAG (ICAG.L) , opens new tab , Wizz Air (WIZZ.L) , opens new tab and Lufthansa (LHAG.DE) , opens new tab fell between 2.5% and 6% in morning trade.
Major U.S. ‚Äčairlines shares were down about 1% to 5% in afternoon trading with JetBlue Airways (JBLU.O) , opens new tab down 5.35% followed by American Airlines (AAL.O) , opens new tab down 3.44%.
Underscoring that pain on the consumer side were jumps in ticket prices. Direct ‚Äčflights from Seoul to London on March 11 with Korean Air Lines, for example, leapt to $4,359, from $564 seven days earlier, according to Google Flights data. Flights from Los Angeles to Lima on LATAM Airlines (LTM.SN) , opens new tab rose to $2,125 compared to $499 in the same period.
"The issue for the airlines now is that travel demand may be curtailed as costs become prohibitive for leisure travellers and as some companies start to limit business travel due to the uncertain outlook," said ‚ÄčLorraine Tan, director of equity research, Asia at Morningstar.
The impact of high airfares could limit travel demand for much of 2026, Tan added.
Fuel is the second-largest expense for air carriers after labour, typically ‚Äčaccounting for a fifth to a quarter of operating expenses. Some major Asian and European airlines have oil hedging in place, but U.S. airlines largely stopped the practice over the last two decades.
"We assume the airlines are ‚ÄĆable to recapture ‚Ā†a portion of the spike in fuel prices, but it's hard to envision margin expansion this year barring a rapid decline in energy prices," said Tom Fitzgerald, TD Cowen vice president of equity research on six major U.S. airlines and Air Canada.
A one-way trip to Quebec City from Newark on March 11 aboard Air Canada nearly tripled to $1,499 compared to a week ago, according to data from Google Flights.
High prices could have severe implications for the industry.
"If crude is rising 20%, jet fuel is rising several times more as it is even more scarce, adding significant cost ‚Äčto operations together with crew resources, which are ‚Äčstretched due to longer flying times when ‚Ā†airspace is closed," said Subhas Menon, head of the Association of Asia Pacific Airlines.
The Deutsche analysts noted that a sharp spike in jet fuel costs in 2005 in the aftermath of hurricanes Katrina and Rita resulted in widespread and significant damage to the industry, including major airlines Delta and Northwest ‚Äčfiling for Chapter 11 bankruptcy that year.
Since February 28, when the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran started, through March 9, more than 40,000 flights to ‚Äčand from the Middle ‚Ā†East have been cancelled, according to data from Cirium.
With airspace severely constrained, airlines have been forced to reroute flights, carry extra fuel or make additional refuelling stops to guard against sudden diversions or longer flight paths through safer corridors.
Combined, Emirates, Qatar Airways and Etihad normally fly about one-third of the passengers from Europe to Asia and more than half of all passengers from Europe to Australia, New Zealand and nearby Pacific ‚Ā†Islands, according to ‚ÄčCirium.
Flights to Iraq, Syria, Lebanon and Jordan by Turkish Airlines (THYAO.IS) , opens new tab , AJet, Pegasus (PGSUS.IS) , opens new tab and SunExpress have been cancelled until March ‚Äč13, Turkish Transport Minister Abdulkadir Uraloglu said on Sunday.

item.title: Exclusive: Canadian oil tycoon backs South Bow pipeline to boost US exports
item.description:
CALGARY, March 9 (Reuters) - The Canadian tycoon who heads one of North America's fastest growing oil companies told Reuters he supports ‚Äča proposal to potentially revive parts of the former Keystone XL pipeline and wants to see Canada use the project as ‚ÄĆleverage in upcoming trade negotiations with the United States.
Still, Adam Waterous, CEO of Strathcona Resources (SCR.TO) , opens new tab , said he would prefer a new West Coast pipeline to increase access to Asian markets.
The comments by Waterous, one of Canada's most aggressive oil industry dealmakers, are the first public statements by a Canadian oil shipper in favour of a new pipeline proposal led by Canadian company ‚ÄčSouth Bow (SOBO.TO) , opens new tab that could increase the country's crude exports to the U.S. by more than 12%.
They are an early indication that the project ‚ÄĒ which would require both ‚Äča green light from U.S. President Donald Trump and the construction of additional links to U.S. refining hubs ‚ÄĒ has at ‚Ā†least some level of commercial support from Canadian oil sands producers at a time when the U.S.-Israel war on Iran has disrupted international supply and sent global ‚Äčcrude prices soaring.
The plan involves a different route through the U.S. than the previous Keystone XL pipeline, which was canceled by former U.S. President Joe Biden ‚Äčin 2021 after years of Indigenous and environmental opposition, but would use some of that project's already built pipe in Alberta.
South Bow launched a competitive bidding process last week offering 450,000 barrels per day of contracted pipeline capacity from Hardisty, Alberta to multiple U.S. delivery points including Cushing, Oklahoma, and destinations on the U.S. Gulf Coast, to gauge potential commercial use.
Waterous ‚Äčdeclined to say if Strathcona will commit barrels to the South Bow project, citing ongoing commercial negotiations. But he pointed to his company's stated goal to expand ‚Äčits own oil production from 125,000 barrels per day in 2026 to up to 300,000 bpd by 2035.
"I've been a very early advocate of this project," Waterous said.
"And we ‚ÄĆare looking ‚Ā†for egress now," he said, referring to oil shippers' ability to transport their product to market.
But Waterous said the South Bow proposal is not the Canadian oil industry's first choice for bolstering crude exports. Currently more than 90% of Canada's oil exports go to the United States, and Waterous said Canadian oil shippers have long wanted greater market diversification.
Canadian oil companies would prefer another pipeline to the Pacific, to supplement the existing Trans Mountain pipeline which runs from Alberta to the British Columbia coast ‚Äčand offers an outlet to Asian ‚Äčmarkets, Waterous said.
Four other large Canadian ‚Ā†oil sands producers did not respond to a Reuters request for comment on whether they support the South Bow proposal.
The Alberta government has said it will submit an application for a new crude oil pipeline to the west ‚Äčcoast for fast-track approval by the federal government, but no private company has said it will build the project.
The ‚Äčmain benefit to ‚Ā†the South Bow proposal, Waterous said, is that it can likely be built faster and more cheaply than a new West Coast pipeline, while also carrying potential political benefits for Canada.
He said Canada should try to leverage Trump's previously stated comments that he would like to see the Keystone XL project built.
Prime Minister Mark Carney has also raised with ‚Ā†Trump the prospect of ‚Äčreviving the Keystone XL oil pipeline from Alberta to the United States as part of ‚Äčhis efforts to ease trade tensions between the two countries.
"The utility of a pipeline to the south is that it is a tool to achieve a tariff agreement to protect principally Canada's steel, ‚Äčauto and aluminum sectors, while also allowing the Canadian oil sector to grow," Waterous said.

item.title: Peru's pipeline disruption tests industrial resilience; businesses blanch at remote work order
item.description:
LIMA, March 9 (Reuters) - Peru's business groups are clashing with the new interim government over sweeping emergency measures imposed after a rupture in the country's ‚Äčmain natural‚ÄĎgas pipeline triggered the worst energy crisis in two decades, an early test of President Jose Balcazar‚Äôs ‚ÄĆinterim mandate weeks before national elections.
The breakdown of Transportadora de Gas del Peru's pipeline, the backbone of the system that supplies nearly half of Peru‚Äôs electricity and most of its LPG, has forced widespread gas rationing, pushing up energy prices and exposing long‚ÄĎstanding vulnerabilities in the Andean nation's energy ‚Äčsystem.
Balcazar, appointed on February 18 after Congress ousted his predecessor, now faces a high‚ÄĎstakes leadership test. More than ‚Äča thousand firms have struggled with halted gas production, and business associations have called for the rollback ‚Ā†of emergency directives.
Authorities say gas flows may not resume until mid‚ÄĎMarch.
The outage could stoke inflation, which has remained among the ‚Äčlowest in the world despite years of political turmoil.
"It can hit inflation, but in a heterogeneous way depending on how energy ‚Äčaffects each activity,‚ÄĚ said former Economy Minister Alonso Segura.
Inflation reached 0.69% in February and could exceed the 0.81% recorded in March last year, Segura said.
Natural gas is used to produce 70% of the LPG consumed domestically. Some 2.1 million households rely on piped gas and about one million vehicles ‚Äčrun on natural gas or LPG.
Peru is the world‚Äôs third-largest copper producer.
Former Energy and Mines Minister Carlos Herrera said the rupture ‚Äčunderscored Peru‚Äôs vulnerability after an alternative pipeline project was canceled in 2017 when the Brazilian construction company Odebrecht was unable to secure financing ‚ÄĆwhile being ‚Ā†investigated in a case involving bribes paid to secure public works contracts in Peru.
"This crisis highlights the need to diversify the country‚Äôs energy matrix," Herrera said. "The serious problem we have is that we haven‚Äôt made exploratory efforts for natural gas over time either."
Industry groups said more than 1,000 companies have been affected, with some halting production. Felipe James, head of the National Society of Industry (SNI), ‚Äčsaid emergency rules allowing firms ‚Äčto switch temporarily to alternative ‚Ā†fuels will be costly and not all companies are equipped to make the change.
"Not all can switch... they are not prepared," James said.
On Friday, the government ordered public employees in Lima to ‚Äčwork remotely for a week and encouraged private firms to do the same. Schools and ‚Äčuniversities will shift ‚Ā†to virtual classes during the contingency.
Business associations criticized the measures, saying they amount to a partial shutdown of the economy.
"Stopping the country can never be the solution. We demand this decision be reversed," said COMEX, the foreign trade guild.
Peru's mining and energy chamber said it ‚Ā†was working "intensely" ‚Äčto mitigate the crisis and seeking to import LPG in "record time."
Spokespeople for ‚Äčthe presidency and the Ministry of Energy and Mines did not respond to a request for comment.
The TGP gas pipeline began operating in 2004 and also ‚Äčenables the export of natural gas to Japan, South Korea and China.

item.title: Two men charged with terrorism after homemade bomb thrown at anti-Islam protesters in New York
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 9 (Reuters) - Two men accused of igniting homemade bombs at a chaotic anti-Islam protest outside the New York City mayor's mansion told police they were inspired by Islamic State, the Islamist militant group, and were charged on Monday with ‚Äčfederal terrorism-related crimes, according to court records.
Emir Balat, 18, and Ibrahim Kayumi, 19, are accused of traveling from Pennsylvania with ‚ÄĆdangerous improvised explosive devices to Manhattan on Saturday, where a small group of far-right activists had planned the anti-Muslim rally outside Gracie Mansion against Zohran Mamdani, New York City's first Muslim mayor.
Balat was captured in photographs and video lighting a small IED and throwing it towards the anti-Muslim protesters before lighting a second device ‚Äčhanded to him by Kayumi and dropping it, according to the criminal complaint. Police were able to intercept the smoking devices, ‚Äčduct-taped jars containing screws and bolts and the explosive substance TATP, and no one was hurt.
After police arrested ‚Ā†them, they said they were inspired by Islamic State, and Balat told police that he wanted to carry out an attack "even bigger" than ‚Äčthe Boston Marathon bombing in 2013 that killed three people, according to the criminal complaint filed in the U.S. district court in Manhattan on ‚ÄčMonday.
Mehdi Essmidi, who represents Balat, did not immediately reply to a request for comment. Kayumi's lawyer Michael Arthus declined to comment.
Speaking alongside police outside Gracie Mansion on Monday, Mamdani condemned the violence. He and his wife were not home at the time, because they had known about the "Stop the Muslim takeover" rally organized by ‚Äčfar-right activist Jake Lang more than a week in advance. More than 100 counter-protesters gathered nearby, the vast majority peaceful, saying they were there ‚Äčto "run Nazis out" of New York.
"New York City will never tolerate violence whether from protests or counter-protests," Mamdani told reporters. He said while he thought the ‚ÄĆfar-right anti-Muslim ‚Ā†rally was "vile", he defended the "sacred right" of protest, even for objectionable speech.
Police said it was the first time an improvised bomb was deployed in New York City in nearly a decade. In 2017, Akayed Ullah, who also cited Islamic State as inspiration, ignited a homemade bomb strapped to his body in a passage near the Times Square subway station, wounding only himself.
Balat and Kayumi are each charged with five federal crimes, ‚Äčaccording to a complaint filed by ‚Äčthe U.S. attorney's office in Manhattan, ‚Ā†including use of a weapon of mass destruction, attempting to provide material support to a designated foreign terrorist organization, and three charges related to the illegal possession or transportation of explosives.
During their separate police interviews, ‚ÄčBalat asked for a piece of paper and wrote on it he had pledged allegiance to Islamic ‚ÄčState, while Kayumi ‚Ā†told police he watched Islamic State videos on his phone, according to the criminal complaint.
A third man, Ian McGinnis, was arrested and appeared in a New York state court on Sunday accused of squirting pepper spray or a similar chemical irritant into the faces of two of the counter-protesters opposing ‚Ā†the anti-Islam ‚Äčrally, according to the Manhattan district attorney's office and police. McGinnis faces six charges, including ‚Äčthird-degree assault.
Police in New York have been on alert since the U.S. and Israel attacked Iran, but did not say there was a sign of Saturday's attack being linked ‚Äčto the war.

item.title: Trump says war against Iran is 'very complete,' CBS News reports
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 9 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump ‚Äčthinks the war against Iran "is ‚ÄĆvery complete" and that Washington was "very far ahead" of his initial four ‚Äčto five week estimated ‚Äčtime frame, according to a CBS News ‚Ā†reporter on X, who cited an ‚Äčinterview with him.
"I think the ‚Äčwar is very complete, pretty much. They have no navy, no communications, they‚Äôve ‚Äčgot no Air Force," Trump ‚Äčwas quoted as saying by CBS News' ‚ÄĆWhite ‚Ā†House correspondent Weijia Jiang.
Trump also said the U.S. is "very far" ahead of his initial 4-5 ‚Äčweek estimated ‚Äčtime frame ‚Ā†for the war.
On Iran's new Supreme leader Mojtaba ‚ÄčKhamenei, Trump told CBS ‚ÄčNews: "I ‚Ā†have no message for him." Trump said he has someone in ‚Ā†mind ‚Äčto replace Khamenei, ‚Äčbut did not elaborate.

item.title: First Brands row hints at banks‚Äô shadow exposure
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 9 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Cockroach troubles tend to extend further than they first appear. JPMorgan boss Jamie Dimon likened high-profile credit ‚Äčblowups to the sneaky insects, implying that deeper problems lurk. And now, six months after heavily indebted auto-parts retailer ‚ÄĆFirst Brands toppled, regional lender Western Alliance (WAL.N) , opens new tab has disclosed , opens new tab a $126 million hit from the mess. It never lent directly to First Brands, but instead to a fund run by Wall Street financiers at Jefferies (JEF.N) , opens new tab . It‚Äôs just one of many smaller banks piling into the murky world of shadow finance.
First Brands‚Äô surprise bankruptcy last ‚Äčyear sent a chill through credit markets. Federal prosecutors alleged , opens new tab that it pledged assets multiple times over to secure billions of ‚Äčborrowings, largely from non‚ÄĎbank lenders. It was not an isolated case, with the government also indicting , opens new tab the ‚Ā†founder of sub-prime auto lender Tricolor on similar claims. Jefferies, meanwhile, also lent to now-teetering real-estate lender Market Financial Solutions.
Western Alliance claims a ‚Äčdeliberate breach of contract, saying that Jefferies has ceased to pay $126 million outstanding on loans to special-purpose vehicles that held First Brands‚Äô ‚Äčaccounts receivable. Jefferies said , opens new tab on Monday that these are non-recourse loans, with no guarantee of payment from the SPVs‚Äô parents, and that the suit lacks merit.
Whatever the case, shares of Western Alliance and Jefferies have tanked 11% and 14%, respectively, since Thursday. Morgan Stanley analysts downgraded their recommendation on Jefferies‚Äô shares, citing ‚Äčthe litigation.
Western Alliance is a relative pipsqueak, at roughly $8 billion in market value. Yet many similarly sized institutions have been swept up ‚Äčin the flood of lending to lightly regulated firms that the Federal Reserve labels ‚Äúnon-depository financial institutions.‚ÄĚ Lending to these shadow banks has climbed to $1.9 trillion, ‚ÄĆup 180% ‚Ā†from $680 billion in 2021, according to Fed data. This growth tracks the vertiginous rise of private credit and asset-backed lending , which have increasingly displaced traditional banks since the 2008 financial crisis. Providing leverage is a way to get a cut of the action back.
The question is how much risk this poses. Banks‚Äô equity is a loss-absorbing buffer, taking the hit if loans go awry en masse. Yet 40 ‚ÄčU.S. lenders with a combined $4.7 ‚Äčtrillion in assets now have ‚Ā†lent the equivalent of more than 100% of their total equity capital to shadow banks, up from 29 banks holding $1.1 trillion two years ago, according to data from KBRA Financial Intelligence. Nearly all borrowers ‚Äčare current on their payments, and much of the activity relates to the mortgage market, which ‚Äčenjoys effective government ‚Ā†support. Yet the territory in which cockroaches can breed is growing.
Follow Stephen Gandel on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .

item.title: Iran war boosts oil price, but oil major shares are stuck on the sidelines
item.description:
LONDON/HOUSTON, March 9 (Reuters) - Oil prices on Monday climbed to the highest level since 2022, but shares of major producers like Shell (SHEL.L) , opens new tab ‚Äčand Exxon Mobil (XOM.N) , opens new tab have so far seen limited gains since the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran broke out late last month, ‚ÄĆsuggesting that traders think the pain in the market may be short term.
While crude oil futures have risen more than 40% since Israel and the U.S. launched joint airstrikes on February 28, the iShares Global Energy ETF - which tracks global energy companies - is up around 2%. That suggests that any immediate gains from increased prices will be temporary, ‚Äčor offset by reduced production.
"The market is anticipating a swift end to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz and a subsequent ‚Äčcollapse in oil prices back to normalized levels," said James West, head of energy and power research at Melius ‚Ā†Research. "The rally in oil prices is primarily contained to near-term spot prices rather than longer-dated crude oil futures."
Brent crude futures and U.S. West Texas Intermediate ‚Äčcrude futures rose by around 30% to start the week, as the war on Iran has disrupted the passage of traffic in the Strait of Hormuz, ‚Äčoil and natural gas exports from the Middle East and has forced production stoppages.
At the same time, Brent's front-month contract hit an all-time high at around $36 per barrel over its 6-month delivery contract, indicating near-term supply tightness.
The rally conjured oil futures' 2008 all-time highs at around $147 per barrel, when tension between the West and Iran over its nuclear program, ‚Äča weak U.S. dollar and inflationary factors pushed prices higher. Continued growth in China and other emerging economies was also expected to offset the ‚Äčimpact of any fall in consumption in developed countries.
However, just months after the all-time high, the 2008 financial crisis and concerns around an overbought market sunk oil to ‚ÄĆbelow $40 per ‚Ā†barrel, at the time the lowest since 2004.
"Go back to 2008," said David Hewitt, senior consultant at Hewitt Energy Perspectives. "Oil stocks followed crude's rally until about $100 then almost completely disconnected as it went to $147."
"The market was right then - $147 per barrel rapidly became $30," he added. "It's a bit of the same now."
Shell shares rose as much as 4.9% on Monday from their close on Feb. 27, while Chevron (CVX.N) , opens new tab gained 2.6% and Exxon gained 0.9% over the same period. ‚ÄčBP (BP.L) , opens new tab gained 7.8%.
Deferred oil contracts are ‚Äčnot as high as the ‚Ā†spot price, signaling that investors do not expect the supply disruption to last, said Simon Lack, portfolio manager of the Catalyst Energy Infrastructure Fund.
U.S. shale producers, which have seen a bigger boost in share prices than the global ‚Äčoil majors, may be benefitting because U.S. energy supply and infrastructure are safe and insulated from the risk, ‚Äčprompting investors to place ‚Ā†a higher value on those companies, he added.
Diamondback (FANG.O) , opens new tab , the largest independent producer in the Permian basin, has seen its shares gain as much as 7% on Monday from their close on February 27.
Simon Wong, a portfolio manager at Gabelli Funds, said share prices of Diamondback, APA Corp (APA.O) , opens new tab and Occidental (OXY.N) , opens new tab had risen less ‚Ā†year-to-date compared with ‚ÄčExxon and Chevron and are now beginning to catch up.
He also believes that the ‚Äčhigher crude prices are temporary and will likely moderate when the disruptions are over.

item.title: Surging oil drives worries for US stock investors
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 9 (Reuters) - A stunning surge in oil prices above $100 a barrel is rattling U.S. stock investors, as they brace for an even sharper rise in energy prices that could stagger the economy and further damage equities.
The 50% jump in U.S. crude to its highest ‚Äčlevel in more than three years is among the most significant financial consequences of the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran, as investors continue to calibrate economic and market fallout following the ‚ÄĆattacks launched just over a week ago.
"It's a shock," said Michael Reynolds, vice president of investment strategy at Glenmede. "It's out of left field, and people and investors are having to figure out what it means in real time as it's unfolding."
Higher oil and gas prices stand to drive up costs for energy-intensive companies, erode the discretionary spending budgets of consumers and lead to worries about inflation that could stop the Federal Reserve from lowering interest rates.
Reynolds and other investors were scrambling to ‚Äčgame out scenarios should oil reach heights that were not on the horizon just days earlier.
U.S. and Brent crude on Monday both broke above $100 a barrel, which investors have pointed to as a ‚Äčlevel that could create more stock turbulence. The commodities at one point on Monday neared $120. U.S. crude settled at $67.02 on February 27, the last session before the ‚Ā†U.S.-Israeli strikes.
Meanwhile, stock volatility has spiked. The Cboe Volatility Index (.VIX) , opens new tab on Monday topped 30 for the first time in nearly a year, up from below 20 in late February.
While the declines for U.S. stocks have been moderate ‚Äčin comparison to other global regions, the benchmark S&P 500 (.SPX) , opens new tab was last down nearly 4% from its late January all-time closing high, after paring steeper declines earlier in Monday's session.
Strategists at Yardeni Research said last Tuesday they ‚Äčexpected a 10% stock market correction.
"Now we can't rule out a bear market and even a recession," the firm said in a note on Sunday.
OIL AND STOCKS: MORE TIGHTLY LINKED
As oil prices have surged, the moves have become more tightly linked to the stock market.
The 20-day correlation between the S&P 500 and U.S. crude stood at -0.813 as of Monday morning, according to LSEG data, a strong inverse relationship that shows they have been tending to move in opposite directions.
Deutsche Bank strategists, watching whether the ‚ÄčIran situation could prompt a larger risk-off move, said in a note on Monday that the oil price shock ranks "among the more serious of history," but that investors are pricing in "a short rather than protracted conflict."
While stocks ‚Äčand oil are typically thought of as separate markets, stock investors have watched oil's trajectory quite closely at times historically, especially after extreme price moves.
In early 2022, a jump in oil over $120 a barrel following the start of the conflict ‚ÄĆin Ukraine coincided ‚Ā†with declines in stock prices. In 2015-2016, stock investors worried that low oil prices, with U.S. crude dropping below $30 a barrel, represented a sign of broad economic weakness.
Focus is again on the economic ramifications of rising oil prices.
Each 10% increase in the oil price stands to translate to roughly a 15 to 20 basis-point drag on GDP growth, according to JPMorgan economists.
"Effects also could be non-linear, with larger oil price spikes producing an even larger hit to growth," the JPMorgan economists said in a note.
The JPMorgan economists and other analysts cautioned the economic fallout likely hinges on how long crude prices stay elevated.
For now, the jump in ‚Äčcrude is driving up prices at the gas pump. ‚ÄčThe national average for gasoline rose to $3.478 a ‚Ā†gallon on Monday, up from $2.902 a month ago, according to data from the motorists' group AAA. That is the highest level since the summer of 2024, the group said.
"From an average consumer perspective, oil prices are about ... as visceral as it gets just because of filling up your gas tank," said Kevin Gordon, head of macro ‚Äčresearch and strategy at Charles Schwab.
Indeed, shares of companies most reliant on discretionary spending are among the potentially most vulnerable to higher oil prices.
Airlines, for which ‚Äčfuel accounts for 20% to ‚Ā†25% of unit costs, according to Morningstar, have seen their shares hammered with the S&P 1500 passenger airlines index (.SPCOMAIR) , opens new tab down 15% since the conflict began.
Of course, higher oil prices could benefit some corners of the market. The S&P 500 energy sector (.SPNY) , opens new tab has gained 1% since late February, while the broader S&P 500 has fallen over 2% in that time.
And some investors are wary that the situation could change at any moment.
In a note ‚Ā†on Monday, Raymond ‚ÄčJames Chief Investment Officer Larry Adam said the wealth management firm expects the conflict to be "relatively short-lived." The firm maintained a year-end ‚Äčprice target for U.S. crude at $55-$60 a barrel.
Investors are also mindful of how U.S. President Donald Trump has changed course on market-sensitive policies during his term. In particular, there was his softening of his blanket "Liberation Day" tariff policies last April that caused a sharp rebound in ‚Äčinitially battered asset prices.
"We're one ceasefire agreement headline away from all of this just reversing in a very aggressive fashion," Gordon said.

item.title: Australia to grant visa to five Iranian women soccer players after Trump appeal
item.description:
SYDNEY, March 10 (Reuters) - Australia on Tuesday agreed to provide ‚Äčvisas to five Iranian ‚ÄĆwomen's soccer players to stay in the country, after ‚ÄčU.S. President Donald Trump ‚Äčurged Canberra not to ‚Ā†send them back to ‚ÄčTehran due to concerns ‚Äčover their safety.
Home Affairs Minister Tony Burke told reporters the other ‚Äčmembers of the ‚Äčteam were welcome to stay back ‚ÄĆin ‚Ā†Australia. The team is in Australia for the Asian Cup tournament.
Trump said ‚Äčon Monday ‚Äčhe ‚Ā†had spoken to Australian Prime Minister ‚ÄčAnthony Albanese about the ‚ÄčIran ‚Ā†women's soccer team after reports that five players ‚Ā†had ‚Äčsought asylum ‚Äčin Australia.

item.title: US weighs easing Russian oil sanctions to cool global price surge, sources say
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - President Donald Trump 's administration is considering reducing oil sanctions on Russia to help cool ‚Äča surge in global energy prices triggered by ‚ÄĆthe U.S. and Israeli war on Iran , with an announcement possible as soon as Monday, according to three sources familiar with the planning.
The ‚Äčmove would be intended to boost world supplies ‚Äčof oil amid massive disruptions to Middle East shipments ‚Ā†from the expanding conflict, but could also complicate U.S. ‚Äčefforts to deprive Russia of revenue for its war in ‚ÄčUkraine.
The discussions could include broad sanctions relief as well as more targeted options that would allow certain countries, such as India, to ‚Äčbuy Russian oil without fear of U.S. penalties, including ‚Äčtariffs, the sources told Reuters, speaking on condition of anonymity.
The United States ‚ÄĆlast ‚Ā†week allowed India to temporarily purchase Russian crude oil already on tankers at sea to help it cope with the cuts to Middle East supply.
The new moves could be ‚Äčannounced as soon ‚Äčas Monday, ‚Ā†the sources said.
"President Trump and his entire energy team have had a strong game ‚Äčplan to keep the energy markets stable well ‚Äčbefore ‚Ā†Operation Epic Fury began, and they will continue to review all credible options," said Taylor Rogers, a White House spokesperson, ‚Ā†using ‚Äčthe administration's term for the war.
"Any ‚Äčpolicy announcement will come directly from the President or his team," Rogers said.

item.title: Kremlin: Putin, in a phone call with Trump, shares¬†proposals to end Iran war quickly
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 9 (Reuters) - Russian President Vladimir Putin, in a telephone conversation on Monday with ‚ÄčU.S. President Donald Trump , put forward proposals for a quick ‚ÄĆsettlement of the conflict around Iran , Kremlin foreign policy aide Yuri Ushakov said.
Ushakov, giving an account of the conversation to reporters, said the two ‚Äčleaders also discussed the conflict in Ukraine and noted that ‚ÄčRussian gains should prompt Kyiv's negotiators to move ‚Ā†towards a settlement.
He described the entire discussion as "very substantial" and ‚Äčsaid it would "likely have practical significance for further work between ‚Äčthe two countries".
Ushakov said Putin "expressed several thoughts aimed at a quick political and diplomatic end to the Iranian conflict, including contacts that have taken ‚Äčplace with leaders of Gulf states, the president of ‚ÄčIran and leaders of other countries".
He said Trump "offered his assessment of developments ‚ÄĆin ‚Ā†the U.S.-Israeli operation. Let me say that a very substantial and, without doubt, useful exchange of ideas took place."
Ushakov said Trump believed it was in the U.S. interest to see a "rapid end ‚Äčto the ‚Äčconflict in Ukraine ‚Ā†with a ceasefire and a long-term settlement".
Developments in Ukraine, he said, were characterised by "Russian troops ‚Äčadvancing quite successfully. This, as was noted, is ‚Äča ‚Ā†factor that should prompt the regime in Kyiv to finally go down the path of negotiating a settlement to the conflict".
Ushakov ‚Ā†also ‚Äčsaid the two presidents also discussed ‚Äčthe situation in Venezuela in the context of global oil markets.

item.title: Iranian women's soccer players given asylum after Trump speaks with Australian PM
item.description:
WASHINGTON/SYDNEY, March 10 (Reuters) - Australia on Tuesday agreed to provide visas to five of Iran's women's soccer team players to ‚Äčstay in the country after they sought asylum fearing persecution in their home nation.
The announcement came after U.S. President Donald Trump said he had ‚ÄĆspoken to Australian Prime Minister Anthony Albanese about the Iran women's soccer team in Australia following reports that the players had requested asylum.
The remaining players are in a hotel on the Gold Coast, Home Affairs Minister Tony Burke told a news conference, adding that he had also offered the other team members the chance to stay in Australia.
"I was able to tell ‚Äčfive members of the Iranian women's soccer team that they are welcome to stay in Australia, that they are safe here, and they should ‚Äčfeel at home here," Burke told reporters.
The Iranian women's soccer team are in Australia for the Asian Cup tournament.
Iran's semi-official ‚Ā†Fars news agency said five players in Australia for the Asian Cup secretly left the team hotel with Australian police, and media reports said they were ‚Äčseeking Australian government assistance after "breaking free".
Having initially posted on social media that Australia was "making a terrible humanitarian mistake" by allowing the team to be sent back home, Trump said in a ‚Äčlater post that he had spoken to Albanese and that the Australian leader was "doing a very good job having to do with this rather delicate situation".
Trump said five members of the Iran squad "have already been taken care of, and the rest are on their way.
"Some, however, feel they must go back because they are worried about the safety of their families, ‚Äčincluding threats to those family members if they don‚Äôt return," he said.
The Iranian team's campaign in the Australian-hosted Asian Cup tournament started just as the U.S. and ‚ÄčIsrael launched air strikes on Iran , killing the Islamic Republic's Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei. They were eliminated from the tournament on Sunday after losing 2-0 to the Philippines .
In his earlier post Trump said members of ‚ÄĆthe team ‚Ā†would "likely be killed" if forced to return to Iran. "The U.S. will take them if you won‚Äôt, " he added.
Soccer's global players' union FIFPRO said on Monday there were serious concerns for the welfare of the Iran team after they were labelled for refusing to sing their national anthem before a game.
The players' decision to stand in silence during Iran's anthem before their first match against South Korea was labelled by a commentator on Islamic Republic of Iran Broadcasting as the "pinnacle of dishonour".
Iranian ‚Äčmedia quoted Farideh Shojaei, vice president for ‚Äčwomen's affairs at the Iranian Football ‚Ā†Federation, as saying the team had left the hotel through the back door with the police.
"We have contacted the embassy, the football federation, the foreign ministry and anywhere possible to see what will happen," she said. "We have even spoken with ‚Äčthe families of these five players.‚ÄĚ
Iranian media named the players as Zahra Sarbali, Mona Hamoudi, Zahra Ghanbari, Fatemeh Pasandideh ‚Äčand Atefeh Ramazani-Zadeh.
It said ‚Ā†other team members were still in Australia and quoted Shojaei as saying the squad had planned to return to Iran via Dubai, but the United Arab Emirates had not allowed them to do so.
It said efforts were now expected to be made for the team to return via Malaysia and Turkey.
The Iranian team sang their national anthem and saluted ‚Ā†before their second ‚Äčmatch against Australia , sparking fears among human rights campaigners that the women had been coerced by ‚Äčgovernment minders.
Australia granted emergency humanitarian visas to over 20 members of the Afghanistan women's cricket team after the Taliban returned to power in 2021.

item.title: Cuban university students stage rare protest amid energy, internet shortages
item.description:
HAVANA, March 9 (Reuters) - A group of just over 20 Cuban university students staged a rare, hours-long protest at ‚Äčthe University of Havana on Monday, angry over class disruptions amid a U.S. oil blockade that has contributed to ‚ÄĆa near collapse of Cuba's electrical grid.
Students sat under a hot mid-morning sun, shading themselves with umbrellas while trading barbs with several of the university's professors and administration staff, who arrived shortly after the "sit-in" began.
"None of us wanted to get up early today in the dark, make a thermos of coffee, and sit ‚Äčhere in the sun," said Anabel Oliva, a 20-year-old university student who spoke before the group. "But there was no other ‚Äčway."
Students said officials had failed to take adequate measures to prevent major disruptions to classes as the ‚Ā†United States has ratcheted up sanctions and pressure on the Caribbean island nation.
The United States in January cut off oil shipments from ‚ÄčVenezuela, which further decimated an already failing electrical grid, starving it of fuel and forcing ripple effects on transportation, food supply and internet ‚Äčcommunications.
Cuba announced a series of measures in January, including distance learning as during the COVID-19 pandemic, that officials said would assure continuity for students of the island's once-robust higher-education system.
Students said those measures were not enough, and opted to protest to ensure their voices were heard.
Jose Julian Diaz, a university professor and ‚Äčformer student representative, told Reuters the extraordinary circumstances required dialogue.
"These kinds of scenarios are really rare," he said in an interview. "I think we ‚Äčhave to learn to have these discussions with students."
University security initially prohibited the protesting students from sitting on the stone steps at the main entrance ‚ÄĆof the ‚Ā†University of Havana, blocking the entrance with a long rope and a sign that read: "Do not pass, do not sit."
The students eventually walked up the steps and past the rope, however, prompting a rush of security guards who were eventually called off by Higher Education Vice Minister Modesto Ricardo Gomez.
The vice minister told reporters the students had raised legitimate concerns, and blamed the ‚ÄčTrump administration's tightening sanctions for ‚Äčthe problems. He said there ‚Ā†would be no repercussions for those who protested.
"There are no consequences whatsoever. They have a concern, and we are addressing it. It will always be like this," Gomez said.
While the minister spoke to reporters, ‚Äčthe students left the steps and entered the university with administration officials and teachers, declining requests for ‚Äčinterviews.
The minister said ‚Ā†the dialogue would continue inside the university.
One student, who did not give his name to a reporter but spoke before cameras, said there would be more protests if the issues were not resolved.
"We are willing to engage in dialogue if it is to represent our demands, but if ‚Ā†dialogue, as ‚Äčhas already happened, does not work, we will sit down here again.‚ÄĚ
Protests of any ‚Äčkind in Communist-run Cuba are exceedingly rare. Cuba's 2019 constitution grants citizens the right to protest, but a law more specifically defining that right is stalled in the ‚Äčlegislature, leaving those who take to the street in legal limbo.

item.title: Trading Day: Wall Street roars back on war resolution hopes
item.description:
ORLANDO, Florida, March 9 (Reuters) - Stocks in Asia and Europe tumbled on Monday as the Iran war entered its second week and oil prices surged as much as 30% above $100 a barrel. But Wall Street rallied and oil later sank after President Donald Trump indicated the war may soon be over.
In my column today I put ‚ÄčWall Street's resilience under the microscope. As selling snowballs across other equity markets, why has the global avalanche not yet engulfed U.S. ‚ÄĆstocks? Are there reasonable explanations, or is complacency setting in?
If you have more time to read, here are a few articles I recommend to help you make sense of what happened in markets today.
Today's Key Market Moves
Today's Talking Points
* Central bank paralysis
There can be no doubt now - the eye-watering surge in oil prices since the U.S. and Israel launched their joint attack on Iran on February 28 has put central banks in an unenviable bind. Price pressures are clearly intensifying, but the impact of $100 a barrel oil on economic activity will be ‚Äčharsh.
Do they hike rates to nip inflation in the bud, or adopt a more dovish stance in the face of rising unemployment and potential recession? In ‚Äčthe U.S., the labor market was already creaking, household savings are run down, and now gas prices are soaring. Higher borrowing costs will hurt the average consumer. But so will ‚ÄĆhigher inflation.
* ‚Ā†Pre-war price pressures
Even before the Middle East crisis erupted, price pressures around the world were bubbling up. Figures on Monday showed that consumer inflation in China jumped in February to the highest in three years, Mexico 's inflation rose above the central bank's target, and real wages in Japan rose for the first time in 13 months.
With oil smashing through $100 a barrel - and now up considerably on the same period last year - inflation signals are only pointing one way. U.S. PCE inflation figures for February due ‚Äčout later this week are expected to ‚Äčfollow a similar path, rising further ‚Ā†above 3%.
* To tap or not to tap?
Countries around the world are suddenly scrambling for ways to cushion the economic blow of $100 oil. Releasing emergency oil reserves is an obvious option, but it seems like it's not going to be exercised ‚Äčjust yet. G7 countries discussed it on Monday but say there's no immediate supply shortfall. Not yet, anyway.
Elsewhere, China has capped ‚Äčfuel prices, South Korea is ‚Ā†considering a similar move for the first time in 30 years, and Japan is preparing for a possible release of crude and could draw down cash reserves set aside for emergency spending. Authorities will be reluctant to raise interest rates, so they may need to get inventive.
What could move markets tomorrow?
Want to receive Trading Day in your inbox every weekday morning? Sign up for my newsletter here .
Opinions expressed are those of the author. They do not reflect the views of Reuters News, which, ‚Äčunder the Trust Principles , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: US considering selling oil from strategic reserve, US energy chief says
item.description:
PLATTEVILLE, Colorado, March 9 (Reuters) - The U.S. is considering coordinating ‚Äčsales of oil from the U.S. Strategic Petroleum Reserve with ‚ÄĆreleases from other countries as prices soar during the war on Iran , Energy Secretary Chris Wright said on Monday.
Wright also said the U.S. has "some other options" on allowing ‚Äčmore sales of Russian oil held in tankers on the ‚Äčwater in Asia. Late last week, for instance, Washington issued ‚Ā†a 30-day waiver allowing the sale of Russian crude currently ‚Äčstranded at sea to continue to India.
"We are talking about coordinated releases ‚Äčfrom the SPR," Wright told reporters gathered at a natural gas plant in Colorado. The U.S. SPR , located on the coasts of Texas and Louisiana, holds 415 million ‚Äčbarrels of oil, more than what the entire world uses in ‚Äčfour days.
Global and domestic oil futures settled on Monday at the highest level ‚ÄĆsince ‚Ā†August 2022 as the war led by the U.S. and Israel stranded tankers and shut in oil production in the Middle East.
The International Energy Agency, the West's energy watchdog, on Monday called for a coordinated ‚Äčrelease of oil, ‚Äčand G7 countries ‚Ā†agreed to closely monitor developments in the energy markets. No release has yet been announced.
Fatih Birol, the ‚ÄčIEA head, told G7 finance ministers on Monday that ‚Äčhis ‚Ā†group's members hold more than 1.2 billion barrels of public emergency oil stocks and a further 600 million barrels of industry stocks are under ‚Ā†government ‚Äčobligation.
The U.S. was not considering imposing restrictions ‚Äčon exports of U.S. energy as a way to control prices, Wright said.

item.title: Canadian PM, Qatari emir discuss diplomacy to prevent wider war in Middle East
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney spoke with Qatar's Emir Sheikh ‚ÄčTamim bin Hamad al-Thani on Monday about avoiding a wider ‚ÄĆconflict in the Middle East , Carney's office said.
Here are some details:
‚ÄĘ They "underscored the importance of intensifying diplomatic engagement to avoid a wider conflict with ‚Äčglobal consequences," Carney's office said in a statement.
‚ÄĘ The statement ‚Äčsaid the leaders condemned Iranian missile and drone attacks ‚Ā†on Qatar and Carney expressed Canada's solidarity with Qatar.
‚ÄĘ The U.S. ‚Äčand Israel began attacking Iran on February 28. Iran responded with its ‚Äčown strikes against Israel and Gulf states with U.S. bases. U.S. President Donald Trump has thus far offered shifting objectives and timelines for the war that ‚Äčhas killed scores in Iran , including Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei .
‚ÄĘ "In accordance with ‚Äčinternational law, the leaders called for an immediate end to attacks on civilians ‚ÄĆand ‚Ā†civilian infrastructure that only serve to destabilise the region and worsen the global economic and energy situation," Carney's office said, adding he and the Qatari emir discussed efforts to de-escalate the Iran war .
‚ÄĘ ‚ÄčAt least 1,230 ‚Äčpeople have been ‚Ā†killed in Iran, including 175 schoolgirls and staff killed in a missile strike on a primary ‚Äčschool in southern Iran, according to non-profit humanitarian ‚Äčgroup Iranian ‚Ā†Red Crescent Society.
‚ÄĘ The Iranian army additionally says over 100 were killed after a U.S. submarine sank an Iranian warship off Sri ‚Ā†Lanka's ‚Äčcoast last week.
‚ÄĘ Eleven civilians have been ‚Äčkilled in Israel, according to Israeli tallies. The U.S. military has said seven of ‚Äčits service members have been killed.

item.title: Alexander brothers, top NY real estate brokers, convicted at sex trafficking trial
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 9 (Reuters) - Three ‚Äčwealthy brothers, including two of New York's top real estate brokers, were found ‚ÄĆguilty on Monday at a closely watched sex trafficking trial over allegations they lured women and girls to exclusive parties where they drugged and raped them.
Oren Alexander, Tal Alexander and Alon Alexander had each pleaded not ‚Äčguilty to all charges they respectively faced in an indictment alleging trafficking and other criminal ‚Äčconduct related to seven victims.
Oren and Tal Alexander co-founded the luxury real ‚Ā†estate brokerage Official, while Alon Alexander was an executive at a private security company.
Defense lawyers ‚Äčhad contended that the women were willing participants who were not paid to have sex.
Tal Alexander ‚Äčfaced seven charges in total, and his twin brothers Oren and Alon faced six.
Over the course of a six-week trial in Manhattan federal court, eleven women testified that one or more of the brothers sexually ‚Äčabused them.
In his closing argument on March 3, prosecutor Andrew Jones said the brothers masqueraded ‚Äčas party boys but really were predators.
"They used a consistent playbook to lure, isolate and rape their victims," ‚ÄĆJones ‚Ā†said. "They did it with callousness and a perverse sense of pride."
Defense lawyers acknowledged that the brothers had discussed sex amongst themselves in crass terms and behaved in ways that angered women with whom they had relationships. But they argued the sexual encounters at issue were consensual.
"He should ‚Äčbe and is embarrassed ‚Äčby how he behaved ‚Ā†over these years," Howard Srebnick, a lawyer for Alon Alexander, said in his closing argument on March 3. "That doesn't make the conduct a ‚Äčcrime."
The three brothers were arrested in December 2024 and have been ‚Äčjailed since ‚Ā†then at Brooklyn's Metropolitan Detention Center.
Each brother had his own legal defense team. Oren Alexander's lawyers include Marc Agnifilo and Teny Geragos, who last year defended rapper and music mogul Sean "Diddy" Combs at his ‚Ā†sex ‚Äčtrafficking trial.
Combs is serving a four-year prison sentence after being ‚Äčconvicted on prostitution charges, but acquitted of the more serious sex trafficking charges he faced that could have landed him ‚Äčin prison for life.

item.title: FBI examines Arizona election records in widening probe of Trump vote fraud claims
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 9 (Reuters) - The FBI has obtained records related ‚Äčto the 2020 election in Arizona , a top state lawmaker said on Monday, as federal ‚ÄĆlaw enforcement continues to pursue President Donald Trump's false claims that his defeat in that campaign was the result of voting fraud.
The FBI subpoenaed the Arizona Senate last week for material connected to a Republican-led audit of the 2020 election ‚Äčresults in Maricopa County, the most populous county in the state, Arizona Senate President ‚ÄčWarren Petersen said in a social media post.
"The FBI has the records," Petersen, ‚Ā†a Republican, wrote in the post.
The demand for information from Arizona is the latest instance of ‚Äčthe Trump administration deploying U.S. law enforcement and intelligence agencies to gather evidence of alleged irregularities in the 2020 ‚Äčelection, despite courts and audits repeatedly rejecting claims of widespread voter fraud.
It was not immediately clear what crimes the FBI may be investigating. An FBI spokesperson did not immediately respond to a request for comment. A spokesperson for ‚ÄčPetersen declined to elaborate on his social media post.
The FBI in January seized 2020 ballots from a Georgia ‚Äčcounty elections center as part of a court-approved search of the facility. Fulton County has sued for the ‚ÄĆreturn of ‚Ā†the material, alleging the search violated the U.S. Constitution and relied on discredited allegations.
The 2021 Maricopa County audit, which was run by Trump's Republican allies in Arizona, largely affirmed the initial 2020 election results, finding slightly more votes for Trump's Democratic opponent Joe Biden . Trump's loss in Maricopa County was key ‚Äčto his narrow defeat to ‚ÄčBiden statewide.
The audit ‚Ā†made several claims of election irregularities, which county officials vigorously disputed .
"What the Trump administration appears to be pursuing now is not a legitimate law enforcement inquiry," ‚ÄčArizona State Attorney General Kris Mayes, a Democrat, said in a statement. "It ‚Äčis the weaponization ‚Ā†of federal law enforcement in service of crackpots and lies."
The investigations into Georgia and Arizona, two states that have been hotly contested in recent presidential elections, come as Trump has spoken openly about the ‚Ā†federal government taking ‚Äčcontrol of elections in certain unspecified areas of the ‚Äčcountry.
Trump has put a lawyer, Kurt Olsen, who backed the "Stop the Steal" movement to overturn the 2020 election results, i n charge ‚Äčof an effort to reexamine the 2020 vote.

item.title: Colombia's ruling party to be top force in Senate, but without majority
item.description:
BOGOTA, March 9 (Reuters) - Colombia's ruling party, the left-leaning Historic Pact, emerged from Sunday's elections as the strongest political force in the incoming Senate, ‚Äčanalysts said on Monday, although its failure to secure an absolute ‚ÄĆmajority means it will need to form coalitions and may struggle if Colombia's divided right wing wins the presidency.
The party, which brought President Gustavo Petro to power in 2022, ‚Äčwon 25 out of 102 Senate seats in Sunday's legislative elections, according ‚Äčto official results. Finishing second was the right-wing Democratic Center, led by ‚Ā†former President Alvaro Uribe, with 17 seats.
The two forces will likely jostle ‚Äčto make deals with other parties in the fragmented Senate, which include Colombia's ‚ÄčLiberal Party with 13 seats, the Green Alliance with 10, the Conservative Party with 10, La U with nine, and Radical Change with seven. Eleven other seats were distributed among ‚Äčvarious smaller parties.
In the House of Representatives, results from the electoral authority ‚Äčshow the opposition Democratic Center leading with 32 out of 182 seats, followed by the ‚ÄĆLiberal ‚Ā†Party with 31, and the Historic Pact with 29. The Conservative Party obtained 18 seats, Radical Change 13, and the Green Alliance 7.
Ivan Cepeda, Historic Pact's presidential candidate in May's presidential election, described the legislative election results as a "categorical ‚Äčvictory," saying his ‚Äčparty had become ‚Ā†the primary political force in Congress.
However, analysts suggest that if Cepeda loses the presidential election to right-wing candidates Paloma ‚ÄčValencia or Abelardo De La Espriella, his party will become ‚Äčthe main ‚Ā†opposition force.
Ariel Avila, a re-elected senator from the Green Alliance, believes that Colombia's next president, whether from the right or the left, will have a tough ‚Ā†time getting ‚Äčthings done, predicting a "vetocracy" where lawmakers block parties ‚Äčsimply because they come from the opposing side.
"It is a very fragmented Congress and it is ‚Äčdifficult to mend," Avila said.

item.title: Alexander Butterfield, White House aide who exposed Nixon's taping system, dead at 99
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 9 (Reuters) - Alexander Butterfield, the White House aide who disclosed Richard Nixon's secret audio taping system, providing the "smoking gun" of the Watergate scandal that brought down the president, has died at age 99.
The death of Butterfield, whose revelation about the listening devices and recording system set off a ‚Äčroaring legal battle over the president's right of executive privilege, was confirmed by his wife Kim to The Washington Post and the New York Times.
Both newspapers said he died a ‚ÄĆmonth shy of his 100th birthday at his home in the seaside La Jolla area of San Diego, but no cause of death was cited.
Butterfield once told journalist Alicia Shepard he disliked being known as the man who exposed the existence of the tapes because it made it seem as if he had "eagerly and breathlessly" told the Watergate congressional committee about them.
A native of Pensacola, Florida, who grew up in California, Butterfield attended UCLA before joining the U.S. Air Force in 1948 and went on ‚Äčto serve as a combat pilot during the Vietnam War, commanding a squadron of tactical reconnaissance aircraft. He later served as a military assistant to a high-ranking Pentagon aide, gaining White House exposure ‚Äčin that job.
Butterfield finally left the Air Force to join the White House staff as a deputy to Nixon's chief of staff, H.R. Haldeman, an old friend ‚Ā†from UCLA. Among Butterfield's White House duties was keeping a historical record of the presidency, which included overseeing installation of the voice-activated taping system.
Butterfield had left the White House for the top job at the Federal Aviation ‚ÄčAdministration by the time the investigation into the June 17, 1972, break-in at Democratic headquarters in the Watergate building was heating up.
He was one of the few in the White House who knew about the recording system, ‚Äčand upon learning that he would be questioned by the Senate Watergate Committee, known officially as the Select Committee on Presidential Campaign Activities, Butterfield resolved neither to lie nor volunteer information.
A Republican staff attorney for the committee was questioning him in a private preliminary session when he asked if the White House had a recording system. Butterfield reluctantly acknowledged there was.
On July 16, 1973, three days after his initial closed-door disclosure, Butterfield appeared before the Senate committee at a televised meeting, and Fred ‚ÄčThompson, then counsel for the Republicans on the committee and a future actor and senator, asked the same question.
After a long pause, Butterfield said, "I was aware of listening devices, yes, sir."
It was stunning news for the nation ‚Äčbecause it meant there was an actual record of what Nixon said, when he said it and to whom he said it.
Butterfield said the recording system had been secretly taping conversations and meetings in the Oval Office, Nixon's office in ‚ÄĆthe Executive Office ‚Ā†Building and the Cabinet room, as well as four White House telephones. The purpose of the recordings, he said, was historical.
In a 1975 interview with People magazine, Butterfield said Nixon often forgot about the recorders and had disregarded advice to destroy the tapes because he never thought the Watergate affair would reach a point where he would have to surrender them.
"I'm sure that he hates me as much as anyone can," Butterfield said of his former boss, who died in 1994.
He said he thought Nixon should have resigned earlier.
"I don't feel awful about the president's resignation," he said. "Not at all."
A recording made six days after the Watergate break-in proved to ‚Äčbe Nixon's undoing - the "smoking gun" that showed he knew ‚Äčof the cover-up. He was heard agreeing to ‚Ā†a plan to have the break-in investigation halted for national security reasons.
The U.S. Supreme Court ultimately rejected White House claims of executive privilege and ordered Nixon to turn over the subpoenaed tapes as his public and political support withered. Rather than face impeachment and a Senate trial, he resigned on August 9, 1974.
Since he was not ‚Äčinvolved with the break-in or cover-up, Butterfield was never indicted, but his old friend Haldeman would be among several Nixon insiders who went to prison in ‚Äčthe scandal.
Butterfield was the focus ‚Ā†of the 2015 book "The Last of the President's Men" by Bob Woodward, the Washington Post reporter who helped break the Watergate story, and he provided Woodward with thousands of documents he had secretly removed from Nixon administration offices.
The documents and interviews with Butterfield portrayed what the former White House aide described as a "cesspool" within the administration and characterized Nixon as odd, isolated and resentful.
Butterfield said he frequently was the target of hostility from Nixon loyalists and told Time ‚Ā†magazine that long-time ‚ÄčNixon secretary Rose Mary Woods, who said she accidentally erased 18 1/2 minutes of those White House tapes, had disparaged him as ‚Äča "son of a bitch" who had "destroyed the greatest leader this country ever had."
Butterfield worked as an adviser to Oliver Stone on his 1995 film "Nixon" and had a cameo role in it as a White House staffer.
Butterfield's first marriage, to Charlotte Maguire, ended in divorce in 1985. ‚ÄčHe also had previously dated Audrey Geisel, widow of the children's author-illustrator Theodor Seuss Geisel, better known as Dr. Seuss.

item.title: Trump on Cuba: May or may not be a "friendly takeover"
item.description:
DORAL, Fla., March 9 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump said on Monday Cuba is in "deep ‚Äčtrouble" on a humanitarian basis and ‚ÄĆthat U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio was dealing with the issue which may or may ‚Äčnot be a "friendly takeover."
"He's dealing (with it) ‚Äčand it may be a friendly takover, ‚Ā†it may not be a friendly takeover. ‚ÄčWouldn't really matter because they're really down ‚Äčto...as they say, fumes. They have no energy, they have no money," Trump, speaking at a news ‚Äčconference in Doral, Florida told reporters.
The Cuban ‚Äčgovernment has said it is not in any high-level ‚ÄĆtalks ‚Ā†with the United States but has not outright denied press reports that U.S. officials may be in informal talks with Raul ‚ÄčGuillermo Rodriguez ‚ÄčCastro, the ‚Ā†grandson of former Cuban President Raul Castro.
Cuban exiles who are largely ‚Äčconcentrated in Miami have long dreamed ‚Äčof ‚Ā†overthrowing the Cuban government or seeing it fall and have in the past plotted ‚Ā†against ‚Äčthe government that was established ‚Äčby the late revolutionary leader Fidel Castro.

item.title: Trump says he discussed Ukraine and Iran conflicts with Putin
item.description:
DORAL, Florida, March 9 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump on Monday told reporters he had a "very good call" with ‚ÄčRussian President Vladimir Putin about Ukraine ‚Äčand the conflict in the Middle East.
"There's ‚Ā†tremendous hatred between President Putin and (Ukraine's) ‚ÄčPresident Zelenskiy. They can't seem to ‚Äčget it together, but I think it was a positive call on that subject," Trump said.
Trump ‚Äčadded that Putin "wants to be helpful" ‚Äčwith the Iran conflict.
"I said, 'You could be more helpful ‚ÄĆby ‚Ā†getting the Ukraine-Russia war over with. That will be more helpful,'" Trump told reporters in a press conference at ‚Äčhis Florida ‚Äčgolf club.
A ‚Ā†foreign policy aide for Putin said on Monday that Putin proposed ‚Äčan idea for a "quick political ‚Äčand ‚Ā†diplomatic end to the Iranian conflict."
Trump recently has expressed dismay about the ongoing conflict ‚Ā†between ‚ÄčRussia and Ukraine, which ‚Äčstarted more than four years ago.

item.title: Russian drone injures six by apartment block in Ukraine's Kharkiv
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - A Russian drone ‚Äčstruck an area near a ‚ÄĆhigh-rise apartment building on Monday in Ukraine's second-largest city, Kharkiv, ‚Äčinjuring six people, smashing ‚Äčwindows and setting cars ablaze, ‚Ā†the city's mayor said.
Ihor ‚ÄčTerekhov said a small child ‚Äčwas among the injured.
Kharkiv, located 30 km (18 miles) from the Russian ‚Äčborder, withstood early advances ‚Äčby Russian forces after their full-scale invasion ‚ÄĆin ‚Ā†February 2022 and has since been a frequent target of Russian air attacks.
Separately, ‚ÄčRussian shelling ‚Äčof ‚Ā†the southeastern city of Dnipro injured seven ‚Äčpeople, regional Governor Oleksandr ‚ÄčGanzha ‚Ā†said on Telegram. Ganzha posted photos online showing rubble ‚Ā†in ‚Äčstreets and damaged ‚Äčbuilding facades.

item.title: Americans expect gasoline prices to keep rising after Iran strikes, Reuters/Ipsos poll finds
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 9 (Reuters) - Most Americans think gasoline prices are ‚Äčgoing to rise in coming months following President Donald Trump 's decision to launch military strikes on Iran , and ‚ÄĆmany expect a protracted conflict, according to a Reuters/Ipsos poll that closed on Monday.
Some 67% of respondents in the four-day poll - including 44% of Republicans and 85% of Democrats - said they expect gas prices in the U.S. to get worse over the next year. ‚ÄčSixty percent of Americans expect U.S. military involvement in Iran will "go on for an extended period of ‚Äčtime," according to the poll.
U.S. and Israeli forces launched coordinated strikes on Iran on February ‚Ā†28, killing the nation's leader in an initial surprise attack.
The latest Reuters/Ipsos poll found just 29% of Americans ‚Äčapprove of the strikes, little changed from a 27% approval rate in a Reuters/Ipsos poll conducted in the hours immediately ‚Äčfollowing the start of the military campaign. Both polls had margins of error of about 3 percentage points.
The latest poll underscores the political risks Trump brings to his Republican Party ahead of the November midterm elections when Democrats hope to seize control from Trump's party ‚Äčof at least one chamber of Congress.
Some 64% of poll respondents - including one in four Republicans and nine ‚Äčin 10 Democrats - said Trump has not clearly explained the goals of U.S. military involvement.
Trump returned to the White House ‚ÄĆlast ‚Ā†year after promising to tame inflation and prevent the military from getting stuck in a foreign conflict. Since he launched strikes on Iran, U.S. gasoline prices have surged by roughly 50 cents a gallon and at least seven U.S. soldiers have been killed.
Gasoline prices are highly sensitive in the U.S., as one of the most immediate and visceral ‚Äčindications Americans have of increases ‚Äčand decreases in their ‚Ā†cost of living.
Energy prices were rising daily last week across the country and internationally, including during the weekend as the Reuters/Ipsos poll was conducted. Analysts expect weeks or months of ‚Äčhigher fuel prices worldwide even if the week-old conflict ends quickly.
Trump is considering ways to ‚Äčcombat surging ‚Ā†prices, but U.S. policy options could have limited sway over global oil markets. On Monday, he told reporters the military operation in Iran is ‚Äúahead of our initial timeline by a lot.‚ÄĚ
Forty-nine percent of Americans - including a third of Republicans and two-thirds ‚Ā†of Democrats - ‚Äčthink the war in Iran will have a mostly negative impact on ‚Äčtheir personal finances. About one in three Republicans said they were not sure how the war would affect their finances.
The Reuters/Ipsos poll, which ‚Äčwas conducted online, surveyed 1,021 U.S. adults nationwide.

item.title: Trump says he is 'disappointed' that Mojtaba Khamenei became Iran's supreme leader
item.description:
DORAL, Florida, March 9 (Reuters) - U.S. ‚ÄĆPresident Donald Trump said on Monday he was "disappointed" that ‚ÄčIran named Mojtaba ‚ÄčKhamenei to succeed his ‚Ā†slain father Ayatollah ‚ÄčAli Khamenei as the ‚Äčsupreme leader of the country.
"We think it's going to ‚Äčlead to just ‚Äčmore of the same problem ‚ÄĆfor ‚Ā†the country," Trump told reporters at a press conference in ‚ÄčFlorida.
When ‚Äčasked ‚Ā†whether the new leader had a ‚Äčtarget on his ‚Äčback, ‚Ā†Trump said it would be "inappropriate" to say ‚Ā†whether ‚Äčor not ‚Äčhe does.

item.title: National Bureau of Economic Research cuts ties with Larry Summers, WSJ reports
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - Former U.S. Treasury Secretary Larry Summers has lost his affiliation with The ‚ÄčNational Bureau of Economic Research, the ‚ÄĆWall Street Journal reported on Monday, citing an email from the research organization's president.
Losing the ‚ÄčNBER affiliation would be the latest ‚Äčfallout for Summers from his ties with ‚Ā†late convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein. ‚ÄčSummers said last month he would resign from ‚Äčteaching at Harvard University at the end of the academic year, months after receiving a lifetime ban ‚Äčfrom The American Economic Association.
NBER said ‚Äčan ad hoc committee reviewed Summers' conduct and recommended ‚ÄĆthe ‚Ā†termination of his appointment as a research associate with the agency, the report said. "He is no longer an NBER affiliate," ‚Äčwrote NBER ‚Äčpresident James ‚Ā†Poterba in an email to the Journal.
Documents released by the ‚ÄčU.S. House Oversight Committee showed a ‚Äčclose ‚Ā†relationship between Summers and Epstein that continued well after the disgraced financier's 2008 ‚Ā†conviction. ‚ÄčThere is no evidence Summers ‚Äčwas involved in Epstein's sex trafficking activities.

item.title: Australia to send missiles to UAE, deploy military surveillance aircraft
item.description:
SYDNEY, March 10 (Reuters) - Australia will deploy a military surveillance ‚ÄĆaircraft to the Middle East and send missiles to the United Arab Emirates but will not put troops on the ground in ‚ÄčIran, Prime Minister Anthony Albanese said on Tuesday.
Australia's ‚Äčmilitary support would help the Gulf countries defend ‚Ā†themselves against unprovoked attacks from Iran, Albanese said, stressing Australia ‚Äčwas "not a protagonist".
"Our involvement is purely defensive," Albanese told ‚Äčreporters. "And it's in defence of Australians who are in the region as well as in defence of our friends in the United ‚ÄčArab Emirates."
Albanese said Australia would deploy one of its ‚ÄčBoeing-manufactured E-7A Wedgetail airborne early warning and control system aircraft for ‚ÄĆan ‚Ā†initial four weeks to protect the airspace above the Gulf countries.
Advanced medium-range air-to-air missiles would be provided to the United Arab Emirates, following a phone call with President ‚ÄčMohammed bin ‚ÄčZayed Al Nahyan, ‚Ā†he added.
The war has effectively shut the Strait of Hormuz, a chokepoint for a ‚Äčfifth of global oil and liquefied natural ‚Äčgas, ‚Ā†as the conflict escalated.
Around 115,000 Australians were in the Middle East when the conflict began 11 days ago and ‚Ā†2,600 Australians ‚Äčhave now returned home by ‚Äčcommercial flights as several Gulf cities came under Iranian bombardment.

item.title: Trump says US is waiving certain oil-related sanctions to ensure supply
item.description:
DORAL, Florida, March 9 (Reuters) - President Donald Trump ‚Äčsaid on Monday that the ‚ÄĆUnited States is waiving certain oil-related sanctions as a way to ensure ‚Äčadequate oil supply and lower ‚Äčprices.
At a news conference at his ‚Ā†Doral golf club, Trump ‚Äčsaid oil prices have not spiked ‚Äčas much as he had feared.
Trump said the United States is waiving certain ‚Äčoil-related sanctions to cut ‚Äčprices.
"We have sanctions on some countries. We're ‚ÄĆgoing ‚Ā†to take those sanctions off till the Strait (of Hormuz) is up," Trump said.
He was not specific, ‚Äčbut the ‚ÄčUnited ‚Ā†States last week issued a temporary, 30-day waiver ‚Äčto allow for the ‚Äčsale ‚Ā†of Russian oil currently stranded at sea to India to alleviate ‚Ā†pressure ‚Äčon the global ‚Äčoil market.

item.title: Petrobras rejects extra diesel orders as prices in Brazil lag global market
item.description:
RIO DE JANEIRO, March 9 (Reuters) - Brazilian state-run oil company Petrobras (PETR3.SA) , opens new tab is refusing requests from fuel distributors for additional diesel volumes as domestic prices fall to a record discount versus ‚Äčglobal levels, four sources familiar with the matter told Reuters.
Industry data shows that diesel ‚ÄĆsold by Petrobras, the main supplier of the fuel in Brazil , is currently 85% cheaper than imports, as global prices rise due to the U.S.-Iran conflict in the Middle East .
The imbalance between the two prices is stalling domestic diesel sales, impacting the country's ‚Äčbooming agricultural sector during harvest season, said sources.
Petrobras is selling only the contractually obligated diesel amount ‚Äčto its distributors, said a company source, adding that the firm won't sell extra ‚Ā†so distributors can store it and make a profit when the state-run oil firm raises prices.
"We ‚Äčcannot hand out additional diesel now so distributors can stock up cheaply and then profit later," said the ‚Äčsource.
Petrobras CEO Magda Chambriard said last week the company does not pass short-term global volatility through to consumers and is still assessing the new oil price level before considering adjustments.
A spike in diesel prices would be the first and most immediate threat to Brazil's ‚Äčfarm sector from the Middle East conflict, raising costs for producers harvesting a record soybean crop and ‚Äčplanting corn they cannot afford to delay.
In Brazil, Petrobras is responsible for around 55% of diesel production, giving it outsized ‚ÄĆinfluence ‚Ā†over the price of fuel, said Sergio Araujo, head of Brazilian fuel importers association Abicom.
The steep discount is drawing buyers away from imported cargoes and private refiners, reshaping fuel flows and creating logistical strains that are leading to supply imbalances, Araujo said.
The uncertainty is already rippling into the ‚Äčagricultural hub of Rio Grande ‚Äčdo Sul, a ‚Ā†state with two refineries and normally ample diesel supply, where farmers have reported difficulty sourcing fuel.
Oil regulator ANP said on Sunday it would investigate complaints from rural ‚Äčproducers about supply constraints during the peak harvest period.
ANP said it contacted major ‚Äčsuppliers over ‚Ā†the weekend and concluded that Rio Grande do Sul "has sufficient stocks to ensure regular diesel supply."
Araujo said the issue is not with supply, but with buyers in the region looking to purchase at current Petrobras prices, while sellers ask ‚Ā†for higher ‚Äčprices to hedge against potential costs if the state-run firm ‚Äčadjusts them in the coming weeks.
"Sellers think they'll have to pay more to restock," said Araujo.
Petrobras did not immediately reply to a ‚Äčrequest for comment.

item.title: Democratic US senators demand immediate hearings on Iran war
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 9 (Reuters) - Six Democrats in the U.S. Senate on Monday said they are embarking on an intensified campaign for public hearings on the Iran war, with top Trump administration officials testifying under ‚Äčoath and to take steps to interrupt regular Senate business if Republicans resist.
Senator Cory Booker ‚ÄĆof New Jersey told reporters that the senior Democrats on the Foreign Relations and Armed Services committees have made the requests to Republican chairmen James Risch and Roger Wicker, respectively.
The senators said their goal is to bring a quick end ‚Äčto the Iran war and protect American troops, who already have suffered some casualties.
Aides to the ‚Äčcommittees did not immediately comment.
Senator Chris Murphy of Connecticut said such hearings should be ‚Ā†held next week, with Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth and Secretary of State and Acting National Security Adviser ‚ÄčMarco Rubio testifying.
Administration officials have held closed-door, classified briefings for Congress. Democrats argue that the American public needs ‚Äčto hear from them on the possible duration of the war, Trump's specific goals and related matters.
Republicans currently hold a narrow, 53-47 Senate majority, which gives them the power to control what legislation comes to the floor for debate.
But Booker noted ‚Äčthat individual senators have "a tremendous amount of power to disrupt the normal functioning of the Senate, as ‚Äčwell as certain privileges that we can exercise."
He said he would not detail the steps this group of senators is ‚ÄĆwilling to ‚Ā†take.
Calling the Iran war "the biggest military engagement since the war in Afghanistan," Booker said, "We are not going to let the Senate continue as business as usual."
Both the House of Representatives, which also is run by Republicans, and the Senate have rejected Democrats' calls for debate and votes on the War Powers Act to authorize ‚ÄčU.S. military attacks on Iran.
Trump ‚Äčhas provided a series ‚Ā†of rationales, often changing, for unilaterally launching the war.
The results of a Reuters/Ipsos poll released on Monday showed deep public discontent over the war.
Sixty percent of Americans expect ‚Äčthe U.S. military engagement to continue "for an extended period of time," causing gasoline ‚Äčprices to ‚Ā†rise and only 29% of adults approve of the attacks, the poll found.
While Republicans mainly have stood by as President Donald Trump has taken actions that have bypassed Congress' powers to appropriate funding and to levy tariffs, ‚Ā†Democrats have ‚Äčhad some successes in getting the Senate to stand up to ‚ÄčTrump on those issues.
Joining Booker and Murphy in this effort are Senators Tammy Duckworth of Illinois, an Iraq war veteran, Adam Schiff ‚Äčof California, Tammy Baldwin of Wisconsin and Tim Kaine of Virginia.

item.title: US says UN aid to Afghanistan needs evaluation
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - Despite what it called a humanitarian "disaster" in Afghanistan, the U.S. said on Monday international assistance to the country should be evaluated, given Taliban "intransigence" and ‚Äčits exclusion of the female population from basic rights.
Speaking to a ‚ÄĆU.N. Security Council meeting on Afghanistan, the U.S. ambassador to the United Nations , Mike Waltz, noted that the budget for the United Nations Assistance Mission in Afghanistan (UNAMA), the mandate of which is ‚Äčup for renewal next week, is the largest of any special U.N. ‚Äčmission in the world.
"In light of the Taliban's intransigence, we must ‚Ā†carefully evaluate the utility of international assistance and engagement in Afghanistan," Waltz said, ‚Äčeven as he highlighted an ongoing "humanitarian disaster" there.
"This council must consider carefully the ‚Äčfunds we collectively provide for this mission's budget, when the mission's female national staff are not even able to go into the office to work," he added.
Afghanistan under the Taliban faces one ‚Äčof the world's most pressing humanitarian crises.
According to the U.N. World Food Programme, ‚Äčmore than 17 million Afghans - or one-third of the population - are facing acute food shortages, ‚ÄĆincluding 4.7 ‚Ā†million facing emergency levels of hunger.
The temporary head of UNAMA, Georgette Gagnon, told the meeting Afghanistan had "urgent" humanitarian needs and the humanitarian crisis there had worsened due to funding cuts. She said humanitarian agencies aimed to assist 17.5 million Afghans ‚Äčin 2026 through an ‚Äčappeal for $1.71 billion, ‚Ā†but this was currently only 10% funded.
Gagnon said Afghanistan's nearly two-week conflict with Pakistan had had "punishing human and economic costs" ‚Äčand the Iran war on its other border was causing ‚Äčprices of ‚Ā†basic commodities to rise.
She said some positive developments showed the value of international engagement, including the Taliban ban on opium poppy cultivation. She warned that if rights and ‚Ā†humanitarian issues ‚Äčwere not dealt with, Afghanistan could "once again become ‚Äča driver of regional and global instability in the form of outmigration, terrorism, narcotics and more."

item.title: Indonesia says 7 killed in landfill collapse as rescue operation ends
item.description:
JAKARTA, March 10 (Reuters) - Indonesian authorities have ended rescue operations at a landfill collapse that killed ‚Äčseven people as all those who ‚ÄĆwere at the site have been accounted for, an official said on Tuesday.
